PART II
MINUTES, THIRTIETH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
First Session - Tuesday Evening
June 18, 2002
30-1 Assembly Called to Order and Opening Worship
The Thirtieth General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America gathered for the opening worship service at 7:30 p.m. on Tuesday,
June 18, 2002 in the Sanctuary of the Briarwood Presbyterian Church in
Birmingham, Alabama.
The Service of Divine Worship
THE MINISTRY OF PREPARATION
The Gathering of the Commissioners
“Prelude and Fugue in E-flat Major”
ST. ANNE Johann Sebastian Bach
Dr. Lawrence C. Roff, Assembly Organist
Professor, Knox Theological Seminary
The Call to Order Mr. Steve Fox, Retiring Moderator
The Prelude “Our Foundation” Arr. Allen
Briarwood Orchestra
The Corporate Confession of Sin Dr. Harry L. Reeder III
Senior Pastor, Briarwood Presbyterian Church
Leader: Lord God! Eternal and Almighty Father, we confess
and acknowledge that we are sinners and guilty of
treason against Your holy law, that we are conceived
in sin and prone to do evil, and that we have both
offended You and rebelled against You.
People: We have left undone those things we ought to have
done. We have done those things which we ought not
to have done. There is no good thing within us apart
from Your mercy. We are unable to save ourselves, O
Lord, and we do confess that we are sinners before
Your holy gaze.

<a id="ga30-p24"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=26 printed_page=24 -->

Leader: O Lord, not only forgive us, but move upon us that we
may grow in grace, deny ungodliness and worldly
desires, and live sensibly, righteously, and godly in
this present age as Your people who are zealous for
good deeds.
All: Our salvation is of Your grace and mercy, so may You
receive all the praise that is due Your holy name. For
from You and unto You are all things; so unto You be
glory forevermore. Amen.
The Private Confession of Sin Chimes
THE MINISTRY OF ADORATION
The Invocation and the Lord’s Prayer Rev. Clay Campbell
Pastor of Worship and Celebration
Briarwood Presbyterian Church
The Assurance of Pardon Romans 8:1
The Call to Worship Dr. Reeder
The Hymn of Adoration and Choir Processional

No. 53 “Praise to the Lord, the Almighty”
LOBE DEN HERREN
Praise to the Lord, the Almighty, the King of creation!
O my soul, praise Him, for He is thy health and salvation!
All ye who hear, now to His temple draw near,
Join me in glad adoration.
Praise to the Lord, who o’er all things so wondrously reigneth,
Shelters thee under His wings, yea, so gently sustaineth!
Hast thou not seen how thy desires e’er have been
Granted in what He ordaineth?
Praise to the Lord, Who doth prosper thy work and defend thee!
Surely His goodness and mercy here daily attend thee;
Ponder anew what the Almighty will do,
If with His love He befriend thee.
Praise to the Lord! O let all that is in me adore Him!
All that hath life and breath, come now with praises before Him!
Let the amen sound from His people again;
Gladly fore’er we adore Him.

<a id="ga30-p25"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=27 printed_page=25 -->

The Salutation of Worship Psalm 50 Rev. Campbell
Leader: The Mighty One, the Lord, has spoken and He has
summoned the earth from the rising to the setting of
the sun.
People: Out of Zion and the perfection of His beauty, God has
shone forth His glory.
Leader: May our God come forth and not keep silence.
People: For we will not be silent but we will praise You, 0
Lord. Let all the people praise You.
Leader: May the Lord be with you.
People: And also with you.
The Psalms, Hymns and Spiritual Songs
“He Is Exalted”1
He is exalted, the King is exalted on high -1 will praise Him.
He is exalted, forever exalted and I will praise His name!
He is the Lord. Forever His truth shall reign.
Heaven and earth - rejoice in His holy name.
He is exalted, the King is exalted on high!
“Almighty”2
(Refrain)
Almighty, most Holy God; Faithful through the ages.
Almighty, most Holy Lord; Glorious almighty God.
The beasts of the field, the birds of the air
Are silent to call out Your name.
The earth has no voice and I have no choice,
But to magnify God unashamed.
Let the rocks be kept silent for one more day.
Let the whole world sing out, let the people say: (Refrain)
Time marches on with innocence gone,
And a darkness has covered the earth.
But His Spirit still dwells; He speaks, “It is well.”
And the hopeless still offered new birth.
He will break the leash of death, it will have no sting.
Let the pris’ner go free, join the dance and sing. (Refrain)
1 Paris, Twila © 1985 Straightway Music (ASCAP) (a div. of EMI Christian Music Publishing)
/ Mountain Spring Music (Admin, by EMI Christian Music Publishing)
2 2. Watson, Wayne © 1990 by Material Music (Admin. By Word Music) and Word Music (a
div. of WORD, INC.) Used by permission, CCLI #124545

<a id="ga30-p26"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=28 printed_page=26 -->

Hymn No. 296 “All Hail the Power of Jesus’ Name!”
All hail the pow’r of Jesus’ name! Let angels prostrate fall;
Bring forth the royal diadem, and crown Him Lord of all;
Bring forth the royal diadem, and crown Him Lord of all.
Ye seed of Israel’s chosen race, ye ransomed of the fall,
Hail Him who saves you by His grace, and crown Him Lord of all;
Hail Him who saves you by His grace, and crown Him Lord of all.
Let ev’ry kindred, ev’ry tribe, on this terrestrial ball,
To Him all majesty ascribe, and crown Him Lord of all;
To Him all majesty ascribe, and crown Him Lord of all.
O that with yonder sacred throng, we at His feet may fall;
We’ll join the everlasting song, and crown Him Lord of all;
We’ll join the everlasting song, and crown Him Lord of all.
THE MINISTRY OF INTERCESSION
The Prayer of Intercession Dr. Frank M. Barker, Jr.
Pastor Emeritus
Briarwood Presbyterian Church
The Choral Response Chancel Choir
THE MINISTRY OF ACCLAMATION
The Worship of God with Our Offerings
The Choral Praise “Joy to the Heart” Scott
The Chancel Choir and Orchestra of
Briarwood Presbyterian Church
Look there! the Christ, our Brother comes resplendent from the gallows tree
And what He brings in His hurt hands is life on life for you and me.
Refrain: Joy! Joy! Joy to the heart all in this good day’s dawning!
Joy! Joy! Joy to the heart all in this good day’s dawning!
Good Jesus Christ inside His pain looked down Golgotha’s stony slope
And let the blood flow from His flesh to fill the springs of living hope. (Refrain)
Good Jesus Christ, our Brother, died in darkest hurt upon the tree
To offer us the worlds of light that live inside the Trinity. (Refrain)
Look there! the Christ, our Brother comes resplendent from the gallows tree
And what He brings in His hurt hands is life on life for you and me. (Refrain)

<a id="ga30-p27"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=29 printed_page=27 -->

The Presentation of the Offering of Praise
“All Creatures of Our God and King”
Let all things their Creator bless, and worship Him in humbleness,
O praise Him, alleluia! Praise, praise the Father, praise the Son,
And praise the Spirit, three in one, O praise Him, O praise Him,
alleluia, alleluia, alleluia!
The Confession of Faith
The Old Scot’s Confession Rev. Campbell
We confess and acknowledge one God alone, to whom alone we must
cleave, whom alone we must serve, whom only we must worship, and
in whom alone we put our trust. Who is eternal, infinite,
immeasurable, incomprehensible, omnipotent, invisible; one in
substance and yet distinct in three persons, the Father, the Son, and the
Holy Ghost. By whom we confess and believe all things in heaven
and earth, visible and invisible, to have been created, to be retained in
their being, and to be ruled and guided by His inscrutable providence
for such end as His eternal wisdom, goodness, and justice have
appointed, and to the manifestation of His own glory. Amen.
The Prayer of Thanksgiving and Illumination Rev. Campbell
THE MINISTRY OF THE WORD
The Sermon “The PCA.. .A Fortified City” Mr. Steve Fox
Jeremiah 1:4-19
THE MINISTRY OF EDIFICATION
The Hymn of Preparation
No. 254 “Alas! and Did My Savior Bleed” MARTYRDOM
Alas! and did my Savior bleed and did my Sovereign die!
Would He devote that sacred head for such a worm as I!
Was it for crimes that I had done He groaned upon the tree!
Amazing pity! Grace unknown! And love beyond degree!
But drops of grief can ne’er repay the debt of love I owe;
Here, Lord, I give myself away, ‘tis all that I can do.

<a id="ga30-p28"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=30 printed_page=28 -->

The Sacrament of Holy Communion Dr. Reeder
The Hymn of Victory
“Rise Up, O Men of God”
Rise up, O men of God! Have done with lesser things;
Give heart and soul and mind and strength to serve the King of kings.
Lift high the cross of Christ! Tread where His feet have trod;
As foll’wers of the Son of Man, rise up, O men of God!
THE MINISTRY OF CONSECRATION
The Pastoral Benediction Dr. Reeder
The Choral Benediction
“Go Forth Into the World in Peace” Rutter
Go forth into the world in peace; be of good courage;
Hold fast that which is good; render to no one evil for evil;
Strengthen the faint-hearted; support the weak;
help the afflicted; honor all people;
Love and serve the Lord, rejoicing in the pow’r of the Holy Spirit.
And the blessing of God Almighty, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost,
Be upon you and remain with you forever. Amen.
The Postlude “Fanfare” Mathias
Jamie McLemore, Organist
Briarwood Presbyterian Church
Following worship, the Assembly recessed for 20 minutes at 9:05 p.m.
30-2 Declaration of Quorum and Enrollment
Moderator RE Steve Fox reconvened the Assembly for business at
9:25 p.m. with prayer by TE Claude McRoberts. TE Harry Reeder, Senior
Pastor of Briarwood Presbyterian Church, greeted the Assembly on behalf of
Evangel Presbytery and Briarwood Presbyterian Church.
The Moderator declared a quorum present with 1028 Teaching Elders
and 453 Ruling Elders. [Note: These figures represent the number of
commissioners checked in at the start of the Assembly. For total enrollment,
see pg. 58.]

Teaching Elders: 1,122
Ruling Elders: 481
Total Enrollment: 1,603
Churches Represented: 784

30-3 Adoption of the Docket
Without objection, the third draft of the docket was declared adopted.
Without objection, Overture 19 [see text and action at 30-31, III, 18, p. 188]
was referred to the Committee of Commissioners on Mission to North
America.
30-4 Election of Moderator
The Moderator opened the floor for nominations for Moderator of the
30th General Assembly. TE Peter Lillback placed in nomination TE Anees
Zaka. TE Ligon Duncan placed in nomination TE Joseph Ryan. In
accordance with RAO 1-4, ballots were distributed. During the collection of
ballots, Hymn 347 “The Church’s One Foundation” was sung. TE Joseph
Ryan was elected Moderator 761-449.
The Chairman of the Administrative Committee, RE William “Bingy”
Moore, IV presented to the retiring Moderator a plaque in token of the
Assembly’s appreciation for his year of service as Moderator. TE Joe
Novenson led the Assembly in prayer.
30-5 Distribution of News Material
A motion that the General Assembly open the areas outside of the sanctuary
to the distribution of non-PCANews materials was adopted 592-524.
30-6 Election of Recording and Assistant Clerks and Assistant Parliamentarians
On nomination by the Stated Clerk, TEs David Dively, J. Robert Fiol,
and Steven Meyerhoff were elected recording clerks; REs Walter Lastovica
and Bill Stanway were appointed timekeepers; Tom Arledge, Frank M. Barker
III and RE Ed Nalley were appointed Sound Engineers; Dale Carrell was
appointed A-V Engineer; TE James A. Smith was appointed Chairman of the
floor clerks and RE Ric Springer Vice Chairman; Steve Lawton was appointed
Photographer; Mr. Jeff Bone was appointed Webmaster; Dr. Larry Roff was
appointed Assembly Organist; Mr. Bill Lloyd was appointed Computer
Technician; and Dr. Cub Culbertson was appointed On-Site Physician.
The Moderator appointed RE Sam Duncan and RE John B. White, Jr.
Assistant Parliamentarians.
30-7 Assembly Recessed
The Assembly recessed at 10:25 p.m. with prayer by TE Rick Tyson.

<a id="ga30-p59"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=61 printed_page=59 -->

Second Session - Wednesday Morning
June 19, 2002
30-8 Assembly Reconvened
The Assembly reconvened at 8:00 a.m. on June 19, 2002 with prayer by
TE Paul Wrigley and the singing of Psalm 121. The Moderator read from Acts
20, gave devotional comments on the text, and led the Assembly in prayer.
30-9 Report of the Stated Clerk
TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk, led the Assembly in prayer and
presented his report. [See Appendix A, p. 346.] The Stated Clerk reviewed
the statistical portions of his reports. He recognized the churches that were
added to or lost from the denomination since the last Assembly:

30-10 Presbytery Votes on BCO Amendments
The Assembly moved to consideration of amendments to the Book of Church Order proposed by previous Assemblies. Item 1 did not receive the
concurrence (2/3rds) of the necessary number of presbyteries. Items 2-6
received the concurrence (2/3rds) of the necessary number of presbyteries and
were adopted by the Assembly.
ITEM 1: Amend BCO 32-19 by deleting the current language (indicated by
strikeouts) and replacing it with the following new language.
M28GA, 28-72, III, 12, p. 289
Deferred To The 30lh General Assembly, 29-12, p. 51
That BCO 32-19 be amended
32 19. No professional counsel shall be permitted as such to appear
and plead in cases of process i-n any court; but an accused person may,
if he desires it, be represented-before the Session by any communing
member of the same particular church, or before any other court, by
any member of that court. A member of the court so employed shall
not be allowed to sit in judgment in the case.
32-19. The accused shall be entitled to be represented before the
courts of this church by counsel, who shall have the right
to be heard by oral and/or written argument. Any such
counsel shall be a communing member of the
Presbyterian Church in America. Any member of a court
so engaged shall not be allowed to sit in judgment of the
case. No person shall accept any fee or other
remuneration for any service rendered as counsel, but
reimbursement may be made to cover any personal

<a id="ga30-p61"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=63 printed_page=61 -->

expenses incurred or to be incurred by such counsel in
the course of representation.
Defeated

ITEM 2 Amend BCO 14-1 (12) by changing the wording in the second
to the last paragraph and by adding a second paragraph as follows:
M29GA, 29-44, III, 1, p. 187

Therefore Be it Resolved that BCO paragraph 14-1(12) in the second
to the last paragraph be revised as follows:
“Committee on Christian Education and Publications,
Committee on Mission to North America, Committee on
Mission to the World, and Committee on Reformed
University Ministries shall consist of fifteen (15) men
divided into five classes of three men each, with two men
being TEs and one RE or two men being REs and one TE on
alternate years on odd years and four men each on even years,
elected to serve five-year terms. Committees on Christian
Education and Publications, Mission to North America,
Mission to the World and Reformed University Ministries

<a id="ga30-p63"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=65 printed_page=63 -->

shall have one ruling elder and one teaching elder as alternates
to fill any vacancy that may occur during the year.”

Be it further resolved, that the first class of three (3) men to serve
a five year term shall begin in 2003, should the 29th
General Assembly approve this Overture, the consent of
two-thirds (2/3) of the Presbyteries is given and the 30th
General Assembly approves the same; and that each year
thereafter there shall be an additional class of three (3)
men who shall serve a five year term.
Adopted

ITEM 3 Amend BCO 32-3 Regarding Sequence of Meetings of a Court
in Judicial Process, Amend BCO 32-4 Regarding Delivery Requirements for
Indictments and Citations, and Amend BCO 43-1 to Allow Complaint After
Appeal is Ruled Out of Order, Withdrawn or Abandoned as follows:
M29GA, 29-44, III, 11, p. 206
32-3. It is appropriate that with each citation the moderator or clerk
call the attention of the parties to the Rules of Discipline (BCO
27 through 46) and assist the parties to obtain access to them.
When a charge is laid before the Session or Presbytery, it shall

<a id="ga30-p65"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=67 printed_page=65 -->

be reduced to writing and nothing shall be done at the first
meeting of the court unless by consent of parties, except:
1. to appoint a prosecutor,
2. to order the indictment drawn and a copy, along with
names of witnesses then known to support it, served
on the accused, and
3. to cite all parties and their witnesses the accused to
appear and be heard at another meeting which shall
not be sooner than ten days after such citation.
At the second meeting of the court, the charges shall be read to
the accused, if present, and he shall be called upon to say
whether hebe guilty or not. If the accused confesses, the
court may deal with him according to its discretion; if he plead
and take issue, the trial shall proceed be scheduled and all
parties and their witnesses cited to appear. The trial shall
not be sooner than fourteen (14) days after such citation.
Accused parties may plead in writing when they cannot be
personally present. Parties necessarily absent should have
counsel assigned to them.
32-4. The citation shall be issued and signed by the moderator or
clerk by order and in the name of the court. He shall also
issue citations to such witnesses as either party shall nominate
to appear on his behalf. Indictments and citations shall be
delivered in person or in another manner providing
verification of the date of receipt. Compliance with these
requirements shall be deemed to have been fulfilled if a
party cannot be located after diligent inquiry or if a party
refuses to accept delivery.
43-1 a complaint is a written representation made against some act
or decision of a court of the Church. It is the right of any
communing member of the church in good standing to make
complaint against any action of a court to whose jurisdiction
he is subject, except that no complaint is to be considered ina
judicial case while an appeal is taken pending.
Adopted

ITEM 4 Amend BCO 35-7 and 32-18 Requiring Recording of
Testimony and Provision for Transcription as follows:
M29GA, 29-44, III, 12, p. 209
35-7 Every question put to a witness shall, if required, be reduced
to writing. When answered, it shall, together with the answer, be
recorded, if deemed by the court or by either party of sufficient
importance, and the testimony of the witness shall be read to him
for his approbation and subscription:
Proposed new 35-7 All testimony shall be recorded (transcription,
audiotape, videotape, or some other electronic means) and
witnesses informed of such prior to testifying. Such recording
becomes part of the Record of the Case. However, in order to be
referenced in written or oral briefs, such recording must be
transcribed and the transcription authenticated by the trial court.
The court of final appeal may assess the cost of transcription
equitably among the parties.
32-18 Minutes of the trial shall be kept by the clerk, which shall
exhibit the charges, the answer, written record of the testimony, as
defined by BCO 35-7, and all such acts, orders, and decisions of
the court relating to the case, as either party may desire, and also
the judgment...
Adopted

ITEM 5 Amend BCO 13-1 to Allow at Least Two Ruling Elders to Represent
Each Congregation in Presbytery Meetings.
M29GA, 29-44, III, 15, p. 218
Current text:
13-1 The Presbytery consists of all the teaching elders and churches within
its bounds that have been accepted by the Presbytery.—When the
Presbytery meets as a court it shall comprise all teaching elders and
one ruling elder from each congregation. Additional ruling elders may
represent their churches on the following basis: One ruling elder for
the first three hundred fifty --(3 50) communing members, plus one
additional ruling elder for each—additional—five hundred—(-500)
communing members or fraction thereof.
Proposed:

Now therefore Be it Resolved that the undersigned Session overtures
the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America
to amend to Book of Church Order, 13-1, to increase the
number of ruling elder commissioners to presbytery meetings
from each church from one (1) to two (2), with additional
commissioners for larger congregations, and

<a id="ga30-p70"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=72 printed_page=70 -->

Be It Further Resolved, that the Book of Church Order 13-1 be
amended by deleting the present paragraphs and substituting
the following, to wit:
The Presbytery consists of all the teaching elders and
churches within its bounds that have been accepted by the
Presbytery. When the Presbytery meets as a court it shall
comprise all teaching elders and ruling elders as elected by
their Session. Each congregation is entitled to two (2)
ruling elder representatives for the first 350 communing
members or fraction thereof, and one additional ruling
elder for each additional 500 communing members or
fraction thereof.
Adopted

30-11 Amendment to RAO
On recommendation from the Committee on Review of Presbytery
Records, the amendment to the RAO 7-5.c was adopted in accordance with
RAO\%.
Amend RAO 7-5.c., inserting the words “in at least”, so that the
section would now read:
“A chairman and vice-chairman for the following year shall be elected
by the committee from members who shall be serving in at least the
second year of their term.”

<a id="ga30-p74"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=76 printed_page=74 -->

[Rationale: This would allow the committee the freedom, should it so
choose, to elect a chairman and vice-chairman from among those
members who will be serving in the second or third year of their term.]

COMMUNICATION 3 from Westminster Presbytery
and the PCA Stated Clerk
“Request Change of Referral for Overture 7”
Dear Dr. Taylor,
At its April 13, 2002 Stated Meeting Westminster Presbytery directed me to
request that the Overture sent in January 2002 by Westminster Presbytery to
the General Assembly regarding the PCA Foundation, be referred to the Bills
and Overtures Committee of Commissioners for the 30th General Assembly.
Thank you for your attention to this matter.
Sincerely, in Christ,
/s/ D. Steven Meyerhoff
Stated Clerk

Dear Dr. Meyerhoff:
I am in receipt of your letter of April 15 stating Westminster Presbytery’s
request that that Overture #7 from Westminster Presbytery be assigned by me
to the Committee of Commissioners on Bills and Overtures.
It would be contrary to the “Rules of Assembly Operations” for me to accede
to your request.
10-5. Upon receipt the Stated Clerk shall refer to the Committee on Constitutional Business all overtures requesting amendment of the
Book of Church Order. Upon receipt the Stated Clerk shall forward
all overtures concerning presbytery boundaries or a new presbytery
to the Subcommittee on Boundaries appointed by and under the
jurisdiction of the Assembly’s permanent Committee on Mission to
North America which shall report to the Permanent Committee and

<a id="ga30-p79"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=81 printed_page=79 -->

shall report to the Assembly through the Committee of
Commissioners on Mission to North America. All other overtures
shall be referred by the Clerk to the appropriate Committee of
Commissioners. All overtures shall be printed in the Handbook with
reference for consideration indicated.
13-1. All business shall ordinarily come to the floor of the Assembly
for final action through Committees of Commissioners, except
reports of the Standing Judicial Commission, the Committee on Constitutional Business, the Committee on Review of Presbytery
Records, the Nominating Committee, and Ad Interim Committees,
which shall report directly to the Assembly.
The following Committees of Commissioners shall handle the
matters indicated:
Reports of Overtures, Committees, Resolutions or Communications
1. Administration Permanent Comm. Touching Administrative
matters
2. Bills and Overtures of general nature
3. Christian Education Permanent Comm. Touching Christian
and Publications Education Publications
4. Covenant College Trustees Touching Covenant
College
5. Covenant Seminary Trustees Touching Covenant
Seminary
6. Insurance & Annuities Trustees Touching insurance &
annuities
7. Interchurch Permanent Comm. Touching comity,
Relations cooperation relations
to other churches
8. Mission to North America Permanent Comm. Touching home missions
9. Mission to the World Permanent Comm. Touching world missions
10. PC A Foundation Trustees Touching PC A
Foundation
11. Ridge Haven Directors Touching Ridge Haven

You may recall that a similar request was made in 2000 that the Westminster
Overture #20 “Reject Covenant College’s Response to Protest” be considered
by the Committee of Commissioners on Bills and Overtures rather than the
Committee of Commissioners on Covenant College. The Stated Clerk did not
accede to that request on the same basis as cited above, namely “RAO” 10-5
and 13-1. The General Assembly sustained the Clerk’s referral.

<a id="ga30-p80"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=82 printed_page=80 -->

I do not have the authority to contravene or ignore the “RAO.” Insofar as I
know, the only way to refer Overture #7 to the Committee of Commissioners
on Bills and Overtures would be to suspend the “RAO.”
ARTICLE XVIII. AMENDMENT OR SUSPENSION OF RULES
The Rules of Assembly Operations may be amended or
suspended only by a two-thirds vote of those voting which must
also be a majority of the total enrollment of commissioners. A
motion to amend is debatable. A motion to suspend is not
debatable.
In short, it would be contrary to the “Rules of Assembly Operations” for me to
accede to your request.
Sincerely,
/s/ L. Roy Taylor
Stated Clerk, PCA

COMMUNICATION 4 from Westminster Presbytery
“Westminster Rescinds Withdrawal, Will Remain in PCA”
Dear Fathers and Brethren,
Greetings in the matchless name of our Savior, Jesus Christ!
We want to thank you for all the time, effort, and energy that you have spent
on our Presbytery over the past couple of years. As you know, we have had a
difficult time wrestling with how to deal with theological and ideological
differences among us. Although there are numerous issues about which not all
of us agree, nevertheless, we are happy to report that there has been a sweeter
spirit in our meetings over the past year or so. We believe that this is due to
the prayers of God’s people, and we thank you for all the prayers which you
have offered up on our behalf.
In January of this year, our Presbytery voted to rescind its determination to
withdraw from the Presbyterian Church in America. The motion carried
overwhelmingly, with less than a handful of votes against it. This is a truly
remarkable occurrence. We appreciate the pleading of the 29th General Assembly, as expressed in its pastoral letter to us, that we reconsider our
intention to break the ties of fellowship, and we are happy to report that we
have indeed determined to continue to walk together with our denomination.

<a id="ga30-p81"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=83 printed_page=81 -->

Our determination to remain in the Presbyterian Church in America is
reflective of our desire that the PCA become a leader church for our culture in
the twenty-first century. We believe that there are tremendous challenges for
the Body of Christ in this new millennium, from ethical issues (cloning, for
example), to persecution of God’s people, to the threat of false religious
systems (including Islam and Roman Catholicism). Throughout church
history, Presbyterians have always been leaders, and we are hopeful that our
beloved denomination can itself fulfill a central role in successfully addressing
these challenges.
We believe that in order for us to fulfill this crucial role, we must have a
balanced approach in the life of our denomination, so that we would exhibit
not only doctrinal purity, but also an evangelistic zeal to reach the lost, and a
personal piety that exemplifies the Lord Jesus Christ. Our deep desire is that
we in the Presbyterian Church in America, based upon the solid foundation of
our Confessional Standards, would be committed to world missions, and
would experience the reality of being grace- and gospel-driven in our lives and
in our dealings with one another.
We join with you in praying that the Lord of the Church would grant us the
grace to be involved in mission, for the transformation of the world. May we
give the Lord no rest until He makes Zion a praise in all the earth, and the
knowledge of the glory of the Lord covers the earth as the waters cover the
sea.
Fraternally yours,
Westminster Presbytery

COMMUNICATION 10 from Korean American Presbyterian Church
“Fraternal Greetings from KAPC”
Dear Dr. Taylor:
Greetings in the name of our blessed Lord and Savior!
On behalf of the Interchurch Relations Committee of the KAPC, I want to thank
you for your very kind invitation to send a fraternal delegate to your 30th General Assembly in Birmingham, AL.
We have a rotate [sic] schedule and send a delegate to all NAPARC assemblies
every other year. Therefore, our Committee will be unable to send a delegate
to the PCA this year.
We do wish you God’s blessings as you meet as a General Assembly. We are
thankful for your fellowship in the gospel, for the faithful witness you bear on
behalf of our Lord Jesus Christ and for the privilege of labouring [sic] together
with you in His kingdom.
Yours in Christ’s service
/s/ Duck Y. Won, General Secretary of KAPC
COMMUNICATION 11 from the United Reformed Churches in North
America
“Fraternal Greetings from URCNA”
Esteemed brothers in Christ,
From the United Reformed Churches in North America we send you hearty
greetings in the name of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. May the King of
the Church bless your deliberations during the busy but rewarding days of the
30th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America.
Thank you for the invitation to send an ecclesiastical observer to your
assembly. At this time we are unable to send one of the brothers, and thus we
convey our heartfelt best wishes and greetings by letter.
At present our small federation of churches consists of some 80 churches with
a little over 18,000 members distributed throughout the United States and

<a id="ga30-p83"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=85 printed_page=83 -->

Canada. Having organized in 1995, the Fourth Synod assembled in Escondido
CA last year. The bulk of the URC members have seceded from the Christian
Reformed Church over the last decade for reasons that relate to the faithful
understanding of the Scriptures and a fervent adherence to the Three Forms of
Unity, the confessions of the Reformed churches.
Synod Escondido focused on the doctrine of creation and decided, “God
created all things good in six days defined as evenings and mornings (Genesis
1, 2 and Exodus 20:11). This means that we reject any evolutionary teaching,
including theistic evolution, concerning the origin of the earth and of all
creatures (Heidelberg Catechism, Lord’s Day 9). ”
The Synod confirmed “Corresponding Relations,” the first phase of
ecumenicity, with the Orthodox Presbyterian Church, the Reformed Church in
the United States, the Free Reformed Churches, and the Orthodox Christian
Reformed Churches, and entered into the second phase of ecumenical relations
with the Canadian Reformed Churches, “Ecclesiastical Fellowship.” The URC
also is pursuing ecumenical relations with five more churches, including the
Presbyterian Church in America. It is our sincere desire to come to know your
denomination better, and our committee would like to begin a regular
correspondence with your ecumenical committee.
Again, we wish you the Lord’s most wonderful blessing,
In Christ’s service,
/s/ Rev. Richard Stienstra, Secretary
Committee for Ecumenical Relations and Church Unity
United Reformed Churches in North America
COMMUNICATION 12 from Reformed Church in the U.S.
“Fraternal Greetings from RCUS”
Dear Dr. Taylor:
Greetings in the name of our Lord!
Please accept my apology for not advising you sooner that the Reformed
Church in the U.S. will not be sending an observer to your 30th General Assembly.
At the time your letter of April 29, 2002 was received I was gearing up for our
own Synod and my good intentions to reply to your invitation in a timely
fashion fell by the wayside. Only this week did I realize my oversight.

<a id="ga30-p84"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=86 printed_page=84 -->

On behalf of the Interchurch Relations Committee of the RCUS, i do want to
thank you for your invitation to us to send an observer. For the present, the
RCUS has determined to maintain contact with NAPARC meeting. We will
look forward therefore to hearing of the work of your General Assembly later
this Fall from your report to NAPARC.
In the interim we wish you God’s blessings on your Kingdom labours [sic] as a
General Assembly and pray that the business of the GA will be done, and that
the issues before you will be resolved, in a Christ centered manner to His glory
and praise.
I remain respectfully yours,
In the service of Christ,
/s/ Rev. Ron Potter,
Interchurch Relations Committee of the RCUS
30-13 Appointment of Committee on Thanks
The Moderator appointed the following men to serve as the Committee
on Thanks: TE Henry Lewis Smith (Convener) and RE William Clay.
30-14 Personal Resolution #1 from RE John Mardirosian
The following resolution was received by the Assembly and adopted.

Whereas, Vincent Donato was ordained as an ruling elder on April 20,
1952 in the Bible Presbyterian Church of East Camden, NJ
(now the Evangelical Presbyterian Church of Mt. Laurel, NJ,
PCA); and

Whereas, he has served continuously in that capacity over the past 50
years; and

Whereas, he has been a faithful and dedicated servant of the Lord in
the discharge of his duties and responsibilities; and

Whereas, he has been faithful in the spreading of the gospel and has
been diligent regarding the purity of the Church of Christ;

Therefore, Be It Resolved that we, the General Assembly of the
Presbyterian Church in America, acknowledge and thank him
for his years of continuous dedicated service; and
Be It Finally Resolved, that we commend Vincent to the Lord for
many more years of service.

<a id="ga30-p85"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=87 printed_page=85 -->

30-15 Personal Resolution #2 from TE Henry Lewis Smith, TE Daren
Dietmeier, TE William Edgar, TE James L. Smith, Jr., RE Royce M. Cox,
TE John W. Thompson, and TE Bart Lester
Personal Resolution #2 was received and referred to the Committee
of Commissioners on Bills and Overtures. [See text and action at 30-53, III,
16, p. 269.]
30-16 Personal Resolution from TE Merle Messer et al.
On vote, a Personal Resolution from TE Messer was not received by
the Assembly.

Personal Resolution #3 was received and referred to the Committee
of Commissioners on Administration. [See text and action at 30-62, III, 28, p.
303.]
30-18 Committee of Commissioners on Interchurch Relations
TE Lewis Ruff, Committee of Commissioners Chairman, led in prayer
and presented the report. [See Appendix G, p. 541.] He introduced TE Will
Barker, member of the Interchurch Relations Committee, who introduced the
following fraternal delegates who addressed the Assembly: TE Nathan Trice
(OPC), TE Bill Evans (ARP), and RE Drew Gordon (RPCNA). TE Charles
Frost introduced TE Turgay Uchal, Istanbul Presbyterian Church, Turkic
World Presbyterian Church (TWPC), who addressed the Assembly and
presented a copy of their new BCO of the TWPC to RE John White, who then
led in prayer. Recommendations 1-6 were adopted.

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

A. Minutes of the Interchurch Relations Committee (IRC)
B. Report of the IRC to the General Assembly
C. Recommendations of the IRC to the GA

#### II. Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

A. The Committee discussed with TE Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk
of the PCA, the IRC policy regarding Fraternal Delegates and
Corresponding Delegates addressing the Assembly. The last
GA requested clarification concerning the policy. The
Committee of Commissioners is satisfied that effective
guidelines are in place.
B. The Committee discussed the recommendation of NAP ARC to
terminate the membership of the CRCNA due to the reasons

<a id="ga30-p86"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=88 printed_page=86 -->

stated in Attachment A, “Rationale for Action Regarding
CRCNA Membership in NAPARC” [see Appendix G, p. 549].
C. The Committee discussed corrections to the report of the
Permanent Committee.

#### III. Recommendations

1. That Fraternal Delegates, Corresponding Delegates, and
Ecclesiastical Observers be welcomed and invited to address
the Assembly. Adopted
2. That other visiting ministers, including the Rev. Turgay Uchal,
be introduced to the Assembly (BCO 13-13). Adopted
3. That the PCA enter into corresponding relations with the
United Reformed Churches of North America. Adopted
4. That the Assembly approve the termination of the membership
in the North American Presbyterian and Reformed Council of
the Christian Reformed Church in North America on the
following grounds:
• We believe that the CRCNA has had opportunity over
the last several years to reverse the controversial
action of CRCNA Synod 1995 in response to
NAPARC’s suspension the CRCNA from voting-
member status in NAPARC. In 1996 the process was
begun in NAP ARC (initiated by the PCA) to suspend
the CRCNA from NAPARC membership after
CRCNA Synod 1995 had approved the ordination of
women to the offices of ruling elder and minister. The
underlying hermeneutical issue was cited by the PCA
in correspondence between the two highest
judicatories of the PCA and CRCNA (Minutes of the
25th General Assembly of the PCA, 1997, pp. 375-
383). In 1998, the suspension from membership was
effected. CRCNA Synod 2000 did not reverse but
reaffirmed and approved the ordination of women to
the offices of ruling elder and minister.
• We believe that the CRCNA has acted contrarily to the
NAPARC constitution (II. Basis of the Council),
against the teaching of the Word of God and the Belgic
Confession, Article XXX by declaring the word male
in Article 3-a of the Church Order to be inoperative
and authorizing the churches under its jurisdiction
(should they desire) to ordain and install women in the

<a id="ga30-p87"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=89 printed_page=87 -->

offices of elder, minister, and evangelist, thus
circumventing her own constitution. Adopted
5. That the General Assembly commend the IRC Permanent
Committee for its work in shaping the report which became
the foundation for the action taken by NAPARC. Adopted
6. That the minutes of the IRC for the meetings of September 25,
2001, December 18, 2001, and March 26, 2002, be approved
without exception and with notations sent to the secretary of
the IRC. Adopted

30-19 Committee of Commissioners on Reformed University Ministries
TE Leo Schuster, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented
the report. [See Appendix M, p. 724.] He introduced TE Rod Mays,
Coordinator of RUM, who addressed the Assembly. He introduced campus
ministers TE Glenn Hoburg, TE Fritz Games, and TE Bill Boyd who
addressed the Assembly. Recommendations 1-6, 8-9 and 11 were adopted.
Recommendation 7 was deferred to the report of Committee of
Commissioners on Administration. Recommendation 10 was adopted in
accordance with RAO 18. TE Lee Ferguson led in prayer, giving thanks for
the 22 years of ministry and service as campus minister of TE Billy Joseph.
RE Brad Bradley closed the report with prayer.

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

A. Minutes for June 21, 2001, October 2, 2001, and March 12,
2002 of the Permanent Committee
B. Rules of Assembly Operations amendments
C. Audit for 2001
D. Budget for 2003
E. Report and Recommendations of the Permanent Committee

#### II. Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

The Committee heard reports from the Coordinator and the Chairman of the
Permanent Committee. Great progress has been made in establishing
Reformed University Ministries as a Permanent Committee of the General Assembly in the past year. The Permanent Committee and Staff have moved
with great care and deliberation to establish the foundation for future work.
The Committee heard a helpful presentation of budgeting rational for the 2003
Budget.

#### III. Recommendations

1. That the General Assembly give thanks to God for the
ministry of Reformed University Ministries, for its
faithfulness to the Scriptures, the Reformed faith, the Great
Commission, for the students reached by RUF, its staff, its
Permanent and Affiliated Committee members, and for those
who support the work of Reformed University Ministries
through their prayers and gifts. Adopted
2. That the General Assembly encourage the congregations and
Presbyteries of the Presbyterian Church in America to form
Affiliated Committees on Reformed University Ministries and

<a id="ga30-p89"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=91 printed_page=89 -->

work together to start and fund new RUF works within their
bounds. Adopted
3. That the General Assembly encourage the congregations of
the Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of
Reformed University Ministries by contributing the
Partnership Shares approved by the General Assembly. [See
Appendix C, Attachment G, p. 412.] Adopted
4. That the General Assembly commend TE Billy Joseph for his
22 years of faithful ministry and express its appreciation and
praise to God for his valuable contribution to the ministry of
Reformed University Ministries at the University of Alabama
and across the denomination. Adopted
5. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the
meetings of the Committee on Reformed University Ministries
for June 21, 2001, October 2, 2001, and March 12, 2002.
Adopted
6. That the General Assembly receive the financial audit for
Reformed University Ministries for the fiscal year ending
December 31, 2001 by Carr, Riggs & Ingram, LLP.
Adopted
7. That the General Assembly approve the 2003 budget of
Reformed University Ministries, and note with thanksgiving
the opportunities and challenges represented by the entire
2003 budget.
Deferred to AC report [See 30-62, III, 7, p. 295.]
8. Thatthe General Assembly receive as information the
Attachment [see Appendix M, p. 729] for a listing of
presbyteries and their affiliated committees, campus staff and
location of ministries and the 2003 budget for the entire
ministry including affiliated committees. Adopted
9. That the General Assembly reelect TE Rod S. Mays as
Coordinator of Reformed University Ministries for the
2002/2003 term; with thanks for his excellent leadership for
the 2001-2002 term. Adopted
10. That the General Assembly make the following necessary
changes to the Rules of Assembly Operation to reflect the new
status of Reformed University Ministries as a Permanent
Committee of the Presbyterian Church in America.
A. RAO Article IV, 4-2. — The line after “Mission to
North America (MNA)” add “Reformed University
Ministries (RUM)”

<a id="ga30-p90"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=92 printed_page=90 -->

B. RAO Article IV, 4-2, paragraph 2 —after “Mission to
North America” strike “and”, insert a comma
C. RAO Article IV, 4-2, paragraph 2 —after “Mission to
the World” insert “and Reformed University
Ministries”
D. RAO Article IV, 4-8, line 3 -- strike “three” and insert
“four”
E. RAO Article IV, 4-9, line 1 —strike “three” and insert
“four”
F. RAO Article V, 5-1, line 2 — strike “nineteen (19)”,
and insert “twenty (20)”
G. RAO Article V, 5-1. — strike period after “(8) Ridge
Haven Conference Center”, insert a semicolon and
insert “(9) Reformed University Ministries.”
H. RAO Article VI — add new section: “6-4. The
Committee on Reformed University Ministries.” -
“The affairs of the Church in the area of campus
ministry are assigned to the Committee on Reformed
University Ministries, whose duties and authority shall
be designated by the General Assembly.”
I. RAO Article XIII, 13-1. -- add new item “12.
Reformed University Ministries - Permanent Comm. -
Touching Reformed University Ministries”
J. Corporate Bylaws, Article V — insert “E. The
Committee on Reformed University Ministries” —
“The affairs of the Church in the area of campus
ministries on college and university campuses are
assigned to the Committee on Reformed University
Ministries, whose duties and authority shall be
designated by the General Assembly.”
K. Corporate Bylaws, Article V — strike “E.” in “E.
Other Committees” and insert “F.”
L. Corporate Bylaws, Article V — strike “F.” in “F.
Boards of Agencies” and insert “G.”
Adopted in accordance with RAO 18
11. That the General Assembly pray for the ministry of Reformed
University Ministries. Adopted

<a id="ga30-p93"></a>

having been encouraged to specifically review recommendations 5-10.

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

A. PCAF permanent committee report, including recommendations,
minutes, audit and budget.
B. Overture 7 from Westminster Presbytery.

#### II. Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

A. The PCAF’s preparation for and response to the Stock Market
and the events of September 11.
B. Overture 7 from Westminster Presbytery and the history/
circumstances surrounding it, including the letter from
Midway PCA, Jonesborough, Tennessee.

#### III. Recommendations

1. That the financial audit for the PCA Foundation, Inc. for the
calendar year ended December 31, 2001 by Capin Crouse,
LLP be received. Adopted
2. That the proposed 2003 Budget of the PCA Foundation, Inc.
be approved.
Deferred to AC report [See 30-62, III, 7, p. 295.]
3. That the Minutes of Board meetings of August 10, 2001 and
March 8, 2002 be approved. Adopted
4. That the General Assembly PCAF 2002 report be approved.
Adopted
5. That the Assembly answer all three items of Overture 7 in the
negative. Adopted

##### Overture 7 from Westminster Presbytery listed three requests for

action: 1) “To instruct the PCAF that it must allow members of
the General Assembly to inspect the records in accord with the
denominational corporation charter and Delaware state law;” 2)
“To instruct all of the committees and agencies, including those
that may be separately incorporated, of the rights of the members
of the General Assembly in this regard;” 3) “That if (the General Assembly) is unable to assert its rightful ownership of and
oversight over these agencies, the Assembly begin the process of
divesting itself of these agencies.”

<a id="ga30-p94"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=96 printed_page=94 -->

Grounds:
Request 1 [see text below]:
1. The PCAF is fully under and submissive to the
General Assembly in all the provisions of the BCO,
RAO and its own by-laws and articles of
incorporation.
2. This authority and submission is implemented by the
Board of Trustees elected at-large by the General Assembly and the work of the Committee of
Commissioners and their report to the General Assembly and the subsequent actions of the GA.
3. The Overture originates from a request for
information that was denied by the PCAF Board of
Trustees in accordance with the General Assembly
approved “Confidentiality Policy.”
4. The Overture cites Delaware Civil law; however, the
PCAF is incorporated under Georgia law.
5. The Overture appears to use civil law to circumvent
the ecclesiastical government of the General Assembly over the PCAF and other Committees and
Agencies.
Request 2 [see text below]: we deem it as
unnecessary because the General Assembly already has a
system of oversight. The committees and agencies of the PCA
are already submissive to the General Assembly in all the
provisions of the BCO and RAO and their own respective
bylaws, articles of incorporation or manuals of operations.
Request 3 [see text below]: The General Assembly
has and does continue to maintain proper oversight over its
committees and agencies.

##### OVERTURE 7 from Westminster Presbytery (to PCAF & AC)

“Allow Inspection of PCAF and All Other Committee and
Agency Records by Individuals or General Assembly Divest
Itself of Committees and Agencies”

Whereas, the PCA Foundation was formed by the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America; and

Whereas, the PCA Foundation is a constituent part of the
Presbyterian Church in America, and all of the
members of the PCA Foundation Board are elected by
the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in

<a id="ga30-p95"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=97 printed_page=95 -->

America (Corporate Bylaws of Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation), Article VI.5);

Whereas, the intimate connection between the Foundation
and the denomination is seen even in the ability of the
Board of the Foundation to designate a member of the
Administrative Committee of the General Assembly
(,BCO 14-1-12); and

Whereas, the PCA Foundation, which was originally a subcommittee, gained separate agency status in 1983,
similar to other agencies, such as Covenant College,
Covenant Theological Seminary, Ridge Haven, and
Insurance, Annuities, and Relief (M9GA, p. 116, 8.e;
Ml 1GA, p. 113, 1.); and

Whereas, the provisions regarding Covenant College and
Covenant Theological Seminary in 1983 included the
following: “Since the General Assembly is
responsible for the conduct of the work which the
whole church does unitedly, the powers and activities
that it has delegated to institutions under its
jurisdiction shall always be subject to review. It shall
not delegate any of its general powers beyond its own
recall so as to cease to be fully responsible for the
spiritual oversight of the entire denomination”
(M9GA, p, 100, 5.i); and

Whereas, recent correspondence from the PCA Foundation,
indicates that the PCA Foundation is maintaining that
the right of a member of the denominational
corporation to inspect the records does not include the
right to inspect the records of the PCA Foundation;
and

Whereas, the position taken by the PCA Foundation would
mean that it is no longer under the effective control of
the General Assembly; and

Whereas, while we have no grounds to believe that there is
presently any malfeasance being practiced by the PCA
Foundation, nevertheless, in order to maintain proper
checks and balances, the General Assembly itself, and
by extension the members thereof, must have the

<a id="ga30-p96"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=98 printed_page=96 -->

ability to inspect the records of the PCA Foundation;
and

Whereas, the position taken by the PCA Foundation, if not
corrected by the General Assembly, will imply that
any and all denominational corporations will no
longer be subject to General Assembly control, but
will essentially be autonomous agencies; and

Whereas, we have an obligation, corporately and individually,
to exercise oversight of the denominational agencies,
and it would be malfeasance on our part to refuse to
assert our oversight responsibility;

Now therefore be it resolved, that Westminster Presbytery
hereby overtures the 30th General Assembly, meeting
in Birmingham, Alabama, in June, 2002, to instruct
the PCA Foundation that it must allow members of the
General Assembly to inspect the records in accord
with the denominational corporation charter and
Delaware state law;
And be it further resolved, that Westminster Presbytery
hereby overtures the 30lh General Assembly to instruct
all of the committees and agencies, including those
that may be separately incorporated, of the rights of
the members of the General Assembly in this regard;
And be it further resolved, that Westminster Presbytery
hereby overtures the 30th General Assembly that if it is
unable to assert its rightful ownership of and oversight
over these agencies, the Assembly begin the process
of divesting itself of these agencies.
6. The committee recommends that the General Assembly
remind individuals and churches that they should show charity
and honor to brothers in dealing with ecclesiastical issues. A
letter that was disseminated this year has brought harm to the
reputation of the PCAF and impeded its ability to do ministry.
This should be avoided. Adopted
7. Commend PCAF President Randal Stair, Administrator Janice
Mutchler, Administrative Assistant Deb Hughes, Senior
Accountant Mark Bailey and the Board of Trustees under the
leadership of Chairman David Clelland for their excellent
work and faithful service to our Lord Jesus Christ. Adopted

<a id="ga30-p97"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=99 printed_page=97 -->

Commend the PCAF Staff and Board of Trustees for their
weathering of the severe financial conditions of the past two
years while increasing disbursements and attaining self-
supporting status. We gratefully acknowledge that in the past
five years the Foundation has distributed over twelve million
dollars to PCA churches, which has tripled the amount given
in the previous five years, furthering the work of the Kingdom
of God. Adopted
9. Commend the Staff, Board and General Counsel of the PCAF
for their diligent, careful and honorable handling of the
concerns surrounding overture seven. Adopted
10. The General Assembly affirms its confidence in the ministry
of the PCAF, “Stewardship Service Providers You Can Trust.”
Adopted

30-25 Partial Report of the Administrative Committee
The Chairman yielded to TE Alan Johnson, Chairman of the

30-26 Personal Resolution
TheModerator ruled that a Personal Resolution was out of order
because the deadline for enteringsuch had passed. The Moderator’s ruling
was challenged, but because the challenge was not offered until after other
business had transpired the challenge was out of order.

Third Session - Wednesday Afternoon
June 20, 2001
30-28 Assembly Reconvened
The Assembly reconvened at 1:30 p.m. with the singing of Hymn 441
“Jesus Shall Reign” and prayer by TE John Yenchko.
30-29 Report of Committee on Constitutional Business [see also 30-36, p. 201]
TE Craig Childs, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented
the report. After review, the Assembly received it as information.
REPORT OF THE
COMMITTEE ON CONSTITUTIONAL BUSINESS

#### I. Introduction

Your Committee on Constitutional Business (CCB) met one time
subsequent to the 29th General Assembly, on April 22-23, 2002 in Lawrenceville,

<a id="ga30-p99"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=101 printed_page=99 -->

Georgia. The CCB was able at that meeting to complete most of the work then
assigned to it. Attendance was as follows:
TE Mark Rowden (05) - Present RE Dan Carrell (05) - Present
TE Karl McCallister (04) - Present RE Frederick (Jay) Neikirk (04) - Absent
TE Craig Childs (03) - Present RE Richard (Ric) Springer (03) - Present
TE Bryan Chapell (02) - Present RE John Weiss (02) - Present
TE Larry Hoop (Alt) - Present RE David Yates (Alt) - Present
in the absence of RE Neikirk, RE Yates was seated, by proper motion,
as a voting member. TE Hoop was seated as a non-voting member with the
privilege of the floor. A quorum was present and the Committee convened to
conduct business at 10:15 am.
II. Proposed BCO Amendment from IAR
A proposed amendment from the IAR recommended that the General Assembly amend the BCO 15-1.12.b.4, to recognize the change in the name of
our agency by replacing the words, “Insurance, Annuities and Relief’ with
“PCA Retirement and Benefits, Inc.”
Response: It is the opinion of the CCB that this proposed amendment is not in
conflict with other parts of the Constitution. The CCB notes, however,
that other documents (i.e. RAO, By-Laws and manuals of operation)
may need concurrent revision. Adopted by CCB
III. Overtures

##### Overture 1 from Western Carolina Presbytery: “Add ‘a nd unity’ to Sixth

Ordination Vow in BCO 21-5 ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-53, III, 6, p. 244.]
Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 3 from Illiana Presbytery, Missouri Presbytery, Evangel Presbytery,

Susquehanna Valley Presbytery, Northern California Presbytery, Central
Florida Presbytery, Northern Illinois Presbytery, South Texas Presbytery:
“Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version 1 ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 2, p. 218.]
Adopted by CCB
(TE Chapell recused himself)

<a id="ga30-p100"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=102 printed_page=100 -->

##### Overture 4 from Ohio Valley Presbytery: “Handling Exceptions to the

Westminster Standards ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that the proposed constitutional language creates
constitutional conflicts with the BCO Preliminary Principles. Furthermore, the
proposed language creates constitutional ambiguity.
1. The proposed overture language “either in his private life” is in
conflict with BCO Preliminary Principle II. 1 and II.7.
2. The proposed overture language requiring Presbyteries to record every
man’s views disagreeing with the Constitution will erode the
Presbyteries’ exclusive authority to determine if a candidate receives
and adopts the Confession of Faith and the Catechism of the PCA as
containing the system of doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures. See
BCO 21-5. This is in conflict with BCO Preliminary Principle II.2
that recognizes the right of each court to determine its membership.
(BCO Preface II.2, BCO 21-1 through 7; BCO 13-1; BCO 13-9 a,c,d;
BCO 11-3 & 4).
3. An ambiguity created by the proposed language is the introduction of the
phrase: “exception to the standards.” This phrase is nowhere else found
in our constitution. The phrase elicits a plethora of interpretations, many
of which go beyond our present Constitutional parameters.
4. It has not normally been the mission of the CCB to decide on the
placement of Constitutional language. [For text and action, see 30-50,
III, 3, p. 238.] Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 5 from Ohio Valley Presbytery, Metropolitan New York Presbytery,

Illiana Presbytery, Missouri Presbytery, Piedmont Triad Presbytery, North
Georgia Presbytery, Evangel Presbytery, Fellowship Presbytery, Tennessee
Valley Presbytery’, Rocky’ Mountain Presbytery, North Texas Presbytery’,
Nashville Presbytery, Susquehanna Valley Presbytery, Northern California
Presbytery, North Florida Presbytery, Heartland Presbytery, Central Florida
Presbytery, Northern Illinois Presbytery: “Require ten percent of
Presbyteries to Assume Original Jurisdiction Over a Minister in BCO 34-1 -
Version 1 ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 1, p. 213.]
Adopted by CCB
(TE Chapell recused himself)

##### Overture 6 from Westminster Presbytery: “Revise BCO 15-4 and 15-5 to

Abolish the Standing Judicial Commission”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this overture is in conflict with other parts of
the constitution, including but not limited to BCO 14-1(15). The overture is
also unconstitutionally vague as to what will be the basis of making the
alternative decisions indicated in the proposed BCO 15-5, thus creating

<a id="ga30-p101"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=103 printed_page=101 -->

constitutional tensions without process for resolution. In addition, it will
create numerous conflicts with our General Assembly RAO.
Not as constitutional advice, but as pastoral concern and brotherly love, the
CCB also asks the members of the B&O committee of commissioners to be
very cautious regarding passing on intemperate language and personal
accusations to the GA through the reasoning, premises and recitals of overtures
(WLC 144 & 145). [For text and action, see 30-53, III, 8, p. 254.]
Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 10 from Ohio Presbytery: “Specify Good Faith Subscription -

Version 2 ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 2, p. 218.]
Adopted by CCB
(TE Chapell recused himself)

##### Overture 11 from North Georgia Presbytery, Tennessee Valley Presbytery>,

Rocky Mountain Presbytery, Nashville Presbytery, North Florida Presbytery,
Heartland Presbytery: “Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version 3 ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 2, p. 218.]
Adopted by CCB
(TE Chapell recused himself)

##### Overture 12 from Metropolitan New York Presbytery: “Specify Good Faith

Subscriptions - Version 4 ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 2, p. 218.]
Adopted by CCB
(TE Chapell recused himself)

##### Overture 14 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery’: “Amend BCO 24-1 to Replace

‘s hould’ With ‘s hall’”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-53, III, 10, p. 259.]
Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 15 from Rocky’ Mountain Presbytery: “Amend BCO 24-1 to Specify

‘men ’Rather Than ‘p ersons ’”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-53, III, 11, p. 260.]
Adopted by CCB

<a id="ga30-p102"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=104 printed_page=102 -->

##### Overture 18 from Potomac Presbytery: “To Amend Both BCO 33-1 and 34-1

to Require Greater Consensus to Assume Original Jurisdiction Over Members
and Ministers. ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that the proposed Constitutional language is not in
conflict with any other parts of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-
50, III, 1, p. 213.] Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 24 from North Texas Presbytery: “Specify Good Faith Subscriptions

- Version 5. ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 2, p. 218.]
Adopted by CCB
(TE Chapell recused himself)

##### Overture 25 from Ascension Presbytery: “Revise BCO 12-2, Add 12-11,

Revise 25-6 and 15-1 Regarding Procedures to Be Used if a Session Cannot
function or needs to be Augmented. ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this Overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-53, III, 17, p. 270.]
Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 27 from Eastern Canada Presbytery: Add to BCO 26-1 Procedure

for dealing with alleged conflicts between scripture and the constitution of
the PCA
It is the opinion of the CCB that the Overture is in conflict with other parts of
the Constitution. Specific areas of conflict include, but are not limited to:
1. The Overture would allow a General Assembly to avoid the provisions
of the BCO without appropriate constitutional process and would
result in serious constitutional uncertainty. (BCO 26-2).
2. The Overture allows modification of virtually any action of the General Assembly or commission thereof, with the sole limitation that an actual
judgment of a judicial commission may not be changed or deleted,
thereby undermining the very principle of orderly Church Government
which the BCO is designed to protect. [See BCO Preface II.2],
3. The Overture is in conflict with BCO 15.1 and 15.5, in that it allows
for modification of a commission report.
4. The Overture requires the CCB to propose business, inconsistent with
RAO 7-2 and the Operations Manual for CCB.
5. Finally, reference is made to the recommendation of the CCB with
respect to Constitutional Inquiry #4 of the 29th General Assembly:
“The presumption that we need to suspend our rules in order to obey
Christ is an opinion that the CCB is not willing to affirm. i four standards

<a id="ga30-p103"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=105 printed_page=103 -->

are shown to be out of accord with Scripture, then there are proper
procedures to follow, at each level, in order to change those standards. ”
[For text and action, see 30-53, III, 18, p. 273.] Adopted by CCB

##### Overture 29 from Westminster Presbytery: Amend BCO 24-1 to Record All

Exceptions with Presbytery and Have Stated Clerk Report All Exceptions to
the General Assembly ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that Overture 29 from Westminster Presbytery is
in conflict with the Constitution. Specifically:
1. it allows a presbytery to determine whether a man mayor may not
teach what he believes, even if it is not out of accord with any
fundamental of the system of doctrine,thus going beyond our
constitution to bind the man’s conscience. (BCO Preface II.1 and II.7)
2. It is also unconstitutionally vague in not identifying what the
prohibition to teach would restrict or allow.
3. The last paragraph unconstitutionally provides a mechanism that puts
the final approval for presbytery membership at the GA instead of the
presbytery level. (BCO Preface II.2, BCO 21-1 through 7, BCO 13-1;
BCO 13-9a,c,d; BCO 11-3&4). [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 2,
p. 218.] Adopted by CCB
(TE Chapell recused himself)

##### Overture 30 from Iowa Presbytery: “Require ten percent of pres by teri es to

Assume Original Jurisdiction Over a Minister in BCO 34-1 - Version 2 ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that this overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 1, p. 213.]
Adopted by CCB
(TE Chapell recused himself)

##### Overture 31 from Iowa Presbytery: “Specify Good Faith Subscription -

It is the opinion of the CCB that this overture is not in conflict with other parts
of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 2, p. 219.]

(TE Chapell recused himself)

of the Constitution. [For text and action, see 30-50, III, 2, p. 219.]

<a id="ga30-p104"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=106 printed_page=104 -->

IV. Advice to the Stated Clerk
The CCB advised the Stated Clerk on questions posed by him. [See Appendix
A, p. 346.]
V. Review of Minutes of the Standing Judicial Commission
1. SJC Minutes Nov. 29, 2001, p. 21 ff.
Response: In accordance with SJC Manual 20.5, the CCB approves
the minutes of the Nov. 29, 2001 meeting of the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) as being in conformity to the Manual of the SJC
and mo 15.
Editorial notations regarding form (e.g., punctuation and
format) were passed to the Stated Clerk.
2. SJC Minutes March 7, 2002, p. 31 ff.
Response: We postpone action on the March 7, 2002 minutes of the
SJC and respectfully request the SJC to request more time of the
General Assembly and/or reconsider its action “to draft and present to
the 30th General Assembly procedures to be placed in the SJC Manual
for examining concurring and dissenting opinions to be sure that such
opinions are in temperate language ...“ prior to the final report that the
CCB makes to the GA.
3. Procedure for Hearing a Memorial
Response: It is the advice of CCB that the “Procedure for Hearing a
Memorial'’ raises significant constitutional issues. These issues
include:
1) our concern that the procedures may reflect a definition of a
memorial inconsistent with our historical Presbyterian usage since
no clear definition is offered and the term has been variously used
in other/former denominations ina manner that allows a higher
court to act for a lower court.
2) The lack of clear BCO or SJC Manual definition of what
constitutes a “memorial” being “administratively in order.”
3) Significant questions of due process such as the preclusion of
appropriate briefing and argument.
As an editorial note, we also remind the Stated Clerk that if the
changes proposed by the SJC relating to new SJC Manual section 18
are approved, then the present references to SJC Manual 18 in SJC
Manual 13-9 (and potentially other places) will need to be RE
numbered. Other conforming changes may also be needed.
Respectfully Submitted; Respectfully Submitted;
/s/ TE Craig Childs, Chairman /s/ RE Richard Springer, Secretary

<a id="ga30-p105"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=107 printed_page=105 -->

SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF THE
COMMITTEE ON CONSTITUTIONAL BUSINESS
A. It was MSP to amend the CCB’s report concerning its response to the
SJC’s proposed manual amendment entitled, “Procedure for Hearing a
Memorial,’ such that the CCB response in Paragraph 3 of section v of
its report was beyond the scope of RAO 15.1 and therefore, the report
of the CCB is hereby amended by striking therefrom Paragraph 3 of
Section V in its entirety.
B. Considered a constitutional inquiry from Philadelphia Presbytery:
“At its May 11, 2002, stated meeting, Philadelphia Presbytery voted to
make a constitutional inquiry to the Constitutional Business Committee
of the General Assembly, to wit:
having accepted an appeal from a church member against a judgment
rendered against him by the session of his church, and having referred
that appeal to a duly appointed commission properly erected by
Presbytery, does the Presbytery have the right:
1. to take back from said commission the matter committed to it
before the commission can complete its task and render a
judgment; and
2. to reverse, without hearing the appeal, the decision of the
Session, which found the appellant guilty and suspended him
from the office of ruling elder?
The actual case in dispute has been in the commission's hands for sixteen
months and the record of the case has still not be enable to be finalized to
the satisfaction of the appellant, the session, and the commission.
Philadelphia Presbytery’ needs and requests the advice of our fathers and
brothers. ”
It is the opinion of the CCB that in reference to question #1 of the
inquiry, our answer is yes, the Presbytery may take back from said
commission the matter committed to it before the commission has
completed its task and rendered a judgment.
In reference to question #2, our answer is no, the Presbytery may not
reverse, without hearing the appeal, the decision of the Session. The
Presbytery must follow the procedures detailed in BCO 42.
Respectfully Submitted,
/s/ TE Craig D. Childs, Sr., Chairman
/si TE Mark A. Rowden, Acting Secretary

<a id="ga30-p106"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=108 printed_page=106 -->

SECOND SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF THE
COMMITTEE ON CONSTITUTIONAL BUSINESS
1. Took up from the table the final approval of SJC minutes of March 7,
2002. Given the excerpts from the minutes of a subsequent meeting
which showed corrective action, MSP to approve of March 7 without
exception.
2. Received a constitutional inquiry asking CCB to give advice on the
constitutionality of the Committee of Commissioners recommendation
#15. The question posed for CCB was as follows, Is it constitutional
for the General Assembly to adopt the following recommendation:
That the General Assembly mandate coverage in the PCA health
plans for all active ministers and church lay employees
who are current residents of the United States and who
are not covered under a spouse’s group health plan, and
that the Presbyteries be responsible for implementing
this mandate.
The CCB answered:
No. It is the opinion of the CCB that in the absence of any evidence
from BCO 14, the Westminster Confession of Faith and Catechisms,
or Scripture as to the right of the General Assembly to make such a
requirement; the General Assembly may not mandate coverage in the
PCA health plan. In addition, BCO 14-1.4 states:
“It is the responsibility of every member and member congregation to
support the whole work of the denomination as they be led in their
conscience held captive to the Word of God.” (Emphasis added.)
3. Received a constitutional inquiry from Southeast Alabama Presbytery,
to wit:
The duties of Elders, as set forth in the Book of Church Order
includes:
To watch diligently over the flock committed to their
charge, that no corruption of doctrine or of morals
enter therein. (BCO 8-3)
Which includes overseeing communicant members who have
vowed:
“...to support the church in its worship and work to
the best of (your) ability.”

<a id="ga30-p107"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=109 printed_page=107 -->

In the fulfillment of their responsibilities, do elders have the right to
access to such attendance and financial giving records of the local
congregation as they deem necessary?
Does the BCO restrict, in any way, the access of elders and/or deacons
of a local church to such records?
The CCB answered:
The inquiry asks about the rights of elders in the context of their duty to
take spiritual oversight over their flock. It is the opinion of the CCB
that BCO 8-3 and 12-5.a,b give a session the right to access attendance
and financial records as they deem necessary in the fulfillment of their
responsibilities. In carrying out these responsibilities there are no
restrictions on the session, as a court, beyond those that would be
imposed by prudence and the Biblical responsibility to protect our
neighbors’ good name (cf. WLCQ 145).
In the furtherance of the duties specified in BCO 9-2, the Deacons may
access financial giving and attendance records. They are restricted by their
responsibility to act under the “supervision and authority of the Session”
(BCO 9-2, para. 2), the parameters of their BCO 9-2 responsibilities, and the
limitations noted in the last sentence of the preceding paragraph.
Respectfully Submitted,
/s/ TE Craig D. Childs, Sr., Chairman
/s/ TE Mark A. Rowden, Acting Secretary

Moderator Ryan yielded the chair to TE Wilson Benton.
30-30 Report of the Standing Judicial Commission
RE John White, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented the
report. He reported on the communications received and considered concerning
the proposed amendment of the SJC Manual Section 13.8. Recommendations
a and b were adopted as amended. [See text below, p. 172.]
Recommendation V was adopted. RE White led the Assembly in prayer.
REPORT OF THE STANDING JUDICIAL COMMISSION
TO THE THIRTIETH GENERAL ASSEMBLYOF THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

#### I. Introduction

The Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) held its fall meeting by
telephone conference call on November 29, 2001 in view of the limited agenda
which did not justify the expense of a face-to-face meeting. The
Commission’s annual meeting was held on March 7 and 8, 2002.

<a id="ga30-p108"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=110 printed_page=108 -->

II. Judicial Cases
The following is a list of Judicial Cases before the Commission over
the period since the last General Assembly:
1. Case 00-9 Complaint TE Sung Keon Kim vs. Korean Capital Presbytery
2. Case 01-1 Appeal RE Charles Kim vs. Korean Capital Presbytery
