<a id="ga30-p109"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=111 printed_page=109 -->

Of these, Cases 01-5, 01-20, 01-21, 01-22, 01-23, 01-24 and 01-29
were withdrawn and 01-26 and 01-27 were abandoned. Since the cases
involving Korean Capital Presbytery were complex and related, a panel of five
members was appointed to hear all these cases. Cases 01-6, 01-30, 01-31, 01-
32, 01-33, 01-34, 02-1, 02-2 and 02-3 were not completed in time for the SJC
March meeting and await final determination by the full SJC in October. The
Standing Judicial Commission has completed its work on Cases 00-9, 01-1,
01-2, 01-3, 01-4, 01-7, 01-8, 01-9, 01-10, 01-11, 01-12, 01-13, 01-14, 01-15,
01-16, 01-17, 01-18, 01-19, 01-25, 01-28, 01-35, 01-36 and 01-37. The report
on those cases is as follows:
III. Report Of The Cases
Case 2000-9
COMPLAINT OF TE SUNG KEON KIM
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of Korean
Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW). Initially the dispute centered
principally on questions about the finances of Washington Theological
Seminary, which was connected with KPCW.
2. On December 30, 1999 the Session of KPCW submitted to Korean
Capital Presbytery (KCP) a reference asking KCP to help the session address
the financial question referred in item 1.
3. The situation escalated to the point that there were two groups both
claiming to be the true Session of KPCW. There was reported physical
violence at the church and police were called to resolve disputes. The
congregation was advised by civil authority that if the turmoil continued, the
church might be prohibited from meeting.
4. Subsequently, additional requests were made of KCP to investigate
financial mismanagement, a petition from members of the congregation of
KPCW asking KCP to dismiss RE Charles Kim and letters reporting on
“divisive and destructive activities of REs Charles Kim and Moon K. Ham.”
A total of 16 references, petitions, accusations and complaints were filed with
KCP regarding the situation at KPCW.
5. Certain KPCW officers sought in civil court to have existing church
trustees replaced without congregational approval.
6. To deal with these 16 petitions, references, complaints and
accusations, on October 9, 2000 at the reconvened portion of its 17th Stated
Meeting KCP appointed a commission to “suspend the functions of the KPCW
church Session and have the Presbytery replace the functions and report to the
Presbytery meeting in three months.”

<a id="ga30-p110"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=112 printed_page=110 -->

7. TE Sung Keon Kim submitted to KCP a complaint dated Nov. 1, 2000
against the action of suspending the KPCW Session and having its commission
replace the session.
8. The Presbytery Commission, known as the Administrative
Commission and the Judicial Commission (it had been assigned both
functions), began its work on October 16, 2000 and commenced to act for the
session.
9. On October 15, 2000 the Commission arrived at KPCW to lead the
Sunday worship service but was prevented from doing so by members of the
congregation.
10. On October 16, 2000 three Ruling Elders and a Teaching Elder, acting
as the interim moderator of KPCW, advised the commission that “the
undersigned fully recognize the authority of the Commission and Judicial
Commission formed by the decision made at the 17th Stated Presbytery
meeting...”
11. The Commission on October 20, 2000 sought and ultimately received
a civil court order “to protect the church properties and to secure the safety of
church members.” The order restrained certain Ruling Elders from interfering
with the commission.
12. On November 1, 2000 TE Kim complained to the Standing Judicial Commission, which complaint was determined to be prematurely filed since it
had not been adjudicated by KCP.
13. The Commission acting for the Session of KPCW called three
congregational meetings on November 5, December 3, and 31, instituted ten
temporary church steering committees, changed the signers of various church
bank accounts, and completed the transfer of the church facilities purchased by
KPCW. At the December 3 meeting, KPCW congregation voted to consent to
the commission acting as its session.
14. The Commission, acting for the Session of KPCW fired TE Sung
Keon Kim as assistant pastor of the church.
15. At a called meeting of KCP on January 8, 2001 Presbytery approved
the actions of its commission and dismissed TE Kim’s complaints.
16. On January 15, 2001 TE Kim refiled his complaint with the Standing Judicial Commission.
17. A five-man panel of the SJC heard the complaint on December 3, 2001
at the McLean Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Did the Korean Capital Presbytery (KCP) err in its action of October
9, 2000 . . to suspend the functions of the Korean Presbyterian Church
Washington (KPCW) Session and have the Presbytery (commission) replace
the functions” (of the KPCW Session) without the prior consent of the
congregation?

<a id="ga30-p111"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=113 printed_page=111 -->

#### III. Judgment

Yes. Therefore, the subsequent actions of the Commission acting as
Session of KPCW are annulled.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

1. The action of KCP clearly violated Preliminary Principle Six in the
preface to the Book of Church Order which states that “... the power to elect
persons to the exercise of authority in any particular society resides in that
society.” (Emphasis added) Here KCP has appointed an Administrative and
Judicial Commission to suspend the Session of KPCW and to replace the
functions of the Session, without the approval or consent of the congregation
of KPCW - as a matter of fact without even contacting the congregation of
KPCW. In this instance, the appointment of the Commission by KCP was
unauthorized by the BCO; and therefore, its actions are not binding on the
session and congregation.
A word of explanation may be helpful to reaffirm the history and
purpose of these eight Preliminary Principles. Since the 16th Century
Protestant Reformation, there have been numerous Reformed denominations
with varying forms of church polity - some more hierarchical and others more
democratic. These eight principles were originally adopted by the first
American General Assembly in 1789. Our American Presbyterian forefathers
had come to America with fresh memories of the persecutions under the Act of
Supremacy fostered by Henry VIII in England. They did not want to form a
denomination that was governed “from the top down” but “from the bottom
up.”
In 1787, when the original four Synods agreed to have a General Assembly, they appointed a committee to first draft a series of preliminary
principles to be approved before the Book of Church Order was written. This
Committee worked for a year and presented these eight Preliminary Principles
to the meeting of the Synods in 1788. These Preliminary Principles were
approved so that the denomination would not be hierarchical in its polity.
they then appointed a committee to draft a Book of Church Order based on
these eight Preliminary Principles. this Book of Church Order was adopted at
the first American Presbyterian General Assembly in 1789.
It is interesting to note that by 1973 when the PCA was formed, both
PCUS and UPCUSA had removed those eight principles from their respective
constitutions and had become hierarchical denominations.
In 1973, after we had decided to separate from PCUS and before the
PCA was actually formed, we called our group THE CONTINUING
CHURCH, meaning that we intended to organize a denomination continuing
the polity that our American forefathers adopted in 1789 based on these eight
principles.
This action of KCP violated Principle Six of the Preliminary Principles
in that it gave authority to its Commission to suspend those Session members

<a id="ga30-p112"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=114 printed_page=112 -->

elected by the congregation “to the exercise of authority” in KPCW, and to
replace them with those of the choosing of KCP or its Commission.
This action similarly violated BCO 16-2 which states that “... the right
of God’s people to recognize by election to office those so gifted is
inalienable. Therefore no man can be placed over a church in any office
without the election, or at least the consent of that church.” (Emphasis added)
The dictionary defines the meaning of the word “inalienable” as follows:
(1) incapable of being surrendered
(2) incapable of being transferred
(3) that which one cannot give away or dispose of, even if one wishes.
KCP argues that it had authority for such action under BCO 13-9
which grants power to Presbytery:
“... In cases in which the Session cannot exercise its
authority, it shall have power to assume original
jurisdiction.”
KCP has misinterpreted this language for their interpretation conflicts
with the provisions of the Preliminary Principles and BCO 16-2, above. The
general rule of interpretation of the BCO is that no provision can be properly
interpreted if it conflicts with any of the Preliminary Principles. Thus, if a BCO
provision is interpreted so that it conflicts with a Preliminary Principle, such
interpretation is erroneous. Every interpretation of the BCO after the
Preliminary Principles in the Preface must be consistent with such Preliminary
Principles - otherwise the interpretation is in error. KCP made such a
misinterpretation.
2. This case sets before the SJC the constitutional status of the Presbytery
(KCP) and its relationship and power to deal with a problem in one of its local
congregations (KPCW). The PCA functions at three separate levels - the
General Assembly, the Presbytery and the local church (with the Session, as
chosen by the congregation, being the authoritative body at the local level).
Each of these levels exists in two ways or with two concurrent identities. First,
as an ecclesiastical body and second, as a civil body (the precise legal nature of
the civil identity is governed by local civil law). Each of these courts of the
church exercises two distinct kinds of authority - first ecclesiastical authority
and the second civil authority. Such ecclesiastical authority is defined in the
Preliminary Principles as “only ministerial and declarative” or “purely moral
or spiritual in its object.” (Preliminary Principles Seven and Eight). Such civil
authority is determined by the civil law where the church is located.
This ecclesiastical authority is morally binding on the lower court, but
it is not legally binding or authoritative over a lower court ina civil sense. In
an effort to explain this distinction the 1986 General Assembly adopted the
final recommendations and concluding proposals of the Ad Interim Committee
on Structure which stated that a higher court may “Act On” or “Act Against” a
matter before it from a lower court; but a higher court cannot “Act For” the

<a id="ga30-p113"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=115 printed_page=113 -->

lower court (14th General Assembly Minutes pp. 104-105). The General Assembly then defined the meaning of the term “Act For” as follows:
“Act For” - a higher court taking an action on a matter
over which a lower court had original jurisdiction, which
action is as conclusive of the matter as if it had been made
by the lower court and is similarly enforceable.” (14th
General Assembly Minutes page 105).
The 15th General Assembly defined the words “church power” to
mean:
“The ability to require (force) compliance with the
decision of the courts.” (Min. 15 G.A. p. 480.)
clearly the preliminary principles in the preface to the Book of Church Order state that such power is “only ministerial and declarative” and “must be
purely moral and spiritual in its nature” (Preliminary Principles Seven and
Eight of BCO Preface).
The remainder of the Book of Church Order is consistent in setting
forth the nature of church power as:
Section 3-2 “Ecclesiastical power which is wholly spiritual...”
Section 3-3 “The sole functions of the Church as a Kingdom and
government, distinct from the civil commonwealth, are to
proclaim, to administer, and to enforce the law of Christ revealed
in the Scriptures.”
Section 3-4 “The power of the Church is exclusively spiritual; and
that of the State includes the use of force.”
In this case, KCP sought to “Act For” the congregation and Session of
ICPCW, and in doing so KCP erred. The congregation and Session elected by
the congregation had original jurisdiction of actions or failures to act by
members of the Session of KPCW and KCP sought to “Act For” the lower
court. If KPCW did not follow the instructions of the Administrative and
Judicial Commission of KCP, then KCP would have had to go to civil court to
enforce its action, which it has no authority to do. KCP has no civil
connection to KPCW for that would be “Acting For” KPCW and is contrary to
the BCO. KCP and its Commission failed to properly interpret the power and
authority given it by the BCO.
3. BCO 14-7 provides for the manner in which previous decisions in
judicial cases “may be appealed to in subsequent similar cases as to any
principle which may have been decided.” (Emphasis added)
We appeal to a similar case and to the principle decided therein
namely:
RE Charles E. Chappell vs. Eastern Carolina Presbytery
(Case 90-4, PCA Digest Volume 1, Part III Pages 430-442).

<a id="ga30-p114"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=116 printed_page=114 -->

In that case, Eastern Carolina Presbytery appointed a commission and
clothed “it with full power of Presbytery to resolve all matters of conflict at
Fuller Memorial Church, Durham” (p. 430).
The Commission of East Carolina Presbytery then suspended the
entire Session with the Commission acting as Session Pro-Tem (p. 430-C).
The Commission of East Carolina Presbytery argued that the basis of the
authority of Presbytery comes from the powers of Presbytery listed in BCO-13
(p. 431-E). This is exactly the same argument that KCP made in this case.
In this East Carolina Presbytery case two of the issues were stated (p.
433) as follows:
“2. Did East Carolina Presbytery have the authority
and power to suspend the members of the Session of
Fuller Memorial Church, without consent of the
congregation and without due process?
“3. Did Eastern Carolina Presbytery or its
Commission have authority and power to constitute
itself as a Session Pro Tem for Fuller Church and
make all decisions therefore?” (p. 433).
The Judgment in this East Carolina Presbytery case on the above two
issues was as follows:
“2. the Book of Church Order does not give a
presbytery the authority and power to suspend
members of a Session without the consent of the
congregation and without due process. The
Commission of Eastern Carolina Presbytery erred in
so doing.
“3. While the Commission may have believed its
replacing the Fuller Session was under taken with
consent (there was no formal vote on this issue), its
action had the effect of removing and replacing the
Session with one that had not been elected by the
Fuller congregation, which action of the Commission
exceeded the jurisdiction given by the Book of Church Order, (p. 433-434).”
We believe the principles in the East Carolina Presbytery case are
applicable in this case; and we appeal to it as precedent for our judgment
herein.
4. Another supporting reason for this Judgment is the lack of original
jurisdiction of KCP over the church members and session members of KPCW.
Consider the following Book of Church Order provisions relating to original
jurisdiction over church members and session members of KPCW, to-wit:

<a id="ga30-p115"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=117 printed_page=115 -->

(1) BCO 33-1 states that “Process against all church members, other
than ministers of the Gospel, shall be entered before the Session
to which the members belong.
(2) BCO 31-1 states that “original jurisdiction in relations to church
members shall be, in the Session of the church of which he/she
is a member.”
Contrary to these explicit statements in the PCA Book of Church Order, KCP sought to exercise original jurisdiction over the members and
session of KPCW. The KCP suspension of the KPCW Session and the
appointment of the Administrative and Judicial Commission to act as and for
the Session, without the approval of the congregation and due process, was an
attempt to exercise original jurisdiction over the members and session of
KPCW. This action was contrary to the PCA - BCO and our historical
standards. Therefore, the commission’s actions are not binding on the session
or congregation.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Issue and Judgment were prepared by the entire Panel of five
members. The Reasoning and Opinion was written by RE W. Jack Williamson
with concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, TE Jim Smith, RE Grant McCabe
and RE John White.
Roll vote on Case 2000-9:
TE Dominic Aquila Concur RE Collie Lehn Concur
RE Mark Belz Absent RE Tom Leopard Concur
RE Frank Brock Dissent TE Lawrence Lunceford Dissent
RE Robert Cannada Concur TE William Lyle Concur
RE M. C. Culbertson Concur RE J. Grant McCabe Concur
RE Howard Donahoe Dissent TE Charles McGowan Concur
RE Samuel Duncan Concur TE David Peterson Concur
TE Robert Ferguson Absent TE G. Dewey Roberts Concur
RE Bruce Gunther Absent TE James Smith Concur
TE William E. Harrell Concur TE Robert Stuart Concur
TE Ben Konopa, Jr. Concur RE John B. White, Jr. Concur
TE Paul Kooistra Absent RE W. Jack Williamson Concur
Adopted: 17 concur; 3 dissent; 4 absent.
Case 2001-1
APPEAL OF RE CHARLES KIM
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of
Korean Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW). Initially the dispute

<a id="ga30-p116"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=118 printed_page=116 -->

centered principally on questions about the finances of Washington
Theological Seminary which was connected with KPCW.
2. On December 30, 1999 the Session of KPCW submitted to Korean
Capital Presbytery (KCP) a reference asking KCP to help the session address
the financial question referred in item 1.
3. The situation escalated to the point that there were two groups both
claiming to be the true Session of KPCW. There was reported physical
violence at the church and police were called to resolve disputes. The
congregation was advised by civil authority that if the turmoil continued, the
church might be prohibited from meeting. A total of 16 references, petitions,
accusations and complaints were filed with KCP regarding the situation at
KPCW.
4. On June 14, 2000 REs Charles Kim and Moon K. Ham held a meeting
and suspended RE David K. Han.
5. REs Kim and Ham on September 7, 2000 held another meeting and
voted to have KPCW withdraw from KCP and restored Choon Soon Lee and
Joo Bok Suh as active elders without a vote of the congregation.
6. Certain church officers including RE Charles Kim sought in civil court
to have existing church trustees replaced without congregational approval.
7. KCP subsequently received requests

- **a)** to investigate financial mismanagement,
- **b)** a petition from members of the congregation of KPCW asking KCP to dismiss RE Charles Kim and
- **c)** letters reporting on “divisive and destructive activities of REs

Charles Kim and Moon K. Ham.” A total of 16 references, petitions,
accusations and complaints were filed with KCP regarding the
situation at KPCW.
8. To deal with these 16 petitions, references, complaints and accusations,
on October 9, 2000 at the reconvened portion of its 17th Stated Meeting KCP
appointed a commission to “suspend the functions of the KPCW church
Session and have the Presbytery replace the functions and report to the
Presbytery meeting in three months.”
9. The Presbytery Commission, known as the Administrative Commission
and the Judicial Commission (it had been assigned both functions), began its
work on October 16, 2000 and commenced to act for the session.
10. On October 15, 2000 the Commission arrived at KPCW to lead the
Sunday worship service but was prevented from doing so by members of the
congregation.
11. On October 16, 2000 three Ruling Elders and a Teaching Elder, acting
as the interim moderator of KPCW advised the commission that “the
undersigned fully recognize the authority of the Commission and Judicial
Commission formed by the decision made at the 17th Stated Presbytery
meeting. . .”

<a id="ga30-p117"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=119 printed_page=117 -->

12. The Commission on October 20, 2000 sought and ultimately received a
civil court order “to protect the church properties and to secure the safety of
church members.” The order restrained certain Ruling Elders including RE
Kim from interfering with the commission.
13. The KCP commission summoned RE Kim to appear at a meeting on
December 6, 2000 to examine and hear testimony before deciding on whether
to discipline and censure. RE Kim did not appear and he was cited to appear
on December 30, 2000.
14. At the meeting on December 30, 2000 RE Kim was suspended from the
sacraments for three years.
15. At a called meeting of KCP on January 8, 2001 Presbytery approved the
actions of its commission including its censure of RE Kim.
16. On January 10, 2001 RE Kim appealed his censure to the Standing Judicial Commission.
17. A five-man panel of the SJC heard the complaint on December 5, 2001
at the McLean Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Was Presbytery and/or its Commission appointed on October 9, 2000
the proper court to assume original jurisdiction to receive and adjudicate the
charges against the Appellant Charles C. Kim?

#### III. Judgment

No, such original jurisdiction should be with the Session; therefore,
the Presbytery’s action and/or the Judicial Commission of Presbytery’s actions
in this matter are reversed.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

1. The action of KCP clearly violated Preliminary Principle Six in the
Preface to the Book of Church Order which states that “. . . the powertoelect
persons to the exercise of authority in any particular society resides in that
society.” (Emphasis added) Here KCP has appointed an Administrative and
Judicial Commission to suspend the Session of KPCW and to replace the
functions of the Session, without the approval or consent of the congregation
of KPCW - as a matter of fact without even contacting the congregation of
KPCW. The appointment of the Commission by KCP was unauthorized by
the BCO; and therefore, any of its actions are not binding on the session and
congregation.
A word of explanation may be helpful to reaffirm the history and
purpose of these eight Preliminary Principles. Since the 16th Century
Protestant Reformation, there have been numerous Reformed denominations
with varying forms of church polity - some more hierarchical and others more
democratic. These eight principles were originally adopted by the first

<a id="ga30-p118"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=120 printed_page=118 -->

American General Assembly in 1789. Our American Presbyterian forefathers
had come to America with fresh memories of the persecutions under the Act of
Supremacy fostered by Henry VIII in England. They did not want to form a
denomination that was governed “from the top down” but “from the bottom
up.”
In 1787, when the original four Synods agreed to have a General Assembly, they appointed a committee to first draft a series of preliminary
Principles to be approved before the Book of Church Order was written. This
Committee worked for a year and presented these eight Preliminary Principles
to the meeting of the Synods in 1788. These Preliminary Principles were
approved so that the denomination would not be hierarchical in its polity.
they then appointed a committee to draft a Book of Church Order based on
these eight Preliminary Principles. this Book of Church Order was adopted at
the first American Presbyterian General Assembly in 1789.
It is interesting to note that by 1973 when the PCA was formed, both
PCUS and UPCUSA had removed those 8 principles from their respective
constitutions and had become hierarchical denominations.
In 1973, after we had decided to separate from PCUS and before the
PCA was actually formed, we called our group THE CONTINUING
CHURCH, meaning that we intended to organize a denomination continuing
the polity that our American forefathers adopted in 1789 based on these eight
principles.
This action of KCP violated Principle Six of the Preliminary Principles
in that it gave authority to its Commission to suspend those Session members
elected by the congregation “to the exercise of authority” in KPCW, and to
replace them with those of the choosing of KCP or its Commission.
This action similarly violated BCO 16-2 which states that “... the right
of God’s people to recognize by election to office those so gifted is
inalienable. Therefore no man can be placed over a church in any office
without the election, or at least the consent of that church.” (Emphasis added)
The dictionary defines the meaning of the word “inalienable” as follows:
(1) incapable of being surrendered
(2) incapable of being transferred
(3) that which one cannot give away or dispose of, even if one wishes.
KCP argues that it had authority for such action under BCO 13-9
which grants power to Presbytery:
“. . . In cases in which the Session cannot exercise its
authority, it shall have power to assume original
jurisdiction.”
KCP has misinterpreted this language for their interpretation conflicts
with the provisions of the Preliminary Principles and BCO 16-2, above. The
general rule of interpretation of the BCO is that no provision can be properly

<a id="ga30-p119"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=121 printed_page=119 -->

interpreted if it conflicts with any of the Preliminary Principles. Thus, if a BCO
provision is interpreted so that it conflicts with a Preliminary Principle, such
interpretation is erroneous. Every interpretation of the BCO after the
Preliminary Principles in the Preface must be consistent with such Preliminary
Principles - otherwise the interpretation is in error. KCP made such a
misinterpretation.
2. This case sets before the SJC the constitutional status of the Presbytery
(KCP) and its relationship and power to deal with a problem in one of its local
congregations (KPCW). The PCA functions at three separate levels - the
General Assembly, the Presbytery and the local church (with the Session, as
chosen by the congregation, being the authoritative body at the local level).
Each of these levels exists in two ways or with two concurrent identities. First,
as an ecclesiastical body and second, as a civil body (the precise legal nature of
the civil identity is governed by local civil law). Each of these courts of the
church exercises two distinct kinds of authority - first ecclesiastical authority
and the second civil authority. Such ecclesiastical authority is defined in the
Preliminary Principles as “only ministerial and declarative” or “purely moral
or spiritual in its object.” (Preliminary Principles Seven and Eight). Such civil
authority is determined by the civil law where the church is located.
This ecclesiastical authority is morally binding on the lower court, but
it is not legally binding or authoritative over a lower court ina civil sense. In
an effort to explain this distinction the 1986 General Assembly adopted the
final recommendations and concluding proposals of the Ad Interim Committee
on Structure which stated that a higher court may “Act On” or “Act Against” a
matter before it from a lower court; but a higher court cannot “Act For” the
lower court (14th General Assembly Minutes pp. 104-105). The General Assembly then defined the meaning of the term “Act For” as follows:
“Act For” - a higher court taking an action on a matter
over which a lower court had original jurisdiction, which
action is as conclusive of the matter as if it had been made
by the lower court and is similarly enforceable.” (14th
General Assembly Minutes page 105).
The 15th General Assembly defined the words “church power” to
mean:
“The ability to require (force) compliance with the
decision of the courts.” (Min. 15 G.A. p. 480.)
Clearly the Preliminary Principles in the Preface to the Book of Church Order state that such power is “only ministerial and declarative” and “must be
purely moral and spiritual in its nature” (Preliminary Principles Seven and
Eight of BCO Preface).
The remainder of the Book of Church Order is consistent in setting
forth the nature of church power as:

<a id="ga30-p120"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=122 printed_page=120 -->

Section 3-2 “Ecclesiastical power which is wholly spiritual...”
Section 3-3 “The sole functions of the Church as a Kingdom and
government, distinct from the civil commonwealth, are to
proclaim, to administer, and to enforce the law of Christ revealed
in the Scriptures.”
Section 3-4 “The power of the Church is exclusively spiritual; and
that of the State includes the use of force.”
In this case, KCP sought to “Act For” the congregation and Session of
KPCW, and in doing so KCP erred. The congregation and Session elected by
the congregation had original jurisdiction of actions or failures to act by
members of the Session of KPCW and KCP sought to “Act For” the lower
court. If KPCW did not follow the instructions of the Administrative and
Judicial Commission of KCP, then KCP would have had to go to civil court to
enforce its action. KCP has no civil connection to KPCW for that would be
“Acting For” KPCW and is contrary to the BCO. KCP and its Commission
failed to properly interpret the power and authority given it by the BCO.
3. BCO 14-7 provides for the manner in which previous decisions in
judicial cases “may be appealed to in subsequent similar cases as to any
principle which may have been decided.” (Emphasis added)
We appeal to a similar case and to the principle decided therein
namely:
RE Charles E. Chappell vs. Eastern Carolina Presbytery
(Case 90-4, PCA Digest Volume 1, Part III Pages 430-442).
In that case, Eastern Carolina Presbytery appointed a commission and
clothed “it with full power of Presbytery to resolve all matters of conflict at
Fuller Memorial Church, Durham” (p. 430).
The Commission of East Carolina Presbytery then suspended the
entire Session with the Commission acting as Session Pro-Tem (p. 430-C).
The Commission of East Carolina Presbytery argued that the basis of the
authority of Presbytery comes from the powers of Presbytery listed in BCO-13
(p. 431-E). This is exactly the same argument that KCP made in this case.
In this East Carolina Presbytery case two of the issues were stated (p.
433) as follows:
“2. Did East Carolina Presbytery have the authority and
power to suspend the members of the Session of Fuller
Memorial Church, without consent of the congregation and
without due process?
“3. Did Eastern Carolina Presbytery or its Commission have
authority and power to constitute itself as a Session Pro Tem
for Fuller Church and make all decisions therefore?” (p.
433).

<a id="ga30-p121"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=123 printed_page=121 -->

The Judgment in this East Carolina Presbytery case on the above two
issues was as follows:
“2. The Book of Church Order does not give a presbytery
the authority and power to suspend members of a Session
without the consent of the congregation and without due
process. The Commission of Eastern Carolina Presbytery
erred in so doing.
“3. While the Commission may have believed its replacing
the Fuller Session was under taken with consent (there was
no formal vote on this issue), its action had the effect of
removing and replacing the Session with one that had not
been elected by the Fuller congregation, which action of the
Commission exceeded the jurisdiction given by the Book of Church Order, (p. 433-434).”
We believe the principles in the East Carolina Presbytery case are
applicable in this case; and we appeal to it as precedent for our Judgment
herein.
4. Another supporting reason for this Judgment is the lack of original
jurisdiction of KCP over the church members and session members of KPCW.
Consider the following Book of Church Order provisions relating to original
jurisdiction over church members and session members of KPCW, to-wit:
(1) BCO 33-1 states that “Process against all church members, other
than ministers of the Gospel, shall be entered before the Session to
which the members belong.”
(2) BCO 31-1 states that “original jurisdiction in relations to church
members shall be, in the Session of the church of which he/she is a
member.”
contrary to these explicit statements in the PCA Book of Church Order, KCP sought to exercise original jurisdiction over the members and
session of KPCW. The appointment by KCP of the Administrative and
Judicial Commission with instructions to suspend the Session of KPCW and to
act as and for the Session, without the approval of the congregation and due
process, was an attempt to exercise original jurisdiction over the members and
session of KPCW. This was contrary to the PCA - BCO and our historical
standards. Therefore, the Commission’s actions are not binding on the session
or congregation.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Issue and Judgment were prepared by the entire Panel of five
members. The Reasoning and Opinion was written by RE W. Jack Williamson
with concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, TE Jim Smith, RE Grant McCabe
and RE John White.

<a id="ga30-p122"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=124 printed_page=122 -->

Adopted: 16 concurred, 1 dissented, 1 recused, 1 disqualified, 5 absent
Case 2001-2
APPEAL OF TE SANG SOO RYOO
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. The Appellant TE Sang Soo Ryoo was called to be an assistant pastor
by the Session of Korean Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW) in 1998.
2. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of KPCW.
Initially the dispute centered principally on questions about the finances of
Washington Theological Seminary which was connected with KPCW.
3. On December 30, 1999 the Session of KPCW submitted to Korean
Capital Presbytery (KCP) a reference asking KCP to help the session address
the financial question referred in item #1.
4. The situation escalated to the point that there were two groups both
claiming to be the true Session of KPCW. There was reported physical
violence at the church and police were called to resolve disputes. The
congregation was advised by civil authority that if the turmoil continued, the
church might be prohibited from meeting. A total of 16 references, petitions,
accusations and complaints were filed with KCP regarding the situation at
KPCW.
5. To deal with these 16 petitions, references, complaints and
accusations, on October 9, 2000 at the reconvened portion of its 17th Stated
Meeting KCP appointed a commission to “suspend the functions of the KPCW
church Session and have the Presbytery replace the functions and report to the
Presbytery meeting in three months.”
6. The Presbytery Commission, known as the Administrative
Commission and the Judicial Commission (it had been assigned both
functions), began its work on October 16, 2000 and commenced to act for the
session and as a judicial commission.

<a id="ga30-p123"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=125 printed_page=123 -->

7. The commission on November 4, 2000 summoned and
admonished TE Ryoo for divisive activities.
8. On November 18, 2000 the commission received accusations of
divisive and disruptive conduct against TE Sang Soo Ryoo in connection with
the division within KPCW.
9. The commission subsequently summoned him to appear for trial on
December 16, 2000.
10. TE Ryoo did not respond to that summons and a second summons was
sent asking that he appear for trial on December 30, 2000. TE Ryoo did not
respond to that summons and was judged to be guilty of heresy and schism
(BCO 34-5) and was suspended from ministry for two years.
11. At a called meeting of KCP on January 8, 2001 Presbytery approved
the actions of its commission and upheld the suspension of TE Ryoo.
12. On January 11, 2001 TE Ryoo appealed his suspension to the Standing Judicial Commission.
13. A five-man panel of the SJC heard the appeal on December 4, 2001 at
the McLean Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Did the Judicial Commission follow BCO procedures in instituting and
conducting judicial process against the appellant?

#### III. Judgment

No, the commission did not follow the procedures of BCO 32; and,
therefore, the case is remanded to Presbytery for a new trial in compliance
with BCO 32.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

It is clear that no indictment was ever served upon the accused. It is
not clear whether or not an indictment was ever prepared. Further, there is
nothing in the Record of the Case to show what occurred when the judgment
was entered by the lower court. It appears to have been entered by default
without any hearing and testimony. The Record of the Case does not contain
any minutes of the proceeding.
BCO 32-3 provides in part that:
“When a charge is laid before the Session or Presbytery, it shall be
reduced to writing, and nothing shall be done at the first meeting of the court,
unless by consent of parties, except:
1. to appoint a prosecutor,
2. to order the indictment drawn and a copy, along with names of
witnesses then known to support it, served on the accused, and
3. to cite all parties and their witnesses to appear and be heard at another
meeting which shall not be sooner than ten days after such citation.”
It is not clear from the Record of the Case whether or not a prosecutor
was appointed formally. The proper service of an indictment together with a

<a id="ga30-p124"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=126 printed_page=124 -->

list of witnesses is critical and because that did not happen here the case must
be remanded.
We are not passing upon the adequacy of the two citations, copies of
which are part of the record. Likewise, we are not passing on the issue of
whether or not a “default” judgment is proper without a hearing and the
testimony of witnesses together with a proper record or minute of same.
Because our decision is on the procedural aspects of the case we do
not comment on the merits.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Statement of the Issue and Judgment were prepared by the entire
panel of five members. The Reasoning and Opinion was written by RE Grant
McCabe with concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, TE Jim Smith, RE Jack
Williamson and RE John White.
Roll call vote on Case 2001-2:
TE Dominic Aquila Concur RE Collie Lehn Concur
RE Mark Belz Absent RE Tom Leopard Concur
RE Frank Brock Concur TE Lawrence Lunceford Recused
RE Robert Cannada Concur TE William Lyle Concur
RE M. C. Culbertson Concur RE J. Grant McCabe Concur
RE Howard Donahoe Concur TE Charles McGowan Concur
RE Samuel Duncan Concur TE David Peterson Concur
TE Robert Ferguson Absent TE G. Dewey Roberts Concur
RE Bruce Gunther Absent TE James Smith Concur
TE William E. Harrell Concur TE Robert Stuart Concur
TE Ben Konopa, Jr. Concur RE John B. White, Jr. Concur
TE Paul Kooistra Absent RE W. Jack Williamson Concur
Adopted: 19 concur, 1 recused, 4 absent
Case 2001-3
APPEAL OF TE SUNG KEON KIM
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. The Appellant TE Sung Keon Kim was called to be a assistant pastor
by the Session of Korean Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW) and began
his ministry on July 15, 2001.
2. Prior to his arrival, division developed within the leadership of KPCW.
3. The situation escalated to the point that there were two groups both
claiming to be the true Session of KPCW. There was reported physical violence
at the church and police were called to resolve disputes. The congregation was
advised by civil authority that if the turmoil continued, the church might be
prohibited from meeting. A total of 16 references, petitions, accusations and
complaints were filed with KCP regarding the situation at KPCW.

<a id="ga30-p125"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=127 printed_page=125 -->

4. To deal with these 16 petitions, references, complaints and
accusations, on October 9, 2000 at the reconvened portion of its 17th Stated
Meeting KCP appointed a commission to “suspend the functions of the KPCW
church Session and have the Presbytery replace the functions and report to the
Presbytery meeting in three months.”
5. The Presbytery Commission, known as the Administrative
Commission and the Judicial Commission (it had been assigned both
functions), began its work on October 16, 2000 and commenced to act for the
session and as a judicial commission.
6. Beginning with a meeting on September 10, 2000 TE Sung Keon Kim
served as the moderator of one of the KPCW sessions.
7. On December 4, 2000 charges were fded with the judicial
commission charging TE Kim with numerous actions including

- **a)** “cooperating with the illegal session as moderator”,
- **b)** participation in a meeting where one of the sessions decided to withdraw KPCW from presbytery without congregational approval,
- **c)** disruption of worship services and
- **d)** obstruction of the commission in its official duties.

8. The commission on November 4, 2000 summoned and admonished
TE Kim for divisive activities.
9. TE Kim was summoned to appear for trial on December 30, 2000. TE
Kim did not respond to that summons. He was judged to be guilty of heresy
and schism (BCO 34-5) and was suspended from ministry for five years.
10. At a called meeting of KCP on January 8, 2001 Presbytery approved
the actions of its commission and upheld the suspension of TE Kim.
11. On January 11, 2001 TE Kim appealed his suspension to the Standing Judicial Commission.
12. A five-man panel of the SJC heard the appeal on December 4, 2001 at
the McLean Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Did the Judicial Commission follow BCO procedures in instituting and
conducting judicial process against the appellant?

#### III. Judgment

No, the commission did not follow the procedures of BCO 32 and 34;
and, therefore, the case is remanded to Presbytery for a new trial in compliance
with BCO 32 and 34.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

It is clear that no indictment was ever served upon the accused. It is
not clear whether or not an indictment was ever prepared. Further, there is
nothing in the Record of the Case to show what occurred when the judgment
was entered by the lower court. It appears to have been entered by default

<a id="ga30-p126"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=128 printed_page=126 -->

without any hearing and testimony. The Record of the Case does not contain
any minutes of the proceeding.
BCO 32-3 provides in part that:
“When a charge is laid before the Session or Presbytery, it shall be
reduced to writing, and nothing shall be done at the first meeting of the court,
unless by consent of parties, except:
1. to appoint a prosecutor,
2. to order the indictment drawn and a copy, along with names of
witnesses then known to support it, served on the accused, and
3. to cite all parties and their witnesses to appear and be heard at another
meeting which shall not be sooner than ten days after such citation.”
It is not clear from the Record of the Case whether or not a prosecutor
was appointed formally. The proper service of an indictment together with a
list of witnesses is critical and because that did not happen here the case must
be remanded.
We are not passing upon the adequacy of the two citations, copies of
which are part of the record. Likewise, we are not passing on the issue of
whether or not a “default” judgment is proper without a hearing and the
testimony of witnesses together with a proper record or minute of same.
Because of our decision is on the procedural aspects of the case we do
not comment on the merits.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Statement of the Issue and Judgment were prepared by the entire
panel of five members. The Reasoning and Opinion was written by RE Grant
McCabe with concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, TE Jim Smith, RE Jack
Williamson and RE John White.

<a id="ga30-p127"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=129 printed_page=127 -->

Case 2001-4
APPEAL OF RE MOON K. HAM
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of Korean
Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW). Initially the dispute centered
principally on the question about the finances of Washington Theological
Seminary which was connected with KPCW.
2. On December 30, 1999 the Session of KPCW submitted to Korean
Capital Presbytery (KCP) a reference asking KCP to help the session address
the financial question referred in item #1.
3. The situation escalated to the point that there were two groups both
claiming to be the true Session of KPCW. There was reported physical
violence at the church and police were called to resolve disputes. The
congregation was advised by civil authority that if the turmoil continued, the
church might be prohibited from meeting.
4. On June 14, 2000 REs Charles Kim and Moon K. Ham held a session
meeting and suspended RE David K. Han.
5. REs Kim and Ham on September 7, 2000 held another session meeting
and voted to have KPCW withdraw from KCP and restored Choon Soon Lee
and Joo Bok Suh as active elders without a vote of the congregation.
6. Certain church officers including RE Moon K. Ham sought in civil
court to have existing church trustees replaced without congregational
approval.
7. KCP subsequently received requests a) to investigate financial
mismanagement, b) a petition from members of the congregation of KPCW
asking KCP to dismiss RE Ham and c) letters reporting on “divisive and
destructive activities of REs Charles Kim and Moon K. Ham.” A total of 16
references, petitions, accusations and complaints were filed with KCP
regarding the situation at KPCW.
8. To deal with these 16 petitions, references, complaints and
accusations, on October 9, 2000 at the reconvened portion of its 17th Stated
Meeting KCP appointed a commission to “suspend the functions of the KPCW
church Session and have the Presbytery replace the functions and report to the
Presbytery meeting in three months.”
9. The Presbytery Commission, known as the Administrative
Commission and the Judicial Commission (it had been assigned both
functions), began its work on October 16, 2000 and commenced to act for the
session.

<a id="ga30-p128"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=130 printed_page=128 -->

10. On October 15, 2000 the Commission arrived at KPCW to lead the
Sunday worship service but was prevented from doing so by members of the
congregation.
11. On October 16, 2000 three Ruling Elders and a Teaching Elder, acting
as the interim moderator of KPCW advised the commission that “the
undersigned fully recognize the authority of the Commission and Judicial
Commission formed by the decision made at the 17th Stated Presbytery
meeting...”
12. The Commission on October 20, 2000 sought and ultimately received
a civil court order “to protect the church properties and to secure the safety of
church members.” The order restrained certain Ruling Elders including RE
Ham from interfering with the commission.
13. The KCP commission summoned RE Ham to appear at a meeting on
December 16, 2000 to examine him and hear testimony before deciding on
whether to discipline and censure. RE Ham did not appear and he was cited to
appear on December 30, 2000.
14. At the meeting on December 30, 2000 RE Ham was suspended from
the sacraments for three years.
15. At a called meeting of KCP on January 8, 2001 Presbytery approved
the actions of its commission including its censure of RE Ham.
16. On January 17, 2001 RE Ham appealed his censure to the Standing Judicial Commission.
17. Appellant and Appellee waived hearing of the case and a five-man
panel of the SJC decided the appeal on December 5,2001 at the McLean
Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Was Presbytery and/or its Commission appointed on October 9, 2000
the proper court to assume original jurisdiction to receive and adjudicate the
charges against the Appellant Charles C. Kim?

#### III. Judgment

No, such original jurisdiction should be with the Session; therefore,
the Presbytery’s action and/or the Judicial Commission of Presbytery’s actions
in this matter are reversed.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

The court of original jurisdiction for communicant members in any
local church is the Session of that church. The Appellant in this case was a
member of the Korean Presbyterian Church of Washington and thus, any
judicial action or censure against a member must originate within the court of
original jurisdiction, the Session. Since neither the KCP nor its Commission
was the proper court of original jurisdiction, the KCP Commission’s action to

<a id="ga30-p129"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=131 printed_page=129 -->

adjudicate charges against the Appellant was contrary to the constitutional
standards, and the action of the lower court is reversed.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Issue and Judgment were prepared by the entire Panel of five
members. The Reasoning and Opinion was written by TE Jim Smith with
concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, RE Jack Williamson, RE Grant
McCabe and RE John White.
Roll call vote on Case 2001-4:
TE Dominic Aquila Concur RE Collie Lehn Concur
RE Mark Belz Concur RE Tom Leopard Disqualified
RE Frank Brock Absent TE Lawrence Lunceford Recused
RE Robert Cannada Concur TE William Lyle Concur
RE M. C. Culbertson Concur RE J. Grant McCabe Concur
RE Howard Donahoe Dissent TE Charles McGowan Concur
RE Samuel Duncan Concur TE David Peterson Absent
TE Robert Ferguson Absent TE G. Dewey Roberts Concur
RE Bruce Gunther Absent TE James Smith Concur
TE William E. Harrell Concur TE Robert Stuart Concur
TE Ben Konopa, Jr. Concur RE John B. White, Jr. Concur
TE Paul Kooistra Absent RE W. Jack Williamson Concur
Adopted: 16 concurred, 1 dissented, 1 recused, 1 disqualified, 5 absent
Case 2001-7
COMPLAINT OF TE ANDY LEE
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of Korean
Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW). Initially the dispute centered
principally on questions about the finances of Washington Theological
Seminary which was connected with KPCW.
2. On December 30, 1999 the Session of KPCW submitted to Korean
Capital Presbytery (KCP) a reference asking KCP to help the session address
the financial question referred to in item 1.
3. The situation escalated to the point that there were two groups both
claiming to be the true Session of KPCW. There was reported physical
violence at the church and police were called to resolve disputes. The
congregation was advised by civil authority that if the turmoil continued the
church might be prohibited from meeting.
4. Subsequently, additional requests were made of KCP a) to investigate
financial mismanagement, b) a petition from members of the congregation of
KPCW asking KCP to dismiss RE Charles Kim and c) letters reporting on

<a id="ga30-p130"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=132 printed_page=130 -->

“divisive and destructive activities of REs Charles Kim and Moon K. Ham.”
A total of 16 references, petitions, accusations and complaints were filed with
KCP regarding the situation at KPCW.
5. Certain KPCW officers sought in civil court to have existing church
trustees replaced without congregational approval.
6. To deal with these 16 petitions, references, complaints and
accusations, on October 9, 2000, at the reconvened portion of its 17th Stated
Meeting, KCP appointed a commission to “suspend the functions of the
KPCW church Session and have the Presbytery replace the functions and
report to the Presbytery meeting in three months.”
7. The Presbytery Commission, known as the Administrative
Commission and the Judicial Commission (it had been assigned both
functions), began its work on October 16, 2000 and commenced to act for the
session.
8. On October 15, 2000 the Commission arrived at KPCW to lead the
Sunday worship service but was prevented from doing so by members of the
congregation.
9. On October 16, 2000 three Ruling Elders and a Teaching Elder, acting
as the interim moderator of KPCW advised the commission that “the
undersigned fully recognize the authority of the Commission and Judicial
Commission formed by the decision made at the 17th Stated Presbytery
meeting...”
10. On October 30, 2000 TE Andy Lee complained about the action of the
KCP in suspending the KPCW session and appointing a Commission to
replace the session. That complaint was denied by KCP at its stated meeting
on April 2, 2001.
11. TE Lee filed his complaint with the Standing Judicial Commission and
a five-man panel of the SJC heard the complaint on December 4, 2001 at the
McLean Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

1. Did the Korean Capital Presbytery (KCP) err in its action of October
9, 2000, in suspending the Session of the Korean Presbyterian Church of
Washington (KPCW) and appointing a Commission to act for the KPCW
Session without the prior consent of the congregation?
2. Are the subsequent actions of the commission, acting as the KPCW
Session, in accordance with the BCO?

#### III. Judgment

1. Yes
2. No, therefore, all subsequent actions of the commission acting as the
Session of KPCW are annulled.

<a id="ga30-p131"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=133 printed_page=131 -->

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

The issue in this case has to do with whether or not the Korean Capital
Presbytery had the BCO authority to suspend the Session of the Korean
Presbyterian Church of Washington and act for the Session of KPCW in the
manner set forth by the facts of the case.
An examination of the facts in the case is sufficient to establish that
there was great distress and turmoil within the corporate life of the Korean
Presbyterian Church of Washington (KPCW) as a result of allegations of
financial impropriety at the Washington Theological Seminary, a ministry of
the KPCW. On December 30, 1999 the Session of KPCW requested the
assistance of the Korean Capital Presbytery (KCP) by mean sofa reference
(BCO 41-1 and ROC p. 5 Attachment 1) in which the Session of KPCW asked
the KCP for “final disposition of these matters”. This request became the basis
for the Korean Capital Presbytery, at its 17th Stated Meeting on October 9,
2000, for taking the following actions: KCP suspend the Session of KPCW
and its functions for three months according to BCO 13-9 and appoint a
Commission to be clothed with judicial powers to resolve all matters of
conflict at KPCW.
The KCP’s appointed Commission acted for the Session of KPCW by
approving various assignments of duties and responsibilities of members of the
Commission for the Session functions, such as worship, finance,
administration and building management.
The KCP maintained that the presence of division and conflict within
the KPCW was such that the KPCW Session could no longer carry out its BCO
responsibilities. The KCP determined that a state of “anarchy prevailed, the
KPCW had completely lost its governing function and, practically speaking,
the KPCW Session ceased to function and exist” (Respondent’s primary brief
p. 3). The issue is whether or not the Korean Capital Presbytery had the BCO
authority to suspend the Session of the Korean Presbyterian Church of
Washington and act for the Session of KPCW without the prior approval of the
KPCW congregation.
The action of KCP to suspend the KPCW Session and appoint a
Commission to act for the KPCW Session was based on BCO 13-9, which
states that
The Presbytery has power to receive and issue appeals, complaints and
references brought before it in an orderly manner. In cases in which
the Session cannot exercise its authority, it shall have power to assume
original jurisdiction...
The Complainant correctly argued, however, that the BCO 13-9 can
not be understood in isolation from other relevant portions of the BCO. For
example, Preliminary Principle Six states in part
Though the character, qualifications and authority of Church officers
are laid down in the Holy Scriptures, as well as the proper method of

<a id="ga30-p132"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=134 printed_page=132 -->

officer investiture, the power to elect persons to the exercise of
authority in any particular society resides in that society.
(emphasis added)
Furthermore, BCO 3-1 underscores the right of the congregation to be
governed by those duly elected by that body.
The power which Christ has committed to His Church vests in the
whole body, the rulers and those ruled, constituting it a spiritual
commonwealth. This power, as exercised by the people, extends to
the choice of those officers whom He has appointed in His Church.
The language of BCO 16-2 confirms the principle of the consent of the
governed as being foundational to our denomination’s understanding of
Biblical church government.
The government of the Church is by officers gifted to represent Christ,
and the right of God’s people to recognize by election to office those
so gifted is inalienable. Therefore no man can be placed over a
church in any office without the election, or at least the consent of
that church.” (Emphasis added)
The BCO of the PCA is clear when it speaks to the issue of
jurisdiction. The higher court may not act for the lower court to remove from
office those duly elected without the prior consent or request of the lower
court. “.. .The right of God’s people to recognize by election to office those so
gifted is inalienable...” (BCO 16-2). Thus, without the prior consent of the
lower court, a higher court may not act to dismiss or suspend the duly elected
officers of the lower court and replace them with others.
Based on the ROC, the KCP did not have the prior consent of the
congregation of KPCW to suspend its ruling elders and appoint a Presbytery
Commission to act for the Session of KPCW. The Korean Capital Presbytery’s
action of October 9, 2000 to do just that, to suspend the Session of the KPCW
without the prior consent of the congregation (BCO 24-6) and to form a
Presbytery Commission to act as the Session of KPCW was, in fact, a violation
of the BCO of the PCA.
The KCP Commission was erected and clothed with powers to act for
the Session of KPCW in a manner not provided for in the BCO. Thus, all
subsequent actions of the KCP Commission, appointed by the Presbytery to act
as the Session of KPCW, are annulled insofar as the Commission was not
constituted in accordance with BCO requirements.
While the ROC indicates that both the Session of KPCW and the
congregation of KPCW took action in which each attempted to recognize the
legitimacy of the KCP Commission to act for the Session of KPCW, such
actions were subsequent to and not prior to the KCP’s action to suspend the
Session and form the Commission. Furthermore, the congregational meeting
of December 3, 2000 in which the congregation did accede to the authority of
the KCP commission was a meeting called by the KCP Commission acting for

<a id="ga30-p133"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=135 printed_page=133 -->

the Session. Since the Commission lacked BCO authority to act for the
Session, this congregational meeting was not in accordance with the BCO and
all actions resulting from such a meeting would have to be regarded as null and
void.
One final issue raised by the KCP does bear some consideration. The
KCP argued in its brief that case 2001 -7 was not properly before the SJC since
it believed the issues in this case to be identical with those found in case 2001-
1 (the Appeal of RE Charles C. Kim v. KCP). The Respondent for KCP
argued that “no complaint is allowable in a judicial case in which an appeal is
taken” (BCO 43-1). The SJC is of the opinion, however, that Case 2001-7 is
properly before it since the issues in the cases 2001-1 and 2001-7 are
fundamentally different and thus BCO 43-1 does not pertain.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Statement of the Issue and the Judgment were prepared and
adopted by the Panel of five members. The Reasoning and Opinion was
written by TE Jim Smith, with the concurrence of TE Charles McGowan, RE
Grant McCabe, RE John White and RE Jack Williamson.
Roll call vote on Case 2001-7:
TE Dominic Aquila Abstain RE Collie Lehn Concur
RE Mark Belz Absent RE Tom Leopard Disqualified
RE Frank Brock Dissent TE Lawrence Lunceford Recused
RE Robert Cannada Concur TE William Lyle Concur
RE M. C. Culbertson Concur RE J. Grant McCabe Concur
RE Howard Donahoe Dissent TE Charles McGowan Concur
RE Samuel Duncan Concur TE David Peterson Concur
TE Robert Ferguson Absent TE G. Dewey Roberts Concur
RE Bruce Gunther Absent TE James Smith Concur
TE William E. Harrell Concur TE Robert Stuart Concur
TE Ben Konopa, Jr. Concur RE John B. White, Jr. Concur
TE Paul Kooistra Absent RE W. Jack Williamson Concur
Adopted: 15 concurred, 2 dissented, 1 recused, 1 disqualified, 4 absent, 1 abstain.
Case 2001-8
COMPLAINT OF TE ANDY LEE
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of Korean
Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW). Initially the dispute centered
principally on questions about the finances of Washington Theological
Seminary which was connected with KPCW.

<a id="ga30-p134"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=136 printed_page=134 -->

2. On December 30, 1999 the Session of KPCW submitted to Korean
Capital Presbytery (KCP) a reference asking KCP to help the session address
the financial question referred in item #1.
3. The situation escalated to the point that there were two groups both
claiming to be the true Session of KPCW. There was reported physical
violence at the church and police were called to address disputes. The
congregation was advised by the civil authority that if the turmoil continued,
the church might be prohibited from meeting.
4. Subsequently, additional requests were made of KCP

- **a)** to investigate financial mismanagement,
- **b)** a petition from members of the congregation of KPCW asking

KCP to dismiss RE Charles Kim and
c) letters reporting on “divisive and destructive activities of REs
Charles Kim and Moon K. Ham.”
A total of 16 references, petitions, accusations and complaints were filed with
KCP regarding the situation at KPCW.
5. One Ruling Elder sought in civil court to have existing church trustees
replaced without congregational approval.
6. To deal with these 16 petitions, references, complaints and
accusations, on October 9, 2000 at the reconvened portion of its 17th Stated
Meeting, KCP appointed a commission to “suspend the functions of the
KPCW church Session and have the Presbytery replace the functions and
report to the Presbytery meeting in three months.”
7. The Presbytery Commission, known as the Administrative
Commission and the Judicial Commission (it had been assigned both
functions), began its work on October 16, 2000 and commenced to act for the
session.
8. On October 15, 2000 the Commission arrived at KPCW to lead the
Sunday worship service but was prevented from doing so by members of the
congregation.
9. On October 16, 2000 three Ruling Elders and a Teaching Elder, acting
as the interim moderator of KPCW advised the commission that “the
undersigned fully recognize the authority of the Commission and Judicial
Commission formed by the decision made at the 17th Stated Presbytery
meeting...”
10. The Commission on October 20, 2000 sought and ultimately received
a civil court order “to protect the church properties and to secure the safety of
church members.” The order restrained certain Ruling Elders from interfering
with the commission.
11. The Commission acting for the Session of KPCW called three
congregational meetings on November 5, December 3, and 31, instituted ten
temporary church steering committees, changed the signers of various church
bank accounts, and completed the transfer of the church facilities purchased by
KPCW.

<a id="ga30-p135"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=137 printed_page=135 -->

12. The Commission, acting for the Session of KPCW fired TE Sung
Keon Kim as assistant pastor of the church.
13. At a called meeting of KCP on January 8, 2001 Presbytery approved
the actions of its commission.
14. On January 22, 2001 TE Andy Lee complained about the action of the
KCP Commission. That complaint was denied by KCP at its stated meeting on
April 2, 2001.
15. TE Lee filed his complaint with the Standing Judicial Commission on
April 5, 2001.
16. A five-man panel of the SJC heard the complaint on December 3, 2001
at the McLean Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

1. Did the Korean Capital Presbytery (KCP) err in its action of October
9, 2000 in suspending the Session of the Korean Presbyterian Church of
Washington (KPCW) and appointing a Commission to act for the KPCW
Session?
2. Did the KCP err when its appointed Commission sought and obtained
an injunction from the civil courts to conduct the Sessional affairs of the
KPCW?

#### III. Judgment

1. Yes
2. Yes, but if the Commission had been established in accordance with
the BCO it would have been proper for the Commission to seek such civil
assistance as may have been “necessary for the protection and security equal
and common to all others.” (Preface II-1(b), BCO)

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

BCO 34-1 establishes the Session as the court of original jurisdiction
for the local church. It is made explicit in BCO 33-1 that process against
church members shall be entered before the Session. The congregation
establishes the Session by electing its members (BCO 24-1,3,4). It is explicit
in the BCO of the Presbyterian Church in America (BCO 25-11) that none of
the rights and responsibilities of the congregation shall ever be taken away
“without the express consent and affirmative action” of the congregation.
Since there is no evidence that Presbytery suspended the Session and
appointed the Commission with the required prior consent of the congregation
of KPCW, this action of KCP is ruled to have been unauthorized by the BCO.
The Record of the Case (ROC) does indicate that the KPCW congregation
subsequently on December 3, 2000 did grant its consent; but it did so at a
congregational meeting that had been called by the KCP Commission acting as
the Session of the Church. Such action by the KPCW congregation is not a
legitimate action since it was taken at an improperly called congregational
meeting.

<a id="ga30-p136"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=138 printed_page=136 -->

Further such action was ex post facto since it was taken on December
3, 2000 and KCP had appointed the Commission and suspended the Session on
October 9, 2000.
Although Complainant in his Complaint, dated April 5, 2001 did not
raise the issue that KCP had authority to appoint the Commission to act as
Session of KPCW under BCO 13-9, the issue was raised briefly in KCP’s Brief
to the SJC and in the oral argument of KCP’s representative. We further note
that at its presbytery meeting on April 2, 2001 KCP changed the language of
its minutes of October 9, 2000 which change noted that KCP’s actions taken
under BCO 13-9. Therefore, we feel we should briefly answer that argument.
We have fully answered that argument in several companion cases such as
Case No. 2000-9, styled TE Sun Keon Kim as Complainant vs. Korean Capital
Presbytery and Case No. 2001-7, styled TE Andy Lee as Complainant vs.
Korean Capital Presbytery and we hereby adopt the extensive answer in those
cases.
Here we simply state that KCP has misinterpreted BCO 13-9 for their
interpretation conflicts with Preliminary Principle Six and BCO 16-2. The
general rule of interpretation of the BCO is that no provision can be inteipreted
properly if it conflicts with any of the Preliminary Principles as set forth in the
Preface or with any express provision of the BCO. if a BCO provision is
interpreted so that it conflicts with a Preliminary Principle, such interpretation
is erroneous. Every interpretation of the BCO after the Preliminary Principles
in the Preface must be consistent with such Preliminary Principles otherwise
the interpretation is in error. In relying on its interpretation of BCO 13-9, KCP
made such a misinterpretation.
Regarding issue #2, it should be made clear that the BCO (II-1(b))
recognizes that in certain circumstances assistance may be requested of civil
authorities to provide protection and security equal and common to all others.
The SJC makes no judgment concerning the appropriateness of having sought
a civil court order and requesting the presence of the civil police in order to
accomplish the business of the Session. It is only as the Commission was
functioning as the Session of the KPCW without authority of the BCO that an
error was made.
While the SJC recognizes that the KCP was, in good faith, seeking to
be of practical assistance to the KPCW, which church apparently was in great
turmoil, it is important to rule KCP’s action unauthorized by the BCO, since
the procedures it followed were in violation of some of the most basic
principles set forth in the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America
guaranteeing certain rights to each congregation and its session.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Statement of the Issues and Judgment were prepared by the entire
panel of five members. The Reasoning and Opinion was written jointly by TE
Charles McGowan and RE Jack Williamson with concurrence by TE Jim
Smith, RE Grant McCabe, and RE John White.

<a id="ga30-p137"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=139 printed_page=137 -->

Roll vote on Case 2001-8:
TE Dominic Aquila Disqualified RE Collie Lehn Concur
RE Mark Belz Absent RE Tom Leopard Disqualified
RE Frank Brock Dissent TE Lawrence Lunceford Recused
RE Robert Cannada Concur TE William Lyle Concur
RE M. C. Culbertson Concur RE J. Grant McCabe Concur
RE Howard Donahoe Dissent TE Charles McGowan Concur
RE Samuel Duncan Concur TE David Peterson Absent
TE Robert Ferguson Absent TE G. Dewey Roberts Concur

Case 2001-9
APPEAL OF RE CHARLES KIM
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of Korean
Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW). In response to the turmoil and the
filing of 16 petitions, references and charges, Korean Capital Presbytery
(KCP) suspended the session and appointed a commission to act for the
session.
2. Complaints against KCP for its action were filed and subsequently RE
Kim participated in efforts to call a special meeting of KCP for among other
things the purpose of dissolving the commission.
3. Charges against RE Kim for allegedly participating in an
“unconstitutional” meeting of Presbytery were filed on December 22, 2000.
4. KCP did have a properly called special meeting on January 8, 2001, at
which it appointed a commission to deal with the charges against RE Kim.
5. The Commission summoned RE Kim to appear and answer charges on
February 3 and 17, 2001 but he did not respond.
6. On March 12, 2001 the commission heard testimony and received
evidence, found RE Kim guilty of the charges of schism and contumacy and
excommunicated him. The sentence was finalized on March 26, 2001.
7. At the 18th Stated Meeting of KCP on April 2, 2001 the report of the
commission along with the excommunication of RE Kim was approved.
8. On April 16, 2001 RE Kim appealed his excommunication to the
Standing Judicial Commission.

<a id="ga30-p138"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=140 printed_page=138 -->

9. A five-man panel of the SJC heard the appealon December4, 2001 at
the McLean Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Was Presbytery and/or its Special Judicial Commission appointed on
January 8, 2001 the proper court to assume original jurisdiction to receive and
adjudicate the charges against the Appellant Charles C. Kim?

#### III. Judgment

No, such original jurisdiction should be with the Session; therefore,
the Presbytery’s action and/or the Special Judicial Commission of Presbytery’s
actions in this matter are reversed.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

The court of original jurisdiction for communicant members inany
local church is the Session of that church. The Appellant in this case was a
member of the Korean Presbyterian Church of Washington and thus, any
judicial action or censure against a member must originate within the court of
original jurisdiction, the Session. Since neither the KCP nor its Commission
was the proper court of original jurisdiction, the KCP Commission’s action to
adjudicate charges against the Appellant was contrary to the constitutional
standards, and the action of the lower court is reversed.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Issue and Judgment were prepared by the entire Panel of five
members. The Reasoning and Opinion was written by TE James Smith with
concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, RE Jack Williamson, RE Grant
McCabe and RE John White.
Roll call vote on Case 2001-9:
TE Dominic Aquila Concur RE Collie Lehn Concur
RE Mark Belz Concur RE Tom Leopard Disqualified
RE Frank Brock Absent TE Lawrence Lunceford Recused
RE Robert Cannada Concur TE William Lyle Concur
RE M. C. Culbertson Concur RE J. Grant McCabe Concur
RE Howard Donahoe Concur TE Charles McGowan Concur
RE Samuel Duncan Concur TE David Peterson Absent

RE Bruce Gunther Absent TE James Smith Concur
TE William E. Harrell Concur TE Robert Stuart Concur

Case 2001-10
APPEAL OF RE CHARLES KIM
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of Korean
Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW). In response to the turmoil and the
filing of 16 petitions, references and charges, Korean Capital Presbytery
(KCP) suspended the session and appointed a commission to act for the
session.
2. On September 7, 2000 REs Charles Kim and Moon K. Ham held a
session meeting (without a quorum) and voted to replace KPCW trustees.
3. On September 15, 2000 certain officers of KPCW including RE Kim
filed in civil court a petition for appointing church trustees. Said petition
indicated that the trustees were determined in the September 7, 2000 session
meeting.
On November 14, 2000 five KPCW members filed a written
accusation against seven KPCW officers and members including RE Kim for
replacing and registering the church trustees improperly.
4. KCP, at a called special meeting on January 8, 2001, appointed a
commission to deal with the charges against RE Kim.
5. The Commission summoned RE Kim to appear and answer charges on
February 3 and 17, 2001 but he did not respond.
6. On March 12, 2001 the commission heard testimony and received
evidence, found RE Kim guilty of
a) contumacy

- **b)** the charges of church division and church destruction by holding an unconstitutional meeting and
- **c)** making a false statement on a official document filed with the civil court.

The commission inflicted the censure of excommunication.
7. At the 18th Stated Meeting of KCP on April 2, 2001 the report of
the commission along with the excommunication of RE Kim was
approved.
8. On April 16, 2001 RE Kim appealed his excommunication to the
Standing Judicial Commission.
9. Appellant and Appellee waived hearing of the case and a five-man
panel of the SJC decided the appeal on December 5, 2001 at the McLean
Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Was Presbytery and/or its Special Judicial Commission appointed on
January 8, 2001 the proper court to assume original jurisdiction to receive and
adjudicate the charges against the Appellant Charles C. Kim?

<a id="ga30-p140"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=142 printed_page=140 -->

#### III. Judgment

No, such original jurisdiction should be with the Session; therefore,
the Presbytery’s action and/or the Special Judicial Commission of Presbytery’s
actions in this matter are reversed.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

The court of original jurisdiction for communicant members in any
local church is the Session of that church. The Appellant in this case was a
member of the Korean Presbyterian Church of Washington and thus, any
judicial action or censure against a member must originate within the court of
original jurisdiction, the Session. Sinceneither theKCP nor itsCommission
was the proper court of original jurisdiction,the KCPCommission’s action to
adjudicate charges against the Appellant was contrary to the constitutional
standards, and the action of the lower court is reversed.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Statement of the Issue and the Judgment were prepared and
adopted by the Panel of five members. The Summary of the Facts was
prepared by RE John White. The Reasoning and Opinion was written by TE
Jim Smith, with the concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, RE Grant McCabe,
RE John White and RE Jack Williamson.
