Case 2001-11
COMPLAINT OF RE BYUNG HAN YOO
VS.
KOREAN NORTHWEST PRESBYTERY
The case was found out of order on the ground that it was not timely filed
under BCO 43-2 (namely the action complained of was taken on October 10,2000
and the complaint was filed on March 12,2001, well outside the 30 day period.)

<a id="ga30-p141"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=143 printed_page=141 -->

Case 2001-12
APPEAL OF DEACON PETER LEE
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of Korean
Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW). In response to the turmoil and the
filing of 16 petitions, references and charges, Korean Capital Presbytery (KCP)
suspended the session and appointed a commission to act for the session.
2. On September 7, 2000 REs Charles Kim and Moon K. Ham held a
session meeting (without a quorum) and voted to replace KPCW trustees.
3. On September 15, 2000 certain officers of KPCW including Deacon
Peter Lee filed in civil court a petition for appointing church trustees. Said
petition indicated that the trustees were determined in the September 7, 2000
session meeting.
On November 14, 2000 five KPCW members filed a written
accusation against seven KPCW officers and members including Deacon Lee
for replacing and registering the church trustees unconstitutionally.
4. KCP, at a called special meeting on January 8, 2001, appointed a
commission to deal with the charges against Deacon Lee.
5. The Commission summoned Deacon Lee to appear and answer
charges on February 3 and 17, 2001 but he did not respond.
6. On March 12, 2001, the commission heard testimony and received
evidence, found Deacon Lee guilty of contumacy and the charges making a false
statement on a official document filed with the civil court. The commission
inflicted the censure of a five-year suspension from the sacraments.
7. At the 18th Stated Meeting of KCP on April 2, 2001 the report of the
commission along with the suspension from the sacraments of Deacon Lee was
approved.
8. On April 25, 2001 Deacon Lee appealed his excommunication to the
Standing Judicial Commission.
9. Appellant and Appellee waived hearing of the case and a five-man
panel of the SJC decided the appeal on December 5, 2001 at the McLean
Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Was Presbytery and/or its Special Judicial Commission appointed on
January 8, 2001 the proper court to assume original jurisdiction to receive and
adjudicate the charges against the Appellant Peter Lee?

#### III. Judgment

No, such original jurisdiction should be with the Session; therefore,
the Presbytery’s action and/or the Special Judicial Commission of Presbytery’s
actions in this matter are reversed.

<a id="ga30-p142"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=144 printed_page=142 -->

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

The court of original jurisdiction for communicant members in any
local church is the Session of that church. The Appellant in this case was a
member of the Korean Presbyterian Church of Washington and thus, any
judicial action or censure against a member must originate within the court of
original jurisdiction, the Session. Since neither the KCP nor its Commission
was the proper court of original jurisdiction, the KCP Commission’s action to
adjudicate charges against the Appellant was contrary to the constitutional
standards, and the action of the lower court is reversed.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Statement of the Issue and the Judgment were prepared and
adopted by the Panel of five members. The Reasoning and Opinion was
written by TE Jim Smith, with the concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, RE
Grant McCabe, RE John White and RE Jack Williamson.

Case 2001-13
APPEAL OF DEACON SAMUEL HONG
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of Korean
Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW). In response to the turmoil and the
filing of 16 petitions, references and charges, Korean Capital Presbytery (KCP)
suspended the session and appointed a commission to act for the session.
2. On September 7, 2000 REs Charles Kim and Moon K. Ham held a
session meeting (without a quorum) and voted to replace KPCW trustees.
3. On September 15, 2000 certain officers of KPCW including Deacon
Samuel Hong filed in civil court a petition for appointing church trustees. Said

<a id="ga30-p143"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=145 printed_page=143 -->

petition indicated that the trustees were determined in the September 7, 2000
session meeting.
On November 14, 2000 five KPCW members filed a written accusation against
seven KPCW officers and members including Deacon Hong for replacing and
registering the church trustees improperly.
4. KCP, at a called special meeting on January 8, 2001 appointed a
commission to deal with the charges against Deacon Lee.
5. The Commission summoned Deacon Hong to appear and answer
charges on February 3 and 17, 2001 but he did not respond.
6. On March 12, 2001 the commission heard testimony and received
evidence, found Deacon Hong guilty of contumacy and the charges making a
false statement on a official document filed with the civil court. The
commission inflicted the censure of a two-year suspension from the sacraments.
7. At the 18th Stated Meeting of KCP on April 2, 2001 the report of the
commission along with the suspension from the sacraments of Deacon Hong
was approved.
8. On April 25, 2001 Deacon Hong appealed his excommunication to the
Standing Judicial Commission.
9. Appellant and Appellee waived hearing of the case and a five-man
panel of the SJC decided the appeal on December 5, 2001 at the McLean
Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Was Presbytery and/or its Special Judicial Commission appointed on
January 8, 2001 the proper court to assume original jurisdiction to receive and
adjudicate the charges against the Appellant Samuel Hong?

#### III. Judgment

No, such original jurisdiction should be with the Session; therefore,
the Presbytery’s action and/or the Special Judicial Commission of Presbytery’s
actions in this matter are reversed.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

The court of original jurisdiction for communicant members in any
local church is the Session of that church. The Appellant in this case was a
member of the Korean Presbyterian Church of Washington and thus, any
judicial action or censure against a member must originate within the court of
original jurisdiction, the Session. Since neither the KCP nor its Commission
was the proper court of original jurisdiction, the KCP Commission’s action to
adjudicate charges against the Appellant was contrary to the constitutional
standards, and the action of the lower court is reversed.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Statement of the Issue and the Judgment were prepared and
adopted by the Panel of five members. The Reasoning and Opinion was
written by TE Jim Smith, with the concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, RE
Grant McCabe, RE John White and RE Jack Williamson.

<a id="ga30-p144"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=146 printed_page=144 -->

Case 2001-14
APPEAL OF RE MOON K. HAM
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of Korean
Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW). In response to the turmoil and the
filing of 16 petitions, references and charges, Korean Capital Presbytery (KCP)
suspended the session and appointed a commission to act for the session.
2. On September 7, 2000 REs Charles Kim and Moon K. Ham held a
session meeting (without a quorum) and voted to replace KPCW trustees.
3. On September 15, 2000 certain officers of KPCW including RE Ham filed
in civil court a petition for appointing church trustees. Said petition indicated that
the trustees were determined in the September 7,2000 session meeting.
On November 14, 2000 five KPCW members filed a written
accusation against seven KPCW officers and members including RE Ham for
replacing and registering the church trustees unconstitutionally.
4. KCP, at a called special meeting on January 8, 2001 appointed a
commission to deal with the charges against RE Kim.
5. The Commission summoned RE Ham to appear and answer charges
on February 3 and 17, 2001 but he did not respond.
6. On March 12, 2001 the commission heard testimony and received
evidence, found RE Ham guilty of
a) contumacy

- **b)** the charges of church division and church destruction by holding an unconstitutional meeting and
- **c)** making a false statement on a official document filed with the civil court. The commission inflicted the censure of indefinite suspension from the sacraments.

<a id="ga30-p145"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=147 printed_page=145 -->

7. At the 18th Stated Meetingof KCP onApril 2, 2001 the reportof the
commission along with the suspension from the sacraments of RE Ham was
approved.
8. On April 25, 2001 RE Hamappealed his excommunication to the
Standing Judicial Commission.
9. Appellant and Appellee waived hearing of the case and a five-man
panel of the SJC decided the appeal on December 5, 2001 at the McLean
Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Was Presbytery and/or its Special Judicial Commission appointed on
January 8, 2001 the proper court to assume original jurisdiction to receive and
adjudicate the charges against the Appellant Moon K. Ham?

#### III. Judgment

No, such original jurisdiction should be with the Session; therefore,
the Presbytery’s action and/or the Special Judicial Commission of Presbytery’s
actions in this matter are reversed.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

The court of original jurisdiction for communicant members in any
local church is the Session of that church. The Appellant in this case was a
member of the Korean Presbyterian Church of Washington and thus, any
judicial action or censure against a member must originate within the court of
original jurisdiction, the Session. Since neither the KCP nor its Commission
was the proper court of original jurisdiction, the KCP Commission’s action to
adjudicate charges against the Appellant was contrary to the constitutional
standards, and the action of the lower court is reversed.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John White.
The Statement of the Issue and the Judgment were prepared and adopted by the
Panel of five members. The Reasoning and Opinion was written by TE Jim Smith,
with the concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, RE Grant McCabe, RE John White
and RE Jack Williamson.

The court of original jurisdiction for communicant members in any

February 3 and 17, 2001 but he did not respond.
6. On March 12, 2001 the commission heard testimony and received
evidence, found RE Lee guilty of contumacy and the charges making a false
statement on a official document filed with the civil court. The commission
inflicted the censure of a three-year suspension from the sacraments.
7. At the 18th Stated Meeting of KCP on April 2, 2001 the report of the
commission along with the suspension of RE Lee was approved.
8. On April 25, 2001 RE Lee appealed his suspension to the Standing Judicial Commission.
9. Appellant and Appellee waived hearing of the case and a five-man
panel of the SJC decided the appeal on December 5, 2001 at the McLean
Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Was Presbytery and/or its Special Judicial Commission appointed on
January 8, 2001 the proper court to assume original jurisdiction to receive and
adjudicate the charges against the Appellant Choon Soon Lee?

#### III. Judgment

No, such original jurisdiction should be with the Session; therefore,
the Presbytery’s action and/or the Special Judicial Commission of Presbytery’s
actions in this matter are reversed.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

The court of original jurisdiction for communicant members in any
local church is the Session of that church. The Appellant in this case was a
member of the Korean Presbyterian Church of Washington and thus, any
judicial action or censure against a member must originate within the court of
original jurisdiction, the Session. Since neither the KCP nor its Commission
was the proper court of original jurisdiction, the KCP Commission’s action to
adjudicate charges against the Appellant was contrary to the constitutional
standards, and the action of the lower court is reversed.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Statement of the Issue and the Judgment were prepared and
adopted by the Panel of five members. The Reasoning and Opinion was
written by TE Jim Smith, with the concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, RE
Grant McCabe, RE John White and RE Jack Williamson.

<a id="ga30-p151"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=153 printed_page=151 -->

RE Mark Belz Absent RE Tom Leopard Disqualified

RE M. C. Culbertson Concur RE J. Grant McCabe Concur

RE Bruce Gunther Absent TE James Smith Concur
TE William E. Harrell Concur TE Robert Stuart Concur

Case 2001-18
APPEAL OF TE SANG SOO RYOO
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. The Appellant TE Sang Soo Ryoo was called to be an assistant pastor
by the Session of Korean Presbyterian Church Washington in 1998.
2. Beginning in 1999, division developed within the leadership of Korean
Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW). Initially the dispute centered
principally on questions about the finances of Washington Theological
Seminary which was connected with KPCW.
3. On December 30, 1999 the Session of KPCW submitted to Korean
Capital Presbytery (KCP) a reference asking KCP to help the session address
the financial question referred in item #1.
4. The situation escalated to the point that there were two groups both
claiming to be the true Session of KPCW. There was reported physical
violence at the church and police were called to resolve disputes. The
congregation was advised by the civil authority that if the turmoil continued,
the church might be prohibited from meeting. A total of 16 references,
petitions, accusations and complaints were filed with KCP regarding the
situation at KPCW.
5. To deal with these 16 petitions, references, complaints and
accusations, on October 9, 2000 at the reconvened portion of its 17th Stated
Meeting KCP appointed a commission to “suspend the functions of the KPCW
church Session and have the Presbytery replace the functions and report to the
Presbytery meeting in three months.”

<a id="ga30-p152"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=154 printed_page=152 -->

6. The Presbytery Commission, known as the Administrative Commission
and the Judicial Commission (it had been assigned both functions), began its
work on October 16, 2000 and commenced to act for the session and as a
judicial commission.
7. Turmoil continued at KPCW and the commission acting as that
session sought and obtained a civil court order to enforce its administration of
the church.
8. The commission also called three congregational meetings including
one that dissolved the official relationships with two ruling elders.
9. Under these circumstances, TE Ryoo participated in efforts to call a
special meeting of KCP. When no meeting was scheduled, TE Ryoo
participated in the call of a meeting in accordance with BCO 13-12.
10. Charges against TE Ryoo for participating in the call of an
“unconstitutional” meeting of Presbytery were filed on December 22, 2000.
11. KCP did have a special meeting on January 8, 2001, at which it
appointed a commission to deal with the charges against TE Ryoo.
12. The Commission summoned TE Ryoo to appear and answer charges
on Februaiy 3, 17 and March 3, 2001 but he did not respond.
13. On March 12, 2001 the commission heard testimony and received
evidence and found TE Ryoo guilty of the charges and imposed on him a
three-year suspension from the sacraments.
14. At the 18th Stated Meeting of KCP on April 2, 2001 the report of the
commission along with the suspension of TE Ryoo from the sacraments was
approved
15. On April 25, 2001 TE Ryoo appealed his suspension from the
sacraments to the Standing Judicial Commission.
16. A five-man panel of the SJC heard the appeal on December 4, 2001 at
the McLean Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Did the Judicial Commission follow BCO procedures in instituting and
conducting judicial process against the appellant?

#### III. Judgment

No, the commission did not follow the procedures of BCO 32, 34; and,
therefore, the case is remanded to Presbytery for a new trial in compliance
with BCO 32, 34.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

It is clear that no indictment was ever served upon the accused. It is
not clear whether or not an indictment was ever prepared. The Record of the

<a id="ga30-p153"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=155 printed_page=153 -->

Case does not contain any minutes of the proceeding whether or not it was a
trial. .
BCO 32-3 provides in part that:
“When a charge is laid before the Session or Presbytery, it shall be
reduced to writing, and nothing shall be done at the first meeting of the
court, unless by consent of parties, except:
(1) to appoint a prosecutor,
(2) to order the indictment drawn and a copy, along with
names of witnesses then known to support it, served on the
accused, and
(3) to cite all parties and their witnesses to appear and be
heard at another meeting which shall not be sooner than ten
days after such citation.”
It is not clear from the Record of the Case whether or not a prosecutor
was appointed formally. The proper service of an indictment together with a
list of witnesses is critical and because that did not happen here the case must
be remanded.
We are not passing upon the adequacy of the citations, copies of which
are part of the record.
Because our decision is on the procedural aspects of the case we do
not comment on the merits.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Statement of the Issue and Judgment were prepared by the entire
panel of five members. The Reasoning and Opinion was written by RE Grant
McCabe with concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, TE Jim Smith, RE Jack
Williamson and RE John White.

RE Frank Brock Disqualified TE Lawrence Lunceford Recused

RE Samuel Duncan Concur TE David Peterson Concur
TE Robert Ferguson Absent TE G. Dewey Roberts Concur
RE Bruce Gunther Absent TE James Smith Concur
TE William E. Harrell Concur TE Robert Stuart Concur
TE Ben Konopa, Jr. Concur RE John B. White, Jr. Concur

<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=156 printed_page=154 -->

Case 2001-19
APPEAL OF TE SUNG KEON KIM
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. The Appellant TE Sung Keon Kim was called to be assistant pastor by
the Session of Korean Presbyterian Church Washington (KPCW) and began
his ministry on July 15, 2000.
2. Prior to his arrival, division developed within the leadership of Korean
Presbyterian Church Washington.
3. The situation escalated to the point that there were two groups both
claiming to be the true Session of KPCW. There was reported physical
violence at the church and police were called to resolve disputes. The
congregation was advised by the civil authority that if the turmoil continued,
the church might be prohibited from meeting. A total of 16 references,
petitions, accusations and complaints were filed with KCP regarding the
situation at KPCW.
4. To deal with these 16 petitions, references, complaints and
accusations, on October 9, 2000 at the reconvened portion of its 17th Stated
Meeting KCP appointed a commission to “suspend the functions of the KPCW
church Session and have the Presbytery replace the functions and report to the
Presbytery meeting in three months.”
5. The Presbytery Commission, known as the Administrative
Commission and the Judicial Commission (it had been assigned both
functions), began its work on October 16, 2000 and commenced to act for the
session and as a judicial commission.
6. Turmoil continued at KPCW and the commission, acting as that
session, sought and obtained as civil court order to enforce its administration
of the church.
7. Under these circumstances, TE Kim participated in efforts to call a
special meeting of KCP. When no meeting was scheduled, TE Kim
participated in the call of a meeting in accordance with BCO 13-12.
8. Charges against TE Kim for participating in the call of an
“unconstitutional” meeting of Presbytery were filed on December 22, 2000.
9. KCP did have a properly called special meeting on January 8, 2001, at
which it appointed a commission to deal with the charges against TE Kim.
10. The Commission summoned TE Kim to appear and answer charges on
February 3, 17 and March 3, 2001 but he did not respond.
11. On March 12, 2001 the commission heard testimony and received
evidence and found TE Kim guilty of the charges and contumacy and deposed
him from the ministry.

<a id="ga30-p155"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=157 printed_page=155 -->

12. At the 18th Stated Meetingof KCP on April 2, 2001 the report of the
commission along with the deposition of TE Kim was approved
13. On April 25, 2001 TE Kimappealed hisdeposition to theStanding
Judicial Commission.
14. A five-man panel of the SJC heard the appeal on December 4, 2001 at the
McLean Presbyterian Church in McLean, VA.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

Did the Judicial Commission follow BCO procedures in instituting and
conducting judicial process against the appellant?

#### III. Judgment

No, the commission did not follow the procedures of BCO 32, 34; and,
therefore, the case is remanded to Presbytery for a new trial in compliance
with BCO 32, 34.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

It is clear that no indictment was ever served upon the accused. It is
not clear whether or not an indictment was ever prepared. The Record of the
Case does not contain any minutes of the proceeding whether or not it was a
trial.
BCO 32-3 provides in part that:
“When a charge is laid before the Session or Presbytery, it
shall be reduced to writing, and nothing shall be done at the first
meeting of the court, unless by consent of parties, except:
(1) to appoint a prosecutor,
(2) to order the indictment drawn and a copy, along with
names of witnesses then known to support it, served on the
accused, and
(3) to cite all parties and their witnesses to appear and be
heard at another meeting which shall not be sooner than ten
days after such citation.”
It is not clear from the Record of the Case whether or not a prosecutor
was appointed formally. The proper service of an indictment together with a
list of witnesses is critical and because that did not happen here the case must
be remanded.
We are not passing upon the adequacy of the citations, copies of which
are part of the record.
Because our decision is on the procedural aspects of the case we do
not comment on the merits.
The Statement of the Facts of this case was prepared by RE John
White. The Statement of the Issue and Judgment were prepared by the entire
panel of five members. The Reasoning and Opinion was written by RE Grant

<a id="ga30-p156"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=158 printed_page=156 -->

McCabe with concurrence by TE Charles McGowan, TE Jim Smith, RE Jack
Williamson and RE John White.

Case 2001-25
COMPLAINT OF TE ANTHONY DALLISON
VS.
NORTH FLORIDA PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. On May 8, 1997 TE’s Jack Arnold, Chuck Holiday, and David Burke,
took the second step of Matthew 18:15 ff with Mrs. Anthony Dallison,
confronting TE Anthony Dallison with allegations of his having committed
physical spousal abuse for the course of his marriage to Mrs. Dallison.
2. At a Called meeting on May 27, 1997 the Presbytery of Central
Florida heard a confession of sin from TE Anthony Dallison., wherein he
freely confessed his guilt of having physically abused his wife throughout
much of the course of their marriage of, at that time, more than 27 years.
3. The Presbytery, upon hearing this confession, acted according to the
provisions of BCO 34-7 and 38-1, adopting the following:
a) That the censure of indefinite suspension from the office of
Teaching Elder for not less than three years be imposed upon Anthony
R. Dallison including the dissolution of the pastoral relation, as per
BCO 34-7,8,9
b) That Anthony R. Dallison and [his wife] be subject to professional
counseling by counselors approved by the Minister and His Work
Committee (MHW) and that Anthony and [his wife] and the
counselors sign release forms with the counselor for consultation
reports to be given to the MHW Committee

<a id="ga30-p157"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=159 printed_page=157 -->

c) That Anthony R. Dallison report by phone to the Chairman of the
MHW Committee (or other designated member of the Presbytery)
eveiy week, and meet personally with the Chairman once every other
month at which time he will review his personal daily journal thereby
keeping the MHW Committee abreast of his progress
d) That Presbytery direct that Anthony and recommend that [his wife]
(and the children when possible) place themselves under the preached
word of a PCA church other than that of Westminster Presbyterian
Church
e) That in the event that Anthony should fail to comply with any of the
stipulations of a) through d) above, that said failure shall be reported
by the MHW Committee to the Presbytery at Presbytery’s next stated
meeting; and in the event that there should be any further physical
abuse that the MHW Committee recommend to the next stated meeting
of Presbytery the censure of deposition upon Anthony R. Dallison
4. Central Florida’s MHW Committee worked with Mr. Dallison
according to the above provisions, and reported regularly to Presbytery
regarding his progress.
5. At a stated meeting on October 17, 1998 the Presbytery of Central
florida approved a motion that a number of its churches in northern Florida,
and a number of its Teaching Elders laboring or residing therein, including TE
Dallison, be transferred as members of the newly formed Presbytery of North
Florida. On the same date, at the first meeting of the Presbytery of North
Florida, Anthony Dallison was received as a member Teaching Elder without
call.
6. North Florida’s Shepherding Committee assumed oversight of Mr.
Dallison, worked with him according to the provisions of #3 above, and
reported regularly to Presbytery regarding his progress.
7. At a stated meeting on May 19, 2000 the Shepherding Committee of
North Florida Presbytery reported to Presbytery that on October 6, 1999 Mr.
Dallison struck a table in his home in anger in the presence of his wife and TE
Neal Ganzel; that on October 7, 1999 Mr. Dallison moved out of his home and
as of May 19 had not returned to live there; that he had severed financial ties
with the household upkeep, except to pay off the mortgage on the house; and
that his psychologist’s reports were not encouraging.
8. At a stated meeting of North Florida Presbytery on July 15, 2000 Mr.
Dallison requested that Presbytery divest him from the office of Teaching
Elder, according to the provisions of BCO 38-2. In response to this request,
Presbytery appointed a committee to determine compliance of TE Dallison
with the terms of the May 27, 1997 censure (#3 above).
9. At a stated meeting on January 12, 2001 the Presbytery of North
Florida, heard the report of the committee formed on July 15, 2000 to

<a id="ga30-p158"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=160 printed_page=158 -->

determine TE Dallison’s compliance with the terms of the May 27, 1997
censure (#3 above). In response to that report, Presbytery adopted the
following motion:
After determining that the three-year minimum period of indefinite
suspension in the case of TE Anthony Dallison of May 27, 1997 of
Central Florida Presbytery has not brought about the signs of
repentance required to move toward restoration, but rather bears clear
evidence of conduct necessitating further censure (BCO 30-3), the
Presbytery of North Florida, with great sorrow anddesire for true
repentance in the life of Anthony Dallison, and the healing of his
marriage, hereby deposes Mr. Anthony R. Dallison from the sacred
office of Teaching Elder, suspends him from the sacraments of the
church, and remands him to the care of the session of Christ Church,
PCA, Jacksonville, FL.
10. Anthony Dallison filed on February 2, 2001 a Complaint alleging that
the Presbytery of North Florida had erred in its action stated in #9 above.
11. At a stated meeting on May 12, 2001 the Presbytery of North Florida
heard and denied the Complaint filed by Mr. Dallison on February 2, 2001.
12. Anthony Dallison on June 8, 2001 filed with the Stated Clerk of the
General Assembly a Complaint alleging that The Presbytery of North Florida
had erred when it denied his Complaint of February 2, 2001.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

1. Did the Presbytery of North Florida errconstitutionally or
procedurally when it acted on January 12, 2001 to depose Anthony Dallison
from the office of Teaching Elder, and to suspend him from the sacraments of
the Church without instituting fresh process against him?
2. Did the Presbytery of North Florida err when on January 12, 2001 it
determined Anthony Dallison to be impenitent with respect to his originally
confessed sin, and increased the original censure to that of deposing him from
the office of Teaching Elder, and suspending him from the sacraments of the
Church?

#### III. Judgment

1. No. Where a Teaching Elder has confessed guilt to presbytery under
BCO 38-1, Presbytery has the authority to impose the censure here imposed on
January 12, 2001 without initiating new charges and conducting a new trial.
2. No. Presbytery’s determination of impenitence, and the imposition of
additional censure based upon that determination, were within the sound
discretion of presbytery, and within Presbytery’s authority, under BCO 37-3’,
37-4, 37-8, and 34-4b.

<a id="ga30-p159"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=161 printed_page=159 -->

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

1. The basic position of the Complainant, TE Anthony Dallison, is that
North Florida Presbytery did not have the right to proceed to additional or
greater censures in his case without instituting a new trial. Dallison confessed
to the sin of physical spousal abuse on May of 1997 when confronted by
members of Central Florida Presbytery with the accusation. In accordance with
BCO 38-1, he was then censured on May 27, 1997 without standing trial. The
Complainant, through his representative, acknowledged at the hearing before
this panel that presbytery had the power in 1997 to censure him according to
their discretion. Those actions of presbytery in 1997 are not at issue in this
case and cannot be reconsidered under any scenario. Rather, the central issue
in this case is the Complainant’s argument that presbytery did not have the
power to increase the censure at a later date without first proving his
impenitence through the full process of a new trial with further charges.
2. There are three principles which the BCO outlines with respect to the
authority and discretion which church courts have over guilty parties who are
under disciplinary action. First, it is the offender who not only must repent but
who must give satisfaction to the court of his repentance in relation to the
charges against him (BCO 33-2; 34-4a and 34-8). Second, it is the
responsibility of the court to “be satisfied as to the reality of the repentance” of
the offender before restoring him (BCO 37-3); to “obtain sufficient evidence of
his sincere repentance” before restoring him (BCO 37-4); to “test the sincerity
of his repentance” before restoring him (BCO 37-8); and, “to proceed with
great caution” (BCO 37-8) in the restoration of a minister.” Third, if the
offender does not satisfy the court that he is repentant or if, in the discretion
and judgment of the court, he remains contumacious, the court may proceed to
higher censures, including deposition and excommunication (BCO 33-3; 34-
4b).
3. The ROC clearly shows that North Florida Presbytery proceeded with
great caution in this case and gave the Complainant numerous opportunities to
exhibit his repentance. The motion of July 15, 2000 to “investigate” Dallison
was for the purpose of determining his compliance to the original censure and
whether he had repented sufficiently of his sin to warrant restoration (ROC, p.
91) or whether deposition from office would be necessary to reclaim him.
During the period he was under the original censure, Dallison did not satisfy
the lower court of his “fruit in keeping with repentance” (Matthew 3:8) and
they had a right to further investigate the matter before proceeding with one of
the following: additional censure, the removal of the censure or the retaining of
the original censure. In order to faithfully exercise its God-given authority of
discretion and judgment in disciplinary cases, courts must investigate
offenders to obtain sufficient evidence of their sincere repentance or lack
thereof. This investigation by North Florida Presbytery was not for the

<a id="ga30-p160"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=162 printed_page=160 -->

purpose of finding new sins with which to charge Dallison. as a result of their
investigation, presbytery did not proffer new charges and a new trial was not
required. Likewise, Dallison’s constitutional rights were not denied him as the
Complainant’s brief argues. Rather, this investigation was only that judicial
consideration which courts always retain “until the declaration of restoration
has been pronounced” (BCO 37-8).
4. Before a trial, the accused is to be considered innocent until proven guilty.
The situation changes, though once the accused either confesses to the offense
or through trial is determined to be guilty of such. At that point, the court no
longer bears the responsibility of proving his guilt. The onus is not on the court
to prove the offender’s impenitence, but on the offender to satisfy the court
that he is repentant. The complaint of Dallison against North Florida
Presbytery, if upheld, would turn Biblical justice on its head and would allow
an offender under censure to retain all the rights of the innocent or the accused.
Yet, those rights with respect to the original charge were effectively waived by
Dallison when he confessed his guilt and sin on May 27, 1997 (BCO 43-1).
5. There are several established and unchallenged facts which support the
position that North Florida Presbytery was correct in its judgment and
discretion concerning Dallison. First, Dallison struck a table in anger and left
his house when his wife questioned his financial integrity in the presence of
TE Neal Ganzel on October 6, 1999 (2 1/2 years after the original censure of
May 27, 1997 and more than a year before deposition from office). To this
date, Dallison still has not provided any proof of his innocence concerning the
particular matter which his wife questioned even though he continues to assert
his complete integrity. Second, he moved out of the house the following day,
October 7, 1999 and has severed financial support of his wife (though he did
pay off the remaining balance of the home mortgage which was approximately
$7,000). Third, he has not exhibited the necessary humility to restore his
marriage and be welcomed by his wife to return home (though he has
demanded that she allow him to return). These actions by Dallison are not
peripheral to the main issue in this case but are a manifestation of that same
spirit which led him on several occasions over his married life to physically
strike and abuse his wife. His conduct, in these respects, has not been of “such
an eminently exemplary, humble and edifying life and testimony as shall heal
the wound made by his scandal” (BCO 34-8). Rather, he has acted in such a
way as to widen the breach in his marriage and further erode the prospects of
his future usefulness in the ministry. These points went unchallenged and
mostly unaddressed by the Complainant (or his representative) in the hearing
before this panel and were the matters investigated by presbytery in
determining to increase Dallison’s censure to deposition from the ministry.
6. The main position of the Complainant is that the lower courts do not
have authority according to the exercise of their discretion and judgment to

<a id="ga30-p161"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=163 printed_page=161 -->

proceed to higher forms of censure without first proving the impenitence of the
offender. If courts do not have such discretion and judgment to move to higher
forms of censure, then neither would they have the authority to restore the
truly repentant. The position of the Complainant would effectively nullify the
binding and loosing authority which Christ gave to His church (Matthew
18:18; John 20:23). Church courts can declare that sins are retained for
evident impenitence and also can declare that sins are forgiven for manifest
repentance. This authority is not suspended when the original censure is
inflicted and then regained when it is time to restore the fallen brother (which
is what the Complainant’s position would require), but is active throughout the
entire time the case is “under judicial consideration until the declaration of
restoration has been pronounced” {BCO 37-8). Thus, BCO 36-2 says “the
degree of censure and mode of administering it shall be within the discretion of
the court.” If the court determines in its mercy that it is going to inflict the
lowest censure possible in the beginning and move to higher censures only if
necessary, that discretion is within their authority and should not be overturned
by the higher court “unless there is clear error on the part of the court” (BCO 3
9-3).
7. In a letter to North Florida Presbytery on May 15, 2000 Dallison
requested the privilege to demit the ministiy according to BCO 38-2 which
necessarily required that court to lift their censure against him. That request
was tantamount to an admission by the Complainant that presbytery has the
power to loose, though his complaint denies she has the power to bind. Thus,
Dallison included in his request statements that he had “fulfilled the
stipulations of the original censure of May 1997” and had “endeavored to
comply fully with the terms of the original censure” (ROC, p. 75). These
statements were a request for North Florida Presbytery to loose the original
censure against him on the basis of his professed fulfillment of their terms.
They constitute a petition that presbytery exercise its discretion in determining
appropriate adjustments to the censure in his case. His complaint, though,
attempts to divide presbytery’s binding power from her loosing power. Yet, the
power which Christ gives His church includes both and the one cannot be
severed from the other without undermining the whole authority.
8. The Complainant’s brief sets forth numerous principles in support of
his position with which all Christians should agree; such as, the right of the
accused to a fair trial, the calling of witnesses, the cross examination of
witnesses, etc. Yet, those principles are not germane to the issues in this case
because presbytery was simply exercising its authority and discretion in the
mode of administering censure against a confessed offender whom it judged to
still be impenitent. Those principles would be germane only if a new trial for
new charges had been required by our constitution and had been neglected by
North Florida Presbytery. Such was not the situation in this case.

<a id="ga30-p162"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=164 printed_page=162 -->

9. If this complaint were upheld, it could lead to the harshest form of
censure being administered by courts from the outset in violation of the true
nature of discipline which “is to be exercised under a dispensation of mercy
and not of wrath” (BCO 27-4). The Complainant’s representative stated at the
hearing before this panel that Central Florida Presbytery could have deposed
Dallison and indefinitely suspended him from the sacraments on May 27, 1997
but North Florida Presbytery did not have the power to do so without a new
trial to prove his impenitence on January 12, 2001. Presbytery’s decision in the
first instance not to suspend Dallison from the sacraments is no waiver of
presbytery’s power to proceed to more severe censures at a later time. The
Complainant’s representative also argued before this panel that North Florida
Presbytery had the power to lift the censure of suspension from the ministry
against him, but not the power to increase the censure. The Complainant’s
argument is not supported by the BCO, and on that basis the complaint is
denied.
TE William Harrell wrote the statement of facts, RE Mark Belz wrote
the judgment, and TE Dewey Roberts wrote the reasoning and opinion.
Roll call vote on Case 2001-25:
TE Dominic Aquila Concur RE Collie Lehn Concur
RE Mark Belz Concur RE Tom Leopard Concur
RE Frank Brock Absent TE Lawrence Lunceford Concur
RE Robert Cannada Disqualified TE William Lyle Disqualified
RE M. C. Culbertson Concur RE J. Grant McCabe Disqualified
RE Howard Donahoe Concur TE Charles McGowan Concur
RE Samuel Duncan Concur TE David Peterson Absent
TE Robert Ferguson Absent TE G. Dewey Roberts Concur
RE Bruce Gunther Absent TE James Smith Concur
TE William E. Harrell Concur TE Robert Stuart Disqualified
TE Ben Konopa, Jr. Concur RE John B. White, Jr. Concur
TE Paul Kooistra Absent RE W. Jack Williamson Concur
Adopted: 15 concurred, 0 dissented, 0 recused, 4 disqualified, 5 absent
Case 2001-28
COMPLAINT OF TE LARRY BALL
VS.
WESTMINSTER PRESBYTERY

#### I. Statement of the Facts

1. On July 25, 2001 TE Larry E. Ball lodged a complaint against the decision
of Westminster Presbytery made on July 21, 2001 that denied his complaint
against the decisions of the Westminster Presbytery on May 15, 2001.

<a id="ga30-p163"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=165 printed_page=163 -->

Background
2. On May 15, 2001 Westminster Presbytery made two decisions. The first
was that Westminster Presbyterian would withdraw from the PCA with effect
from the date of the stated meeting of the Westminster Presbytery in January
2002 (ROC 29). The second decision was to send an overture to the 29th
General Assembly held in June 2001 requesting the General Assembly to erect
a new PCA Presbytery to receive ministers and churches from the Westminster
Presbytery who do not wish to withdraw from the PCA (ROC 29), and
requesting that the date of effect of the new Presbytery should be the date that
the Westminster Presbytery leaves the PCA in January 2002.
3. Details of the plan for withdrawal are set out in full in the ROC (page 32-
34). This plan provided for the present Westminster Presbytery to leave the
PCA and to retain the heritage and history of the Presbytery. It provided
ministers and churches with the option of voting to remain with the PCA in
any new PCA Presbytery that the 30th General Assembly might erect, and it
provided for an equitable distribution of the assets of the Presbytery between
the continuing Westminster Presbytery and the new PCA Presbytery.
4. The Westminster Presbytery submitted overture 38 to the 29th General Assembly in 2001 concerning this decision and plan to withdraw from the
PCA. While the 29th General Assembly rejected the request to establish a new
Presbytery by denying the overture, the General Assembly recognized that
from the date of the January 2002 stated meeting of the Westminster
Presbytery, the Presbytery (along with all its ministers and churches) would
cease to be a Presbytery in the PCA unless the Westminster Presbytery
changed its proposed course of action. Further, the 29th General Assembly
requested the Westminster Presbytery to consider changing its effective date of
withdrawal from January 2002 to a date after the PCA’s 30th General Assembly meeting in June 2002.
The Complaint
5. Mr. Ball’s complaint, as heard by the Westminster Presbytery on July 21,
2001, raised one issue namely, that the Westminster Presbytery had erred in
approving on May 15, 2001 the proposed plan for the Westminster
Presbytery’s withdrawal from the PCA (see ROC 45-48). Mr. Ball gave eight
reasons in support of his complaint. In considering this complaint the
Presbytery had before it a proposed set of answers (ROC 35-38). The
Presbytery did not adopt these proposed answers but did deny the complaint on
a vote of 32 to 19 (ROC 25).
6. On July 25, 2001 Mr. Ball complained to the General Assembly’s
Standing Judicial Commission stating one issue, namely, that the Westminster
Presbytery erred in denying his complaint (ROC 3). He listed specifications in
support of his complaint and requested the SJC to grant amends; namely, that
the decision of May 15, 2001 be declared null and void, that Westminster

<a id="ga30-p164"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=166 printed_page=164 -->

Presbytery be continued within the PCA, and that churches and minister who
wish to leave the PCA be advised to act in accordance with BCO 25.
The Hearing
7. The Panel, after establishing the ROC and receiving briefs from both
parties, met on October 11, 2001 to hear the complaint. The Complainant and
the respondents presented oral arguments.
8. After the formal hearing the panel went into closed session to consider its
decision and prepare a written judgment.

#### II. Statement of the Issue

1. Did Westminster Presbytery err when it denied the complaint of Larry Ball
against the decision of Westminster Presbytery to withdraw from the
Presbyterian Church in America pursuant to the procedures adopted at its May
15, 2001 meeting?

#### III. Judgment

1. No. Thus the complaint of TE Larry Ball against the decision of
Westminster Presbytery to withdraw from the Presbyterian Church in America
pursuant to the procedures adopted at its May 15, 2001 meeting is denied.

#### IV. Reasoning and Opinion

Other General Assembly Decisions on this Issue
1. Before proceeding to examine the constitutional issues and the detailed
arguments presented by this case, the SJC notes that the issue of whether or not
the Westminster Presbytery (WP) had a constitutional right to withdraw from
the PCA had been before the General Assembly (GA) in two ways before it
came to the SJC. On both those occasions, a swell a son this occasion, the
issue raised has received the same response.
2. The first, an overture came to the 29lh GA from WP. The 29th GA received
communication # 5 and overture 30 from WP concerning WP’s plan to
withdraw from the PCA in January 2002. in response the ga sent tow pa
letter of advice. In that advice the 29th GA raised “no question concerning
your ecclesiastical and civil right as a presbytery to withdraw from the PCA.”
The GA asked the WP to reconsider its decision and reverse its judgment. The
GA did not argue for this reversal on the basis that WP did not have a
constitutional right to withdraw but raised concerns about the necessity,
prudence, and testimony of such action.
3. The second, was via a non-judicial reference to Committee on Constitutional Business in which WP requested advice concerning the
proposed plan of withdrawal. While the Committee recognized there was no
specific provision in the BCO fora presbytery to withdraw as there is fora

<a id="ga30-p165"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=167 printed_page=165 -->

local church, the Committee noted certain procedures that should be followed
in such a group withdrawal situation. This advice was presented to the 29th
General Assembly. in an amendment to the Committee’s report to the 29th
General Assembly the Committee noted the action of the 2nd General Assembly when enrolling WP and commented: “We recognize the action of the
2nd General Assembly, which enrolled Westminster Presbytery with its proviso,
which included its right to withdraw. We also recognize, however, that the
procedures we have outlined in items B-E below must be followed,”3
The Overall Constitutional Issues
4. This case sets before the SJC the constitutional status of a Presbytery and
its relationship to both the GA and the ministers and congregations that make
up the Presbytery. Does a Presbytery have the right to withdraw from the PCA,
along with all or some of its churches on terms and conditions it determines?
5. The PCA functions at three separate levels, the GA, the Presbytery and the
local church (with the Session being the authoritative church court at the local
level). Each of these levels exists in two ways or with two concurrent
identities. First as an ecclesiastical body and then secondly as a civil body (the
precise legal nature of the civil identity is affected by local civil law). For
example, the GA exists as an ecclesiastical body when meeting each year. The
“civil” identity of the GA is exercised through the trustees of the GA operating
as a civil corporation, known as “PCA Inc”. Each of the courts of the church
exercises two distinct kinds of authority, first ecclesiastical authority, which is
ministerial and declarative, and the second is civil authority, which is legal and
binding.
6. In the PCA, the relationship or connection between a higher and a lower
court is defined by the ecclesiastical authority a higher court exercises over a
lower court. Such ecclesiastical authority is spiritual, that is, it is ministerial
and declarative. It is thus morally binding and authoritative over the lower
court, but it is not legally binding or authoritative over a lower court ina civil
law sense. In this regard the 1986 General Assembly adopted the final
recommendation and concluding proposals of the Ad Interim Committee on
Structure which asserted that a higher court may “Act On” or “Act Against” a
matter before it from a lower court, but a higher court cannot “Act For” the
lower court.4
7. For example, a higher court may, both ecclesiastically and legally,
determine the membership of its own body, but a higher court may only
3 See Minutes 29-28, III, pg. 133.
4 the philosophical and theological basis for our PCA structure section of the report
of ad interim committee on structure presented to and adopted by the 14th General Assembly.

<a id="ga30-p166"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=168 printed_page=166 -->

constitutionally make ministerial declarations on the issue of membership in a
lower court. It then falls to the lower court to exercise the legal authority in
relation to its own membership. Thus the GA has both ecclesiastical and civil
authority, within the terms of the BCO, to determine to admit or exclude
commissioners to the GA. Thus in the present context, a presbytery has the
authority to determine under what conditions it will join the PCA. If those
conditions are accepted by the General Assembly and are not outside the
authority granted the General Assembly by our BCO, then those conditions
continue after the presbytery joins the PCA. Conditions of joining granted to a
presbytery, whether requested or simply granted by the General Assembly,
would be conditions granted by the General Assembly and would be
conditions expressly recorded in the minutes of the General Assembly.
8. This understanding of the separate identities of the three levels of
governance in the PCA is the foundation that protects the local church’s
ownership of property. This is also one of the major bases for constitutionally
establishing “grass roots” Presbyterianism in the PCA. it is a historic form of
Presbyterianism. In some ways this “grass roots” presbyterianism is to be
significantly distinguished from some other historic forms of Presbyterianism,
such as those forms that existed in Scotland and in Europe where there was a
“magisterial” form of Presbyterianism which was more “top down” or
“hierarchical.”
9. In the light of this understanding of our historic “grass-roots”
Presbyterianism, it is clear that the relationship between the General Assembly
and the Presbyteries is similar in nature to the relationship between a
Presbytery and the local church. The relationship is one that protects the
individual identity of each level of our governance and ensures that only
ecclesiastical authority is exercised over a lower court, and that at no time does
a higher court “Act For” a lower court.
Relevant Provisions of the BCO
10. Before applying this understanding of our constitution to the facts in this
case, we must first note how our constitution sets out this understanding.
11. While BCO 25 is of great significance to this matter and will be considered
momentarily, it is essential to first note the nature and role of the Preface to the
BCO. It is the principles set forth in the Preface that set the direction for
understanding all that follows in the BCO. It is the principles set forth in the
Preface that form the foundation for the historic PCA American “grass-roots”
Presbyterianism.
12. The Preface to our BCO is a clear reflection of the eight principles of
church polity adopted by the General Assembly in America in 1789. These
eight principles established the American form for Presbyterianism. This was a

<a id="ga30-p167"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=169 printed_page=167 -->

form that rejected state established religions. These principles, with some
minor alterations, also appear in the Preface to the 1925 edition of the BCO of
the Presbyterian Church in the United States. Thus, as the PCA came into
existence with a commitment to historic American Presbyterianism, it went
back to more fully set out the original principles of American historic
Presbyterianism as adopted in 1789.5
13. Turning now to the specifics of the Preface to our BCO. Section I affirms
the headship of Christ over the Church and in that context affirms our polity of
governance. Section III defines the constitution of the PCA. Section II sets out
the “Preliminary Principles.”
14. Section II asserts that these Principles are central and foundational, “The
Presbyterian Church in America, in setting forth the form of government
founded upon and agreeable to the Word of God, reiterates the following
principles which have governed the formation of the plan:” (emphasis in
bold added). Those reiterated principles act as principles by which all the
following provisions of the BCO are to be interpreted in a manner consistent
with those Principles. Principle One affirms the overarching significance of
the Confession’s commitment to freedom of Conscience (WCF XX). Principle
Two affirms that each level or court of the church has reserved to itself the
right to determine how BCO standards for admission to membership are to be
applied in that court. Principle Three affirms that church discipline is more
than right preaching and involves the officers of the church exercising church
discipline. Principle Four affirms the “inseparable connection between faith
and practice.” Principle Five affirms the necessity for the proper examination
and approval of all those teaching the Word. Principle Six affirms that officers
of any church must not only meet Biblical standards but must also be elected
by the constituency of the society or of the level of the Church over which they
are to exercise authority. Principle Seven affirms that the authority of church
courts is “ministerial and declarative” and empowers the right to challenge the
exercise of such authority when the freedom of conscience is at stake. This
position is recognition that church counsels and courts can err. A challenge to
the exercise of such ecclesiastical authority would need to be lodged by way of
the procedures set out in the BCO or by way severing of the relationship with
the Church. Principle Eight affirms that ecclesiastical discipline is not an
exercise of civil power but of moral or spiritual authority.
15. In the context of this case these Preliminary Principles shape the
relationship between the General Assembly and a presbytery (as well as the
relationship between a pres byte ryan da local church). These principles also
make it very clear that the kind of relationship that exists between the local
5 See Appendix A in Robert C. Cannada and W. Jack Williamson, The Historic Polity
of the PCA. (A Press, Greenville, SC 1997)

<a id="ga30-p168"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=170 printed_page=168 -->

Church and the presbytery also exist between the presbytery and the General Assembly.
16. BCO 25 expressly provides for the right of an individual church to affiliate
with whomever it will. A higher court is prohibited from impinging in any way
upon this right. (BCO 25-11; cf. 11-3, 4). This principle of the BCO is closely
related to BCO 25-11, which allows, “A particular church may withdraw from
any court of this body at any time for reasons, which seem to it sufficient”
17. It is important to note that the provisions of BCO 25 that give a local
church the right to withdraw from the PCA and to own exclusively its own
property, set out an important distinctive of historic American “grass-roots”
Presbyterianism, namely the right to withdraw and retain ownership and
control of property. In the light of the Preface to the BCO and the underlying
foundations of our constitution, these rights must be extended to a Presbytery
unless the BCO expressly excludes such rights from a Presbytery.
Specific issues raised in this case
18. The Complainant raised one issue before the WP when, in May 2001, he
brought his complaint before the WP (ROC 17) and gave eight reasons in
support. After the WP denied his complaint at the continued May meeting of
WP that was held on July 21, 2001 (ROC 25), the Complainant brought his
complaint to the SJC on July 25, 2001 (ROC 3). That complaint brought to the
SJC raised the same issue as presented to WP and gave seven specifications in
support of the complaint. The Complainant requested the addition to the
Record of the Case of two documents, namely, the minutes of WP meeting of
July 20, 1974 (ROC 60-66) and a copy of the Manual of Westminster
Presbytery (ROC 60-76).
19. The Complainant’s Brief asserted that this raised eight issues not one. Of
the eight alleged issues raised by the Complainant, all but two were
specifications listed in his complaint. The SJC agrees with WP that there was
one issue and stated that as, “Did Westminster Presbytery err when it denied
the complaint of Larry Ball against the decision of WP to withdraw from the
Presbyterian Church in America pursuant to the procedures adopted at its May
15,2001 meeting?”
20. The Complainant, in his brief, raised two new specifications or reasons in
support of his complaint.
21. The Complainant asserted that WP was in error when it relied upon the
GA’s minutes of the 2nd General Assembly that recorded the terms under
which WP and other Presbyteries joined the PCA in 1974. One of those
conditions granted by the General Assembly was that a joining Presbytery had
reserved to itself the right to withdraw, as a Presbytery from the PCA any time
and under any circumstance the Presbytery might deem appropriate. The

<a id="ga30-p169"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=171 printed_page=169 -->

Complainant argued that the WP minutes of July 20, 1974 did not reveal any
record that WP had approved those conditions. The respondent pointed out that
the minutes of the 2nd General Assembly contained requests from various
presbyteries, including WP, to join the PCA, the request from WP was
accompanied by the proper attestations from the Presbytery’s then Moderator
and Stated Clerk, that both WP and the General Assembly have conducted
their affairs since that time under the terms of those minutes of the 2nd General Assembly and the BCO. Thus in the absence of positive proof to the contrary
the record of the General Assembly minutes ought to stand as reflecting the
conditions under which WP became a presbytery within the PCA. The SJC did
not find it necessary to determine anything about what was or was not in the
minutes of WP, because the terms under which WP was admitted to the PCA
are terms approved by the General Assembly and are recorded in the GA
minutes. As noted above it falls to the GA to determine, subject to the BCO,
the conditions under which the General Assembly will receive an ecclesiastical
body into the PCA. It is clear that the GA had granted WP and other
Presbyteries the right to withdraw from the PCA, a right not only recorded in
the minutes of the 2nd General Assembly but a right that is an essential element
of historic American Presbyterianism as expressed in the Preliminary
Principles of the BCO. Thus, unless this right to withdraw was expressly
excluded in the BCO, the terms set out in the minutes of the 2nd General Assembly stand, not on the basis of WP’s approval of them but on the basis
that the 2nd General Assembly was acting constitutionally when it approved
them.
22. It is important to again note that the provisions of BCO 25, that give a
local church the right to withdraw from the PCA and to own exclusively its
own property, set out an important distinctive of historic American “grass
roots” Presbyterianism, namely the right to withdraw. it is important to note
that this right must be extended to a Presbytery under the Preliminary
Principles, unless the BCO expressly prohibits such a right. The BCO does not
prohibit a presbytery from taking such action. This right does not prevent the
GA from making ministerial declarations about a particular decision to
withdraw, or from making such declarations about the manner or terms of such
a particular decision to withdraw. However, the GA is not free to make a
declaration that a presbytery does not have aright to withdraw. Indeed, as has
already been noted, the 29th General Assembly has properly made ministerial
declarations about the details of the plan for WP to withdraw from the PCA
but has not asserted that WP has no right to withdraw.
23. The second new specification by the Complainant concerned an alleged
violation of the freedom of conscience of the members and churches of WP.
He argued that the terms under which WP was planning to withdraw from the

<a id="ga30-p170"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=172 printed_page=170 -->

PCA in January 2002 necessitated or compelled the ministers and churches of
WP to make a decision as to whether or not they would continue in WP or
remain in the PCA. The Complainant argued that this violated their freedom of
conscience, for he argued that some in the Presbytery felt that the fact that they
were being put in a position where they had to decide one way or the other was
a violation of conscience and thus a violation of the WCF XX. The respondent
argued that placing someone ina position of making a decision could never be
a violation of conscience. a violation of conscience could only occur if a
member of WP was being forced to make the decision in only one way. WP’s
plan for withdrawal ensured that all ministers and churches had the freedom to
remain in the PCA or to continue with the separating WP.
24. The principle arguments of the Complainant were that WP acted in a
manner inconsistent with the doctrinal standards and the form of government
of the PCA (Complainant’s Brief specifications #1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 7).
25. The Complainant argued that the WP’s decision violated historic
Presbyterianism. As already noted above there is more than one tradition of
“historic Presbyterianism.” On the one hand, there is the hierarchical system of
state churches in the United Kingdom and Europe, and in the northern
Presbyterian Church tradition, to the extent that they adopted that foreign
hierarchical system. On the other hand, there is the historic American “grass
roots” Presbyterianism adopted in America in 1789 and adopted by the PCA.
Our historic Presbyterianism provides a constitutional right of withdrawal and
a protection of each court’s internal civil operations and property.
26. The Complainant argued that the ministerial declaration of the 29th General Assembly pointed out “errors” in the proposed plan of withdrawal. While the
SJC does not feel it is appropriate to discuss the character of the ministerial
declarations of the 29th General Assembly, it is appropriate for the SJC, in
exercising it judicial and constitutional function, to note that whatever “errors”
were cited in that ministerial declaration to WP, that same ministerial
declaration recognized the constitutional right of WP to withdraw. Further, at
no place in that ministerial declaration does the GA declare that what WP is
doing is dishonest or deceptive to the membership of WP so as to call into
question the veracity of the actual decisions taken by the Presbytery. Even
when freely and strongly expressing his views before the SJC, the
Complainant never called into question the formal honesty of the actual
decisions taken by the Presbytery. Indeed, the ministerial declaration of the
29th General Assembly made it very clear that all parties to these decisions in
WP are being fully and rightly informed.
27. The Complainant argued that the nature of the WP plan violated the
principles of historic “grass-roots” Presbyterianism because the plan for
withdrawal amounted to WP “Acting For” the local church. He cited Case

<a id="ga30-p171"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=173 printed_page=171 -->

1995-7, Edgemont Session versus Westminster Presbytery.6 While that case
related to the relationship between a local church and a presbytery, we agree to
follow the same line of reasoning in this case in deciding who and what make
up the membership of any court of the church, in this case we are concerned
with a presbytery, and in deciding with what ecclesiastical body that
presbytery will affiliate. It is that presbytery itself that has both the
ecclesiastical and a civil authority to make those determinations. WP, within
the framework of the BCO, has the authority to decide the composition of its
own membership and its ecclesiastical affiliation. Such a decision is not acting
for a local church. While such an action clearly affects a local church within its
bounds, it does not affect that local church’s internal operation, nor does it
affect the church’s ownership of property, nor does it abrogate any of the local
church’s rights under the BCO to complain or appeal. Thus the plan of
withdrawal is not an action that “Acts For” the local church. This action by the
Presbytery is an action by an ecclesiastical body and at the same time it is an
action by civil or legal entity acting for itself and only for itself. This action,
of course, involves all of the members of the presbytery, that is all the local
churches and all the teaching elders that are members of the presbytery.
This judgment was prepared by TE Robert M. Ferguson after
discussions with and with the concurrence of TE Dominic Aquila and RE
Robert C. Cannada.

RE M. C. Culbertson Concur RE J. Grant McCabe Concur
RE Howard Donahoe Concur TE Charles McGowan Concur
RE Samuel Duncan Concur TE David Peterson Concur
TE Robert Ferguson Concur TE G. Dewey Roberts Absent
RE Bruce Gunther Concur TE James Smith Absent
TE William E. Harrell Concur TE Robert Stuart Absent
TE Ben Konopa, Jr. Recused RE John B. White, Jr. Concur
TE Paul Kooistra Concur RE W. Jack Williamson Concur
Adopted: 17 concurred, 1 dissented, 1 recused, 5 absent.

6 Minutes of the 25th General Assembly - pages 69-74.

<a id="ga30-p172"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=174 printed_page=172 -->

Case 2001-35
COMPLAINT OF MOON K. HAM, et AL.
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY
The case was ruled administratively out of order because it deals with
allegations presented to Presbytery but not in the form or a complaint against
presbytery action or inaction.
Case 2001-36
COMPLAINT OF MOO S. LIM, et AL.
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY
The case was ruled moot due to the decisions in cases 2000-9 and 2001-8.
Case 2001-37
COMPLAINT OF MOO S. LIM, et AL.
VS.
KOREAN CAPITAL PRESBYTERY
The case was ruled administratively out of order because it was not
properly before Presbytery and the SJC due to not being filed with and acted
upon first by the session (BCO 33-1).
IV. Proposed SJC Manual Changes
The following changes in the SJC Manual were approved and
recommended for adoption to the General Assembly:
A. Delete the word “VACATED ” and insert the following:
Adopted as amended
16. PROCEDURE FOR HEARING A MEMORIAL {BCO 40-5)
16.1 The only Memorial which the Commission may entertain is one
which purports to demonstrate an important delinquency or grossly
unconstitutional proceeding of a lower court which could not have been
addressed through appeal or complaint {BCO 40-5).
(a.) When such a Memorial is received by the Stated Clerk, a
determination shall be made by chairman and secretary of the SJC as to
whether or not the Memorial is found administratively in order.
(b.) If the Memorial is found to be administratively in order, the
Officers of the Commission shall determine whether or not the
Commission should address the concerns memorialized.

<a id="ga30-p173"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=175 printed_page=173 -->

(c.) When the officers decide that the Commission should not address
the concerns memorialized, the matter shall be placed on the docket of the
next stated or called meeting of the Commission for final determination.
16.2 A Memorial accepted for consideration by the Commission under
Section 16.1(a) above may be addressed by a Panel established in
accordance with RAO 15.3
16.3 The court alleged to have offended shall be cited to appear by
representative or in writing, providing such documents as bear on the
alleged important delinquency or grossly unconstitutional proceedings,
showing what it has done or failed to do in the matter in question (BCO
40-5). The Stated Clerk shall collate these documents which shall be
considered the Relevant Documents.
16.4 any hearing on a memorials hall not be conducted as ina formal
judicial case but in accordance with BCO 40-5 and 40-6.
16.5 All hearings shall convene with prayer. The Panel or Commission
shall then proceed to hear the response of the representative of the court alleged
to have committed an important delinquency if such a Respondent is present.
The Respondent shall have a maximum of 30 minutes to respond to the citation.
16.6 At any time during which the Respondent is presenting an
argument a member of the Panel or Commission may ask questions of the
parties if present; the time taken for such questions shall not form a part of
the response time. The time for response may be extended one time for an
additional 10 minutes by majority vote of those qualified members present.
16.7 The party memorializing the General Assembly shall not present a
brief nor will he be given time for argument, except to answer questions
posed by the Panel or Commission.
16.8 After the response has been heard, the Panel or Commission shall
go into closed session to discuss the Memorial and consider its merits.
16.9 if heard by a Panel, the Panel shall then make a full report to the
Commission including such recommendations as
(a) to reverse or redress the proceedings of the court below in other
than judicial cases; or
(b) to censure the delinquent court; or
(c) to remit the whole matter to the delinquent court with an injunction
to take it up and dispose of it in a manner consistent with the BCO; or
(d) to stay all further proceedings and declare that all matters relating
to the Memorial, now or previously pending before the General Assembly,
are ended, concluded and terminated.
16.10 When the full Commission has completed its discussion and is
ready to vote, the vote shall be taken on each recommendation. The report
as a whole shall then be voted on by roll call without change or
amendment. All qualified members present shall participate in the vote.

<a id="ga30-p174"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=176 printed_page=174 -->

16.11 If heard by the full Commission, the Commission shall proceed to
deliberate the merits of the matter and make a determination as provided in
Section 16.9 (a), (b), (c), or (d). A vote shall be taken on the decision(s).
The report as a whole shall then be voted on by roll call. All qualified
members present shall participate in the vote.
16.12 After a decision has been reached by the Full Commission, any
member may file, within 20 days after the day the decision is reached, a
concurring or dissenting opinion, which shall be appended to the decision.
16.13 When a report with its recommendations on the Memorial has been
adopted, a copy thereof shall be mailed immediately to the parties and shall
be included in the final report of the SJC to the General Assembly.
B. Proposed amendment to the Standing Judicial Commission Manual
Section 13.8 and a new section to be inserted as 18 with the remaining
sections renumbered. Adopted
18. PROCEDURES FOR ASSUMING ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
OVER A MINISTER {BCO 34-1)
18.1 upon proper receipt of a case filed against a minister under BCO
34-1, the SJC shall in accordance with the provisions set out below:

- **a)** Appoint a panel in accordance with RAO 15.3 to investigate the matter and determine whether or not that the case is in order, i.e., that the provisions of SCO 34-1 have been followed.
- **b)** if the case is determined to be in order, the panel shall conduct an investigation of allegations against the minister under the provisions of BCO 31-2.
- **c)** The panel’s findings and recommendation shall be mailed to the full SJC, said minister and the stated clerks of the involved presbyteries that instituted this action under BCO 34-1. The matter shall be scheduled for review at the SJC’s next stated meeting or a meeting called under the provisions of SJC Manual

3.2.
d) Within 14 days of receipt of the panel’s findings and
recommendation said minister and the stated clerks of the involved
presbyteries may submit a brief citing reasons as to whether the
panel’s recommendations should be approved or rejected. Such
brief must comply with the requirement of Section 9 of this
Manual.
e) Briefs under these paragraphs shall comply with the requirements
for briefs in SJC Manual 9.4, but shall not exceed 5 pages.

<a id="ga30-p175"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=177 printed_page=175 -->

18.2 At its next stated meeting or meeting called to hear the matter
under the provision of Manual 3.2, the SJC in closed session shall discuss
the panel’s findings and recommendation. The SJC shall vote to accept or
reject the panel’s findings and recommendation.
18.3 The SJC decision shall be mailed to said minister and stated clerks
of the involved presbyteries. Within 14 days of receipt of the decision a
party may request a reconsideration of the decision by filing a
supplemental brief as set forth in Section 9.2. This supplemental brief
shall state the reasons for requesting reconsideration of the decision.
Copies of the supplemental brief and the SJC decision shall be mailed to
members of the SJC. Reconsideration of the judgment shall be granted
only by majority vote of the full Commission either by mail or by
conference call, as determined by the officers.
If reconsideration is granted, the Officers shall call a special
meeting, under Manual 3.2 (a) and (b), to discuss the case, consider its
merits, and render a final judgment. If reconsideration is not granted, the
previous judgment shall become final in accordance with BCO 15.5 (a).
18.4 If the SJC’s final judgment is that the above investigation does not
raise “a strong presumption of the guilt of the party involved,” {BCO 31 -2)
the SJC shall dismiss the case and advise the parties to the case.
18.5 If, however, the SJC’s final judgment is that the above
investigation raises “a strong presumption of the guilt of the party
involved,” {BCO 31-2) it shall institute process under BCO 31. In such
event, the SJC shall conduct a trial in accordance with the provisions of
BCO 32, BCO 35 and SJC Manual 13.5 through 13.10.

13.8(c)(5) The Commission shall go into closed session and the roll shall
be called and the members of the Commission may express their opinion
in the case. After discussion
(A) the vote shall be taken; and
(B) the preliminary verdict shall be announced determined, and
judgment entered on the record.
(6) The Commission shall rise from closed session and announce the
preliminary verdict. Since there is no higher court of appeal to which
written briefs may be submitted, each party (or the minister in a trial
arising from BCO 34-1) may submit a written supplemental brief in
accordance with Section 9.2. If the parties state to the court that they
all waive their right to submit written briefs the judgment shall be
entered on the record.
[Note: Change notations in 13.8(c)(5) and (6) were submitted by the SJC.]

<a id="ga30-p176"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=178 printed_page=176 -->

[Communication 8 was not explicitly dealt with by the SJC, however, it is
answered by the Assembly’s action on the recommended SJC Manual
changes.]
COMMUNICATION 8 from Mississippi Valley Presbytery (to SJC)
“Reject SJC Manual Changes”
Dear Dr. Taylor,
Greetings.
Mississippi Valley Presbytery at our May 7th meeting discussed the
recommended changes to the Standing Judicial Commission Manual as
requested by the General Assembly. The presbytery voted to record our
opposition to changes to the Standing Judicial Commission Manual and that
our opposition to amending the Manuel [sic] be communicated to you as the
Stated Clerk of the GA.
Blessings upon you and your work.
Sincerely,
/s/ Roger Collins [Stated Clerk of Mississippi Valley Presbytery]
V. Assignment from the 29th General Assembly
The SJC officers appointed a committee to review the assignment from the
29th General Assembly. That committee brought forward a preliminary report
to the full SJC at its March meeting. That report noted the complexity of the
issues involved including apparent conflicts among the Book of Church Order,
the Rules of Assembly Operation and the Manual of the Standing Judicial Commission. After adopting a preliminary recommendation, the SJC was
unable to complete its work on this important matter. The SJC, therefore, asks
the indulgence of the General Assembly in granting it an additional year to
further study the matter and complete its work to be brought to the 31st
General Assembly. Adopted
VI. The Officers of the SJC Chosen for Next Year
Chairman Dr. Robert M. Ferguson
Vice Chairman Dr. W. Jack Williamson
Secretary Rev. Lawrence N. Lunceford
Assistant Secretary Mr. Samuel J. Duncan
Respectfully submitted,
/s/ John B. White, Jr., Chairman Is/ Lawrence N. Lunceford, Secretary
