TE Zaka led the Assembly in prayer [see 30-21, p. 91], and Moderator
Ryan reassumed the Moderator’s chair.
30-31 Committee of Commissioners on Mission to North America
TE Kennedy Smartt, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and
presented the report. [See Appendix H, p. 560.] He introduced TE James
Bland, Coordinator, who addressed the Assembly. Recommendations 1-10
and 12-18 were adopted. Recommendation 11 was deferred to the report of
Committee of Commissioners on Administration. RE David Newman led in
prayer thanking God for church planting blessings and seeking further blessing
on this ministry. Mr. Richard Roethers, Director of Development for Bethany
Christian Services, addressed the Assembly. TE Smartt closed the report with
prayer.

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

A. Report of Mission to North America
B. Overtures 8, 22, 13, 16, 23, Communication 9
C. Minutes for the following meetings: June 5th, 2001, October 4-
5th, and 2001 March 7-8th, 2002,

#### II. Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

Formation of Blue Ridge Presbytery, Recommendation 12 regarding
the establishment of a Korean-American Bilingual Presbytery,
Inclusion of Montana in Rocky Mountain Presbytery and the
Redrawing of New York State Presbytery

#### III. Recommendations

1. That having reviewed the work of the GA/MNA Coordinator
during the 2001 according to the General Assembly
Guidelines, the GA/MNA Committee commends TE James C.
Bland, III for his excellent leadership, with thanksto the Lord
for the good results in GA/MNAministry during 2001, and
recommends his re-election as GA/MNA Coordinator for
another year. Adopted

staff of Mission to North America and for their faithful and
effective work.

That the General Assembly thank God for the increasing
church planting that is occurring under local church and
presbytery initiatives; thank God for the labor in the Gospel of
all PCA church planters, multicultural church planters,
missionaries, and chaplains. Further, that the General Assembly encourage faithful prayer that the Lord will send
additional laborers through GA/MNA in the fields that are ripe
for harvest in North America. Adopted
That PCA Churches be reminded of their covenant obligation
to give high priority to giving GA/MNA and all other PCA
Committees and Agencies the Partnership Share/Askings, so
that the Gospel is advanced in greater measure. Adopted
5. That the General Assembly express its gratitude to the Lord
for a Thanksgiving Offering of more than $62,000, and
commend to the churches of the PCA the opportunity to
support Urban and Mercy Ministries, preferably through an

<a id="ga30-p179"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=181 printed_page=179 -->

offering taken during the Thanksgiving season; and further, that churches consider how they may directly participate in greater measure in Urban and Mercy Ministries. *Adopted*

6. That the General Assembly express thanks to God for the long and effective ministry of Bethany Christians Services in the area of pregnancy counseling and adoption, and encourage continued support by churches and presbyteries. *Adopted*

7. That the General Assembly commend to the churches these resources and opportunities for ministry available through GA/MNA:

- The opportunity to seek the Lord’s leading in church planting or the chaplaincy.
- The opportunity to participate in prayer and financial support (designated support in addition to Partnership Share/Asking Giving) for:
- Church Planting among the many different regions, cultures and ethnic groups in North America;
- Church Planting regional coordinators and movement leaders
- Church Planting apprenticeships (through the Timothy Fund);
- Church Vitality;
- Chaplain Ministries (especially through the Chaplain Guardian Corps).
- The opportunity to build relationships of fellowship and ministry with Korean churches and other ethnic groups within the PCA, as well as with other true believers in our local communities.
- Utilize the resources of Church Vitality materials and consultation services. *Adopted*

8. That Overture 22 from New River Presbytery is amended by removing Fauquier County from section B as part of the Blue Ridge Presbytery and putting it in section D as part of the continuing Potomac Presbytery. *Adopted*

##### OVERTURE 22 from New River Presbytery (to MNA)

“Form Blue Ridge Presbytery”

Whereas the churches of New River Presbytery located within the Commonwealth of Virginia have for some time prayed about, considered, and most recently held planning meetings (including several churches in

<a id="ga30-p180"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=182 printed_page=180 -->

James River and Potomac Presbyteries) concerning
the establishment of a new Presbytery in western
Virginia, and

Whereas the demographic, sociological and economic
connections of churches throughout western Virginia
- principally along and to the west of the Blue Ridge
Mountains - make working together as a Presbytery
highly desirable, and

Whereas the churches currently located in western Virginia
have discovered that they are of one mind in their
desire to do church planting in this region, as well as
develop an enlarged work of Reformed University
Ministries among the many colleges and universities
in this region (in addition to the other normal
functions of a Presbytery), and,

Whereas, while the remaining churches located in West
Virginia fall just short of the minimum membership
and attendance goals, they have a special cohesiveness
that their leadership believes will enable them to
overcome their somewhat small size,

Therefore, New River Presbytery, meeting at Nitro, West
Virginia in its stated meeting of March 9th, 2002,
hereby overtures the 30th General Assembly that,
effective September 1, 2002 a new Presbytery of the
Blue Ridge be formed by the division of New River
Presbytery (along with churches from James River
Presbytery and Potomac Presbytery - see separate
overtures) as follows:
a. New Boundaries of New River Presbytery: “All of
West Virginia except for the counties of Morgan,
Berkeley and Jefferson.”
b. Boundaries of the newly formed Blue Ridge
Presbytery: “All churches and missions in Virginia,
bounded on the north and east and including the
counties (and contained independent cities) of
Frederick, Clarke, Fauquier, Culpeper, Orange,
Louisa, Fluvanna, Albemarle, Nelson, Appomattox,

<a id="ga30-p181"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=183 printed_page=181 -->

Campbell, and Pittsylvania and bounded on the west
and including the counties of Giles, Pulaski, and
Carroll (east of 1-77).
c. Revised boundaries of James River Presbytery will be
as follows: “All of Virginia south and east of and
including the following counties, except for the
counties of Accomac and Northampton on the Eastern
Shore: Northumberland, Westmoreland, King
George, Stafford, Spotsylvania, Hanover, Goochland,
Cumberland, Buckingham, Prince Edward, Charlotte,
and Halifax.”
d. Revised Virginia boundaries of Potomac Presbytery
will be as follows: “The Virginia counties of
Arlington, Fairfax, Prince William, and Loudoun,
including the cities within their borders.”
9. That Overture 8 from James River Presbytery be answered by
referring to Recommendation 8. Adopted

##### OVERTURE 8 from James River Presbytery (to MNA)

“Revise Boundaries of James River Presbytery”

Whereas, having been appraised by formal overture of the
desire of several of the churches in our Presbytery
located in the western area of Virginia desiring to
unite with the newly forming Presbytery of the Blue
Ridge, and

Whereas, having reviewed the proposed overture from New
River Presbytery asking the General Assembly to
establish this new Presbytery, and

Whereas, having reviewed the proposed new boundaries of
James River Presbytery contained in this proposed
overture from New River Presbytery,

Therefore be it resolved that James River Presbytery, voting
at Hopewell, Virginia in the One Hundred and Fifth
Stated Meeting of January 19, 2002 does hereby
approve of the following change to our boundaries
effective September 1, 2002:

<a id="ga30-p182"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=184 printed_page=182 -->

Revised boundaries of James River Presbytery will be
as follows: “All of Virginia south and east of and
including the following counties, except for the
counties of Accomac and Northampton on the Eastern
Shore: Northumberland, Westmoreland, King
George, Stafford, Spotsylvania, Hanover, Goochland,
Cumberland, Buckingham, Prince Edward, Charlotte,
and Halifax.”

Douglas E. Lee to another term on the Presbyterian and
Reformed Joint Commission on Chaplains and Military
Personnel (PRJC) as members of the Class of 2006. Adopted

Administration.

Recommendation 12 of the Permanent Committee’s report
until the MNA Committee has consulted with the Korean
Language Presbyteries regarding the integrating of 1.5 and 2nd

13. That the General Assembly give thanks to the Lord for the
generous response of the churches and members of the PCA to
the special needs resulting from the September 11, 2001,
terrorist attacks in New York City and Washington, D.C. And
that the General Assembly give thanks for God’s grace
through the network of PCA churches in the metropolitan
cities who were able and willing to minister to their
communities in significant ways. Further, that churches be
encouraged:
• To develop plans for ministry in case disasters should
occur in their communities, including ministry not only to
PCA families, but to other members of the Body of Christ,
as well as ministries proclaiming Christ’s mercy in Word
and deed to those outside the Body of Christ.
• To respond with generous giving when appeals for
disaster response are issued through MNA for disasters
occurring in the United States and Canada. Churches and
individuals are encouraged to give through MNA, in order
to reduce accounting and recipient costs for recipient
churches and presbyteries, noting that MNA charges no
administrative fees for the channeling of these gifts .Adopted

<a id="ga30-p183"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=185 printed_page=183 -->

14. That Overture 13 from Session of Faith Reformed
Presbyterian Church be answered in the negative. Adopted
Rationale:
• This overture originated from the Session of Faith
Reformed Church in Fairmont, W. Virginia. It was
rejected by New River Presbytery. Per RAO 10-10, the
Session forwarded the overture to General Assembly.
• Two other PCA churches are located in the counties which
this Session asks to have moved to Pittsburgh Presbytery.
These two churches are opposed to the change. Thus, of
the three churches directly affected by the change, two are
opposed.
• Pittsburgh Presbytery has not taken any action on this
change.

##### OVERTURE 13 from Session of Faith Reformed Presbyterian

Church (to MNA)
“Revise Boundaries of Proposed Blue Ridge Presbytery”
Overture To New River Presbytery and the 30th General Assembly of the PCA from Faith Reformed Presbyterian
Church, Fairmont, WV

Whereas the New River Presbytery is in the process of a
major change in that the churches located in Virginia
have proposed the formation of a separate Presbytery
as the Presbytery of Blue Ridge - and

Whereas Allegheny and Garrett counties in Maryland are
within the bounds of the Pittsburgh Presbytery - and

Whereas the present churches in the northern part of West
Virginia are much closer to the churches in the
Pittsburgh Presbytery [than] they are to all the other
churches in the New River Presbytery - and

Whereas the closer proximity to these churches in the
Pittsburgh Presbytery would give greater opportunity
for fellowship and shared activities such as missions
conferences, church planting efforts and participation
in Presbytery meetings and Presbytery Committee
responsibilities - and

<a id="ga30-p184"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=186 printed_page=184 -->

Whereas the travel time to attend Pittsburgh Presbytery
meetings would be at least an hour less than required
to attend meetings of the New River Presbytery in the
Charleston area

Therefore Be It Resolved that the northern most counties of
West Virginia, namely, Preston, Marion, Monongalia,
Wetzel (and those in the northern pan handle),
Marshall, Ohio, Brook and Hancock be included
within the boundary of the Pittsburgh Presbytery -
and

Be It Further Resolved that the present churches within these
counties: Christ Community, Faith Reformed and
Trinity be transferred to the Pittsburgh Presbytery and
that any future churches in these counties that may be
developed shall be under the jurisdiction of the
Pittsburgh Presbytery - and

Therefore by action of the Session of Faith Reformed
Presbyterian Church, Fairmont WV, meeting at their
Stated Meeting, December 19, 2001 hereby overtures
the New River Presbytery, and the 30th General Assembly of the PCA that effective September 1,
2002 that the above listed counties and churches be
made part of the Pittsburgh Presbytery of the PCA.
15. That Overture 16 from the Rocky Mountain Presbytery to
redefine the Geographical Boundaries of Rocky Mountain
Presbytery to include the State of Montana be answered in the
affirmative. Adopted

##### OVERTURE 16 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery (to MNA)

“Redefine the Geographical Boundaries of Rocky Mountain
Presbytery to Include the State of Montana”

Whereas, Rocky Mountain Presbytery is already overseeing
churches and missions in the great state of Montana;
and

Whereas, we are establishing additional churches in the state
of Montana; and

<a id="ga30-p185"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=187 printed_page=185 -->

Whereas, the state of Montana is not in any other PCA
Presbytery;

Therefore, Rocky Mountain Presbytery respectfully overtures
the General Assembly to add the state of Montana to the
geographical bounds of Rocky Mountain Presbytery.
16. That Overture 23 from the New York State Presbytery to redraw
New York State Presbytery Boundary be answered in the
affirmative. Adopted
[The substance of Communication 5 (text below) was dealt with,
though not explicitly, by Assembly action on Overture 23.]

##### OVERTURE 23 from New York State Presbytery (to MNA)

“Redraw New York State Presbytery Boundary”

Whereas, the current western boundary of the New York State
Presbytery includes central New York east and including
Monroe, Ontario, Livingston and Steuben counties; and,

Whereas, the current eastern boundary of the Ascension
Presbytery includes western New York west and
including Orleans, Genesee, Wyoming, and Allegany
counties; and,

Whereas, the Presbyterian Church of Wellsville and its teaching
elder desires to leave the Ascension Presbytery and
affiliate with New York State Presbytery; and

Whereas, the Presbyterian Church of Wellsville is located in
Allegany county which is currently within the boundary
of the Ascension Presbytery; and,

Whereas, the New York State Presbytery desires to receive the
Presbyterian Church of Wellsville and its teaching elder;
and

Whereas, the New York State Presbytery desires to initiate and
oversee the planting of healthy and reproducing PCA
churches in every major city in upstate New York
including Buffalo; and

<a id="ga30-p186"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=188 printed_page=186 -->

Therefore, Be It Resolved that the New York State
Presbytery hereby overtures the 30th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to
redraw the western boundary of New York State
Presbytery and the northeastern New York boundary
of Ascension Presbytery, to wit, that the counties
currently belonging to the Ascension Presbytery
which are in New York State be transferred to New
York State Presbytery, thus having all the churches
and counties within the geographical bounds of New
York State, excepting those churches and counties
belonging to Metropolitan New York Presbytery,
within New York State Presbytery to be effective July
1, 2002 .
COMMUNICATION 5 from Ascension Presbytery (to MNA)
“Ascension Concurs with New York State Presbytery on
Boundaries”
Dear Dr. Taylor,
This is to inform you that the Presbytery of the Ascension
hereby concurs with the request of the New York State
Presbytery that those counties in the state of New York
currently within the bounds of the Presbytery of Ascension be
ceded to the New York State Presbytery; and that the church
(the Presbyterian Church of Wellsville) and teaching elder (TE
Kenneth Thompson) currently situated within those counties
be transferred to the New York State Presbytery pending their
reception by that Presbytery.
/s/ Frederick R. Neikirk, Stated Clerk
17. That Communication 9 from Potomac Presbytery be answered
by referral to Recommendation 8. Adopted
COMMUNICATION 9 from Potomac Presbytery (to MNA)
“Potomac Presbytery Opinion on Proposed Blue Ridge Presbytery”
Dear Dr. Taylor,
Potomac Presbytery, voting at McLean Presbyterian Church,
in the Stated Meeting of June 4, 2002, agreed to communicate

<a id="ga30-p187"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=189 printed_page=187 -->

to the 30th General Assembly the following recommendations
concerning the proposed Blue Ridge Presbytery:
1) that the Committee on Mission to North America, and
the committee of commissioners appointed with
respect to the same, carefully review the boundaries of
the proposed Blue Ridge Presbytery in light of the
General Assembly Guidelines for Presbytery
Boundaries (PCA Digest, 1973-1993, p. 215) [see
below], with the understanding that in Potomac’s
judgment a presbytery including counties divided by
the Blue Ridge Mountains, with such an extensive
expanse north and south, appears inconsistent with
Guidelines 1, 5, 7, 8, and 10;
2) that it be understood that Potomac agrees to dismiss the
Virginia counties of Clarke and Frederick to the
proposed Blue Ridge Presbytery, should its creation be
approved;
3) that it be understood that Potomac does not agree to
dismiss the Virginia county of Fauquier, on the
grounds that the county in question is clearly a part of
the Washington Metropolitan Area (e.g., see Unites
[sic] States Census Bureau definitions), and therefore
under Guidelines 4, 5, 7, 8 and 10 ought properly to
be within the boundaries of Potomac Presbytery; and
4) that, should the General Assembly approve the
creation of the proposed Blue Ridge Presbytery, the
General Assembly approve as well the following as
the Virginia portion of Potomac Presbytery’s
boundaries, effective September 1, 2002:
“Virginia counties, together with the cities
contained in their border: Arlington, Fairfax,
Fauquier, Loudoun, and Prince William;”
5) that the Vice Chairman of the Boundaries Committees,
TE David Silvernail, and committee member, TE
David Coffin, be appointed by Potomac Presbytery to
represent the views of Potomac Presbytery before the
General Assembly and its relevant committees.
Sincerely,
/s/ Richard R. Larson, Clerk

<a id="ga30-p188"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=190 printed_page=188 -->

Guidelines for Presbytery Boundaries
15th General Assembly, 1987
(1) a presbytery should have a radius of iv z hours
maximum driving distance.
(2) a presbytery should have a minimum of 10 churches.
(3) a presbytery should have a total communicant
membership of at least 1000.
(4) Presbytery boundaries should not partition metropolitan
areas.
(5) A presbytery should have regional cohesiveness.
(6) A presbytery should have at least 3 churches each
having a membership of at least 125 communicant
members.
(7) Presbytery boundaries should be such that its member
churches have the potential for shared ministries.
(8) Presbytery boundaries should be such that its member
churches have a common commitment to the region
within the boundaries and sense their shared
responsibility to cover the region with the gospel.
(9) When a presbytery reaches 30 churches, it should
consider whether subdivision would lead to more
effective ministry.
(10) A presbytery should limit its boundaries to that
geographic area for which it is able to take meaningful
responsibility for evangelism and church development.
18. That in answer to Overture 19, the GA/MNA staff be asked to:
1) Explore the challenges presented to our ministry by
the increasing ethnic diversity of our nation,
2) Suggest ways our General Assembly Committee,
Presbyteries, and churches might meet these
challenges, and
3) Offer a statement of biblical values and commitments
to God, to the world and to ourselves our convictions
in these matters, and to report to the 31st General Assembly. Adopted

<a id="ga30-p189"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=191 printed_page=189 -->

##### OVERTURE 19 from Chesapeake Presbytery (to B&O & AC)

“Erect a Study Committee on Ministry Amidst Ethnic
Diversity”

Whereas Christ calls us to penetrate cultural boundaries with
the gospel, and consequently embrace the multi
cultural fellowship our Lord creates, and

Whereas our own nation has not escaped the ravages of
racism and ethnic oppression that typically afflict our
fallen world, and

Whereas Chesapeake Presbytery adopted the following
affirmations at its stated meeting on 16 February
2002 :
Affirmations of Ethnic Diversity.
1. Evangelism of people from various racial
backgrounds and social classes, with the
accompanying reconciliation of such believers
in the Body, is an essential element of the
Church’s calling to preach the Gospel (Acts
15).
2. A faithful presentation of the gospel to the
particular people groups in our region
demands cultural sensitivity (1 Cor 9:19-23).
3. The gospel that reconciles Jews and Gentiles
in the New Testament is the same gospel that
reconciles all believers regardless of race and
class. This work of reconciliation is not
optional (Gal. 3:26-29; Eph. 2:14-18; Col.
3:11).
4. We must guard against superficial unity that is
an affront to the dignity of God’s image
bearers and damages an authentic presentation
of the gospel (Acts 6:1-7).
5. Our leaders and church members need to be
equipped in order to reach the diverse people
of their changing cultural environment (Eph.
4:1-13).
6. We confess and repent of sins of disunity
(racism, prejudice, injustice, ethnic
superiority, bitterness, lack of forgiveness,

<a id="ga30-p190"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=192 printed_page=190 -->

cultural idolatries, etc.,) (Gal. 2:11; 1 Cor
11:17-22; James 2:1-7).
7. The church must have a continued infusion of
the grace of the gospel, through prayer, the
preaching of the Word and the celebration of
the sacraments in the power of the Spirit, in
order effectively implement the biblical
principles of unity (Eph 3:14-19; Acts 20:32).
8. The power of the Spirit through the ministry
of the Word equips God’s people to become a
transforming community characterized by
love for Christ and one another.
9. We celebrate our unity in Christ with all those
who embrace the gospel.

Whereas Chesapeake Presbytery also adopted the following
applications at its stated meeting on 16 February
2002:
Applications of Ethnic Diversity.
1. Communicate to all approved seminaries
Presbytery’s recommendation that if urban,
cross-cultural ministry and ethnic church
history do not find a place in the core
curriculum for M.Div. students, that the
Seminary consider adding such subjects.
2. Direct the Committee on Christian Education
to make urban and cross-cultural ministry
training a part of the internship curriculum.
3. Direct the Committee on Christian Education,
in partnership with the Committee on Mission
to North America, to prepare a guide to
readings and other resources in urban and
cross-cultural ministry to assist in equipping
local churches and lay leadership to reach the
people groups in their target regions.
4. Direct the Committee on Mission to North
America to pursue church planting strategies
that are sensitive to the ethnic and cultural
diversity of the region.
5. Direct the Committee on Reformed University
Ministries to encourage present works and

<a id="ga30-p191"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=193 printed_page=191 -->

develop new works that are sensitive to the
ethnic and cultural diversity of the region.
6. Encourage all elders of the Presbytery to
renewed diligence in setting before the people
of our churches the Scriptural calling to
pursue mercy and justice. And

Whereas Potomac Presbytery has adopted similar
affirmations and applications, and

Whereas the challenges of ministry amidst increasing ethnic
diversity has been much on the mind of one or more
other Presbyteries in the PCA,

Therefore, Chesapeake Presbytery overtures the 30th General Assembly of the PCA to erect a special study
committee to:
1) Explore the challenges presented to our ministry by the
increasing ethnic diversity of our nation,
2) Suggest ways our General Assembly Committees,
Presbyteries, and churches might meet these
challenges,
3) Offer a statement of biblical values and commitments
declaring to God, to the world, and to ourselves our
convictions in these matters.

TE John Kinser, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented
the report. [See Appendix E, p. 474.] Recommendation 1 was deferred to
the report of Committee of Commissioners on Administration.
Recommendations 2-8 were adopted. Dr. Niel Nielson, President-Elect,

Assembly in prayer.

B. Covenant College Financial Report

Appendix E, p. 476.]

the tenure of President Frank Brock, who will be stepping
down as President on June 30, 2002
B. The Board of Trustees’ search process for the newPresident
and God’s gracious provision of RE Niel Nielson as the
President-elect, who will take office on July 1, 2002
C. The Institutional Effectiveness Report for 2000-2001

#### III. Recommendations

1. That the General Assembly approve the Budget for 2002-2003
as submitted through the Administrative Committee.

Financial Report dated June 30, 2001, and prepared by
Hazlett, Lewis, and Bieter, PLLC. Adopted

meetings of the Board of Trustees for October 4-5, 2001;
December 4, 2001; and March 21-22, 2002; with notations.
Adopted
4. That the General Assembly receive as information the 2000-
2001 Institutional Effectiveness Report. We recognize God’s
gracious and abundant blessing and commend the College in
its desire to continue pursuing excellence in higher education
for the glory of God. Adopted
5. That the General Assembly thank and praise God for the
excellent work and faithfulness of the Board of Trustees,
faculty, and staff of Covenant College in serving the
Presbyterian Church in America in its mission to educate
students for the Kingdom of God. Adopted
6. That the General Assembly give God hearty thanks for RE
Frank Brock’s service to Christ and the Presbyterian Church in

<a id="ga30-p194"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=196 printed_page=194 -->

America through his 15 year tenure as President of Covenant
College, a tenure characterized by vision, integrity, and wise
management, and blessed with spiritual, physical, numerical,
and financial growth. Adopted
7. That the General Assembly give praise and thanks to God for
providing President-elect Nielson and take time to pray that God
will continue to provide grace, wisdom, and guidance to Dr.
Nielson as he begins his tenureat Covenant College. Adopted
8. That the General Assembly designate October 13, 2002 as
“Covenant College Sunday” and encourage the congregations
of the denomination to pray forthe ministryof the College
especially on that day. Adopted

30-34 Committee of Commissioners on Insurance, Annuities and Relief
RE James Wert, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented
the report. [See Appendix L, p. 679.] He introduced RE Jim Hughes, Director
of Insurance, Annuities and Relief, who reported on the various ministries of
IAR. RE Gary Campbell, Chairman of IAR Investment Committee, reported
on IAR investments. RE Tom Stein, Chairman of Insurance Committee,
reported on the insurance plans. Recommendations 1-7 and 9-14 were
adopted. Recommendation 8 was adopted in accordance with BCO 26-2.
In anticipation of the retirement of RE Jim Hughes, the Assembly paused for a
time of prayer and thanksgiving for the 21 years of faithful service [with IAR,
plus 4*/2 years with MTW] and the Assembly gave him a standing ovation. By
order of the Moderator, Recommendation 15 was referred to the Committee on Constitutional Business. The report was closed with prayer by RE Wert.

B. Recommendations of the IAR Permanent Committee

for insurance, retirement, and relief

November 2, 2001 and March1, 2002 be approved. Adopted

Crouse LLP be received. Adopted

LLP, to conduct the 2002 audit. Adopted

it is a spending plan and will be adjusted as necessary by the
Board of Trustees to accommodate changing circumstances
during the year. Adopted

participate in the annual Christmas Offering or budget regular
benevolence giving to support relief activities through the
Ministerial Relief Fund. Adopted

Amendment and Proposed Termination of the Presbyterian Church in America Money Purchase Pension Plan, the
Twenty-first Amendment and Approval of Rollover
Contributions to the Presbyterian Church in America Tax-
Sheltered Annuity Plan, and the Twenty-second Amendment
to the Presbyterian Church in America Tax-Sheltered Annuity
Plan [see Appendix L, Attachment Two, p. 688] and that the
resolutions related to these amendments be adopted and
included in the Minutes of the General Assembly. Adopted
That the General Assembly be informed that during early 2002
the Insurance, Annuities and Relief agency incorporated under
the direction of the Board of Trustees, as permitted in RAO 4-3,
and changed its name from “Insurance, Annuities and Relief’
to “PCA Retirement and Benefits, Inc.”; and that the General Assembly expressly authorize and ratify such incorporation
[see Appendix L, Attachment Three, p. 700]. Adopted
That the General Assembly amend the Book of Church Order
14-1.12.b.4, to recognize the change in name for our agency
by replacing the words “Insurance, Annuities and Relief’ with
“PCA Retirement and Benefits, Inc.”
Adopted in accordance with BCO 26-2
That the General Assembly approve the use of the name PCA
Retirement and Benefits, Inc. (PCA-RBI) immediately after
the adjournment of the 2002 General Assembly, while BCO
name change approval by the Presbyteries and 2003 General Assembly is pending. Adopted
That the General Assembly amend the Rules of Assembly Operations 4-3 and 5-1(4) by replacing the words “Insurance,
Annuities and Relief’ and “Insurance & Annuities” in 13-1.6
with the words “PCA Retirement and Benefits, Inc.” and
changing the words “Touching insurance & annuities” to
“Touching retirement, insurance and ministerial relief.”
Adopted
That the General Assembly amend the PCA Corporate Bylaws
Article VI. 1, paragraph 3, line 2 by replacing the words
“Insurance, Annuities and Relief’ with the words “PCA
Retirement and Benefits, Inc.” Adopted
That the General Assembly amend the PCA Corporate Bylaws
Article VI, 1, paragraph 2 to delete references to the “Trust
Agreement for the Annuity Fund for Ministers of the
Presbyterian Church in America,” the “Trust Agreement for
the Annuity Fund for Lay Employees of the Presbyterian Church in America,” and the “Trust Agreement for the Money

<a id="ga30-p197"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=199 printed_page=197 -->

Purchase Pension Plan of the Presbyterian Church in America;” and to replace “the Trust Agreement for the
Voluntary Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan of the Presbyterian Church in America” with “the Trust Agreement for the Tax-
Sheltered Annuity Plan of the Presbyterian Church in America.” Adopted
13. That the General Assembly ratify the Fifth Amendment to the
Trust Agreement for the Presbyterian Church in America
Voluntary Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan [see Appendix L,
Attachment Four, p. 722], and that the resolution related to
this Amendment be adopted and included in the Minutes of
the General Assembly. Adopted
14. That the General Assembly express sincere appreciation to
James L. Hughes for the 21 years of faithful service [plus AZi
years with MTW], dedication to this ministry of Christ’s
church, and for his Christian demeanor and leadership, as
Director of Insurance, Annuities, and Relief Committee; and
that the General Assembly now show this appreciation by
standing for a time of prayer of thanksgiving and intercession
for Jim and his wife, Regina. [Note: Recommendation 14
was not included with the list of original recommendations
provided to the Committee of Commissioners by the IAR
Board of Trustees.] Adopted
15. That the General Assembly mandate coverage in the PCA
health plans for all active ministers and church lay employees
who are current residents of the United States and who are not
covered under a spouse’s group health plan, and that the
Presbyteries be responsible for implementing this mandate.
[Note: the original recommendation of the IAR Board of
Trustees in its final phrase read, “and that the Presbyteries be
responsible for implementing and enforcing this mandate”
(emphasis added). The Committee of Commissioners’
recommendation removes the words “and enforcing” from this
final phrase.] [See 30-36, p. 201.] Referred to the CCB
by order of the moderator

seminary ina number of areas, including:

church, that transform the world by producing leaders
that are rooted in grace for a lifetime of ministry.

record enrollment.

themselves out practically in the lives of students and
the broader CTS community.

area of Youth ministry and worship leadership, and
how CTS is seeking to accommodate these needs.

and that the minutes of the meeting of the Executive

notations. Adopted

for the fiscal year ending June 30, 2001 by Flumes and
Barrington, CPA be received. Adopted

Seminary be approved.
Deferred to AC report [See 30-62, III, 7, p. 295.]

30-36 Report of Committee on Constitutional Business on Committee of
Commissioners on Insurance, Annuities and Relief Recommendation 15
[see 30-34 above]
TE Childs, Chairman of CCB, reported it was the opinion of the CCB
that the Constitution does not provide for such a mandate to be imposed on the
lower courts.
Recommendation 15, that the Assembly highly recommend to the
presbyteries and churches of the PCA that they participate in the PCA health
plans for all active full-time ministers, was amended by adding “full time”
after “active” (354-267), then amended by substituting for “mandate” that the
Assembly “highly recommend” to the presbyteries and the churches of the
PCA that they participate in the PCA Health Insurance Program. A motion to
include the cost of insurance coverage for all ministers and lay employees in
the Askings was ruled out of order by the Moderator as new business. His
ruling was challenged, and the chair was sustained. The amended motion
was adopted, as follows:
That the General Assembly highly recommend coverage in the PCA
health plans for all active full-time ministers and church lay
employees who are current residents of the United States and
who are not covered under a spouse’s group health plan, and
that the Presbyteries be responsible for implementing this
recommendation.
30-37 Recessed
The Assembly recessed with prayer by TE Charles Reese at 5:25 p.m.
to reconvene for business at 7:30 p.m.

Fourth Session - Wednesday Evening
June 19, 2002
30-38 Assembly Reconvened
The Assembly reconvened at 7:30 p.m. with the responsive reading of
Psalm 66, the singing of Hymn 571 “Stand Up, Stand Up for Jesus” and prayer
by RE Zack Hills.
RE Dale Carrell requested prayer for his coworker David Bell, who
was in an accident this afternoon here in Birmingham. The Assembly was
informed that TE Phil Anderson’s wife, TE Wylie Collins, and RE RozelFs
wife, Lois, all have become ill while here at the Assembly. TEs Kuiper and
Ryan led the Assembly in prayer for these individuals. The Assembly was
also led in prayer for the Committee of Commissioners on Bills and Overtures
as they continue their work, and for the Assembly when we take up their report
tomorrow.

<a id="ga30-p202"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=204 printed_page=202 -->

TE Rod Whited, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented
the report. [See Appendix I, p. 613.] The Chairman introduced TE Paul

the world. Recommendations 1-2,4 and 6-7 were adopted.
Recommendation 3 was amended by adding, “according to each church’s
practice” after the word “fasting,” and adopted. Recommendation 5 was
deferred to the report of the Committee of Commissioners on Administration.

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

A. MTW Permanent Committee Minutes
1. March 7-9, 2001
2. November 7-9, 2001
B. Report and Recommendations of the Permanent Committee

#### II. Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

#### III. Recommendations

1. That the month of October 2002, be set aside as a month of
prayer for the “10/40 Window” and world evangelization, and
that God would send many more laborers into His harvest
field. Adopted
2. That the General Assembly urge the churches to set aside a
portion of their giving for the suffering peoples of the world;
to that end be it recommended that a special offering for relief
and mercy (Compassion Ministry) be taken during 2003 to be
distributed by MTW. Adopted
3. That the General Assembly set aside Sunday, November 10,
2002, as a day of prayer and fasting, according to each
church’s practice, for the persecuted church worldwide.
Adopted as amended
4. With gratitude to God for the life, ministry, and service of
Paul D. Kooistra, the Committee on Mission to the World
enthusiastically recommends that Dr. Kooistra be re-elected as
Coordinator of Mission to the World. We strongly commend
his performance during 2001-2002 especially in the following
areas:
• His forthright demeanor and management have
instilled trust between members of the Atlanta staff,
between the Atlanta staff and field missionaries and
between MTW and the churches of the PCA.
• His exemplary spiritual leadership of MTW especially
in the development and teaching of Living in Grace.

• His encouragement and leadership of the
organizational redesign process as the implementation
of the results of this redesign resulting in even greater
efficiency and unity throughout the entire MTW
organization.

construction, and move to the new building. Adopted
5. That the proposed budget of MTW, as presented through the
Administrative Committee, be approved.
Deferred to AC report [See 30-62, III, 7, p. 295.]
6. That the minutes of the meeting of March 7-9, 2001, be
accepted. Adopted

accepted with notations. Adopted

30-40 Objection by TE Frank Smith
Without objection, in accordance with BCO 45-4,5, the Assembly
recorded the following Objection [amended by action at 30-65, p. 342]:
“In accord with BCO 45-4,1 hereby file a formal objection with regard
to the decision in Case 01-25.
First, I object that the adjudication did not include two of the
major points, viz., that Anthony Dallison was never given opportunity
to present his complaint to the court of original jurisdiction, and that
he was denied a copy of the proceedings until after complaint was
filed with the General Assembly. The failure to redress the first point
violates the principle established in Jupiter Session vs. Southern
Florida (M25GA, p. 66), and the failure to redress the second violates a
principle affirmed in Carl Fox vs. Mississippi Valley, et al. (M16GA,
p. 222).
Second, I object that the decision was not just, in that it did not
redress the fact that Mr. Dallison was never given opportunity to
demonstrate his innocence before the court itself. During the six
month period from the appointment of the investigative committee
until his deposition, that committee never met with him despite
repeated requests from him to do so. He was never allowed the
opportunity to meet with two committees to which had been sent
letters which were extremely damaging to his reputation. And, he was
not allowed to address the court at its January 2001 stated meeting
with regard to the judicial matter until after the sentence had already
been determined.
Third, without impugning the intentions of the Standing Judicial Commission, [These underlined words were added to the
published Minutes at this point by action of the Assembly. See 30-65,
p. 342.] I object that there are statements in the decision that in my
opinion either are inaccurate and/or are misleading, including the
following:

<a id="ga30-p205"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=207 printed_page=205 -->

“At a stated meeting on May 12, 2001 the Presbytery of North
Florida heard and denied the Complaint filed by Mr. Dallison on
February 2, 2001.” In point of fact, the report of the commission
appointed by the Presbytery in March 2001 was approved two months
later. But there was never any “hearing” of the complaint, in that Mr.
Dallison never was allowed to appear before the Presbytery to argue
the complaint.
“This investigation by North Florida Presbytery was not for
the purpose of finding new sins with which to charge [Mr.] Dallison.”
In point of fact, the committee was appointed upon recommendation of
a special ad hoc committee, which raised matters broader than those of
the original conviction.
“To this date, [Mr.] Dallison still has not provided any proof
of his innocence concerning the particular matter [regarding financial
dealings] which his wife questioned even though he continues to assert
his complete integrity.” In point of fact, the Record of the Case (p.
127) demonstrates that he provided the relevant financial records at a
meeting between him and his wife at which two elders were present.
“The complainant’s representative also argued before this
panel that North Florida Presbytery had the power to lift the censure of
suspension from the ministry against him, but not the power to
increase the censure.” In point of fact, the consistent position of the
complainant and his representative has been that the court did have the
power to increase the censure, but only after proper judicial process
had been followed.
[A paragraph was omitted from the published minutes at this
point by action of the Assembly. See 30-65, p. 342.]
Fourth, I object to the decision because of the manner in
which the case was adjudicated. In August 2001, the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) officers ruled that the complaint was more properly
an appeal, and designated the case as such. Accordingly, Mr.
Dallison’s brief argued the case as an appeal. When the parties
appeared before the panel two months later for the hearing, the panel
informed the parties that the case had been changed back to a
complaint. When the complainant’s representative protested that this
was totally unfair and improper, one of the panel members stated that
they were all busy men and, after all, the material that had been
presented was not exactly “bedtime reading.” In making this
objection, I would particularly note that the hearing before the panel
was held sooner than would normally obtain, because of the panel’s
request that the process be speeded up.
Fifth, I object to the statement that the complainant’s position
“would effectively nullify the binding and loosing authority which

<a id="ga30-p206"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=208 printed_page=206 -->

Christ gave to His church” and “attempts to divide presbytery’s
binding power from her loosing power.” In point of fact, the
complainant consistently argued that further censure required process
(which would include opportunity to rebut testimony and to present
exculpatory evidence), while exoneration need not require that type of
judicial process. The principle regarding censure was maintained by
the Constitutional Business Committee against Louisiana Presbytery’s
request for action against Tennessee Valley Presbytery for tolerating
women preaching in one of its churches (M28GA, p. 78). The
principle of exoneration was affirmed in Eric Dye, et al. vs. Missouri
Presbytery (M14GA, p. 219), and by the SJC itself as recently as the
John Wood matter (“it is clear that TVP [Tennessee Valley
Presbytery] did act on the allegations when . . . TVP adopted two
resolutions on this matter”; “But this ‘crossing of the line’ does not
require the institution of process against TE Wood” [M28GA, p. 239;
M29GA, p. 72]).
30-41 Committee of Commissioners on Christian Education and Publications
TE Tom Osterhaus, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and
presented the report. [See Appendix D, p. 467.] The Assembly viewed the
2002 WIC Love Gift video. TE Charles Dunahoo was introduced, who then
gave the CE&P report. Recommendations 1-10 and 12 were adopted.
Recommendation 11 was deferred to the report of the Committee of
Commissioners on Administration. The report was closed with prayer by TE
Tom Osterhaus.

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

A. Minutes of CE&P Permanent Committee
B. Recommendations of the CE/P Permanent Committee
C. 2001 Budget and Audit Report

#### II. Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

A. Review of the Mission and Vision of CE/P, with special
emphasis on upcoming training events and available resources
B. General Recommendations of the CE/P Committee

#### III. Recommendations

1. That the above report be received as an accurate summary of
the ministry of CE&P for the 2001 -2002 year. Adopted

<a id="ga30-p207"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=209 printed_page=207 -->

2. That the CE&P Minutes of September 20-21, 2001 and
February 21-22, 2002 be approved with notations. Adopted
3. That the PCA Assembly on behalf of CE&P and the Women
In the Church express our thanks for the gifts directed to
Ridge Haven Conference center as the 2001 recipient of the
annual WIC Love Gift. The amount contributed was $72,000.
Adopted
4. That local churches be encouraged to participate in the March
13-15, 2003 Mercy Ministry Conference in Atlanta sponsored
by CE&P and Mission to North America. Adopted
5. That churches and individuals be encouraged to participate in
the week of prayer coordinated by CE&P May 4-10, 2003.
Adopted
6. That, the Committee having completed its annual review and
evaluation of CE&P Coordinator TE Charles Dunahoo, he be
elected to serve in that position for the coming year, with
thanks for his excellent leadership, commitment to Christian
education, discipleship, truth and passion for reaching this
generation with the gospel. Adopted
7. That special prayer be offered at the end of this report for
CE&P’s curriculum publishing arm, Great Commission
Publications, praying specifically for the upcoming major
curriculum revision from a three-year to a two-year cycle.
Adopted
8. That the General Assembly express its thanks to the members
of the Women’s Advisory Sub-Committee for their assistance
to CE&P in overseeing the Women in the Church ministry.
Adopted
9. That in light of the desire of the Administrative Committee to
publish a denominational print magazine, the General Assembly with the concurrence of the AC, transfer that
responsibility from CE&P to the AC, realizing that this action
does not include other denominational publications produced
by Christian Education and Publications. Adopted
10. That the Assembly join with CE&P in expressing thanks to
RE Jeff Oxman, RE Jeff Hall, and TE Rod Mays for their
service as members of the CE&P Committee. Adopted
11. That CE&P’s 2003 budget be approved as presented by the
Administration Committee.
Deferred to AC report [See 30-62, III, 7, p. 295.]
12. That the audit for the year 2001 be found in order. Adopted

30-46 Moderator’s Exhortation and Prayer

30-47 Partial Report of Committee of Commissioners on Bills and Overtures
TE Ligon Duncan, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and
presented the report. [See text and action at 30-50, p. 212. The Bills and
Overtures Report is continued at 30-53, p. 243.] The Moderator ruled against
the following point of order [see also protest at 30-61, p. 290 and response to
protest in Appendix O, p. 739]:

<a id="ga30-p211"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=213 printed_page=211 -->

That the Bills and Overtures Committee Report be found out of order,
in that the Committee throughout its proceedings violated
RAO 13-6.e.; Manual of Operations for Committee of
Commissioners (C.3.c. on page 3) which reads:
“All overtures, resolutions and communications must
be dealt with and reported back to the Assembly with a
recommended answer. An overture may be answered by
being adopted or not adopted. The Committee
recommends to the Assembly whether to adopt or not. It
may be referred to another Committee for study or action.
It may be answered with a statement. A simple answer in
the affirmative implies the approval of the overture as a
whole. The Committee may propose amendments to the
overture, when it deems such would improve the
statement, if adopted by the Assembly.
Where several overtures, resolutions or
communications deal with the same question or substance,
the group should be answered with a single answer, if
possible. If such overtures are reported to the Assembly
separately, it may prove confusing to the Assembly to be
dealing with the same question more than once.”;
as well as our long-standing interpretation and practice under
these rules, by forbidding amendments to Overtures proposing
BCO Amendments; (and that in the context of proposed BCO
changes of tremendous importance and potential controversy);
and that the Committee be instructed to reconvene, and adopt
its report lawfully under the rules as so specified above.
His ruling was challenged, and after discussion the Moderator’s ruling
was sustained. The following commissioners requested that their negative
vote be recorded on the vote to sustain the chair: TE Roland Barnes, RE Tom
Bingham, TE Philip Blevins, TE Carl Bogue, TE Jim Bowen, TE G. Brent
Bradley, TE David Brown, RE E. Todd Buchner, TE Robert Burridge, RE
George Caler, TE Mike Chastain, TE David Coffin, TE Michael Coleman, RE
Reed DePace, TE Trevor Downie, TE J. Dusenbury, RE Paul English, RE
Peter Gagliardi, RE Fredrick Greco, RE Vaughn Hamilton, RE Paul
Hammond, TE Irfon Hughes, TE Jeff Hutchinson, TE Travis Hutchinson, TE
Chris Hutchinson, TE Daniel Jarstfer, RE Marshall Joiner, RE Daniel Joner,
RE Vince Lanier, TE Jerry Maguire, RE Dick McBane, TE H. Timothy
Miessler, RE Steven Morley, RE James Nabours, RE Michael Ramsey, RE
Fredrick Neikirk, TE Randolph Patterson, RE M. Dale Peacock, TE Robert
Peterson, TE Joseph Pipa, TE Gary Roop, TE Benjamin Shaw, TE David
Sherwood, RE Patrick Shields, TE Bill Smith, TE Morton Smith, TE Byron

<a id="ga30-p212"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=214 printed_page=212 -->

Snapp, TE Decherd Stevens, TE Ted Trefsgar, TE Thomas Troxell, TE
Andrew Webb, TE C. N. Wilbom, TE Norm Wise, TE Scott Wright, TE James
MacGregor, TE Frank J. Smith.
Recommendations 1, 4 and 5 were adopted. TE Thomas T. Ellis
requested that his negative vote on Recommendation 1 be recorded.
Recommendation 2 was moved, and a minority report was presented as a
substitute. A point of order was taken that the minority report constitutes new
business. Before the moderator ruled, another point of order was taken asking
the Moderator to yield the chair during discussion of this matter. The
Moderator put this question to the Assembly, and on vote the Assembly
expressed its desire for the Moderator to remain in the chair. The Moderator
then ruled against the previous point of order and declared the minority report
to be in order. After debate, the minority report was defeated. After
extended debate, the question was called on all matters before the Assembly.
Before this vote was taken, a point of order was made that the
Moderator had made allowances for certain speakers to address the Assembly
and these ought to be honored. The Moderator ruled against the point of order
on the grounds that the Assembly’s action takes precedence over any
concessions made by the Moderator. The Assembly defeated the motion to
end debate on all matters before the Assembly.

#### III. Recommendations

1. That Overture 5 from Ohio Valley Presbytery, et al.,
(“Require Ten Percent of Presbyteries to Assume Original
Jurisdiction Over a Minister in BCO 34-1 - Version 1”) be
answered in the affirmative, and that Overture 18 from
Potomac Presbytery (“Amend Both BCO 33-1 and 34-1 to
Require Greater Consensus to Assume Original Jurisdiction
Over Members and Ministers) and Overture 30 from
Philadelphia Presbytery, et al., (“Require Ten Percent of
Presbyteries to Assume Original Jurisdiction Over a Minister
in BCO 34-1 - Version 2”) be answered by reference to the
Assembly’s answer to Overture 5. [See also 30-29, III, p.
100 ff.] Adopted

<a id="ga30-p214"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=216 printed_page=214 -->

##### OVERTURE 5 from Ohio Valley Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)

and Metropolitan New York Presbytery, Uliana Presbytery,
Missouri Presbytery, Piedmont Triad Presbytery, North
Georgia Presbytery, Evangel Presbytery, Fellowship
Presbytery, Tennessee Valley Presbytery, Rocky Mountain
Presbytery, North Texas Presbytery, Nashville Presbytery,
Susquehanna Valley Presbytery, Northern California
Presbytery, North Florida Presbytery, Heartland Presbytery,
Central Florida Presbytery, Northern Illinois Presbytery, South
Texas Presbytery and Southeast Alabama Presbytery
“Require Ten Percent of Presbyteries to Assume Original
Jurisdiction Over a Minister in BCO 34-1 - Version 1”

Whereas, the grassroots history of the Presbyterian Church in America indicates her desire to act in unity with the
consensus of her churches; and

Whereas, the present wording of Book of Church Order 34-1
allows only two presbyteries to require the General Assembly to decide whether to assume original
jurisdiction in judicial process against a minister in
another Presbytery; and

Whereas, this standard can allow presbyteries of a particular
perspective, and potentially quite few churches, to
challenge the ordinary rights of a Presbytery to
determine its own membership according to the men
and circumstances best known by the local Presbytery;
and

Whereas, this standard, in effect, can allow two presbyteries
and a few churches, to bring charges against one or
many presbyters across Presbytery boundaries and
potentially to flood the General Assembly with
charges that express the concerns of very few; and

Whereas, such narrow concerns should not be allowed to
challenge the ordinary rights of any Presbytery to
determine its own membership unless there is a wide
consensus in the church that a Presbytery has failed to
act properly;

<a id="ga30-p215"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=217 printed_page=215 -->

Therefore, to ensure that a small minority of presbyteries or
churches does not exercise inordinate influence over
other churches, presbyteries and the General Assembly in pursuing the discipline of ministers; and
to ensure that there is a significant indication of a likely
consensus in the denomination that the GA must
assume original jurisdiction in such a case for the sake
of the purity and testimony of the church, Ohio Valley
Presbytery / Metropolitan New York Presbytery /
Illiana Presbytery / Missouri Presbytery / Piedmont
Triad Presbytery / North Georgia Presbytery / Evangel
Presbytery / Fellowship Presbytery / Tennessee Valley
Presbytery / Rocky Mountain Presbytery / North
Texas Presbytery / Nashville Presbytery /
Susquehanna Valley Presbytery / Northern California
Presbytery / North Florida Presbytery / Heartland
Presbytery / Central Florida Presbytery / Northern
Illinois Presbytery / South Texas Presbytery /
Southeast Alabama Presbytery overtures the General Assembly to amend the Book of Church Order by
changing the words “two other” in BCO 34-1 to “at
least ten percent of all the Presbyteries.”

##### OVERTURE 18 from Potomac Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)

“Amend Both BCO 33-1 and 34-1 to Require Greater
Consensus to Assume Original Jurisdiction Over Members
and Ministers”

Whereas the history of the Presbyterian Church in America,
with respect to the former denominational connection
of many of its officers and congregations, indicates
the great importance of the voice of a minority in
attempts to preserve the faithfulness of a
denomination;
whereas the present wording of the Book of Church Order
wisely makes provision for the correction of one court
through the efforts of others of like grade, through an
appeal to a more inclusive court;

<a id="ga30-p216"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=218 printed_page=216 -->

Whereas the grassroots history of the Presbyterian Church in America indicates her desire to act in unity with the
consensus of her churches;
whereas the present wording of Book of Church Order of 33-
1 allows two Sessions to require a Presbytery to
assume original jurisdiction injudicial process against
a member in another congregation, and BCO 34-1
allows two presbyteries to require the General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction in judicial
process against a minister in another Presbytery;

Whereas, as the Presbyterian Church in America grows, this
standard, expressed in a fixed number, will allow an
increasingly smaller representation of the
denomination to challenge the ordinary rights of a
Session or Presbytery to fulfill its Constitutional
duties;

Whereas the ordinary rights of a court to fulfill its
constitutional responsibilities should not be liable to
challenge unless there is a significant proportion in the
church that is persuaded that a court has failed to act
properly; and

Whereas the parallel provisions (BCO 33-1 and BCO 34-1)
have slightly different language, which may lead to
confusion, and any change introduced in the standard
of one section should reasonably be introduced in the
other on similar grounds;

Therefore, to ensure that a disproportionate representation of
churches or presbyteries does not exercise undue
influence over other churches, presbyteries and the
General Assembly in pursuing discipline; and, to
ensure that there is a significant indication of the need
for a higher court to assume original jurisdiction in
such a case for the sake of the purity and testimony of
the church, Potomac Presbytery overtures the 30th
General Assembly to amend The Book of Church Order as follows:

<a id="ga30-p217"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=219 printed_page=217 -->

1. In BCO 33-1, strike the words “two other” and add
in that place “at least five percent, but not less than
two, of all of the” so that the section reads:
and at least five percent, but not less than two,
of all of the Sessions of churches in the same
Presbytery request the Presbytery of which
the church is a member to initiate proper or
appropriate action in a case of process and
thus assume jurisdiction and authority, the
Presbytery shall do so.
and
2. In BCO 34-1, strike the words, “and two other
Presbyteries request the General Assembly to assume
original jurisdiction (to first receive and initially hear
and determine), the General Assembly shall do so.”
and add in that place:
and at least five percent of all of the
Presbyteries request the General Assembly to
initiate proper or appropriate action in a case
of process and thus assume jurisdiction and
authority, the General Assembly shall do so.

##### OVERTURE 30 from Philadelphia Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)

and Iowa Presbytery
“Require Ten Percent of Presbyteries to Assume Original
Jurisdiction Over a Minister in BCO 34-1 - Version 2”

Whereas, the grassroots history of the Presbyterian Church in America indicates her desire to act in unity with the
consensus of her churches; and

Whereas, the present wording of Book of Church Order 34-1
allows only two presbyteries to require the General Assembly to decide whether to assume original
jurisdiction in judicial process against a minister in
another Presbytery; and

Whereas, this standard can allow presbyteries of a particular
perspective, and potentially quite few churches, to

<a id="ga30-p218"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=220 printed_page=218 -->

challenge the ordinary rights of a Presbytery to
determine its own membership according to the men
and circumstances best known by the local Presbytery;
and

Whereas, this standard, in effect, can allow two presbyteries
and a few churches, to bring charges against one or
many presbyters across Presbytery boundaries and
flood the General Assembly with charges that express
the concerns of very few; and

Whereas, such concerns should not be allowed to challenge
the ordinary rights of any Presbytery to determine its
own membership unless there is a wide consensus in
the church that a Presbytery has failed to act properly;

Therefore, to ensure that a small minority of presbyteries or
churches does not exercise inordinate influence over
other churches, presbyteries and the General Assembly in pursuing the discipline of ministers; and
to ensure that there is a significant indication of a
likely consensus in the denomination that the GA
must assume original jurisdiction in such a case for
the sake of purity and testimony of the church,

Therefore be it resolved Philadelphia Presbytery / Iowa
Presbytery overtures the General Assembly to amend
the Book of Church Order by changing the words
“two other” in BCO 34-1 to “at least ten percent of all
the presbyteries.”
2. That Overture 10 from Ohio Valley Presbytery (“Specify
Good Faith Subscription - Version 2”) be answered in the
affirmative as amended; and that Overture 3 from Illiana
Presbytery, et al. (“Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version
1”), Overture 11 from North Georgia Presbytery, et al.
(“Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version 3”), Overture
12 from Metropolitan New York and Southern Florida
Presbyteries (“Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version 4”),

##### Overture 24 from North Texas Presbytery (“Specify Good

Faith Subscription - Version 5”), Overture 29 from
Westminster Presbytery (“Amend BCO 24-1 to Record All

<a id="ga30-p219"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=221 printed_page=219 -->

Exceptions with Presbytery and Have Stated Clerk Report All
Exceptions to the General Assembly”), Overture 31 from
Iowa Presbytery (“Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version
6”), and Overture 32 from Philadelphia Presbytery (“Specify
Good Faith Subscription - Version 7”) be answered by
reference to the Assembly’s action on Overture 10. [See
also 30-29, III, p. 99 ff.]
Adopted

##### OVERTURE 10 from Ohio Valley Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)

“Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version 2”

Whereas, the purity and unity of our church require her to
establish that her officers sincerely receive and adopt
the Westminster Standards as containing the system of
doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures, and for those
officers to declare if at any time they find themselves
out of accord with any of the fundamentals of this
system (BCO 21-5; WCF XXXI.2); and

Whereas, the purity and unity of the church also require that
her leaders understand both the restrictions and
allowances of her Standards that are derived from
Scripture but, unlike it, are not the only infallible rule
of faith and practice (WCF 1.8, 10; XX.2; XXXI.3;
WLC # 3; BCO Preface II. 7); and

Whereas, there is a longstanding tradition within American
Presbyterianism, including the PCA, that officers may
in good faith take exception to certain particulars of
the Westminster Standards, if such particular
exceptions are not inimical (i.e. hostile or injurious) to
the more comprehensive system of doctrine; and

Whereas, the PCA’s original Good-faith subscription position
has served the church well, but recently been
challenged by some who desire either a more Broad-
or Strict-subscription position; and

Whereas, the church must guard against a broad, “substance-
of-doctrine” subscription approach, which has no
definitive meaning and leaves to the candidate himself
rather than to the wisdom of the Presbytery to

<a id="ga30-p220"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=222 printed_page=220 -->

determine what areas of his disagreement with
particular statements of the Constitution are inimical
to the system of doctrine and what are allowable
differences with the Westminster Standards; and

Whereas, the assertion that the PCA was consciously founded
as a Strict-subscriptionist Church in 1973 cannot be
sustained (Minutes of the Twenty-First General Assembly, cf. pp. 89 and 149 with pp. 146 and 166-
168); and

Whereas, the General Assembly of the PCA did not declare
itself to be a Strict-subscriptionist Church in the
joining and receiving process with the Reformed
Presbyterian Church Evangelical Synod in 1982; and

Whereas, attempts to change the PCA into a Broad- or Strict-
subscriptionist Church are a departure from the
original position of the PCA, and such attempts may
bring discord and possibly division within the Church;
and

Whereas, the inclusion of a Good-faith subscription statement
in our Book of Church Order is needed to state
explicitly what has been the understanding and
practice of the majority of the PCA from its
beginning;

Therefore, to clarify and support the original ordination
standards of the PCA, Ohio Valley Presbytery
overtures the General Assembly to amend the Book of Church Order by inserting the paragraphs in bold
below as new paragraphs of BCO 21-4 between the
following sentences, as indicated:
“Whenever a Presbytery shall omit any of these parts,
it shall always make a record of the reasons for such
omissions and of the trial parts omitted.
[Insert new paragraphs here]
“The Presbytery being fully satisfied of his
qualifications for the sacred office....”

<a id="ga30-p221"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=223 printed_page=221 -->

While our Constitution does not require the
candidate’s affirmation of every statement and/or
proposition of doctrine in our Confession of Faith
and Catechisms, it is the right and responsibility of
the Presbytery to determine if the candidate is out
of accord with any of the fundamentals of these
doctrinal standards and, as a consequence, may not
be able in good faith sincerely to receive and adopt
the Confession of Faith and Catechisms of this
Church as containing the system of doctrine taught
in the Holy Scriptures (cf. BCO 21-5, Q. 2; 24-5,
Q.2).
Therefore, in examining a candidate for ordination
the Presbytery shall inquire not only into the
candidate’s knowledge and views in the areas
specified above, but also shall require the
candidate to state the specific instances in which he
may differ with the Confession of Faith and
Catechisms in any of their statements and/or
propositions. The court may grant an exception to
any difference of doctrine only if in the court’s
judgment the candidate’s declared difference is not
out of accord with ne any fundamental of our
system of doctrine because the difference is neither
hostile to the system nor strikes at the vitals of
religion.

##### OVERTURE 3 from Illiana Presbytery (to CCB & B&O)

and Missouri Presbytery, Evangel Presbytery, Susquehanna
Valley Presbytery, Northern California Presbytery, Central
Florida Presbytery, Northern Illinois Presbytery, South Texas
Presbytery and Southeast Alabama Presbytery
“Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version 1”

Whereas, the purity and unity of our church require her to
establish that her officers sincerely receive and adopt
the Westminster Standards as containing the system of
doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures, and for those
officers to declare if at any time they find themselves
out of accord with any of the fundamentals of this
system (BCO 21-5; WCF XXXI.2); and

<a id="ga30-p222"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=224 printed_page=222 -->

Whereas, the purity and unity of the church also require that
her leaders understand both the restrictions and
allowances of her Standards that are derived from
Scripture but, unlike it, are not the only infallible rule
of faith and practice (WCF 1.8, 10; XX.2; XXXI.3;
WLC # 3; BCO Preface II. 7); and

Whereas, there is a longstanding tradition within American
Presbyterianism, including the PCA, that officers may
in good faith take exception to certain particulars of
the Westminster Standards, if such particular
exceptions are not inimical (i.e. hostile or injurious) to
the more comprehensive system of doctrine; and

Whereas, the PCA’s original Good-faith subscription position
has served the church well, but recently been
challenged by some who desire either a more Broad-
or Strict-subscription position; and

Whereas, the church must guard against a broad, “substance-
of-doctrine” subscription approach, which has no
definitive meaning and leaves to the candidate himself
rather than to the wisdom of the Presbytery to
determine what areas of his disagreement with
particular statements of the Constitution are inimical
to the system of doctrine and what are allowable
differences with the Westminster Standards; and

Whereas, the assertion that the PCA was consciously founded
as a Strict-subscriptionist Church in 1973 cannot be
sustained (Minutes of the Twenty-First General Assembly, cf. pp. 89 and 149 with pp. 146 and 166-
168); and

Whereas, the General Assembly of the PCA did not declare
itself to be a Strict-subscriptionist Church in the
joining and receiving process with the Reformed
Presbyterian Church Evangelical Synod in 1982; and

Whereas, attempts to change the PCA into a Broad- or Strict-
subscriptionist Church are a departure from the
original position of the PCA, and such attempts may

<a id="ga30-p223"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=225 printed_page=223 -->

bring discord and possibly division within the Church;
and

Whereas, the inclusion of a Good-faith subscription statement
in our Book of Church Order is needed to state
explicitly what has been the understanding and
practice of the majority of the PCA from its
beginning;

Therefore, to clarify and support the original ordination
standards of the PCA, Illiana Presbytery / Missouri
Presbytery / Evangel Presbytery / Susquehanna Valley
Presbytery / Northern California Presbytery / Central
Florida Presbytery / Northern Illinois Presbytery /
South Texas Presbytery / Southeast Alabama
Presbytery overtures the General Assembly to amend
the Book of Church Order by inserting the paragraphs
in bold below as new paragraphs of BCO 21-4
between the following sentences, as indicated:
“Whenever a Presbytery shall omit any of these parts,
it shall always make a record of the reasons for such
omissions and of the trial parts omitted.
[Insert new paragraphs here]
“The Presbytery being fully satisfied of his
qualifications for the sacred office....”
While our Constitution does not require the
candidate’s affirmation of every statement and/or
proposition of doctrine in our confession of faith
and Catechisms, it is the right and responsibility of
the Presbytery to determine if the candidate is out
of accord with any of the fundamentals of these
doctrinal standards and, as a consequence, may not
be able in good faith sincerely to receive and adopt
the confession of faith and catechisms of this
Church as containing the system of doctrine taught
in the Holy Scriptures (cf. BCO 21-5, Q. 2; 24-5,
Q.2).

Therefore, in examining a candidate for ordination
the Presbytery shall inquire not only into the

<a id="ga30-p224"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=226 printed_page=224 -->

candidate’s knowledge and views in the areas
specified above, but also shall require the
candidate to state the specific instances in which he
may differ with the confession of faith and
Catechisms in any of their statements and/or
propositions. The court may only grant an
exception to any difference of doctrine if in the
court’s judgment the candidate’s declared
difference is out of accord with no fundamental of
our system of doctrine because the difference is
neither hostile to the system nor strikes at the vitals
of religion.

and Tennessee Valley Presbytery, Rocky Mountain

“Specify Good Faith Subscription - Version 5”

below as new paragraphs of BCO 21-4 between the

“Whenever a Presbytery shall omit any of these parts,
it shall always make a record of the reasons for such
omissions and of the trial parts omitted.
[Insert new paragraphs here]
“The Presbytery being fully satisfied of his
qualifications for the sacred office....”
While our Constitution does not require the
candidate’s affirmation of every statement and/or
proposition of doctrine in our Confession of Faith
and Catechisms, it is the right and responsibility of
the Presbytery to determine if the candidate is out
of accord with any of the fundamentals of these
doctrinal standards and, as a consequence, may not
be able in good faith sincerely to receive and adopt
the Confession of Faith and Catechisms of this

the Presbytery shall inquire not only into the
candidate’s knowledge and views in the areas

may differ with the confession of faith and
Catechisms in any of their statements and/or
propositions. The court may only grant an

difference is out of accord with no fundamental of
