<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=294 printed_page=292 -->

#### I. Business Referred to the Committee

A. Minutes of the Administrative Committee (AC)
1. 6/19/2001
2. 10/5/2001
3. 3/15/2002
B. Minutes of the Board of Directors
1. 6/19/2001
2. 10/5/2001
3. 3/15/2002
C. 2001 Audits of the Building and Administrative Committee
General Fund
D. Overtures 7, 19, 33, and 35
E. Communication 7
F. Report and Recommendations of the Permanent Committee

#### II. Statement Of Major Issues Discussed

A. Recommendation #4 - That Overture #7 from Westminster
Presbytery be answered in the negative.
B. Recommendation #5 - That the Administrative Committee
through the local Host Committee be responsible for all
services at General Assembly.
C. Recommendation #8 - that the AC be authorized to proceed to
develop “PCANews” [a print magazine].
D. Recommendation #21 - That if the General Assembly adopts
Overture #19 from Chesapeake Presbytery, “Erect a Study
Committee on Ministry Amidst Ethnic Diversity”, to approve a
study committee, that a budget or $10,000 be approved for this
study committee, and that this be funded by designated gifts.
E. Recommendation #22 - That if the General Assembly adopts
Overture #33 from Central Carolina Presbytery, “Erect Study
Committee Regarding ‘Today’s New International Version’ of
the Bible”, to approve a study committee, that a budget of
$5,000 be approved for this study committee, and that this be
funded by designated gifts.

#### III. Recommendations

1. That the General Assembly hear “The Joint Report of the PCA
Strategic Planning Committee and the PCA Strategic Planning
Steering Committee.” [See Appendix C, p. 398.] Adopted
2. That “The Framework of Strategic Direction of the PCA” [see
Appendix C, p. 403] be commended to the Presbyteries (and
through them to their local churches) for their consideration,
discussion and suggestions on how to perfect and implement the
plan (especially indicating their measure of agreement with the

<a id="ga30-p293"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=295 printed_page=293 -->

four priorities under Section 5 “The PCA Strategic Priorities” and
any additional examples of action steps (see bulleted sub-points))
and that the Presbyteries report their response by March 31, 2003
to the Strategic Planning Steering Committee. Adopted
3. That the Strategic Planning Steering Committee and the
Strategic Planning Committee be extended for another year to
report back to the 31st General Assembly. Adopted
4. That Overture #7, “Allow Inspection of PCAF and All Other
Committee and Agency Records by Individuals or General Assembly Divest Itself of Committees and Agencies,” from
Westminster Presbytery be answered in the negative.
Answered with reference to
the Assembly’s answer to Overture 7
[See text and action at 30-24, III, 5, p. 93.]
Grounds:
• There are certain legal requirements to be met before a
member of the General Assembly may secure copies of
specified records of General Assembly within a
reasonable time frame.
• All Committees and Agencies are related to the Assembly
as committees (“RAO” 4-3). As such their books are
audited annually by outside auditing firms and such audits
are duly reported to the Permanent Committees or Boards
and subsequently reported to the respective Committees of
Commissioners of the General Assembly, and
subsequently approved by the General Assembly.
• The General Assembly as a whole, not individual
members of the PCA, has oversight of General Assembly
Committees and Agencies.
• The overture is far reaching to include “the records” not
only of the Foundation but all Committees and Agencies.
• It would be illegal to allow individual members to inspect
some records, for example, academic records of individual
students at Covenant College or Covenant Seminary, or
personnel records of any Committee or Agency.
• It is a common practice of PCA churches that donor
records are confidential records and individual donor
records are not open for inspection to any church member
who is curious as to what another member gives.
• PCA Committees and Agencies would be severely
hindered in raising funds to carry out their respective
missions, if the inspection of any and all records,
particularly donor records, were allowed.

<a id="ga30-p294"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=296 printed_page=294 -->

5. That the Administrative Committee through the local Host
Committee be responsible for all services at General Assembly (“RAO” 9-3). Adopted
Grounds:
• In 1980 we had twenty-two Presbyteries [when the present
practice of rotating worship leadership responsibilities
among Presbyteries was instituted]. We now have sixty-
three presbyteries. With three morning services during the
week, it will take twenty-one years to have all Presbyteries
lead services.
• For the last two Assemblies the Local Arrangements
Committee (LAC) has requested to be responsible for
worship leadership of all services. This has been
beneficial in the LAC maintaining some theme throughout
the Assembly week.
Rationale of the Committee of Commissioners:
•— Because -some presbyteries will likely never host a
General Assembly they would consequently never lead
worship at General Assembly. The previous practice of
rotating worship leadership responsibilities among
Presbyteries is therefore a more equitable method of
addressing this issue.

represented by the schedule below be approved by the General Assembly (BCO 14-1.13). The increase over 2002 is
calculated at 4% (four percent). Adopted

teaches it.” Adopted
13. That the AC recommend to the General Assembly that it
continue with the general type of Assembly it has been
operating, while the AC makes every effort to control costs;
and further, that it be understood that the AC will take the
initiative in selecting facilities where all functions can be
carried out “under one roof’, and further, that the AC response
to 2001 Overture #6 be disseminated to the full Assembly, that
2001 Overture #6 is answered by the AC response [found in
Appendix C, Attachment B, p. 385], Adopted
14. That the Biblical Conflict Resolution [see Appendix C,
Attachment A, p. 378 ff] be sent to General Assembly for
approval as a non-binding informational Appendix to the
BCO, and that Communication 7 be spread on the Minutes of
General Assembly. Adopted
COMMUNICATION 7 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery (to AC)
“Approve Biblical Conflict Resolution Appendix”
Subject: Item 7 for Information, Discussion and Comment
“Biblical Conflict Resolution” (Handbook for Presbytery
Clerks, page B-l 15).
Dear Dr. Taylor,
This constitutes a report of our response to the direction from
the 29th General Assembly action calling on Presbyteries to

<a id="ga30-p300"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=300 printed_page=null -->

“discuss the proposal for ‘Biblical Conflict Resolution’ before
the next General Assembly.”
At our January Stated Meeting, subject materials were
distributed to Presbyters and the Chairman of the Judicial
Business Committee encouraged each man to study the
materials and be prepared to discuss same at the next meeting.
At our April Stated Meeting, after prayer, comments and
discussion/debate on the floor of Presbytery, the following
action was taken: Moved, seconded and carried the Stated
Clerk report to the General Assembly that Rocky
Mountain Presbytery is in favor of and strongly
recommends that the General Assembly adopt the concept
of “Biblical Conflict Resolution” and incorporate the
proposal into an Appendix of the BCO.
Since GA is only a month away, I will e-mail this letter as a
MS Word file attachment and follow it with a hardcopy
through the U.S. mail.
Looking forward to seeing you in Birmingham.
In His service with you,
/s/ Lyle E. Lagasse [Stated Clerk of Rocky Mountain
Presbytery]

##### OVERTURE 35 from Ascension Presbytery (to AC)

“Clarify Relationship of Separately Incorporated Agencies to
Assembly”

Whereas, denominational management personnel openly
participated in and led a meeting of an organization
billing itself as “Presbyterian Pastoral Leadership
Network (PPLN)” on April 10, 2002; and

Whereas, participation by denominational management
personnel in such a group is in apparent violation of
the Conflict of Interest Policy adopted by the General Assembly (M21GA, pp. 174-77); and

Whereas, although the Conflict of Interest Policy was adopted
in light of questions of propriety with regard to
financial transactions, the Policy itself is quite broad,
and includes involvement with non-profit
organizations; and

Whereas, among the examples of conflict of interest are the
following: “Using the PCA’s personnel, equipment,
supplies, or good will for other than formally
approved activities, programs, and purposes” and
“Expending unreasonable staff time during normal
business hours for ... other organization to the
detriment of work performance for the PCA”; and

Whereas, assistants to one or more management personnel
have been used to create a website, to e-mail others,
and to assist in the April 10, 2002 meeting; and

Whereas, the purposes of this organization include the
violation of the regulative principle of worship and the
Presbyterian ideal of uniformity of worship as
evidenced in the PPLN’s “Our Vision” viz. “We want
our church’s future to be characterized by the
following Gospel priorities ... Appreciating different
forms of worship within the Reformed tradition”; and

Whereas, it is inappropriate for denominational servants to
lend their power, prestige, and influence to such
endeavors, especially when they are aiming to
overturn established denominational doctrine and
policy, whether through Constitutional amendment

<a id="ga30-p304"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=304 printed_page=null -->

and/or other means such as publicly supporting and
promoting overtures to change the PCA’s historic
understanding of subscription to the Westminster Confession of Faith (see PPLN’s “Our Vision”), and

Whereas, no teaching or ruling elder may legitimately
undermine denominational doctrine; and

Whereas, this is especially true when such teaching or ruling
elders are using General Assembly resources; and

Whereas, whenever denominational servants engage in such
activity, they undermine the confidence of the church
as a whole in her committees and agencies, and could
threaten financial giving to them;

Now therefore, be it resolved, that Westminster Presbytery
hereby overtures the General Assembly to counsel
denominational management personnel that the use of
denominational resources and/or personnel to advocate
and/or promote organization such as the Presbyterian
Pastoral Leadership Network is inappropriate.
And be it further resolved, that Westminster Presbytery
hereby overtures the General Assembly to counsel all
paid employees on the General Assembly level that
such involvement in leadership positions with PPLN or
any similar entities are considered to be incompatible
with continued occupation of denominational posts.
Make appropriate changes in the “Corporate Bylaws” to
reflect the change of status of Reformed University Ministries
to that of a permanent Assembly-level committee, namely,

B. PCA “Corporate Bylaws,” Article V, strike “E.” in “E.
Other Committees” and insert “F.”
C. PCA “Corporate Bylaws,” Article V, strike “F.” in “F.
Board of Agencies” and insert “G.’That TE L. Roy

28. That Personal Resolution #3 from RE James E. Mezzanotte
of Central Carolina Presbytery be answered in the negative:

accountable Committees/Agencies.
• Each Committee/Agency has its own policy for review
and approval of material posted on their associated web
sites.
• Those concerned about content on the official web site of
the PCA may contact the responsible Committee/Agency
to voice their concern. Adopted

RESOLVED, that any material submitted for publication on
the official website of the Presbyterian Church in America, including imbedded links, shall be screened
to eliminate any material that is not Biblical, is
slanderous, or otherwise may reasonably be expected
to be against the peace, unity and purity of the
Church. Such website is currently known as
www.pcanet.org.

1. That the Minutes of Ascension Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: January 27, 2001, April 27-28, 2001, July 28, 2001, October 19-20, 2001
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: None.
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance: None.
- **d.** No response to the 29th GA or previous assemblies is required. Adopted

2. That the Minutes of Calvary Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: None.
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: January 27, 2001, April 26, 2001, July

28, 2001, October 25, 2001
c. Be approved with exceptions of substance stated
below:
Exception: January 27, 2001, April 26, 2001, July
28, 2001: Minutes of presbytery meetings have not
been approved. RAO 14-3.c.8
Exception: July 28, 2001: Ordination exam approved
without record of internship being completed. BCO
21-2; 21-4
Exception: July 28, 2001: A call approved for labor
Out of Bounds gives no reasons for considering it a
valid Christian ministry. BCO 20-1

<a id="ga30-p306"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=308 printed_page=306 -->

Exception: September 10, 2001: Presbytery meeting
held without minutes being submitted. BCO 13-11
Exception: General: No record of committee
members, licentiates or interns in Directory or Roll.
RAO 14-4.C.1
d. That the following responses to the 28th GA
exceptions be found unsatisfactory. Response
should be submitted to the 31st General Assembly:
Exception: July 25, 1998: There is no documentation
(calls, etc.) for two men for changes from assistant to
associate pastor. There are no commissions approved
for installation. BCO 15-1; 15-2.
Response to 28th GA: These men were already
ordained and installed in their particular churches.
This was simply a change in their title designation.
Rationale: Change in call from Assistant to Associate
requires a new call from the congregation to replace
previous call from the session. BCO 20-6, 22-2,3
Response to 29th GA: These two men in question
were serving in their churches and still are.
Congregational Meetings were called and calls were
extended to them as Associate Pastors. There were no
changes in the terms of their calls at that time.
Calvary Presbytery felt that since these men were
already ordained and installed, that a further
installation service would not be necessary. If we
were wrong in this we will gladly correct the situation,
if you will so instruct us. We thought we did
everything correctly, but we willingly submit to your
wisdom and humbly seek your guidance in this matter
if further action is needed.
Rationale why response to 29th GA is
unsatisfactory: Change in call from assistant to
associate requires a new installation by the Presbytery
because the assistant previously called by the session
has now been called by the congregation (BCO 22-
2,3), and because additional questions must be posed
to both the new associate and the congregation at a
service of installation (BCO 21-9, 10; footnotes).
Therefore, it would be most appropriate for Calvary
Presbytery to erect a commission or commissions to
install the two men in question as associate ministers.
Adopted

<a id="ga30-p307"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=309 printed_page=307 -->

Response: This was indeed a serious oversight on our
part, and Presbytery has rectified the situation by
approving the following motion at our stated meeting
October 27, 2001 to dissolve the relationship between
TE [name deleted] with Redeemer PCA, Winston
Salem, NC as Associate Pastor (minutes of this
congregational meeting are attached as Appendix #6).
Also, at its October 27, 2001 meeting CCP approved
the call of TE [name deleted] as Assistant Pastor of
Grace Church, Kemersville. We believe what
happened is that all the appropriate transfer steps were
not recorded because Presbytery viewed this as an in-
house transfer. We will be more careful in the future.
Adopted

Exception: January 23, 2001, May 8, 2001: No
record of congregational meeting requesting
dissolution of pastoral relation. BCO 23-1
Exception: July 31, 2001: No record of call included in
minutes after approved ordination exam. BCO 20-1; 21-1
d. That the following responses to the 29th GA
exceptions be found satisfactory:
Exception: May 9, 2000; September 26, 2000: No
indication that a congregational meeting was held to
approve dissolution of pastoral relationship. BCO 23-1.
Response: I have spoken to the Chairman of the
Ministers Committee who is responsible for the action
taken concerning the dissolution of pastoral
relationship. There was a congregational meeting
which was appropriately called and conducted. The
Secretary of the committee failed to include the
minutes of that meeting in the minutes of the
committee meeting. The Chairman of this committee
will ensure that from this point on all minutes of
Congregational Meetings which dissolve the pastoral
relationship will be recorded in their minutes and
reported to Presbytery.
Exception: January 25, 2000: Item 19.C & 19.D:
Teaching Elders were approved to labor out of bounds
without following BCO 8-7 and 13-2.
Response: We acknowledge that we failed to follow
the BCO 8-7 and 13-2 in approving Teaching Elders to
labor outside the bounds of Presbytery. Since being
made aware of our error we will be careful to follow the
proper procedures as outlined in BCO 8-7 and 13-2.
Adopted

- **a.** Be approved without exception: January 27, 2001, April 28, 2001, September 20, 2001
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: None.
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance stated below: None.
- **d.** That the following responses to the 29th GA exceptions be found satisfactory:

Exception: Jan 22, 2000: No record of
congregational meetings to dissolve pastoral
relationship. BCO 23-1.

<a id="ga30-p310"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=312 printed_page=310 -->

Response: The congregational meeting to concur with
Mr. [name deletedj’s resignation was held January
2000 and concurred. The fact was not reported in
Presbytery’s meeting. Presbytery will seek to be more
diligent to include such details in our minutes in the
future.
Exception: July 20, 2000: Minister examined for
transfer with no record of previous ecclesiastical
connection. BCO 13-6.
Response: Presbytery failed to request transfer of
credentials in a timely manner. The Stated Clerk
spoke by phone to the Stated Clerk of the dismissing
Presbytery and sent a written request for the transfer.
Presbytery failed to enter these in the minutes.
Presbytery will seek to be more diligent in the future
to do these things in a timely manner and to enter them
in the minutes. Adopted

concurrence of at least 3 Teaching Elders and 3 Ruling

his exam. BCO 19-2.
Response: Great Lakes Presbytery verifies that the
candidate did, indeed preach a sermon at that meeting
and that the sermon was approved by Presbytery. We
regret that the itemizing of the individual requirements
for licensure was not consistent with the action of
Presbytery recorded as ‘On separate motions all areas
of his exam were sustained’, and will be more careful
to be consistent in the future. Adopted

d. That the following responses to the 29th GA
exceptions be found satisfactory:
Exception: August 18-19, 2000, November 10-11,
2000: No records of commission reports. BCO 15-
1; 13-11.
Response: Heartland Presbytery agrees with the
exception and apologizes for failing to include these
commission reports in its minutes. As is evident from
the minutes, the clerk intended to insert them, but forgot
to do so. We have attempted to locate these reports for
your review, but have only been able to find one: the
Report of the Commission to Install [name deleted] on
August 20, 2000. We will submit this report for review
by the 30th General Assembly CRPR. The other reports
were purged with the assumption that they had been
included in the minutes. We will be more careful in this
area in the future. Adopted

25. That the Minutes of Louisiana Presbytery:
a. Be approved without exception: April 21, 2001, July
21,2001

- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: January 20, 2001, October 21, 2001

26. That the Minutes of Metropolitan New York Presbytery:
a. Be approved without exception: March 9, 2001, June
28,2001

- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: January 20, 2001, September 7, 2001, November 9, 2001
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance stated below:

Exception: January 20, 2001: Man approved for
ordination who takes the following exceptions (among
several others) to certain language in the Confession of
Faith: does not agree that “vows of poverty,
obedience, and singleness are necessarily sinful and
superstitious” WCF XXII. 7; takes exception to the
language that “none who are not then present in the
congregation” should partake of the Lord’s Supper
WCF XXIX.3; takes exception to the language of “the
making any representation of God, of all or any of the
three persons, either inwardly in our mind or
outwardly in any kind of image or likeness of any
creature whatsoever” WLC #109; takes exception to
the language “Although all are not to be permitted to
read the word publicly to the congregation...” WLC
#156. BCO 21-5, q. 2; BCO 26-1.
Exception: June 8, 2001: Extraordinary clause
invoked without record of reasons, record of super
majority vote, or commission to install. SCO 21-4
d. That the following responses to the 29th GA
exceptions be found satisfactory:
Exception: March 12, 2000, September 8, 2000:
Commissions were erected to install TEs, but there are
no subsequent reports in or attached to the minutes.
BCO 15-1.

<a id="ga30-p321"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=323 printed_page=321 -->

Response: Presbytery regrets not having approved
and appended the minutes of the Commissions to
install TE [name deleted] and TE [name deleted] in
their November 10, 2000 meeting minutes. The report
of the Commission for TE [name deleted] was
approved and appended in our January 20, 2001
meeting minutes and likewise the Commission to
install TE [name deleted] in our March 9, 2001
meeting minutes. The records for the Commission to
install TE [name deleted] seem to have been lost. He
was however (re)installed on November 4, 2001 and
that Commission report was approved and appended in
our November 9, 2001 meeting minutes.
Exception: June 9, 2000: A TE, transferring from
another denomination was not examined as required
by BCO 21 -4, nor were there extraordinary provisions
invoked, nor was he asked the ordination questions.
BCO 13-6.
Response: Presbytery now understand its BCO 13-6
requirement to examine ministers transferring from
another denomination in both views and knowledge in
all areas of BCO 21 -4 and has started that process with
its January 12, 2002 meeting when it examined a
minister coming from the KAPC. Presbytery also
understands its requirement to orally ask the ordination
questions of BCO 21-5 at the examination. We also
note that we accomplished this in written form when
the transferring minister signed the Ministerial
Obligation Form. Presbytery notes that our minutes of
June 8, 2001 (the examination of Rev. [name deleted]
for transfer from the Free Methodist Church) will fail
to conform to the above standards and requests the
2002 Committee on Review of Presbytery Minutes to
note our current resolve to meet our BCO
requirements. (This June 8, 2001 examination was
conducted before the 2001 Committee drew our
attention to this expanded requirement)
Exception: June 9, 2000: Approval of the
examination for ordination of a candidate who states as
part of his exceptions to the WCF, WLC, and WSC that
“I also believe the Sabbath rest to mean any one day in
seven.” WCF 21-7; WLC 116; WSC 59

<a id="ga30-p322"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=324 printed_page=322 -->

Response: In the examination of Candidate [name
deleted] for ordination, Presbytery regrets allowing the
candidate’s own statement “I believe the Sabbath rest
to mean any one day in seven,” to appear in our
minutes with all its ambiguity. We understand how
that statement might be interpreted as the view that we
might choose another day of the week besides Sunday
as the day for corporate worship. The Committee’s
references to WCF 21-7 and to WLC 116 imply that
they so interpreted [name deleted]’s statement. But
that is not his view. His intention is to affirm that
ministers, unable to rest on the Lord’s Day due to their
official duties, are to choose another day of the week
on which to rest. Presbytery does not consider this
view an unacceptable exception to our Standards and
trusts the Committee agrees. Adopted

d. That the responses to the 29th GA exceptions be
found unsatisfactory. The responses to these
exceptions should be submitted to the 31st GA.
Exception: Man licensed who “holds exception in his
view of the Sabbath (he does not believe that the
Sabbath Day and Lord’s Day are the same), the law
(he is not absolutely sure that the Decalogue is the
New Testament Ethic), and on who may administer the
Sacraments (he cannot see that the administration of
the sacraments is the sole prerogative of teaching
elders; he believes that ruling elders and others who
have demonstrated knowledge, understanding, and
maturity concerning the sacraments should be allowed
to administer them.)” (WCF 21-7, 27-4, 19-2).
Rationale: The Presbytery has submitted word-for-
word the same response submitted to the 29th GA and
the same response in substance was submitted to the
27th and 28th General Assemblies. Therefore, the 30th
GA believes that the Presbytery still needs to submit a
substantive response to the action of these previous
General Assemblies.
Response to 28th and 29th GA: See 29th GA Minutes
29.64.32

<a id="ga30-p325"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=327 printed_page=325 -->

Exception: March 12-13, 1999 (p. 3): Items 75-
17.f., 75-17.g., and 75-17.i, refer to appendices which
are not attached.
Response: None submitted. Adopted

34. That the Minutes of North Georgia Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: February 24, 2001, July 21, 2001, October 16, 2001
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: January 20, 2001, April 17, 2001
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance stated below:

Exception: January 20, 2001: Incomplete record of
exam elements. BCO 21-4; RAO 14-3.e.5
d. That the following responses to the 29th GA
exceptions be found satisfactory:
Exception: General: Candidates are consistently
received with no record that the provisions of BCO 18-
2 were met.
Response: Presbytery regrets the failure to record
meeting the provision of BCO 18-2 for Candidates
coming under care, and will endeavor to be more
inclusive in the future.
Exception: April 18, 2000, August 29, 2000,
December 19, 2000: p.4, #1,3; No evidence of

<a id="ga30-p326"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=328 printed_page=326 -->

congregational concurrence in dissolution of calls.
BCO 23-1.
Response: Presbytery regrets the failure to record the
congregational concurrence to the dissolution of
pastoral relationship in items 1 and 3. The
congregational concurrence was received in each case
but not recorded.
Exception: April 18, 2000: p.8 , #4,5; Two
commissions are established with only one ruling elder
member.
Response: Presbytery regrets the failure to record the
proper number of Ruling Elders appointed to
commissions. In each case the commissions were
compliant with the BCO but the names were not
recorded. Presbytery will take measures to ensure
proper recording in the future.
Exception: July 15, 2000: p.5, A candidate is
brought under care when he is not present so he could
not answer the question or receive a charge. Also,
there is no record of a charge to or prayer fora
candidate who is present. BCO 18-3.
Response: Presbytery regrets the failure to properly
record all of the details concerning examination and
charge. In the future all candidates will be present and
all elements will be recorded, as required.
Exception: July 15, 2000: p.5, A candidate is
approved for ordination with no record in the minutes
of a call or of provisions for ordination. BCO 20-1;
21-5.
Response: Presbytery regrets the failure to include
the candidate’s call and commission and will endeavor
to be more careful in the future. Adopted
35. That the Minutes of North Texas Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: January 26-27, 2001, April 27-28, 2001, July 27-28, 2001, September 4, 2001, October 26-27, 2001
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: None.
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance stated below: None.
- **d.** That the following responses to the 29th GA exceptions be found satisfactory:

Exception: January 28-29, 2000: There is no reason
given or documentation for removal of a candidate
from Under Care Roll. BCO 18-7.

<a id="ga30-p327"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=329 printed_page=327 -->

Response: The two candidates were removed because
both were ordained.
Exception: January 28-29, 2000: Divesting of TE
should report 2nd reading. BCO 38-2. Also should
assign to church membership. BCO 46-8.
Response: The action taken was the second reading
(see previous meeting Minutes of the 60th SM of NTP,
Page 5, ppg 2101). A letter was received 6 months
earlier from the TE wherein he requested divestiture.
It was reported to the Presbytery and he was divested
at the next meeting. This was not unilateral but based
on a request. Our action was in accordance with BCO
38-2. Regarding the exception to BCO 46-8, the TE
requesting divestiture had moved to an adjoining state
and a town without a PCA church. The TE indicated
that he would be attending a different denomination.
Exception: July 28-29, 2000: Examination of TE for
transfer from another denomination: He was not
examined in Bible content, church history or history of
the PCA. BCO 13-6; 21-4.
Response: The candidate was examined in the
previously mentioned fields but the SC failed to record
same. The transferring TE was formerly the
Moderator of the National Presbyterian Church of
Mexico and was examined thoroughly in Committee.
However, the point is moot since the TE has been
transferred back to Mexico and is no longer a member
of NTP or the PCA. In the future, the SC will attempt
to record examination action more thoroughly and
completely.
Exception: July 28-29, 2000: No record that man was
a member of a church of nt p for 6 months or that he
had the endorsement of his session. BCO 20-1; 13-10.
Response: The Stated Clerk inadvertently omitted
this information. Letters and records in the files
indicate them an was a member of ant p church for 6
months and the files do contain a letter of sessional
endorsement (dated 6/11/2000). The SC will take
greater pains not to leave this detail out of the Minutes
in the future. Adopted

52. That the Minutes of Southeast Louisiana Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: None.
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: January 27, 2001, April 28, 2001, July

28, 2001, October 27, 2001
c. Be approved with exception of substance stated below:
None.
d. That the following response to the 29th GA
exception be found satisfactory:
Exception: July 22, 2000: paragraph 31.10,
concerning the ordination examination of a man. BCO
21-4. There is no indication of completion of
internship. There is no indication of approval of
educational requirements and original languages.
There was no sermon approved and no documentation
of his call. ashe was coming as a Licentiate, there
was no indication of his being asked if he changed his
previous views concerning the WCF, WSC, or the
BCO. BCO 21-4.
Response: Southeast Louisiana Presbytery agrees
with the exceptions, except for the preaching portion.
We will make every attempt to be more careful in
reporting all actions taken by Presbytery. All actions
cited in the exception were accomplished but not
recorded.

<a id="ga30-p335"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=337 printed_page=335 -->

In reference to a sermon not being preached by one
who is licensed, the examination for licensure requires
a sermon to be preached and BCO 21-4 par. #2
specifically states that “If the Presbytery previously
approved all parts of the licensure examination, it need
not re-examine the intern in those areas at this time.”
Thus, since the license had preached a satisfactory
sermon nine months previously and satisfactorily
completed all trails for licensure, he was not required
to preach again by Presbytery. Adopted
53. That the Minutes of Southern Florida Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: January 16, 2001, July 17, 2001, October 16, 2001
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: None.
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance:

Exception: April 17, 2001: In view of the
candidate’s exception to WCF 11:3, as stated below,
which strikes at the fundamentals of our system of
doctrine, the Presbytery of Southern Florida erred in
sustaining his examination for transfer.
With regard to WCF 11:3, “I do not believe
that the Holy Spirit eternally proceeds from the Father
and the Son, but, in the integrity of the Holy Trinity,
from the Father alone. I believe that the references
such as John 14:16-17, 26, 15:26, 16:7-15, refer to the
out-pouring of the Spirit at Pentecost and not to His
eternal procession. While it is true, of course, that the
Holy Spirit is the Spirit of Christ, he is also referred to
as the Spirit of God, and, as far as that goes, in the
mystery of the Holy Trinity, Jesus is referred to as the
Everlasting Father. Historically, the addition of the
filioque was made unilaterally by the Roman Catholic
Church centuries after the Council of Nicea and
subsequently inherited by Protestantism.”
d. The response to the July 20, 1999 and October 19,
1999 exceptions of the 28th GA be found
satisfactory.
Exception: July 20, 1999: BCO 13-6 requires
thorough exam of transfers in all areas. No
extraordinary clause stated as rationale.
Response: Presbytery has discontinued using D-21,
Check List For Reception of a Minister From Another
Denomination, which is part of the “Handbook for

<a id="ga30-p336"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=338 printed_page=336 -->

Presbytery Clerks.” Presbytery is now following BCO
13-6 in all such transfers.
Exception: October 19, 1999 (11.1): BCO 13-6
requires thorough exam of transfers in all areas. No
extraordinary clause found.
Response: See above response,
e. That the following response to the January 19,1999
exception of the 28th GA be found unsatisfactory
with the reminder that the Presbytery will need to
submit a response to the 31st General Assembly.
Exception: January 19, 1999 (16.h): Theological
paper approved in which candidate stated, “I believe
that God gives extraordinary gifts (miracles, healing,
and tongues).” WCF 18-3; WLC #80; BCO 7-1.
Response: The Teaching Elder in question is no
longer a member of Presbytery and has left the PCA
and joined another denomination.
Rationale: RAO 14-10.b requires that the Presbytery
respond by stating whether they agree or respectfully
disagree with the exception. Adopted

55. That the Minutes of Southwest Presbytery:

- **a.** Be approved without exception: None.
- **b.** Be approved with exceptions of form reported to presbytery: January, 18, 2001, September 20, 2001
- **c.** Be approved with exceptions of substance:

Exception: April 19, 2001: Incomplete record of
exam elements. BCO 21-4; RAO 14-3.e.5
Exception: September 20, 2001: Incomplete record
of exam elements. BCO 19-2c; RAO 14-3.e.5
d. The following responses to the 29th GA exceptions
be found satisfactory:
NOTE: The GA appreciates the efforts taken by the
presbytery to correct their action. However, a
presbytery may not change minutes reviewed by a
previous GA.
Exception: June 30, 2000: The purpose of the called
meeting is not stated with the minutes. BCO 13-12.

<a id="ga30-p337"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=339 printed_page=337 -->

Response: The purpose of this Called Meeting was
specified in the Call of May 17, 2000. A copy of which
is attached [with the minutes turned in this year]. There
were only two items of business to be conducted:
(1) Approve the Pastoral Call from Covenant
Presbyterian Church, Sun City West, AZ, to TE [name
deleted], (2) Conduct appropriate examination of
[name deleted] as the RUF Representative at the
University of Arizona and approve his moving to the
field.
As you will note from paragraphs 1 and 2 of
the June 30, 2000 minutes, these were the only two
items discussed and acted on. BCO 13-12 does not say
that the purpose of the Called Meeting has to be
explicitly restated in the minutes if it has been clearly
stated in the Call which in this case it was.
The moderator, RE [name deleted], explicitly
ruled that we would follow the order as the business
items were listed on the docket. This was done as
paragraphs 1 & 2 so indicate. It appears, therefore,
that the Southwest Presbytery fully complied with
BCO 13-12. However, we have retroactively changed
out June 30, 2000 minutes to specifically reflect the
two items of business mentioned in the May 17, 2000
Call. Adopted

IV. General Recommendations
That the 30th General Assembly instructs the Presbyteries to give
diligence in the following matters:
1. When presbytery approves its minutes with any correction and
amendments of substance, those corrections and amendments
should be recorded. SCO 13-11 Adopted
2. Calls to a particular ministry should be included in the minutes
(written in the record or as attachments). BCO 13-11
Adopted
3. Complaints should be recorded in the minutes (written in the
record or as attachments). BCO 13-11; RAO 14-3.e.7
Adopted
4. Presbyteries are encouraged to adopt the RAO guidelines for
the examination and approval of sessional records. RAO 14
Adopted
5. Record of examination of candidates should list specific parts
of examinations and trials for licensure, candidacy, internship,
transfer, and ordination. RAO 14-3. e. 5 Adopted
6. Attachments for Presbytery minutes should be clearly labeled
and included with the set sent to GA. Adopted
7. Record the establishment of commissions and their quorums
and include commission reports in Presbytery records. RAO
14-3. e. 4 Adopted
8. A commission is only authorized to deliberate upon and
conclude the business referred to it. Each commission shall
keep a full record of its proceedings, which shall be submitted
to the court appointing it. Adopted
9. Minutes of executive sessions should be included in the body
of the minutes ora san attachment. RAO 14-4.e.6 Adopted
10. Responses to GA exceptions of substance should be adopted
by Presbytery and included in its minutes. A letter stating
Presbytery’s response to the Assembly concerning disposition

<a id="ga30-p341"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=343 printed_page=341 -->

of any exceptions of substance taken by the past Assembly
should be couched in temperate and respectful language. RAO
14-4. c. 3 Adopted
11. Record exceptions taken by teaching elders and licentiates
when examined. Postponed to the 31st GA
12. Ensure that every candidate for the ministry under its care
make a report at least once a year and secure from his
instructors an annual report upon his deportment, diligence,
and progress in study. BCO 18-6 Adopted
13. Ensure that Committees overseeing interns report on every
intern at each stated meeting of Presbytery. In addition, an
annual report of all interns shall be submitted and included in
the minutes. BCO 19-12 Adopted
14. Ensure that teaching elders laboring out of bounds make report
to Presbytery at least once each year. BCO 8-4 Adopted
15. State clearly the reasons for invoking the extraordinary clause in
waiving portions of an exam and/or trial. BCO 18-2; 19-2; 21-4
Adopted
V. Officers Elected to Serve at the 31st General Assembly
Chairman: TE Grover Gunn (Covenant Presbytery)

Secretary: TE Allen Vargo (Warrior Presbytery)

30-64 Constitutional Inquiry
The Assembly received and referred the following Constitutional
Inquiry to the Committee on Constitutional Business:
Does either the Constitution of the PCA, or the RAO, presently require
a presbytery to record a minister’s exceptions to the WCF,
WLC, WSC, or BCO in its minutes?
30-65 Common Consent to Amend Objection
Without objection, TE Frank Smith was permitted to amend his
objection received earlier as follows: 1. To add after “third” the following:
“without impugning the intentions of the Standing Judicial Commission”, and
2. Eliminate the paragraph which begins, “The complainant’s representatives
stated...”. [See 30-40, p. 204.]
30-66 Report of the Committee on Thanks
TE Henry Lewis Smith led the Assembly in prayer and presented the
report, which was adopted as amended.

<a id="ga30-p343"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=345 printed_page=343 -->

RESOLUTION AND THANKS
“The Lord has done great things for us, and we are glad.” (Psalm 126:2b)
In the year of our Lord 1973, a courageous and committed company of
338 ruling and teaching elders, representing 260 churches and 41,232
members, gathered at the Briarwood Presbyterian Church to launch what
became the Presbyterian Church in America. This year as the General Assembly returns to the city, church and Presbytery of our birth for this
thirtieth General Assembly, this part of Christ’s vineyard has grown by God’s
grace and the empowerment of the Holy Spirit to encompass over 1500
churches with 300,000 plus members, with 1603 attending this assembly.
Both spiritually and topographically, this year’s Assembly has been a
mountaintop experience here at the splendid facilities and grounds of
Briarwood Church on ol’ Rocky Ridge. The lavish hospitality of the host
church and Presbytery has provided for every need of the Commissioners, their
wives and Covenant children, and our esteemed visitors.
We have profited from the rich spiritual nourishment provided by
pastors and Birmingham churches and the other well-selected worship leaders
and preachers:
To the Briarwood staff in all departments, the Session and
Congregation, the Host Committee members, pastors and
Birmingham-area churches, as well as Co-Chairmen Houston
Welch, Tom Harris and Administrative Assistant, Catherine
Trinkle (the listing whereof fills seven pages!) - y’all have opened
your church, your homes and your hearts to us;
To the Music Ministries of Briarwood and other Presbytery
churches - to singers, directors, instrumentalists - for their high-
quality and uplifting praise;
To our Moderator, Joseph “Skip” Ryan for his skilled, unflappable
and impeccably impartial presiding over the largest Assembly
ever. Although he has confessed with his own mouth to having
bom into human depravity and a Yankee, he has unfailingly
displayed gentleness, kindness and other fruits of the Spirit;
To the exhibitors and providers of services and equipment for our
use, and
Finally, to the Commissioners themselves whose conduct has been
marked by courtesy and restraint, even as we have grappled with
issues upon which deep differing convictions are held;

<a id="ga30-p344"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=346 printed_page=344 -->

TO THESE ALL and countless others, we now voice our heartfelt and
continuing appreciation.
“It is the Lord’s doing, and it is marvelous in our eyes.” (Psalm 118:23)
Committee on Resolution and Thanks,
/s/ TE Henry Lewis Smith (Southeast Alabama)
/s/ RE Bill McClay (Westminster)
30-67 Excused from Part or All of the Assembly
Presbytery Commissioner
Covenant Jon Anderson
Covenant Brad Steward
Grace Steven B. Shuman
Grace Philip McRae
Nashville Len Hendrix
North Georgia Kennedy Smartt
North Georgia Chuck DeBardeleben
North Georgia, Ken Doll
Southwest Florida Robert Byrne
Southwest Florida Joe DeNitto

30-68 Adjournment
The Assembly adjourned at 1:05 p.m. with the singing of Psalm 133 and
pronouncement of the benediction by the Moderator, to reconvene in
Charlotte, NC on June 10, 2003 as the 31st General Assembly.

PART III

The Appendices include the Reports of the Permanent Committees as
originally submitted to the General Assembly, except where the text may have
been amended by the Assembly. The recommendations in this section are
those originally submitted by the Permanent Committees and may not have
been adopted by the Assembly. See the report of the Committee of
Commissioners for each of the respective committees and agencies in Part II
The Journal, to find the recommendations as they were adopted by the
Assembly.
The budgets, as approved by the Assembly are found in Appendix C,
Attachment H, beginning on p. 413.

<a id="ga30-p346"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=348 printed_page=346 -->

STATED CLERK’S REPORT TO THE
THIRTIETH GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
Interchurch Relations
I serve on the Interchurch Relations Committee as part of my responsibilities
(RAO 3-2 j.)
■ I have been elected to the Executive Committee of the National
Association of Evangelicals.
■ I will be one of the representatives of the PCA working within
NAPARC to discuss the possibilities of Church union among its
constituent Churches.
Lawsuits
i am pleased to report that the two suits to which the PCA was a party, Andrew
S. Robertson v. the Presbyterian Church in America, a Corporation, et altera,
and the Presbyterian Church in America, a Corporation v. Hyatt Hotels, have
both been settled satisfactorily out of court. As of the date of this writing, the
PCA is not party to any pending lawsuits.
Advice to the Stated Clerk from the Committee on Constitutional Business
Since the last General Assembly I have sought the advice of the CCB on
several matters.
1. maya church be a member of the PCA without being a member of a
Presbytery?
Clerk’s Response: My response was that ordinarily a particular church
is a member of the PCA by virtue of its being related to a PCA
Presbytery. BCO 25-11 does contain some language that may be
applied to transitional situations.
While a congregation consists of all the communing
members of a particular church, and in matters ecclesiastical
the actions of such local congregation or church shall be in
conformity with the provisions of this Book of Church Order,
nevertheless, in matters pertaining to the subject matters
referred to in this BCO 25, including specifically the right to
affiliate with or become a member of this body ora
Presbytery hereof and the right to withdraw from or to sever
any affiliation of connection with this body or any Presbytery
thereof, [emphasis added] action may be taken by such local
congregation or local church in accordance with the civil laws
applicable to such local congregation or local church; and as
long as such action is taken in compliance with such
applicable civil laws, then such shall be the action of the local

<a id="ga30-p347"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=349 printed_page=347 -->

congregation or local church. . . . Particular churches need
remain in association with any court of this body only so long
as they themselves so desire.
Response: It is the opinion of the CCB that the Stated Clerk’s written
and subsequent verbal advice was correct in that a local church
withdrawn from a PCA presbytery is ordinarily no longer a member
congregation of the PCA.
We further conclude that while there are some ambiguities in the BCO
25-11 language quoted it is our opinion that there is no constitutional
provision fora local church to withdraw from a presbytery and
continue to belong to the PCA.
2. maya presbytery nominate to a General Assembly committee a man
other than the alternate from their Presbytery (who will automatically
be considered)?
Clerk’s Response: Yes, a presbytery may nominate to a General Assembly Committee a man other than the alternate from their
Presbytery who will automatically be considered under BCO 14-1, 11
and “RAO” 7-4 g. Reasoning: 1) once a presbytery has nominated a
man and he is elected by an action of the General Assembly as an
alternate member of a General Assembly Committee or Agency, he is
the Assembly’s elected member (somewhat analogous to a motion’s
being made and seconded is the property of the body and may not be
withdrawn without the consent of the body). 2) “RAO” 7-4 cis a not
an absolute (note the qualifying term “ordinarily”) and 3) “RAO” 7-4,
g. distinguishes between “new nominee” and “alternates not assuming
any vacancies during a year.”
Response - The CCB concurs with the advice of the Stated Clerk in
this matter.
3. May an alternate member of a General Assembly Permanent
Committee serve on a Committee of Commissioners? “RAO” 13-2?
Clerk’s Response: No, an alternate member of a General Assembly
Permanent Committee may not serve on a Committee of
Commissioners. “RAO” 13-2 states, “Commissioners serving on
permanent committees or sub-committees of the Assembly or the
staffs thereof are not eligible to serve on any Committees of
Commissioners.” An alternate is a committee member who votes only
in the absence of a principal member, but he is a member,
nevertheless.

<a id="ga30-p348"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=350 printed_page=348 -->

Response – The CCB concurs with the advice of the Stated Clerk in this matter.

4. May a member of the staff of a General Assembly Permanent Committee or Agency serve on a Committee of Commissioners? RAO 13-2.

**Clerk’s Response**: No, a member of the staff of a General Assembly Permanent Committee or Agency may not serve on a Committee of Commissioners. A commissioner who is a staff member of a given General Assembly or Agency could be elected to the permanent committee of a committee or agency, as long as he is not elected to a permanent committee, which administers matters directly related to his employment (“RAO” 4-7). “RAO” 13-2 states, “Commissioners serving on Permanent Committees or sub-committees of the Assembly or the staffs thereof are not eligible to serve on any Committees of Commissioners [emphasis added].”

**Response**: The CCB concurs with the advice of the Stated Clerk on this matter with the proviso that standards regarding Agencies are ambiguous.

5. May a Session use a judicial commission to adjudicate a judicial case, or must all judicial cases be heard by the entire Session?

**Clerk’s Response**: The primary, though not exclusive, section of the BCO relating to the issue of commissions is 15-1.

15-1. A commission differs from an ordinary committee in that while a committee is appointed to examine, consider and report, a commission is authorized to deliberate upon and conclude the business referred to it, except in the case of judicial commissions of a Presbytery appointed under BCO 15-3. A commission shall keep a full record of its proceedings, which shall be submitted, to the court appointing it. Upon such submission this record shall be entered on the minutes of the court appointing, except in the case of a presbytery commission serving as a session or a judicial commission as set forth in BCO 15-3. When a commission is appointed to serve as an interim Session, its actions are the actions of a Session, not a Presbytery. Every commission of a Presbytery or Session must submit complete minutes and a report of its activities at least once annually to the court which commissioned it.

My response is, yes; a Session may elect to use a judicial commission to adjudicate judicial cases. I answer in the affirmative for several reasons:

<a id="ga30-p349"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=351 printed_page=349 -->

Chapter fifteen of the BCO deals with “Ecclesiastical
Commissions” for all three levels of the courts of the church.
The Presbytery is explicitly named in 15-1, 15-2, and 15-3. The
General Assembly is explicitly named in 15-4, 15-5, and 16-6.
The Session is explicitly named in 15-1 in the last sentence. In
that reference, it is not a commission of presbytery acting as a
Session, but a commission of the Session. “Every commission
of a presbytery or session [emphasis added] must submit
complete minutes and a report of its activities at least once
annually to the court which commissioned it.”
The BCO is not exactly analogous to secular civil and criminal
law. The BCO is not written as an exhaustive catalog of most
possible eventualities, but is primarily a set of principles, with
deference to lower courts, and the expectation that courts will
use their discretion. Indeed, our constitution (The Westminster
standards and the Book of Church Order) is written in such a
way that it sets forth general principles, setting forth acceptable
parameters on many instances, and gives exacting details in a
few instances. For example, on the issue of baptism, WCF
XXVIII.7 states that, “Baptism is but once to be administered
unto any person.” Therefore, it would not be proper for a PCA
minister to rebaptize someone who had previously received a
valid Christian baptism. a church member might ask a PCA
pastor for a baptism by immersion because he or she had been
baptized by pouring and now wanted to be immersed. The PCA
pastor would refuse to rebaptize such a person. But what about
the validity of Roman Catholic baptisms? The issue has been
addressed by General Assemblies in America in the 18th, 19th,
and 20th centuries each coming to the same conclusion, that
some sessions may accept Roman Catholic baptisms and some
may reject Roman Catholic baptisms. WCF chapter II “Of God
and of the Holy Trinity” is very exacting, following the Ancient
Creeds; chapter III, “Of God’s Eternal Decree,” however, may
be interpreted to be infralapsarian, or suprarlapsarian, though
sublapsarianism is definitely excluded. The Directory of
Worship was originally written as a replacement for the Book of
Common Prayer. The BCP was prescribed worship, a
prescribed liturgy that is to be followed word-for-word, with
little variation allowed, with a premium set on uniformity; the
DW is directed worship, essentially setting forth a collection of
rubrics, or directions to be followed, allowing for variations,
with a premium set on liberty of conscience. The PCA has
taken it a step further by adding a preface to the DW, declaring
only chapters 56, 57, 58 to be part of the constitution. It has
been my observation over a period of years, that some

<a id="ga30-p350"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=352 printed_page=350 -->

erroneously look on our constitution in a quasi-congregational
perspective, thinking of it as simply advisory in all respects and
binding in no respects. It is also erroneous to look upon our
constitution as if it were canon law of an hierarchical Church or
a secular law code that is highly detailed. The insistence that a
Session may not erect a Judicial Commission is, in my opinion,
based on the latter erroneous perspective.
■ BCO 32-11 speaks of a “Judicial Committee”:
In every process, if deemed expedient there may be a
committee appointed, which shall be called the
Judicial Committee, and whose duty it shall be to
digest and arrange all the papers, and to prescribe,
under the direction of the court, the whole order of
the proceedings. The members of this committee
shall be entitled, notwithstanding their performance
of this duty, to sit and vote in the case as members of
the court.
That Judicial Committee of BCO 32-11 is not a commission.
The role of the Judicial Committee is explicitly stated. The
erection of a Judicial Committee by any level of the church
courts does not negate the right of the church court to form a
Judicial Commission, if it so desires.
Having given a rationale for the constitutional permissibility of the
use of a sessional judicial commission, it would be helpful to deal
with some objections.
■ It may be objected that only Presbytery and General Assembly
Judicial Commissions are explicitly named in the BCO. The
Presbytery is explicitly named in 15-1, 15-2, and 15-3. The
General Assembly is explicitly named in 15-4, 15-5, and 16-6.
The answer is that the Session having the power to erect
commissions is explicitly named in 15-1 in the last sentence.
In that reference, it is not a commission of Presbytery acting
as a Session, but a commission of the Session. A commission
may be erected by the Session to fulfill virtually any of the
responsibilities of the Session (see BCO 12-5) including, “To
inquire into the knowledge, principles and Christian conduct
of the church members, under its care; to censure those found
delinquent.”
■ It may be objected that, since there are no step-by-step
procedures for an appeal arising from a trial conducted by a
Judicial Commission of the Session, like there are for a
Presbytery Commission (15-3) or the General Assembly’s
Standing Judicial Commission (15-5, c.[4]), then no Sessional
Judicial Commission is possible. The answer is that BCO 15-

<a id="ga30-p351"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=353 printed_page=351 -->

1 gives the power to the Session to erect a judicial
commission. Exact detailed procedures for handling cases by
a Sessional Judicial Commission need not be included in the
BCO. Though appellate civil or criminal procedures are
codified in law, the BCO is not written as an exhaustive
catalog of most possible eventualities, but is primarily a set of
principles, with deference to lower courts, and the expectation
that courts will use their discretion. Even the General
Assembly’s Standing Judicial Commission specifies most of
the details of its procedures in the “Operating Manual of the
Standing Judicial Commission.” Some Presbyteries use the
“Operating Manual of the Standing Judicial Commission” as
guidelines for their procedures. It seems to me that a Session
could have similar appellate procedures, 1) the Sessional
Judicial Commission would make its report to the Session on
each given case committed to the Sessional Judicial
Commission, 2) the Session would then, without debate,
approve or disapprove the judgment, following the principles
of BCO 15-3, 3) if the Session disapproved the judgment, it
could appoint another commission to try the case, or the
Session could elect to try the case before the Session as a
whole, 4) or the Session could refer any strictly constitutional
issue(s) to a study committee to report back to the Session, 5)
a session that uses a sessional judicial Commission, may be
well advised to use the procedures of the Standing Judicial Commission, of a review of each Sessional Judicial
Commission’s decision, and/or a rehearing of the case by the
full Session (See section 18 of the “Operating Manual of the
Standing Judicial Commission”), 6) if the decision of the
Sessional Judicial Commission were averse to a church
member, and the Session approved the judgment, the church
member in question, could appeal directly to Presbytery,
following the procedures of BCO 42. A particular church
member’s appellate rights are not removed or diminished by
the use of a Sessional Judicial Commission as long as just
procedures are followed.
It may be objected that BCO 15-3 speaks of a Presbytery’s
Judicial Commission, but does not mention a Sessional
Judicial Commission. The answer is that this is an argument
from silence. BCO 15-3 is written for Presbytery use. The
Session is explicitly named in 15-1 in the last sentence. In that
reference, it is not a commission of presbytery acting as a
Session, but a commission of the Session. Though BCO 15-3
refers only to Presbytery, BCO 15-1 does include the Session
and gives Sessions the authority to erect many sorts of

<a id="ga30-p352"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=354 printed_page=352 -->

commissions, including judicial commissions authorized to
deliberate upon and conclude the business [judicial cases]
referred to them.
* It may be objected that BCO Chapter 30 refers to censures
inflicted by “church courts,” that a Sessional Judicial
Commission is not a court, and, therefore, a Session may not
utilize a Sessional Judicial Commission. The answer is that a
commission is authorized by the court appointing it to
deliberate and conclude the business referred to it. The
actions of a commission are the actions of the court itself.
■ It may be objected that the court of original jurisdiction of
members of particular churches is the Session (BCO 33-1, 33-
2) that a sessional judicial commission is not a court, and,
therefore, a session may not utilize a sessional judicial
Commission. The answer is that a commission is authorized
by the court appointing it to deliberate and conclude the
business referred to it. The actions of a commission are the
actions of the court itself. Again this goes back to BCO 15-1
and BCO 15-1 does include Session and gives Sessions the
authority to erect many sorts of commissions, including
judicial commissions authorized to deliberate upon and
conclude the business [judicial cases] referred to them.
■ It may be objected that the use of a Sessional Judicial
Commission is merely a pragmatic expedient, particularly in
larger churches that may have thousands of members and
scores of elders. That, of course is not a constitutional
argument; the issue is whether the use of a Sessional Judicial
Commission is constitutional. To assert that sheer pragmatism
and mere expediency lie at the root of the use of a Sessional
Judicial Commission is to question the motives of fellow
elders. It could be argued, on the other hand that larger
churches may either ignore church discipline due to the
magnitude of the task of trying dozens of cases before the full
Session, or they may use Sessional Judicial Commissions to
fulfill justly and responsibly the duty of church discipline.
Response - The CCB concurs with the advice of the Stated Clerk in
this matter.
6. maya presbytery conduct a presbytery meeting via teleconferencing?
Clerk’s Response: BCO 13-4 while speaking to the matter of a
quorum states, “Any three ministers belonging to the Presbytery,
together with at least three ruling elders, being met at the time and
place appointed, shall be a quorum competent to proceed to business.”

<a id="ga30-p353"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=355 printed_page=353 -->

“However, any Presbytery, by a majority vote of those present a ta
stated meeting, may fix its own quorum provided it is not smaller than
the quorum stated in this paragraph.”
Response: It is the opinion of the CCB that the Stated Clerk’s advice
correctly referenced BCO 13-4 parameters governing the meeting of a
presbytery. The CCB further concludes that until the BCO is amended
to reflect current technological opportunities, the present language in
BCO 13-4, which specifies that the presbytery meet at the “place
appointed” presents a constitutional bar to presbytery meeting via
teleconferencing. Additionally, the BCO 13-4 language requiring the
“vote of those present at a stated meeting” confirms the understanding
that the people must be present at the “place appointed.”
7. must a congregation approve a “severance package” to be given to a
minister whose pastoral call is about to be dissolved by Presbytery?
Clerk’s Response: No, a congregation need not approve a “severance
package” for a minister when the congregation votes to petition
Presbytery to dissolve a pastoral relationship. The congregation
approves the terms of the call initially (BCO 20-6), but the BCO does
not directly speak to the issue of a “severance package.” Therefore, it
would be a budgetary matter to be approved by the Session (BCO 12-5 b.).
Response - The CCB concurs with the advice of the Stated Clerk that
a congregation is not required to approve a severance package.
8. Must Sessional commissions have an equal number of Ruling Elders
and Teaching Elders?
Clerk’s Response: BCO 15-2 addresses the issue of Presbytery
commissions, which must have at least two Ruling Elders and two
Teaching Elders. The BCO does not directly address the matter of the
ratio of Ruling Elders and Teaching Elders on a sessional commission.
Therefore, it would be a matter to be determined by the Session. From
a practical standpoint, a presbytery would have a larger pool of elders
of both types from which to draw. A Session has fewer Teaching
Elders available to serve compared to the pool of Ruling Elders.
Response - The CCB concurs with the advice of the Stated Clerk in
this matter.
9. What is the status of Presbytery Standing Rules or By-laws and church
Standing Rules or By-laws in relation to the Book of Church Orderl
Clerk’s Response: as a matter of general principle, of Presbytery
Standing Rules or By-laws and church Standing Rules or By-laws,
may complement the Book of Church Order but may not contradict the

<a id="ga30-p354"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=356 printed_page=354 -->

Book of Church Order. In some instances allowable parameters for
variation from the BCO are set forth (ex. Quora BCO 12-1, 13-4).
However, a local church ora presbytery may not adopt procedures that
violate the rights of persons or entities as specified in the BCO. The
General Assembly operates with the constitution of the Church being
its primary authority, “The Rules of Assembly Operation” as a
secondary authority, and Rober/s Rules of Order as a tertiary
authority. In like manner, Presbytery Standing Rules or By-laws and
church Standing Rules or By-laws are a secondary authority to the
constitution of the Church.
Response - The CCB concurs with the advice of the Stated Clerk in
this matter.
10. must a session receive by transfer as a communicant member a young
child who has been received as a communicant member by another
PCA church Session?
Clerk’s Response: a session is not obligated to receive a young child
into communicant membership by means of transfer, even if the child
has been admitted into communicant membership by a previous PCA
church (jBCO 12-5 a.).
Response - The CCB concurs with the advice of the Stated Clerk in
this matter.
11. May the General Assembly require General Assembly Committees
and Agencies to take actions that are contrary to law?
(Westminster Presbytery has submitted an overture [Overture 7] which
may require some committees and agencies to open records of all
committees and agencies to any member of the General Assembly. It
is illegal to make certain records available without permission of the
individual who is the subject of the records, for example, academic
records. Personnel records, individual retirement account records may
be other instances).
Clerk’s Response: The General Assembly may not require its
Committees and Agencies to supply records to any member of the
General Assembly, if supplying such information is a violation of the
law.
Response: It is the opinion of the CCB that we concur with the advice
of the Stated Clerk in accordance with WCF 23-4.
12. In voting on proposed BCO amendments, should Presbyteries that are
being divided wait to vote on proposed BCO amendments until a new
Presbytery has been formed?

<a id="ga30-p355"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=357 printed_page=355 -->

Clerk’s Response: All commissioners to a specific church court have
the right to vote as long as they are commissioners to that court.
Basic fairness, however, would suggest that commissioners vote only
once on a constitutional issue. It would be wise and appropriate that
those who anticipate becoming members of Iowa Presbytery would
abstain from voting on proposed BCO amendments in Heartland
Presbytery and wait to cast their votes on proposed BCO amendments
until Iowa Presbytery is formed, at which time they would vote as
members of Iowa Presbytery.
Response - The CCB concurs with the advice of the Stated Clerk in
this matter.
Revision of Korean Translation of the BCO
The translation is not yet complete. Therefore there is no proposal before the
Assembly this year.
Presbytery Votes on the Book of Church Order Amendments
BCO 26-2 requires an affirmative vote of two-thirds of the Presbyteries as part
of the amendment process. The items carried over for last year, when there
were sixty Presbyteries, require an affirmative vote of forty Presbyteries. The
items originally sent down for vote by the Twenty-ninth General Assembly
require the affirmative vote of forty-two Presbyteries, since we now have
sixty-three Presbyteries.
Statistics
Not all churches submit annual reports. Some of the reports submitted are
incomplete. Therefore the following observations should be understood in
light of those limitations. Our total number of churches and mission churches
increased by forty; we now have 1, 498. Our total membership showed a
slight increase to 306,784. Our number of ministers increased by 102; we now
have 3,082. Taking into account ordained missionaries, ordained professors,
staff ministers, and ministers laboring out of bounds, we still have
considerably more ministers who wish to serve than opportunities for ministry.
Sunday School attendance was down to 116,402, consistent with trends in
other Churches. Though total reported local church disbursements were up to
$498,977,302 (almost a half billion dollars), two trends similar to other
churches are evident, 1) per capita benevolent giving was down slightly, and 2)
giving to General Assembly ministries was down slightly as well.

<a id="ga30-p356"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=358 printed_page=356 -->

BOARD OF DIRECTORS
Presbyterian Church in America
June 19, 2001
The Board of Directors of the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation)
held a regular meeting on June 19, 2001 at the Adam’s Mark Hotel in Dallas,
Texas. The meeting was called to order at 11:42 a.m. by Chairman of the
Board of Directors and President of the Presbyterian Church in America (A
Corporation) RE William H. Moore, IV. RE Dwight Allen led in the opening
prayer.
A quorum was declared present with the following in attendance:
TE William Fox, Jr., Fellowship RE Dwight Allen, CC
TE Robert S. Homick, Gulf Coast RE R. B. Gustafson, Jr., Grace
TE William C. Hughes, MS Valley RE Edwin Hackenberg, SE Louisiana
TE Richard Lindsay, RH RE William Moore, IV, Potomac
TE Curtis McDaniel, Nashville RE Kenneth Rush, Philadelphia
TE Michael Milton, C. Georgia
TE Michael Preg, MTW
RE John (Jack) Sullivan, CE&P
Absent: RE Charles Bobyack, TE Al Herrington, TE H. Andrew Silman, RE
William Joseph, Jr., RE Hal Shepherd
VISITORS:
TE Jimmy Lyons, MTW (retired)
RE David Edling, Peacemaker Ministries
TE Frank J. Smith, P&R News
STAFF:
TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk, Secretary and Treasurer
TE J. Robert Fiol, Assistant to the Stated Clerk
TE John W. Robertson, Business Administrator, Assistant Secretary and
Assistant Treasurer
Mrs. Laurel De Bert, Office Administrator/Event Planner, Assistant Secretary
and Assistant Treasurer
Mrs. Diane Hitzfeld, Office Manager
APPROVAL OF MINUTES:
The minutes of the March 16, 2001, BOARD OF DIRECTORS meeting were
approved as printed and distributed.

<a id="ga30-p357"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=359 printed_page=357 -->

BD-6/01-01 MSP that Mrs. Diane Hitzfeld, Office Manager, be designated
an Assistant Secretary and Assistant Treasurer of the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation).
BD-6/01-02 MSP that the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation)
amend its bylaws of Article VI, Section 4 with an additional sentence
to read: “In addition, the Ridge Haven Board may make request to
presbyteries to nominate specific men to the Board and may submit to
the Assembly Nominating Committee letters of recommendation
concerning particular nominees from the presbyteries.”
TE L. Roy Taylor reported that the lawsuit filed in the Commonwealth of
Virginia against the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) and
Mission to the World, Inc. has been re-instated.
There was a season of prayer.
The next meeting of the Board of Directors will be in Atlanta on October 5-6,
2001; permission was granted to change the dates of the meeting, if necessary.
The meeting was adjourned at 11:48 a.m. with prayer by TE Michael Preg.
Respectfully submitted,
/s/ RE William Moore, IV, President /s/ L. Roy Taylor, Secretary

BOARD OF DIRECTORS
Presbyterian Church in America
March 15, 2002
The Board of Directors of the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation)
held a regular meeting on March 15, 2002 at the PCA Office Building in
Lawrenceville, GA. The meeting was convened at 4:50 p.m. by Chairman of
the Board of Directors and President of the Presbyterian Church in America (A
Corporation) RE William H. Moore, IV. TE H. Andrew Silman opened with
prayer.
The following men were in attendance
RE Dwight Allen, CC RE William Joseph, Jr. SE Alabama
TE David Clelland, PCAF TE William Joseph, III, Warrior
TE Timothy Diehl, Iowa TE Curtis McDaniel, Nashville
TE William Fox, Jr., Fellowship RE William Moore, IV, Chesapeake
RE R. B. Gustafson, Jr., Grace TE Michael Preg, MTW
RE Edwin Hackenberg, SE Louisiana TE H. Andrew Silman, CTS
RE Tom Harris, IAR RE John (Jack) Sullivan, CE&P
RE Pat Hodge, Calvary
The following Board members were excused: TE Michael Milton, RE John
Jardine, TE Al Herrington, TE W. Wilson Benton, TE Robert S. Hornick, RE

approved as printed and distributed.
BD-3/02-1 MSP that the minutes reflect that the following annual
corporate filings have been accomplished in a timely manner in all states
where the corporation is registered to conduct business. The Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) in the state of Delaware is currently

<a id="ga30-p361"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=363 printed_page=361 -->

registered as a foreign corporation in Georgia, Missouri and Mississippi. The
annual registrations in Delaware, Georgia and Missouri have been completed.
BD-3/02-2 MSP that the minutes reflect, as aboard of Directors, that the
annual RPCES corporate filings have been accomplished in a timely manner
where required:

Presbyterian Missions, Inc; Christian Training, Inc.
Michigan Corporation: Board of Home Ministries
Pennsylvania Corporation: Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod
The Board received the following update from the Secretary on two
outstanding lawsuits:
At the March 2000 Board of Directors’ meeting I informed
you of a suit that was filed in the Commonwealth of Virginia, Andrew
S. Robinson v. the Presbyterian Church in America, et al. (Case No.:
CLO1-493). We received a letter from Mr. Mark Nanavati, attorney
for Guide One Insurance Company dated February 13, 2002, along
with a copy of the settlement drafts, release and dismissal order. The
settlement was $16,000, Guide One Insurance to pay $4,000 and
Church Mutual Insurance to pay $12,000. The case is now closed.
The suit filed as a result of the breaking of the 29th General Assembly contract by the DFW Hyatt Hotel: PCA v. Hyatt Hotels is
currently in arbitration. John Robertson and I are scheduled to meet
with the parties concerned in Dallas, March 22, 2002.
BD-3/02-3 MSP that the Administrative Committee recommend to the
General Assembly that it make necessary changes to the PCA
Corporate Bylaws to reflect the new status of Reformed University
Ministries as a Permanent Committee of the Presbyterian Church in America:
A. Corporate Bylaws, Article V. — insert "E. The Committee on
Reformed University Ministries" — "The affairs of the Church
in the area of campus ministries on college and university
campuses are assigned to the Committee on Reformed
University Ministries, whose duties and authority shall be
designated by the General Assembly."
B. Corporate Bylaws, Article V. strike “E.” in “E. Other
Committees” and insert “F.”
C. Corporate Bylaws, Article V. strike “F.” in “F. Boards of
Agencies” and insert “G.”

<a id="ga30-p362"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=364 printed_page=362 -->

BD-3/02-4 MSP that the Administrative Committee recommend to the
General Assembly that it make necessary changes to the PCA
Corporate Bylaws Article VI. 1., paragraph 3, line 2 to reflect the
incorporation and name change of Insurance, Annuities and Relief to
PCA Retirement and Benefits, Inc.:
replace the words “Insurance, Annuities and Relief’ with the
words “PCA Retirement and Benefits, Inc.”
BD-3/02-5 In order to correct an omission in the transference of a March
2, 2001 change recommended by the PCA Foundation, MSP that the
words, “and deacons” be added to Article VI.5, so that the last
sentence reads:
All of the members of the Board of Directors shall be either
Teaching Elders, Ruling Elders or Deacons, and at least one-half
of such members shall be Ruling Elders and Deacons.
BD-3/02-6 MSP that the Secretary/Treasurer, L. Roy Taylor, and
Assistant Secretary/Treasurer, John Robertson, be authorized to negotiate and
finally accept all terms in resolution of the pending litigation with the DFW
Hyatt Hotel.

2002 at Briarwood Presbyterian Church in Birmingham, AL, in conjunction
with the 30th General Assembly.
