<a id="ga30-p403"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=405 printed_page=403 -->

THE FRAMEWORK OF STRATEGIC DIRECTION
OF THE PCA

Under the kind hand of God’s blessing, the Presbyterian Church in America
plans to move into the future with a clear sense of (1) identity, (2) mission, (3)
values, (4) vision, and (5) strategic priorities.
1. The PCA Identity
The Presbyterian Church in America is a covenant community of churches
committed to:
• Biblical inerrancy and authority. The denomination’s foundational
commitment is to the Bible as the inspired Word of God, the only infallible
rule of faith and practice.
• A reformed-covenantal theology. The denomination’s office-bearers
subscribe in good faith to The Westminster Standards.
• Mutual accountability. The denomination follows “grass-roots”
Presbyterian church government and Biblical church discipline, as
reflected in the Book of Church Order.
• Cooperative ministry. The denomination seeks to accomplish more
together than the separate units of the church could accomplish on their
own (local church, presbytery, General Assembly, networks, and like-
minded brothers and sisters in the Church of Jesus Christ world-wide).
2. The PCA Mission
The Presbyterian Church in America was founded in the crucible of the battle
over the authority and inerrancy of the Bible. The denomination’s mission for
the future is built on previous statements approved by the General Assembly. 1

1 The First General Assembly in 1973 adopted “An address to all Churches throughout
the World from the General Assembly.” in that document the purpose of the church
was set forth, “We declare that the ultimate purpose of the church is to glorify God.
We believe that this is done by our obedience to the Great Commission. In this
connection we affirm the same position as that taken by the founding fathers of our
former church regarding the mission of the Church.” The Second General Assembly
adopted the introduction to the report of the committee on christian education and
Publications (M2GA, p. 149) and the Introduction to the report of the Committee on
Mission to the United States (M2GA, pp. 157-158) as further statements on the
mission of the church. The Tenth General Assembly in 1982, upon recommendation
of the Committee on Mission to the United States, adopted the following purpose

<a id="ga30-p404"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=406 printed_page=404 -->

The Mission Statement: The mission of the Presbyterian Church in America is to glorify and enjoy God by equipping and enabling the
churches of the PCA to work together to fulfill the Great Commission by
making disciples of all nations, so that people will mature as servants of the
triune God, will worship God in spirit and truth, and will have a reforming
impact on culture.
This mission statement implies that the Presbyterian Church in America will be
a covenant community of churches that have the following attributes:
1. Worshipping churches, where elders proclaim, teach, and apply the
Word of God to all areas of life and the congregations worships God in
spirit and in truth.
2. Equipping and enabling churches, where members practice love for
God, for fellow Christians, and for all people and where there is
genuine fellowship and community.
3. Discipling churches, where Christians seek the truth while depending
on the grace of God, develop godly leaders for future generations, plant
churches, and minister to a broad range of peoples throughout the
world.
4. Reforming churches, where the denomination reflects the many ethnic
peoples of North America and displays the compassion of the Lord
Jesus Christ in various ministries of mercy, and where members
transform the culture in which they work and live.
3. The PCA Values
The PCA as a denomination and among its member churches will pursue
all the following defining values:
Faithfulness to the Holy Scriptures as the inspired, inerrant Word of
God.
Fidelity to the reformed faith as the system of doctrine most consistent
with the Word of God.
Obedience to fulfilling the Great Commission.

statement: “It is the purpose of the PCA to bring glory to God as a worshipping and
serving community until the nations in which we live are filled with churches that
make Jesus Christ and His Word their chief joy, and the nations of the world, hearing
the Word are discipled in obedience to the Great Commission.”

<a id="ga30-p405"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=407 printed_page=405 -->

Worship that is God-centered, Biblically based, participatory,
historically informed, and culturally appropriate.
Love to the one true and living God, to fellow Christians, and to our
unbelieving neighbors.
Accountability to one another privately and through the Church
corporately for personal holiness and gospel witness.
Cooperation by engaging in ministry together as local churches,
presbyteries and as a denomination, and with like-minded churches in
North America and the world.
Prayer that fulfills biblical models and instructions.
4. The PCA Vision
The Third Millennium has dawned full of crisis, fear and uncertainty.
The PCA must urgently and effectively proclaim to the world the message
that Jesus Christ is the only Savior and Lord. By God’s grace and for His
glory, the PCA seeks to be a denomination of churches transforming the
lives of people who transform cultures.
The PCA, through the General Assembly, its Committees, Agencies and
Presbyteries, should guide, connect and support local churches and
presbyteries so that they work together to fulfill our mission statement.
To guide means, in this context, to identify opportunities, common goals, and
values for each local church and presbytery.
To connect means to facilitate the complementary roles of each local church
and presbytery.
To support means to provide or suggest resources and expertise to assist each
local church and presbytery.
To work together means to create purposeful, unified, and effective efforts
among the local churches and presbyteries.
As a result, the PCA will become a renewed denomination committed to
revival and reformation in North America and the world.

<a id="ga30-p406"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=408 printed_page=406 -->

A succinct motto for this vision is “Being revived and bringing
reformation."
The vision implies that the PCA will strive to be a denomination full of healthy
churches glorifying and enjoying God, working together as the Body of Christ
to fulfill the Great Commission, and as a result through these churches,
cultures and society will be more greatly affected and directed toward
worshiping the Creator rather than the creation.

5. The PCA Strategic Priorities
Out of its unwavering commitment to the inerrancy of Scripture and the
reformed faith, and in obedience to the Great Commission, the PCA will
pursue the following four strategic priorities to accomplish the vision. The
bullet items that follow are offered as examples of how each strategic priority
might be addressed.
1. Empower church health and growth for new and existing churches, local
and worldwide
• Accountability and discipline
• Biblical understanding of the Gospel, conversion, discipleship,
membership in the Body of Christ, and worship
• Christian Education - including focus on emerging generations
• Communication of a Biblical world and life view
• Enabling women to fulfill their Biblical ministry
• Encouraging outward concern - evangelism, church planting and
missions
• Facilitating discipleship
• God-centered and Gospel-driven worship
• Increasing racial and ethnic diversity
• Passionate, vital preaching and teaching
• Prayer becoming a central activity of the church
• Regular observance of the Sacraments according to Biblical standards
• Stewardship education
• Unconditional love and full forgiveness through God’s grace
2. Develop leadership for the future
• Furnishing methods of preventing teaching elder burnout and
increasing pastoral effectiveness through non-formal education,
support, and life-long learning
• Greater access for ordained leadership of ethnic minorities

<a id="ga30-p407"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=409 printed_page=407 -->

- Identification, motivation, and training lay leaders, especially elders and deacons, and development of structures that provide opportunities for service
- Preparing future denominational leaders
- Providing methods to identify individuals with potential leadership
- Providing spiritual resources and training for local churches
- Reaching people in their 20’s and 30’s
- Revitalization ministry—for example, pairing experienced pastors with inexperienced pastors
- Youth ministry and Christian education track in seminaries

3. Increase denominational understanding and effectiveness
- Articulation of a compelling church planting vision
- Communicating the history and value of the denomination
- Developing loyalty to and identification with the denomination
- Effective cooperation and communication among General Assembly committees and agencies in accomplishing the Mission of the PCA.
- Encouraging increased participation in world missions
- Establishing a denominational magazine
- Furnishing opportunities for spiritual growth and fellowship
- Improving funding methods for committees and agencies
- Increasing active participation of ruling elders in presbytery and General Assembly
- Make all church courts more edifying, encouraging, and effective by such means as conducting licensure examinations primarily in committee.
- Providing more time and opportunity to focus on significant issues affecting the future of the PCA.
- Explore ideas for re-engineering and re-energizing so that ruling elders are better prepared and equipped to participate in discussions.
- Pursue means of facilitating more informed debate on issues.
- Providing safe forums for discussion of theological issues
- Sharing models of effective Kingdom work between presbyteries
- Structuring meeting time according to proper priorities, such as
- Prayer
- Church planting
- Missions
- Leadership development

4. Engage the culture – timeless truth for our times
- Application of Biblical truths to citizenship responsibilities

<a id="ga30-p408"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=410 printed_page=408 -->

• Creating doorways in the church to welcome all kinds of people, so
that the face of the church will more closely mirror the face of the
culture
• Creating outward focused events that engage non-Christians in their
own environment
• Developing specific strategies for multi-cultural churches ~ especially
Hispanic, African-American, and Asian churches.
• Distinguishing the PCA approach to cultural engagement from current,
evangelical para-church expressions by being Gospel-centered and
teaching the whole counsel of God, encouraging members to be
involved in the community, local ministries, education, government,
public forums, media, and the arts
• Furnishing apologetic materials that address the main trends in the
culture, science, and education.
• Inculcating Biblical values in family life, vocation, activities, and
recreation
• Offering training in thinking with a Biblical world view
• Promote Biblical conflict resolution
• Providing Biblical responses to women’s issues, gender debates, and
the cultural definitions of the family
• Summoning members to public service and ministries of mercy

<a id="ga30-p409"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=411 printed_page=409 -->

ATTACHMENT F
REPORT OF THE
PCA HISTORICAL CENTER
TO THE
ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE
Our Mission: Since its establishment in 1985, the PCA Historical Center has
sought to preserve and promote the record of what God has done and is doing
through the people of the PCA. We do this by:
• Preserving the official records of the PCA, its agencies and
committees.
• Collecting the personal papers of key individuals in the PCA.
• Promoting the writing of church histories by our local congregations.
• Working to develop in the people a deeper appreciation of the value of
our legacy.
The Historical Center houses the records of four conservative Presbyterian
denominations (PCA, RPCES, BPC and RPC,GS) and the manuscript
collections of over fifty individuals connected with these church bodies. But
we need to continue to develop this collection, in order to preserve the whole
story. We especially need to gather the papers of the “church fathers” while
there is still time. For this, the Historical Center needs everyone’s help—we
need you to encourage the donation of materials and we may even need your
physical assistance in gathering these donations. In short, we need people in
every presbytery to serve as volunteers in this work of preserving our history.
Facilities Development: One of the greatest needs still facing the Historical
Center is the purchase of shelving, to properly house the many boxes in our
collection. Three ranges (i.e., a row) of shelving have been purchased and
installed, but another seventeen still need to be purchased in order to complete
the installation. We would particularly appeal to individual churches to
consider sponsoring the purchase of a shelving range, at a cost of $2500.00 per
range.
Progress in the work of the Historical Center during the last year can be
enumerated in the following categories:
Accessions for 2001
1. Sixteen new collections or collection-additions were received in 2001.
2. 12 rolls of microfilm were added, comprising primarily PCUS Presbytery
records, and at no cost to the Center.

<a id="ga30-p410"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=412 printed_page=410 -->

3. 137 new book titles were added to the Center’s research library, with a
large percentage of these being donated works. A searchable database catalog
of the Center’s books and pamphlets is currently being compiled and this will
be added to the Center’s web site later this year.
Patronage levels for 2001
1. Over 400 people walked through the doors of the Historical Center this past
year. Most received tours of the facility, in addition to whatever reason may
have brought them in. A guest book, not used since 1989, was uncovered
during the move into the new facility and this was put back into service.
2. 325 email posts from 194 different patrons, covering such topics as
document requests, genealogy, polity, books in print and out of print, local
church history, denominational histories, etc. Requests came in from Wales,
South Africa, Australia and (early in January) Mongolia!
3. The Center’s web site (http://www.pcanet.org/history) had an increase in
traffic of over 300% for the year, going from an average of 42 visitor sessions
per day, to over 140 sessions per day. Hits per day went from under 200 to
over 800. By year’s end, the web site was being visited over 4,000 times per
month, with a total of over 22,000 hits per month. In annual terms, we had
30,000 visitors and 190,000 hits at the web site. On average the Center’s site
was the 9th most trafficked of the PCA sites.
Publications & Services:
1. A new brochure, Using the Archive: An Introduction to Its Resources, has
been sent to the printer. A Press Printing Company of Greenville, SC has
kindly offered to print this title at no cost to the Historical Center.
2. Two other publications are currently being revised and should be reprinted
later in 2002:

- **a.** The Historical Center’s main or promotional “overview” brochure
- **b.** An Historian’s Handbook (the Historical Center’s largest title)

3. Copies of PCA Minutes (and some RPCES & BPC Minutes) were placed in
the libraries of Union Theological Seminary (NY); Biblical Seminary
(Hatfield, PA); Reformed Theological Seminary at Jackson, MS; Christian
Theological Seminary, Indianapolis, IN; and several others.
Equipment and Furnishings:
1. Four five-drawer map files were donated through Covenant Seminary by a
St. Louis business. The acquisition of these files will allow the Center to
expand its services to PCA churches as we seek to help them preserve their
architectural records.
2. Two two-drawer lateral file cabinets were donated to the Center by Evelyn
MacNair.

<a id="ga30-p411"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=413 printed_page=411 -->

3. Seventeen linear feet of wall-hung cabinetry was installed in the workprocessing area of the Stacks Room. The cost of this cabinetry, including
installation, was just over $1100.00.
4. An hexagonal oak display case was donated to the Center by Covenant
Seminary. Currently it houses a silver bowl formerly used as an annual CTS
Faculty Award.
Volunteer Help:
1. Florence Graham, who has served as a volunteer in the Historical Center
since its inception, has continued to serve the Center this past year, though less
frequently than in years past. A letter to the St. Louis area PCA churches will
soon be sent out soliciting additional volunteers.
2. I have taken several opportunities to enlist CTS student volunteers. Masaji
Nakayama has been working in the Center, providing basic bibliographic
information in English for the many Japanese language books and periodicals
which we have from the Mcllwaine, McAlpine and Young collections. A
handful of other students have expressed serious interest in working here.
While we have no budget to pay them, they can earn up to half of their
required field education credits by working in the Center. Another CTS
student, a computer programmer, has indicated a willingness to assist in
developing some databases for addition to the web site.
Historical Center Subcommittee: The Historical Center Subcommittee
continues to faithfully provide guidance and oversight for the operations of the
Center. It is a distinct honor to be able to work with these men and women of
God. Members of the Subcommittee are:
Dr. Will Barker Miss Lannae Graham
Dr. David Calhoun Mr. Edward S. Harris
RE David Cooper Dr. Henry Lewis Smith
Mrs. Shirley Duncan Mr. John Spencer
Dr. L. Roy Taylor and TE John Robertson are ex-officio members of this
committee.
Respectfully submitted,
/s/ RE Wayne Sparkman, M.A.R., M.Div.
Director, PCA Historical Center

<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=414 printed_page=412 -->

(1) The Partnership Share is that portion of the approved expense budget which is
dependent on contributions from the PCA churches and individuals. When the
contributions are less than the amount of the partnerships hare , the Committees.
Aeencies and Institutions cannot fulfill the programs and services which the General Assembly approved at the June 2002 meeting.
(2) The PCA Office Building budget is not included in the Partnership Share. Gifts for
the Office Building Fund are over and above the Partnership Share, and benefit all of
the Committees and Agencies in Atlanta.
(3) The Mission to the World Partnership Share includes the consolidated portion of the
MTW budget identified as Unrestricted or General Fund. It does not include any items
which are considered Restricted or “donor designated” funds, including, but not limited
to, such items as: missionary support, program ministries and project budgets, etc.
(4) These two Institutions are on a July 1 through June 30 fiscal year.
(5) Covenant College will ask $ 10/member for its Church Scholarship Promise Program for
2003.
NOTE: Insurance, Annuities and Relief, and the PCA Foundation, do not share in the
Partnership Shares program.

ATTACHMENT H
ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE
PROPOSED BUDGET 2003
BUDGET PHILOSOPHY
The budget is built primarily on the job description of the Stated Clerk in the
RAO, which determines the services that are to be provided by the Office of
the Stated Clerk to churches, presbyteries, committees and agencies, and to the
General Assembly.
GENERAL COMMENTS
Many of the activities and responsibilities of the Administrative Committee are
directly affected by the size and growth of the PCA, which in turn are reflected
in annual budget increases for many line items. The economic inflation rate
also affects most budget items and is applied to most budget line items, except
as noted in the Economic Assumptions shown below.
The budgets are presented in a format to comply with the standards for not-forprofit organizations adopted by the Financial Accounting Standards (FASB).
The FASB standards provide a definition of “supporting activities” which they
call “management and general”. Therefore, compensation for the Stated Clerk
and his staff is allocated according to the estimated time spent by each person
in “program,” administration, and fund raising areas.
2002 Economic Assumptions
A. Stated Clerk/Administration
3.0% PCA Growth Rate
2.9% Atlanta Consumer Price Index (CPI) and inflation rate
2.8% National
10.0% Health Insurance Premiums (Per IAR)
-3.7% Transportation (as of 11/31/01)
The full time equivalent (FTE) employees budgeted at the beginning and end
of the year will be 12.00.
B. PCA Office Building
0% Rent remains at $10.00 per square foot for 2003
2.8% Consumer Price Index (CPI) and inflation rate
The full time equivalent (FTE) employees budgeted at the beginning and end
of the year will be 0.5.

CHRISTIAN EDUCATION AND PUBLICATIONS
2003 PROPOSED BUDGET

Philosophy:
CE&P's ministry program is driven by the Strategic Faith Plan. The plan
defines CE&P's mission, goals, strategies and programs. The proposed budget
is a "program-based" budget and represents the resources needed to adequately
fund the planned activities, which have been approved and/or directed by the
Permanent Committee and the General Assembly.
Summary:
The proposed 2003 expense budget represents a total increase of $102,039 or
3.76% from the 2002 budget. CE&P's contribution need only increases by
$49,039 or 2.91%. These increases are detailed and explained below.
Assumptions:
Underlying assumptions include: 1) the PCA will grow between 3% and 5%;
2) the consumer price index or inflation rate will be approximately 3%; 3)
Occupancy cost will be no higher than $11 per square foot; and 4) health
insurance premiums are expected to increase 15%. 5) Due to CE&P
reclassifying one full-time employee to a contract consultant, CE&P’s full time
equivalent (FTE) employee count drops to 23.5 from 24.5 in the 2002 budget.
Notes:
• Other Revenues (page 2, line 2) consists of book sales, conference fees,
membership fees, subscriptions, advertising and reimbursements for
postage and other services. The increase is due primarily to the
anticipated registrations for the conference on mercy ministry, which will
be held in March 2003 offset by a decrease in subscription income for
Equip for Ministry.
• Seminars, Conferences and Consulting (page 2, line 3) is estimated to
decrease, not because of a decrease in activity, but rather due to the
reassignment of CE&P personnel to oversight of the “Resource” side of
CE&P’s ministry (included in increases to line 9 and 10). Along with
CE&P’s normal training schedule, the conference on mercy ministry is
expected to attract 800 participants, which will account for over
$175,000 of expenses. These expenses are also represented in Travel,
Facilities and Events, and Honorariums (page 1, lines 20, 29, 31).

<a id="ga30-p423"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=423 printed_page=null -->

Also, CE&P’s Regional Teacher Trainers continue to be called into more
churches for seminars and consultations.
Women in the Church (page 2, line 4) will remain essentially
unchanged due to the fact that CE&P increased the budget for this
ministry in 2002 when eight new trainers were selected for a new
training program for use in local churches and presbyteries.
CE&P continues, in a limited way, to help local churches that request
assistance in developing Men's Ministries (page 2, line 5). Currently,
most of CE&P’s ministry to men occurs through officer training events
and consultation.
Multi-cultural ministries (page 2, line 6) remains the same as the 2002
budget as CE&P believes it would be most effective to contract with
various consultants who have expertise working with different ethnic
groups as need arises.
The slight increase in Youth Ministries (page 2, line 7) represents
additional costs of the facilities for the two Presbyterian Youth of
America (PYA) camps in the summer of 2003. These expenses are also
represented in Travel, Facilities and Events, and Honorariums (page
1, lines 20, 29,31).
While CE&P has always provided training to those who work with
children, CE&P is committed to establishing a Family and Children's
Ministries department (page 2, line 8) with a full time staff person to
assist PCA churches in training workers, staffing positions and
identifying resources available to them. In 2001, CE&P hosted a
conference for children's workers and retained an ongoing contract
consultant.
Publications and Curriculum (page 2, line 9) includes the periodicals
Equip for Ministry and the Bulletin Supplement and a portion of the WIC
Resource Letter. It also includes the costs associated with developing
and producing other annual materials for Stewardship season, PCA Week
of Prayer and Christian Education Sunday as well as other video series
and training manuals. The planned increase is due to: 1) CE&P’s desire
to increase the circulation of Equip for Ministry, 2) plans to enhance the
resources available on the CE&P web site and 3) the aforementioned
reassignment of CE&P personnel from “Training” to oversee these
activities.
The Bookstore (page 2, line 10) is the largest portion of the proposed
budget. The increase of the Bookstore budget represents staff

<a id="ga30-p422"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=424 printed_page=422 -->

reassignment as well as additional resources needed to further enhance
the Bookstore’s product offerings, services and related promotion.
• The small increase in Management and General (page 2, line 12) is due
to higher liability insurance premiums and other essential office support
functions.
• Fund Raising (page 2, line 15) represents the costs associated with
contacting churches, presbyteries and individuals and informing them
about the ministry of CE&P and their potential role in a giving toward
the partnership share. This budgeted amount includes one full time
equivalent staff person as well as two part-time regional representatives,
travel expenses and mailing costs. This amount also includes the costs
associated with production of the Love Gift video which will be used to
raise support for Children’s Ministry.
• Though CE&P has been relieved of the responsibility of funding a
portion of the PCA Foundation (page 2, line 16 and page 1, line 25),
CE&P’s management desires to cooperatively develop long-term giving
through this agency.
• The Coordinator, his assistant and related expenses are allocated to the
various expense categories as follows: Training 25%, Fund Raising 10%,
Administration 10%, Bookstore 15%, WIC 10%, Youth Ministries 10%,
Publications and Curriculum 20%.

COVENANT COLLEGE
GENERAL NOTES TO ACCOMPANY
2002-2003 PROPOSED BUDGET
The college uses the NACUBO (National Association of College and University
Business Officers) definitions of revenue and expense categories. This insures
that the college will be able to directly compare various ratios with other
colleges and assess our effectiveness in accordance with our assessment systems.
While the categories do not exactly parallel the definitions used by the
Accounting and Financial Reporting Guide for Christian Ministries, there is
some similarity. NACUBO categories including Instructional, Academic
Support, Library, Student Services, Public Service and Student Aid could be
broadly considered “Program Services.” Maintenance of Plant and Institutional
Support could be considered “Supporting Activities.”
Covenant accounts for depreciation as an operating expense. The college does
so fora variety of reasons. First, generally accepted accounting principles
require depreciation to be accounted for as an operating expense and these rules
must be followed to obtain an external audit in good standing. Also, funding
depreciation is a good thing for an organization as it provides funds to repair and
replace facilities as they age without additional fund raising. Finally, the
accrediting body in which Covenant participates requires a balanced bottom line
for continued accreditation, including depreciation. For these three reasons, the
college must actually fund the depreciation expense from increased revenues.
Due to this change, tuition has risen over the last several years more quickly than
otherwise would have been the case and will continue to do so until depreciation
is fully funded in the operating budget. These tuition increases will fund
depreciation, allow Covenant to show a net positive bottom line and reduce
pressure on future capital campaigns by providing funds for facility and
equipment replacement and repair. This expense is shown on the expense
spreadsheet divided between Maintenance of Plant Depreciation, representing
the depreciation costs of the facilities used for educational purposes, and
Auxiliary Depreciation, representing the depreciation costs of the residence
halls, food service and for-profit facilities. The 2002-2003 budget as proposed
funds $1,250,000 of the total depreciation expense of approximately $1,750,000.
Covenant accounts for capital gifts as revenue in the year an unconditional
pledge is made, as accounting rules dictate. Capital expenses are accounted for
through depreciation expense over time. This means capital projects tend to
positively overstate the operating results of the college. In order to show the
results more clearly, the operating net results for the college are shown on the
expense budget at the bottom of the page on the line titled “Increase (Decrease)
in Undesignated Net Assets.” This line will show a negative figure until the
depreciation expense is fully funded through the tuition increases mentioned in
the previous paragraph.

COVENANT THEOLOGICAL SEMINARY
NOTES

Comments about the proposed budget:
1. Overall, the budget increase is 2.7%. This is a very tight budget, with
staff and faculty receiving just a 2% increase.
2. The revenues from tuition reflect a large increase when compared to
this year’s budget; however, our actual experience for this year has been much
better than we expected.
3. In 1999-00, our request for Partnership Shares included funds that
must be raised for the Francis Schaeffer Institute. We did not included that
request the next two years, but are adding back that request this year, since we
are convinced that FSI is a regular ministry of the seminary that greatly
benefits the church and is the way that we raise staff and faculty salaries for
the seminary.
4. The decrease in budgeted expenses for Instructional activities is due to
a down-sizing of the Youth in Ministry Institute, which was planned for in its
original budget, and is reasonable now that the start-up is complete.
5. The large increase for Physical Plant reflects much higher insurance
costs. Our broker has warned us that our renewal in July may show an
increase as large as 85%. Because of our location, earthquake coverage is
expected to be very expensive.

INSURANCE, ANNUITIES AND RELIEF
PROPOSED BUDGET

Statement of Purpose
The purpose of the Board of Trustees of Insurance, Annuity and Relief Funds
of the Presbyterian Church in America is to glorify God through serving His
servants who minister in the Church by offering financial products to provide
for the health and welfare, retirement and relief needs of the denomination’s
ministers, missionaries, lay church workers and their families, and to serve the
churches, committees and agencies of the PCA and related organizations by
sponsoring and endorsing a denominational property and liability insurance
program.
• This budget reflects the costs incurred to administer the trust
funds for Insurance, Retirement, and Relief. This budget does
not reflect the financial activity in those trust funds. (Complete
financial activity in the trust funds may be found in the IAR
Annual Report, which includes audited financial statements.)
• The 2003 budget reflects a minimal increase of $2,410 over
the 2002 budget. 2001 actuals are unaudited.
• The increase in the Retirement and Relief portions and the
decrease in the Insurance portion of Support and Revenue is a
more accurate representation of the allocation of expenses
between supporting activities.
• The total number of staff budgeted for in 2003 is 11 FTE, an
increase of one FTE over 2002. Currently, ten of these
positions are filled.
• The 2002 budget included transition costs for a new director
and new Relief representative. The new director will be in
place in 2002 but the Relief representative retirement date has
been postponed from the original projected date of December
2002 .
• Because the Relief Representative may retire at the end of
2003, we have budgeted salary and benefits for a new Relief
Representative at five months overlap.

<a id="ga30-p433"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=435 printed_page=433 -->

• An overall net increase of 11.1% in salaries and benefits is
assumed in 2003. Budgeted positions include a 4% average
salary increase and a 10% health insurance increase. The
11.1% increase does not take into account the 2002 Director
transition nor the 2003 Relief transition positions.
• Occupancy costs for the shared facility are expected to remain
stable through 2003 so no increase is anticipated.

MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA
PROPOSED BUDGET
2003
The MNA Mission:
In obedience to and in dependence upon our sovereign God, the purpose of
MNA is to enable PCA churches and presbyteries to start and develop healthy,
reproducing churches in North America by providing vision communication,
resource development, strategic planning, training, support services, and
special ministries, so that a movement of multiplying reformed churches is
initiated and sustained.
♦ Due to the transition of Reformed University Ministries to separate
committee status during 2001, the budget for 2001 is being presented
without the revenue and expense associated with campus ministries.
♦ While the total revenue and expenses will increase 4.2% compared to the
2002 budget, the portion included in the “partnership share” will increase
only by 3.5%.
♦ An overall net increase of 5% in salaries and benefits is assumed. That is
an aggregate of cost of living, merit increases and health insurance costs.
♦ The total number of multicultural missionaries budgeted for is four.
Between 59 and 78 projects are budgeted to receive direct support in
church planting, multicultural church planting and urban and mercy
ministries.
♦ The total number of full-time equivalent staff budgeted for in the 2003
budget is 22, which is one more than the 2002 budget. Twenty of these
positions are currently filled. The unfilled positions are for a recruitment
coordinator and an administrative assistant for church planting.
♦ The cost being charged by the Administrative Committee for office space
is assumed to beat $10 per square foot.

<a id="ga30-p441"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=443 printed_page=441 -->

MISSION TO THE WORLD
PROPOSED CONSOLIDATED 2003 BUDGET
I. Methodology and Background:
The 2003 budget is proposed from an analysis of key factors that influence the
income and expenses of Mission to the World operating in a global context with
a rapidly changing global economy. We reviewed the results of 2001 and
extended these indicators into 2002 and 2003.
The year 2001 saw the US dollar remain relatively strong through out the year
gaining slightly against the Euro and even move against the Yen. For mission
work this can sometimes be helpful. Our overseas missionary expenses in US
dollars ended the year at approximately ninety six percent of budget, which
included some expansion to program content. However, we saw a faltering us
economy especially after September 11th resulting in a loss in the value of the
stock market fora second year ina row. The economic patterns following
September 11th had little impact on monthly contributions from churches but we
saw a nine percent drop in the large year-end giving to our missionaries and
programs. We continued to see additional gifts and trusts coming into the
endowment fund. The total income for the year was just over one hundred one
percent of budget.
Remembering that the entire program of Mission to the World is by the grace of
God, we want to give God praise for a very positive year, which saw a growing
number of home churches and an increasing force of missionaries in the midst of
the cooling US economy and a weak stock market.
II. Future Directions for 2003:
There are several major goals coming from MTW’s strategic direction that will
be part of the 2003 program. The Global Training and Development (GTD)
team, under the direction of Bill Goodman, implemented several new training
programs in 2002 and we plan to continue to expand in 2003. The goals of the
GTD team are to actively support and involve our missionaries and national
church planters in proven methods and best practices in advancing churchplanting movements.
Several new ministry sites were approved in 2002 and more sites are a part of
the growth and expansion plans for 2003. We plan to open church planting sites
in all five of our international regions, as well as initiate several new medical and
street children programs. We experienced a significant net growth in the number
of long-term missionaries in 2001 and we hoping that number will continue to
increase in 2002 and 2003. We also have continued growth goals for the

<a id="ga30-p442"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=444 printed_page=442 -->

number of short-term workers (two-year, interns and two-week church groups)
for 2003.
We have begun to see church partnership models begin to materialize for church
planting ministry around the globe. Our new focus on Muslim ministry,
Enterprise, is planning to use this as their primary model for new ministry
initiatives. We believe we will see various models of church partnerships in the
coming years as we more proactively engage local churches in focused church
planting ventures.
Plans in the home office for 2003 are to strengthen ongoing support of the
missionaries and churches we serve. With the expansion of church partnerships
we anticipate additional staffing needs to facilitate those efforts. We also
anticipate an increase in our additional staffing needs with the further
development of our Living in Grace offerings both overseas with our
missionaries and national partners, as well as with PCA churches here in the
U.S. Other increases in home office staff in 2003 are for short-term programs
that are projected to expand and general administrative growth to keep pace with
the ministry growth.
Major development efforts will continue to focus on raising endowment funds
that will go to reduce the administrative factor and to fund major new programs.
Our partner relations teams have also set goals to continue to strengthen
relationships with churches who are the major revenue source for MTW and a
growing factor in funding the home office.
Plans for information technology in 2003 focus mainly on upgrading the short
term project management system and the human resource system, a major
redesign of global communications (the Internet) and the integration and sharing
of data between the donor services system, the human resource system, the
contact management / recruiting system and the short-term project management
system.
III. Projection Base for 2003:
Projections have been made regarding the number of missionaries, home office
staff, annual income and annual expenses. In making these projections the
following assumptions have been used:
We anticipate that continued efforts to recruit missionaries in 2002 will show
additional results during 2003. Using 1996 as the comparison year to 2003,
career missionaries will grow from 512 to 608, two-year missionaries from 117
to 174, internship missionaries from 50 to 186 and two-week missionaries from
2036 to 7910.

<a id="ga30-p443"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=445 printed_page=443 -->

We will have limited home office staff increases in 2003 to support the strategic
ministry initiatives that have been recently started. (See charts I - V for further
details.)
We have anticipated that the US dollar will continue to be strong and stable
similar to 2001. We expect other global economic factors to be unstable, with
higher inflation than here in the US, but somewhat offset by currency values.
Income (excluding endowment and gift annuities) and expenses have grown
from $7.8 million in 1985 to $35.7 million in 2001 and is projected to be $39.7
million in 2003. Income projections have assumed a continuing strong support
for missionaries from churches and individuals in a reasonably stable US
economy and in a gradually growing PCA denomination. We have projected the
support requirements of missionaries, adjusted for inflation, as a basis for future
income. For expenses, the combined effect of salary adjustments, growth and
currency value, using historic trends indicates an increase of approximately 8
percent for 2002 and we have used the same percentage for 2003.
Missionary support accounts with deficit balances continue to remain low. Total
deficits for all missionaries have gone from $400,000 in 1994 down to only
$118,897 in 2001 indicating the strong support of MTW’s ministry partners.
Partnership share giving for the home office grew from $300,00 in 1994 to
$1,206,000 in 2001 and is projected to grow to $1,310,000 in 2003. We have
assumed that good church relations and increased services to churches will allow
a gradual annual increase in partnership share giving. (See charts VI - VIII for
further details.)
Project and field income is calculated as a part of the support requirements of
missionaries and we expect a 4 to 5 percent growth in 2003.
Investment income projections assume that interest rates will increase gradually
from their current levels. In 2003, we will see some endowment earnings
available as needed in the general fund and for global training and development
(GTD) to cover operational costs. In addition, the GTD has set goals for
additional annual project income to cover operational expenses.
The 2003 proposed budget for short-term ministries is based on a summer
program of 7910 students, an internship program of 186 persons and a two-year
missionary staff of 174 missionaries. The following major factors form the basis
for projecting the income and expenses for short-term ministries:
A. Office Staff—The cost in the home office reflect several changes
and new staff added during 2001. One additional full-time
equivalent position is added for 2003.

<a id="ga30-p444"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=446 printed_page=444 -->

B. Expanded programs in most areas will generate both higher income
and expenses that are being designed to stay in balance.
The medical insurance fund (MIF) had an average expense year in 2001. We
expect that costs will increase slightly faster than inflation. The operating capital
reserve showed significant improvement in 2001 but will have smaller increases
in 2002, as there was no premium increase.
The fixed monthly administrative assessment charge per missionary will
increase per the fixed formula linked to the consumer price index. With
controlled or specially funded costs in the home office, we expect to keep the
general fund in balance.
Major programs that will influence expenses in 2003 are the church-planting
movement training and mentoring (GTD), the growth of a foreign university
ministry in partnership with RUM, the major capital campaign for the
Philippines and the continued growth of the summer two-week program.
There are four tables showing the consolidated income and expenses budget
proposed for 2003. The first table presents a historical perspective showing
2001, 2002 approved at General Assembly and 2003 information. The second
shows a functional breakdown between program, administration and fund
raising. The third table shows changes in budget from 2002 to 2003. The forth
table shows a five year history of income and expenses.
In addition to the income and expense budget, the capital expense budget is
presented on the last table in the amount of $90,000. The major items to be
purchased relate to information technology, improved telecommunication and
some office reconfigurations to maximize space utilization for staff growth.

<a id="ga30-p452"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=454 printed_page=452 -->

PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION, INC.
2003 PROPOSED BUDGET SUMMARY
GENERAL COMMENTS:
The PCA Foundation has shown significant growth in the last several
years due to the re-engineering that took place in 1997-1998. Also, as a result,
the Foundation successfully achieved self-supporting status in 2000. Gifts,
distributions and total assets have increased considerably. This has resulted in
actual increases in Support and Revenue. Likewise, the expenses necessary to
operate the Foundation and implement new initiatives have increased.
However, due to recent events, primarily the general weakness in the economy,
coupled with a drastic reduction in interest rates (from which the Foundation
derives much of it support), the 2003 Budget reflects lower revenues and
expenses than does the 2002 Budget.
NOTES TO NATURAL EXPENSE STATEMENT - (Notes generally
relate to notable comparisons of 2003 Budget to 2002 Budget.)
SUPPORT & REVENUE
UNDESIGNATED EARNINGS (line 1) - These are earnings on funds
held by the PCA Foundation, mainly from Advise & Consult Funds and the
PCAF Endowment, which help underwrite the Foundation’s operating
expenses. The 2003 Budget of $334,000 represents a 27% decrease from the
2002 amount budgeted. This decrease is primarily the result of significant
decreases in the interest rates throughout 2001 (the Federal Funds Rate
decreased from 6.50% to 1.75% during 2001).
C & A SUPPORT (line 2) - General Assembly mandated support from
the four remaining Committees and Agencies (Covenant College, Covenant
Theological Seminary, Mission to North America, and Mission to the World)
was eliminated in 2000. Their mandatory support in 1999 was $15,000 each,
for a total of $60,000, having been reduced from $30,000 each in 1998,
$34,000 each in 1997 (after a $10,000 refund to each), and $44,000 each in
1996. The Foundation successfully achieved self-supporting status in 2000.
FEES (line 3) - 2003 budgeted fees are administrative fees charged on
funds held for long term administration such as Charitable Remainder Trusts,
Endowments, and Designated Funds, etc. However, the 2002 budgeted fees
included both these administrative fees and investment management fees on the
accounts held by the Foundation. The Foundation’s lead bank presently
provides investment management services for those Foundation accounts held
in custody at the bank and those accounts are charged accordingly. The PCAF
had anticipated that, beginning in 2002, it might have begun performing the

<a id="ga30-p453"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=455 printed_page=453 -->

investment management of these accounts itself, and accordingly, the PCAF
operating fund would have then received the investment management fee
income. There would have also been an offsetting operating expense
associated with performing this function. However, the 2003 budget does not
include such investment fee income and expense as the Foundation does not
presently plan on performing the investment management of the accounts held
in custody at the bank.
CONTRIBUTIONS (line 6) - Gifts made to help underwrite the
Foundation Operating Budget. The budgeted amount of $200,000 consists of a
$60,000 matching challenge contribution and the corresponding matches
budgeted at $75,000 for a period of 10 years, plus normal contributions to the
Foundation. The normal contributions for the 2003 Budget show a significant
increase from the 2002 Budget, due to the need to partially offset the budgeted
decrease in the Undesignated Earnings. The matching challenge contributions
helped provide the resources necessary for the Foundation to successfully
achieve self-supported status in 2000.
OPERATIONS EXPENSES
STAFF WAGES & BENEFITS (line 8) - 2003 Budget increase is
primarily due to the hiring of a Senior Accountant and budgeted compensation
increases of 3%. The accounting function was brought “in-house” during late
2001. Through 2002, accounting expenses had been budgeted under
Professional Services.
PROFESSIONAL SERVICES (line 10) - The significant decrease in
2003 budgeted expense from the 2002 budgeted expense was primarily due to
eliminating $60,000 in investment management expense and $40,000 paid to an
outside CPA firm to handle the Foundation’s accounting. The Foundation does
not presently plan to handle the investment management of its accounts (see
explanation for Fees - line 3 above) and the accounting is now performed “inhouse” (see explanation for Staff Wages and Benefits -line 8 above).
PROMOTIONAL EXPENSES (line 11) - 2003 budgeted reduction
from 2002 Budget is primarily due to not retaining Marketing Partners, Inc. (a
marketing and research firm) and decreased marketing and advertising. These
reductions are in response to the reduction in budgeted 2003 Undesignated
Earnings.
RENT (line 15) - 2003 Budget decrease due to space rented in new
PCA Building at a rate of $10.00/sq. ft. rather than the $14.00/sq. ft. budgeted
for in 2002.

<a id="ga30-p457"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=459 printed_page=457 -->

REFORMED UNIVERSITY MINISTRIES
PROPOSED BUDGET RATIONALE
2003
The RUM Mission:
Reformed University Ministries has the goal of building the church now and
for the future by reaching students for Christ and equipping students to serve.
This is accomplished by supporting the RUF works of presbyteries and
churches in the areas of administration, finance, development, intern program,
training, conferences, recruiting, and general ministry operation.
♦ This budget reflects our continuing growth as we assist and work with
presbyteries and churches to develop new RUF works on campuses
nationwide. For 2003 we project to have 74 campus ministries with well
over 130 field staff, including 59 interns.
♦ The total expense growth of 8.1% can be attributed to: the promotion of
the 2003 WIC Love Gift, increased depreciation related to the new
building and furniture, a more detailed budgeting for anew assistant
Coordinator, and increased travel expenses.
♦ The total number of full-time equivalent staff budgeted for 2003 is 10.
This is the same as the 2002 budget. Nine of these positions are currently
filled. The unfilled position is an Assistant Coordinator.
♦ An overall net increase of 5% in salaries and related adjustments to
benefits is assumed for all existing staff positions. That includes aggregate
of cost of living and merit increases.
♦ The 2003 WIC Love Gift promotional costs make up most of the 68.6%
expense increase of Support Services. This includes significant additions
of such expenses as video production and copies, brochures, postage,
banquet expenses and mass distribution letters. Moderate increases in
General Assembly relations and operation costs are a factor as well.
♦ The cost being charged by the Administrative Committee for office space
is projected to beat $12 per square foot. We are currently paying $10 per
square foot, however rent could go up if any of the major donors fail to act
on their pledges to the new building campaign.
♦ The 17.1% increase in budgeted Contributions is attributed to the expected
loss of interest due to low rates and the 8.1% expense growth.
The 2003 Budget for the entire ministry of $7,788,262, including affiliated
committees, is included in the RUM General Assembly report for information.

RIDGE HAVEN
2003 BUDGET COMPARISONS STATEMENT
AND PROPOSED BUDGET NOTES
Introduction
As for many of the Committees and Agencies of the PCA, 2001 was a hard
year for Ridge Haven. A quick analysis of the support/revenue section reveals
the first part of the story. We were helped by the steady influx of business
though the number of camp/conference registrations was down. This is
indicated by the figures in the following line items:
Projected revenue from Camps and Conference registrations, lines 1
and 2 was $160,944. Actual receipts were $157,747, down by $3,197.
Projected support from Other Contributions, line 6, including WIC
groups and individuals, was down by $15,517.
Projected revenue from the Bookstore and Campstore, line 10, was
down by $2,450.
The total shortfall in these areas was $21,164.
The shortfall however was offset by steady and increased revenue from Non-
Camps and Conference Facilities use, line 3, in the amount of $33,054 and NC
tax refunds in the amount of $6,395 for a total of $39,449.
In the revenue/support section, the problem continues to be the lack of
partnership share support. In 2001 we received only 24.5% of what was
needed, i.e. we received only $172,556 of the $704,346 needed and requested.
By way of the decrease of actual dollars, we were down $10,228 from the year
2000. The percent decrease was 1.25. It is for that reason that we continue to
struggle to bring staff compensation to acceptable levels, refurbish aging
facilities, upgrade needed vehicles, and proceed to additional programming,
some of which is in the planning stage but hindered by the lack of financial
support.
The second part of the story has to do with expenses. Though expenses were
less than budgeted in most categories, we still found it necessary to dip into
reserves and balances carried forward from the previous year due to the fact
that necessary expenses exceeded actual income by $32,988.

<a id="ga30-p462"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=464 printed_page=462 -->

As we propose the Ridge Haven 2003 budget, we hope that each church will
take note that an important part of the PCA’s ministry is suffering because of
the lack and in great need! We also ask each church to take note that the oftenmade excuse for non-support that “Ridge Haven is too far away for use of the
services...” in essence says, “I have no need of you.” Our humble plea asks
each one to note that this response violates the word and the principles of 1
Corinthians 12 that “the eye cannot say to the hand, ‘I have no need of you’”
especially as the one is suffering need because of the neglect of the other.
With our 2003 budget proposal our partnership share request is increased for
the first time in four years. With this budget we are praying for an increasing
awareness of the value of the ministry and opportunities that Ridge Haven is
providing to the PCA and Christian community at large. We are praying that
by His provision, our stewardship of all resources, and the spreading of fixed
costs over a greater number of users we may also be able to control expenses.
Notes
1. Line 4, Resident Leases - This category includes income from lot
leases, lot lease interest, lot lease maintenance fees (the administrative amount
we charge for keeping all lot information on file and current), water hookups,
water usage fees and road maintenance fees. The amount projected for 2003 is
the actual amount expected without lot sales or water hookup fees of which we
have no guarantee and therefore planned income.
2. Line 5, Other Contributions - The category includes contributions
from WIC groups, other groups, and individuals toward the daily operations of
Ridge Haven.
3. Line 6, Non-medical Reimbursements - The category includes
amounts paid to Ridge Haven by individuals or groups for the use of Ridge
Haven postage, office copier, vehicle parts, fuel, pay phones, etc.
5. Line 12, Sale of Assets - The category reflects the liquidation of
material items no longer needed. The amounts reported are unpredictable.
There is no certainty of income from the sale of assets from year-to-year.
6. Line 16, Other Salary/Benefits - The Ridge Haven staff consists of a
total of 13 year-around employees. The Administrator, Administrative
Assistant, Ministry Director, Maintenance Superintendent, and Food Service
Director are salaried, full-time employees. The Maintenance Assistant is full
time hourly-paid. The Office Assistant, Resident Manager, Accounting
Manager, Food Service Assistant Director, and three House Keeping staff are

<a id="ga30-p463"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=465 printed_page=463 -->

all year-around part-time hourly-paid employees. The present (2002) budget
allows for the Office Assistant position to be full-time a san employee is
available. The present (2002) budget also includes temporary food service
staff. The amount provided in the 2003 budget reflects the addition of a
Camps Director (intern status), increased compensation for the Maintenance
Superintendent bringing him up to the average compensation level for
employees in comparable positions in other area camps and conference
ministries, for the Food Service Assistant Director to become a full-time
salaried position, and a 3% cost-of-living increase for all other employees.
7. Line 18, Counselor and Camp Support Staff - The budgeted figure
provides for an amount needed to allow us to remain competitive in
compensating counselors and camp support staff The budgeted figure also
provides for additional camp counselors and camp support staff for larger
camps and a junior counselor or CIT (counselors in training) program
involving upper-grade high school students.
8. Line 20, Workers’ Comp - The amount reflects the corresponding cost
for increasing compensation and added staff identified in the line 16 note.
9. Line 21, Other Fees - This category includes North Carolina Fees
(usually a small amount), Miscellaneous fees (also usually a small amount),
Solid Waste Fees, Pool Fees (the amounts we pay for county inspection at the
beginning of each season), etc. The 2001 Actual amount was inflated by the
annual loan-maintenance fee created by the establishment of a line of credit to
finance the water system upgrades approved by the 2000 General Assembly.
10. Line 23, Planning/Promotion/and Recruiting - This category includes
all printing costs.
11. Line 32, Equipment/Furnishings - We continue to need additional
equipment and furnishings for several of our buildings. These items are
purchased as the money becomes available.
12. Line 33, Grounds - We have several erosion and landscape projects we
are seeking to implement. The amount indicated will allow us to do these as
the money comes in.
13. Line 35, Property Taxes - Ridge Haven pays property taxes on all
unsold lot leases as well as the 500-plus acres on which the center is located.
Property taxes on owned lot leases are paid by individual owners. In March of
this year we received notice of a substantial property tax increase. The percent

<a id="ga30-p464"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=466 printed_page=464 -->

increase is approximately 100%. The dollar amount increase is being changed
from $8,038 to $15,555.
14. Line 38, Vehicles - Ridge Haven owns and operates a fleet of 8
vehicles. The budgeted amount allows for us to repair and/or replace aging
and/or failing vehicles as the money comes in.
15. Line 43, Facilities Renovation - All 21 of the Ridge Haven
buildings/facilities need renovations and updating from time-to-time that are
not otherwise provided for in the day-to-day maintenance category. The
amount provided in this category reflects renovations for 9 different buildings
that could use immediate attention. The renovations desired were
professionally estimated.
16. Line 44, Water System - The decrease in the budgeted amount reflects
the actual projected cost of maintenance for the water system once the
Assembly approved $300,000 upgrading project mandated by the State of
North Carolina is complete.
17. Line 45, Water System Loan - Budgeting this amount will allow us to
repay the money borrowed in the event that contributed funds are not sufficient
to cover upgrading costs as they are progressing through 2002. As an
example, six months of interest on $100,000 would be $2,874. if we had to
carry a loan twelve months the cost would be $5,748. Payout for a twelve
month loan of $100,000 would be $105,748.
18. Line 46, New Programming - The addition of new conferences and/or
other similar ministries requires an upfront investment of about $10,000-
$12,000 per conference. It takes approximately three years for a conference to
reach the "break-even" point. At least two new conferences are being planned
for 2003-2004.

<a id="ga30-p465"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=467 printed_page=465 -->
