<a id="ga30-p467"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=469 printed_page=467 -->

REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE ON
CHRISTIAN EDUCATION AND PUBLICATION
TO THE 30™ GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
TELLING THE NEXT GENERATION
While Christian Education and Publications continues focus its ministry of
“equipping the saints,” targeting adult leaders and teachers in local churches,
our particular focus for the last several years has been to equip them to reach
the next generation for Christ. CE&P does not work directly with PCA
children and we interact with a limited number of young people through the
annual Presbyterian Youth of America conference. We do have the
opportunity to target the most powerfully influential group which can reach the
younger generations: the older generation.
Our training seminars, consulting services, and resources—curricula,
bookstore, and video library—are aimed at helping adults understand all that is
involved in reaching the next generation. In working out our strategy for
ministry, we believe we are starting with God’s emphasis. In the context of
the covenant community, God said, “I will be your God and the God of your
children” (Genesis 17:7) and “For the promise is for you and your children and
all who are far off’ (Acts 2:39).
Our strategy to challenge, train, and equip adults to pass on the Christian faith
to the next generation is not only worthwhile, it is biblical. The Apostle Paul
told Titus to train the older men and women to reach the younger men and
younger women. That is God’s pattern. However, over the past forty to fifty
years, North American culture has experienced a growing chasm between the
older and younger people. At the same time, if we listen carefully, we will
hear the younger generations openly asking for relationships with older people
in their immediate family and broader community.
At CE&P, our aim is to understand the Word of God, but we realize that we
cannot do that in a vacuum. Hence, we study trends in the culture to determine
what is happening around us and what God would have us to do. We not only
take the Word of God seriously, we also take seriously the need to understand
the world. We realize the importance of communicating God’s Word to our
world—including the younger generations. In taking this “covenantally
reformed” approach, we are convinced that we cannot understand the Word

<a id="ga30-p468"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=470 printed_page=468 -->

unless we understand it in our context. While we cannot put words into the
mouths of the Westminster Divines without being guilty of anachronism, we
do believe that when they wrote in The Westminster Confession of Faith 1-4
that the Word of God is to be translated into the vulgar (common) language of
the people, they envisioned more than just Bible translation. Their goal was
understanding.
as a simple way to understand, define, and explain our ministry in the PCA,
we use the phrase “training and resources.” The following is a summary to the
Assembly.
Training
In the area of training, CE&P focuses on a variety of topics and areas.
Primarily, we train leaders, both formal (ruling elders and deacons) and
informal (men and women who serve the local church in a variety of roles).
CE&P continues to assist local leaders in church ministry evaluation, strategic
faith planning, conflict management, and understanding our world through
seminars on postmodern culture and generational demographics and
characteristics. We also offer training in stewardship and worship.
The content of our teacher training workshops is organized into the categories
who we teach, what we teach, why we teach, and how we teach. They also
cover curriculum, individual and group learning styles, teaching methods, and
small group activities. These are designed for local Sunday school and small
group Bible study teachers, as well as those involved in one-on-one ministry
and other classroom settings.
We have increased our training in diaconal (mercy) ministries, and have made
a more intentional attempt to sensitize the church to the urgent need in this
area. We have held several conferences at the assembly and local level. The
most recent was the March 2001 conference in Atlanta, which we co
sponsored with MNA. Over 650 people attended. We are currently planning
the next conference, which will be held in Atlanta, March 13-15, 2003.
The Women in the Church arm of CE&P has provided a tremendous boost in
dealing with mercy ministries and in training local women in the foundations
of biblical womanhood. Through the WIC Leadership Training Seminar, 150-
200 Presbytery WIC presidents (PresWICs) and directors of women’s ministry
gather for a weekend of training and fellowship. Our ten WIC trainers work
with local churches to help them implement a women’s ministry.

<a id="ga30-p469"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=471 printed_page=469 -->

CE&P also has twenty regional trainers who interact with those who teach
children, youth, and adults. They are trained by the CE&P staff to do teacher
training, program evaluation and general Christian education consultation. We
trained approximately three thousand people through more than one hundred
training events in the past year.
At our annual Children’s Ministry Conference, we bring together around 150
people involved in local church children’s ministry. Through this conference,
we have developed resources, networks, and encouraging friendships among
those involved in this challenging ministry. We have also added a Children’s
Ministry Coordinator to our staff to help facilitate local church ministry to
children and families.
Our youth ministry department conducts seminars to train youth workers and
serves as the clearing house for children and youth ministry personnel. This
year we helped over fifty churches acquire staff for their youth ministries.
Churches and youth workers can use our web site to submit resumes and
request information on available positions or personnel. We have also begun
to establish networks among those involved in this vital ministry. Over a
dozen people are involved in CE&P’s internship training for youth ministries
and plans are underway to expand that number.
Nearly two hundred youth from local churches participated in PYA, a weeklong leadership training conference. The conference is held at Horn Creek in
Westcliffe, Colorado and Covenant College in Lookout Mountain, Georgia.
One of our concerns in ministering to families, especially children and youth,
is the statistics relating to church dropouts among young adults—some who
are still in high school or just entering college. We are concerned about the
growing number of youth who do not see church as important, relevant, and
real-life oriented. We see an increasing need to develop a biblical, reformed
world and life view among all ages, but especially the younger generations.
We have also held conferences to encourage ministry couples and have
conducted a conference on worship and music. CE&P plans to keep these
things on our list of strategically important events.
Resources
The other arm of CE&P’s ministry focuses on resources. We have six primary
resources that are available to local churches.

<a id="ga30-p470"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=472 printed_page=470 -->

CE&P Bookstore
Through the CE&P Bookstore, located in our office, CE&P serves churches
and individuals with some of the best and the newest books available. The
bookstore staff is trained to consult with local churches and customers
regarding the best of materials for personal and corporate use. Through our
toll-free number (1 -800-283-1357) and web site (www.cepbookstore.com), the
bookstore has been able to give prompt, effective service. This part of
CE&P’s ministry has continued to expand with a 12 percent growth during
2001-2002. The bookstore is also a key to linking local churches with CE&P
staff and vice versa.
Great Commission Publications
Great Commission Publications, a major part of the publishing arm of CE&P,
continues to make the best biblically reformed curriculum available to our
churches. This joint venture with the Orthodox Presbyterian Church has
enabled the PCA to have a full curriculum that is biblically focused,
pedagogically sound, and doctrinally shaped. The GCP staff is in the process
of transitioning the three-year cycle into two-year segments in response to
trends in curriculum development and the needs of local churches.
Through the GCP joint venture, CE&P now offers two vacation Bible school
curricula. GCP serves over two-thirds of PCA churches through the Sunday
school curriculum, Trinity Hymnal, and church bulletins. The CE&P staff and
regional trainers work with the GCP staff to service those products in our
churches. The materials may be ordered from the CE&P Bookstore or GCP
(www.cepbookstore.com, www.gcp.org).
Video Library
Several years ago, with help of the Women In the Church Love Gift and other
individuals, CE&P developed a video lending library. Today over seven
hundred different titles are available to more than three hundred member
churches. The membership is offered at a special rate to mission churches.
The Video Library catalog is now available on our website
(www.pcanet.org/cep) in a searchable format. We regularly evaluate and add
new titles to the inventory and are presently considering the inclusion of
DVDs. The staff is available to advise and consult with local churches over
the resources available through the Video Library.

<a id="ga30-p471"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=473 printed_page=471 -->

WIC Resources
CE&P’s Women In the Church ministry offers numerous resources to assist
local churches in their ministry to and through women. Last year we published
Paul’s Letters to Maturing Churches, the first book in a four- or five-year
curriculum. The second study, The Formation of God’s People, Israel, is now
available. This study covers Exodus through Deuteronomy. To develop the
series, staff consultants and writers are using The Adult Bible Education Series,
which was produced by CE&P several years ago.
Another WIC resource is the quarterly Resource Letter, which is mailed to
pastors’ wives and local WIC presidents. Each Resource Letter includes the
WICK, a reproducible bulletin insert for all women in local churches.
This year the WIC Love Gift, which rotates among the committees and
agencies, will go to CE&P to promote and develop the family and children’s
ministry. The seventeen-minute video and accompanying fliers explaining the
project, “Let the Children Come,” are available from the CE&P office.
Publications
CE&P provides several other publications in addition to the two mentioned
above. Equip for Ministry brings current topics, books, and other resources to
the attention of local church leadership. This bi-monthly magazine is also a
part of CE&P’s attempt to promote and publicize our training and resources to
local churches. It also contains items of general denominational interest, plus
it has become a medium to keep local church leaders and members aware of
other resources available to them.
The CE&P Committee agreed at its February 2002 meeting to distribute Equip
more broadly to those who participate in, support, or are interested in
supporting CE&P’s ministry. We will continue to make bulk copies available
to churches and will fulfill individual subscriptions that currently exist. The
primary purpose and design of the magazine will remain unchanged.
Over three hundred churches subscribe to the monthly Bulletin Supplement, a
bulletin insert designed to inform people in the pew of creative ministries and
activities in other PCA churches. The material is selected from the hundreds
of church bulletins and newsletters sent to our office.
Booklets on stewardship and materials on prayer and leadership training are
also among the publications available from CE&P. As the publications
committee, CE&P continues to assist the Stated Clerk of the General

<a id="ga30-p472"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=474 printed_page=472 -->

Assembly in producing and distributing the official denominational
documents. Also responsible for the annual week of prayer, CE&P is
partnering with Mission to North America on the current Fifty Days of Prayer
for the PCA.
Website
The CE&P Website, www.pcanet.org/cep, serves the church with timely, upto-date materials and information. More materials are being prepared for the
website to enable individuals and churches to quickly find resources to meet
their needs. Through this medium, we attempt to quickly, efficiently and
effectively keep the church in touch with CE&P and all of our training and
resources.
Conclusion
A number of items are on the drawing board that we share for the Assembly’s
awareness and to encourage churches’ prayers and support. They include: a
comprehensive kingdom model of making disciples, youth study materials, a
series of booklets on a variety of doctrinal and ecclesiastical topics, expansion
of men’s resources, and more focused ministry for the older generations.
It is our privilege and delight to serve Lord Christ and the Presbyterian Church in America with the best and most strategic training and resources. We ask for
your continued prayers and support and we present to you the following
recommendations for the coming year:
Recommendations
1. That the above report be received as an accurate summary of the ministry of
CE&P for the 2001-2002 year.
2. That the CE&P Minutes of September 20-21, 2001 and February 21-22,
2002 be approved.
3. That the PCA Assembly on behalf of CE&P and the Women In the Church
express our thanks for the gifts directed to Ridge Haven Conference center as
the 2001 recipient of the annual WIC Love Gift. The amount contributed was
$72,000.
4. That local churches be encouraged to participate in the March 13-15, 2003
Mercy Ministry Conference in Atlanta sponsored by CE&P and Mission to
North America.

<a id="ga30-p473"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=475 printed_page=473 -->

5. That churches and individuals be encouraged to participate in the week of
prayer coordinated by CE&P May 4-10, 2003.
6. That, the Committee having completed his annual review and evaluation,
CE&P Coordinator TE Charles Dunahoo be elected to serve in that position for
the coming year.
7. That special prayer be offered at the end of this report for CE&P’s
curriculum publishing arm, Great Commission Publications, praying
specifically for the upcoming major curriculum revision from a three-year to a
two-year cycle.
8. That the General Assembly express its thanks to the members of the
Women’s Advisory Sub-Committee for their assistance to CE&P in overseeing
the Women in the Church ministry.
9. That in light of the desire to publish a denominational print magazine, the
General Assembly with the concurrence of the Administrative Committee,
transfer that responsibility from CE&P to the AC Committee, realizing that
such actions do not include other denominational publications produced by
Christian Education and Publications.
10. That the Assembly join with CE&P in expressing thanks to RE Jeff
Oxman, RE Jeff Hall, and TE Rod Mays for their service as members of the
CE&P Committee.
11. That CE&P’s budget be approved as presented by the Administration
Committee.

<a id="ga30-p474"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=476 printed_page=474 -->

REPORT FROM
THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF
COVENANT COLLEGE
TO THE THIRTIETH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
Greetings in the name of Jesus Christ.
It is my pleasure to report on behalf of the Board of trustees of Covenant
College, to the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America.
Under the direction of the Board, the College administration is continually
evaluating the strengths and weaknesses of the College in light of its mission
and purpose, with the expectation that continual improvements will be made to
strengthen the ability to educate students. The attached Institutional
Effectiveness Report will help you better understand and evaluate the work of
Covenant College in the most recent year. The Board is pleased with the
continued growth and development of the College, the quality of the most
unusual education that is being given, and the strong sense of momentum that
we currently enjoy.
In addition to the attached report, there are several other matters that need to be
brought to the attention of the Assembly. The first is that we would like to say
that the Covenant College Board elected Dr. Niel B. Nielson as the next
president of Covenant College effective July 1, 2002. Following a thorough
national search that surfaced more than 150 prospects, Dr. Nielson was chosen
by the Board to succeed Frank Brock as president. Dr. Nielson and his wife,
Kathleen Buswell Nielson, have three children. Dr. Nielson is uniquely
qualified for the position with experience in education (Ph.D. in philosophy
from Vanderbilt), business, and ministry. It is rare to find someone with such
breadth of experience, and when this experience is combined with the personal
characteristics of character, leadership, and personal commitment to Christ, we
are most thankful that god has raised ups uc haman for such a time as this.
Momentum is hard to gain and easily lost, and the Board is confident that Dr.
Nielson can continue to sustain the momentum that the College currently
enjoys.
As evidence of the Board’s commitment to the College and its future, the College
has undertaken a capital campaign to build a new, $21 million library ($12 million
for the library; $8 for library endowment; and $1 million to convert the present
library into a faculty offices/classroom building). The library is the first step in the
next phase of campus growth toward a campus of 2,000 students, and is part of a
campus master plan that has also been approved by the Board. Knowing that other

<a id="ga30-p475"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=477 printed_page=475 -->

donors will look to the Board in this time of transition, the Board itself has
committed $1.8 million toward the library, and the administration continues to
work with others to create the building that will become the academic center for an
expanded academic program and an enlarged campus.
the covenant college board and administration believe that a vital part of a
healthy denomination is education for all ages, in all venues, and in all
subjects. To this end, the College wants to cooperate with agencies,
committees, presbyteries and local churches to provide education that will
strengthen the Church. We are pleased to report the following developments.
For the first time, Reformed University Fellowship has a campus
minister whose primary responsibility is Covenant College. The initial
reception has been positive. Many students participate weekly in large
and small group meetings. The subject matter of the Covenant RUF
program has been tailored to suit the needs of Covenant students.
Since Covenant students are required to take Bible and Doctrine, the
emphasis of RUF has been on practical theology.
Covenant College’s Chalmers Center exists to help train members of
the Church so that they can be more effective in helping the poor by
enabling the poor to develop the values that will enable them to
become self-sufficient, values such as honesty, industriousness,
savings, investment, etc. The Chalmers Center has worked with both
MTW and MNA in providing such training.
The trustees of Covenant College continue to believe that a denominational
college has the advantage of a unified theological perspective, a pool of
committed students, and the opportunity for broad-based church support. In an
age of highly specialized vocations, the development of a biblical world-view
applicable in each vocation is no easy task, but essential if the Church is to be
relevant to the larger culture. Covenant faculty in disciplines as diverse as
biology, literature, missions, business, and community development stand
ready and willing to assist churches by putting on conferences and seminars.
We are grateful for the opportunity of strengthening the denomination and of
presenting our report toward that end.
Respectfully submitted,
Robert G. Avis
Chairman
Board of Trustees
Covenant College

<a id="ga30-p476"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=478 printed_page=476 -->

The 2000-2001
Institutional Effectiveness Report
Two streams of educational awareness need be sounded if a college is to
remain effective in today’s culture. As we examined the institutional
effectiveness of Covenant College over the years, we have waded in both.
In the first, student outcomes of the educational process, questions are asked
about how and what the student has learned and how the experience has
affected the student’s life both in college and in later years. The gathering of
the evidence takes many forms. Some schools give more credence to issues
such as the caliber and credentials of the teaching faculty. These are solely
input issues. Over the last decade, measurements of student learning outcomes
have become the more widely accepted means of gathering evidence. A newer
methodology (National Survey of Student Engagement) looks at the various
types of educational engagements the student encounters throughout the
learning process. An integral part of all of these methodologies is the feedback
loop, which enables a college to see where improvements should be made.
The second important area to ensure effectiveness is the realization of the rapid
changes taking place in our society. Changes in technology, the acceleration of
life, the family unit, the media and many others have impacted the students
who enroll in college and the world they enter as graduates. An educational
system that denies these changes in its curriculum, style of teaching and
residential life, ill prepares graduates. Institutional effectiveness issues
addressed by the College may appear good, but students may not be prepared
to enter the changing society. If that happens, the College was not effective
despite good outcome evidence.
Over the years, the Institutional Effectiveness Reports have examined both.
They have addressed issues of input and outcomes and changes from what
we’ve learned. Effectiveness in the lives of alumni has also been presented.
We have tried to demonstrate how Covenant College has adapted to the
changing world in which we live.
The format of the report will be quite different from previous years. In the past
it was easy to find specific outcomes from various departments as the report
was presented in a divisional format. It seemed fitting this year to change the
format for two reasons.
Firstly, the 2000-2001 Institutional Effectiveness Report marks the beginning
of a new millennium. Just as King David often recalls God’s grace through an
historical accounting in a Psalm, we want to review findings over the last ten
years (where information is available) to see God’s grace and faithfulness.

<a id="ga30-p477"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=479 printed_page=477 -->

Secondly, this report marks the end of a long and fruitful career of Dr. Frank
Brock as President of Covenant College. The College has grown much under
his leadership. As we examine the last ten years in the growth of the College,
we can see how God has used the talents and dedication of His servant,
President Frank Brock.
The report is divided into three main sections:
Part I College Profile - Educational Inputs
This portion of the report presents the many educational inputs that
impact the educational process. It demonstrates the changes that have
taken place in the make-up of our student body and faculty, the
generous giving to the College, issues of academic support and
teaching, and other financial issues.
Part II Educational Outcomes
The fullness of an educational experience at Covenant College
includes time spent in the classroom as well as out of time out of
class. This section will report on issues of educational attainment in
these areas. It examines typical academic outcomes and areas that
address the spiritual and social growth of the student body.
Part III Planning
Part of a reformed perspective includes giving God our best. While
the term striving for excellence may define a humanistic endeavor for
some, at Covenant, it is an a ct of worship. This section of the report
will highlight the three College Plans that have been written and
implemented over the last ten years. It is a chronicle of how God has
used these efforts of the College to bring glory to Himself at
Covenant, therefore throughout the world.
The following excerpts of the Covenant College Mission Statement provide
the basis for the College assessment plan and this report.
Covenant College is a Christ-centered institution of higher education
emphasizing liberal arts.
It is the College’s purpose to help students make significant progress
toward maturity in the following areas:
1. Identity in Christ. a covenant student should be a person who is
united with Christ and committed to Him. This union and
commitment should lead to an understanding both of one’s sin and
of one’s significance as a person redeemed by Christ, resulting in a
growing awareness of purpose. This awareness of purpose should

<a id="ga30-p478"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=480 printed_page=478 -->

facilitate the development of goals, priorities, and practices that
foster spiritual effectiveness and well-being, including the
emotional, social, physical, and intellectual aspects of the
individual student.
2. Biblical frame of reference. Students should be acquiring the
ability to orient their whole lives by a perspective based on
scriptural revelation. For realization of this goal the following are
important:

- **a.** Scriptural knowledge
- **b.** Academic inquiry
- **c.** Analytical skills
- **d.** Communication skills.

3. Service that is Christ-like.

- **a.** Students should be assuming responsibilities within a local congregation as well as in the community of all believers.
- **b.** Students should be assuming responsibilities in society as servants of God.

Section I: College Profile - Inputs for Education
In the business world, consumer satisfaction is a strong means of measuring
the effectiveness of a company’s product in the marketplace. Many years ago,
the spirit of consumerism helped usher in what education now calls
assessment. Colleges “compete” for students just as businesses do for
customers. The product must be good and the consumer must be satisfied if the
long-term viability is to be maintained. While student satisfaction is only one
part of measuring an institution’s effectiveness, it does matter.
There are several means of tracking these issues of satisfaction in a college.
From the student’s perspective, three come to mind - new student enrollment,
retention, and graduation rate. There is some truth to the thought that students
vote with their feet. First, they select a college with an understanding that the
college will meet their particular needs. Once they finish their first semester,
they make the decision at least six more times. At the end of every semester, a
student makes a willful choice to return or to change directions. That decision
is best seen in the retention rate. The third, graduation rate, is a direct result of
the public’s desire for accountability. This national standard is set by the
government and reported on an annual basis. The graduation rate is a reflection
of the percentage of entering first time freshmen who graduate from the same
institution within 150% of the normal allotted time. For Covenant, as a fouryear college, that time would be six years.

<a id="ga30-p479"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=481 printed_page=479 -->

Enrollment
In 1990, the total student body at Covenant numbered 613 students. This past
fall, the college enrollment reached 1254, a 105% growth since 1990. There
were 174 new students entering Covenant in the fall of 1990 compared to 299
this fall. This represents a growth in new students of 72%.

Enrollment Growth

800
600
400
200

1990 1991 1992 1993 1994 1995 1996 1997 1998 1999 2000 2001

□ Traditional 9 Quest ■ master of education

Retention
Every semester students must face the decision to return or not. Factors that
enter that decision-making process include family situation, available major,
friendships, academic progress, feeling of acceptance, faculty interaction,
perceived academic quality, spiritual condition, financial, and many others.
The reporting of retention in most institutions is based on a fall-to-fall report.
While this is the accepted standard of reporting, there are still some colleges
that report differently. Some colleges report on spring to fall. Since the spring
semester always has a slight decrease in enrollment, that would enhance the
retention statistics. Some report class numbers while others use a name-toname comparison. Using class totals would include transfers, which artificially
inflate the statistic. Covenant College reports on a name by name basis from
fall semester to fall semester. This ensures more accurate retention
information based on matching the names of the students enrolled in the fall
with the names of the returning students enrolled in the following fall.
Covenant’s retention rate has increased significantly since 1990. While the
College has worked very hard on this issue, the blessings of God are evident.
Through His grace, more and more students are deciding to continue their
education at Covenant. Increased retention permits the College to help more
students develop a biblical frame of reference through scripture knowledge,
academic inquiry, analytical skills, and communication skills.

<a id="ga30-p480"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=482 printed_page=480 -->

Graduation Rate
The measure required by the federal government for reporting the graduation
rate is used in this report. Calculations are done by tracking the number of
first-time freshmen who enter the school. These students are allotted 150% of
the time normally expected for graduation. The graduation rate calculation is
determined by reporting the percentage of entering freshmen in 1994 who have
graduated from Covenant by fall 2001. Transfers who graduate within that
time frame are not reported by their starting or graduating college. This
naturally slants graduation rate reporting in favor of the larger colleges that
have all the majors an entering freshman might want.
However, even given that restriction, Covenant College’s graduation rate has
increased 89% - from 31.8% in 1990 to 60% in 2001. The most recent issue
(2002) of the U. S. News & World Report America’s Best Colleges reported
the average graduation rate of 49% among the top tier Southern Liberal Arts
Colleges. The following graph details that history.

<a id="ga30-p481"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=483 printed_page=481 -->

Student Profile
We track several factors that help describe our student body make-up. Among
them are gender, cultural diversity, academic preparedness, and percentage
from PCA churches. Just as student satisfaction can only be received as one
piece of evidence in measuring effectiveness, so too with these items.
Gender
There has been a trend throughout the United States that more young women
are going to college than young men. Covenant College reflects the same
trend.
gender mix

1991 1992 19 9 3 1994 1995 1996 1997 1998 1999 2000 200 1

Diversity
For many years Covenant has been trying to bring a stronger presence of
culturally diverse students to campus. We have made some, but not
satisfactory, progress through the years. One major change was the
development of the Diversity Scholarship Recipient program. The DSR was
developed to attract and reward students that bring a level of cultural diversity
to the campus that would enhance both their learning experience at Covenant
and that of the majority student. During the 2000-2001 academic year, the
DSR Program provided $344,540 in financial assistance for seventy students.
Eight students, who were not on scholarship, also participated in the program.

<a id="ga30-p482"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=484 printed_page=482 -->

Black

85.0% Caucasian

The DSR program continued its emphasis on leadership for the 2000-2001
year. In the previous year, the program provided leadership training for ten
upperclassmen who served as mentors to their fellow students. This past year,
the leadership conference was made available to all DSR students. This year’s
program focused on the works of Francis Schaeffer dealing with relationships
among Christians. Twenty-two students attended this winter leadership retreat.
Eight DSR students graduated from Covenant College in May, 2001, and the
expected DSR enrollment for the 2001-2002 academic year is fifty-five
students. In addition to the returning thirty-five students, there are twenty new
awards.
Academic Preparedness
Students coming to Covenant are more prepared for the rigors of academic life
today than they had been in the past.

<a id="ga30-p483"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=485 printed_page=483 -->

In 1996, the College Board modified the scoring of the SAT, making a tenyear comparison difficult. After modifying history to reflect the new system,
the chart above demonstrates the difference in scores between Covenant’s
entering students and the national average. The trend line for Covenant has
remained over 13% higher than the national average and increase at a rate of
7% during that period.
Secondary Marks of Entering Freshmen
As demonstrated by the graph below, a growing percentage of our freshmen
are reporting grades of mostly A’s. Students with mostly A’shave increased
by 143% and mostly C students have reduced by 93%.

10-year high school grades of entering freshmen

<a id="ga30-p484"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=486 printed_page=484 -->

Admissions
The history of admissions at Covenant College shows a cycle of peaks and
plateaus. Covenant’s growth pattern, typically, is a few years of little to no
increase followed by an upward spike of 10 to 15 percent. As the table below
demonstrates, Covenant has been in a plateau stage for the last five years.
However, applications for the fall of 2001 increased sharply to nearly 20%.
Total applications jumped from 606 to 682. The total number of matriculants
also increased from 244 to 299. The yield from application to matriculant rose
from 40% last year to 44% this year.
There were three major changes made this year. One was the addition of the
Student Partnership Program (SPP). This program encourages current
students to actively invite their friends and acquaintances to attend Covenant
College. Students whose friends matriculate become recipients of a $100 or
$200 credit in the Tuck Shoppe. This year nearly 30 Covenant students
received credit for their efforts. Another change in our approach this year was
a heightened emphasis on recruiting in PCA churches. Finally, the entire
student body, faculty and staff helped to make Covenant more known in a
variety of ways. Even with all these efforts, the enrollment increase this year is
surely a result of much prayer and God’s blessing on the endeavors of many
people.
Application Yield History
1995 1996 1997 1998 1999 2000 2001
Total Applications 463 577 616 603 683 606 682
Total Matriculants 185 250 246 260 258 244 299
Conversion Rate 40% 43% 40% 43% 38% 40% 44%

Quest
In May 2001, Quest graduated a record class of 157 students.
Two major improvements in Quest included a new instructor evaluation form
and an update of student and faculty materials. The new instructor evaluation
instrument was designed and tested and will give our instructors better
feedback to use in strengthening their teaching skills. Also, materials for seven
of the nine modules were reviewed and updated.
Quest enrollment has increased 179% since 1990.

<a id="ga30-p485"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=487 printed_page=485 -->

Human Resources
Covenant continues to improve salaries as part of a six-year plan to reach the
median pay for comparable work elsewhere. The salary management system’s
hiring target for 2001-2002 is 85% of the market median for each particular
job. Assuming the funding is available, increases are planned over the next
several years until the College reaches 100% of the market median for our
employees. The College does not have the funds to bring all salaries up to the
appropriate level immediately, so newer employees hired under the new
system often have higher salaries than the current employees, even in the same
jobs.
The self-funded health and dental plans again ended calendar 2000 plan year
with a deficit, requiring a significant premium increase for calendar year 2001.
Covenant has joined a consortium of employers in southeastern Tennessee and
northwestern Georgia to find ways to reduce medical costs for our plans.
Average faculty and staff tenure at June 30, 2001, is 7.4, up from 6.3 at June
30, 2000. We spent 0.6% of staff and faculty payroll on training and
development in 2000-2001, compared to our goal of 1.0%.
Faculty Profile

Covenant has been blessed through the years with faculty members who have
dedicated most of their teaching careers to Covenant students. Even as recently
as 1999, the average length of service was over 13 years. In the last two years,
we have witnessed the retirement of several long-time faculty members. We
rejoice that God brought these learned teachers to Covenant to serve these
many years. While their retirement has somewhat reduced the length of
average service, God has again shown his blessings to the College by bringing

<a id="ga30-p486"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=488 printed_page=486 -->

new men and women to serve as teachers and mentors to our students. The
percentage of doctorates or terminal degrees in field still remains over 81%.
Ph.D or Terminal Degrees

by bringing these men and women to serve as teachers and mentors for our

<a id="ga30-p487"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=489 printed_page=487 -->

Academic Support
Library
The 2000-2001 year was another active year for the library, with positive
developments in program building and personnel. A building committee was
formed and is working on the library program statement that will serve as a
basis for designing the new building. The College contracted with Mr. Jay K.
Lucker, well-known library building consultant and retired director of MIT
libraries, to prepare a preliminary written program for anew library building.
The library also received a budget increase of $18,000 and purchased print
materials, electronic database access (such as additional JSTOR titles, and
Project MUSE, which provides access to a considerable amount of full text
journal articles), and twenty-four new periodical subscriptions. An additional
technical full-time staff position was also added this year.
Audio Visual Services
For at least the past decade, the demand for AV services has increased each
year, and has made staffing issues much more prominent. Courses such as
CHOW, Art and Music, Christ and Culture, Reading Film, and Self in Society
(now The Christian Mind) are heavily dependent upon technology. The chapel
program also continues to demand greater technological support. To address
the resulting staffing need, two additional work-study positions were added for
the 2000-2001 academic year. One worker’s main task was to manage the AV
schedule and work assignments. This person also tracked the number of hours
submitted per week for each AV assistant.
President Brock has asked for an estimate for the completion of the sound
system in the chapel auditorium, i.e. to remove the Genie lifts and to install the
speakers permanently. The Services of Pro-Audio have been requested; but, to
date, scheduling an appointment with all interested parties has not been
possible. This will be pursued in 2001-2002.
All classrooms are fully equipped by current standards: TV/VCR, overhead
projector with equipment cart, chalkboard or whiteboard. Beginning in the
2001-2002 school year we will start furnishing each classroom with a DVD
player.
Technology Services
This past year was extremely busy for the Technology Services Department. It
marked a major technological improvement for the college as anew software
package (Banner) was implemented, bringing many departments into a
common database. While this change is already providing a remarkable
improvement in the manner and use of data, it has not been without pain.
Many hours were spent in departmental need assessment, system adaptation,

<a id="ga30-p488"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=490 printed_page=488 -->

training, data conversion, and testing. The change has impacted every
employee and student of the College.
The Banner Finance module and Web for Employees was activated in July
2000 followed by the Student module in October 2000. The Human Resources
module was activated in January 2001 followed by the Alumni module in
February 2001 and the Financial Aid module that same month. The
Technology Department also converted historical data for the Records and
Development departments. Banner Web for Alumni was turned on in July
2001. Alumni from around the world are now able to view the address
information the Development Office has on file, request address changes and
even search a real-time directory of other graduates — all within a secure web
browser.
The Banner Web for Students was also activated in August so that when
completed and announced, students will be able to view their current class
schedule, unofficial transcript, financial aid package and current bill.
Student Development
Residence Life Growth
Enrollment growth has had a major impact on residence life. Occupancy this
fall is at 91% male and 102% female for a total residential occupancy of 95%.

Residence Hail Occupancy

800

709
600
500

10()

1993 1994 1995 1996 1997 1998 1999 2000 2001

Total residential space available for the fall 2001 enrollment, including student
apartments, Founders, Carter and Maclellan/Rymer, was 766 beds, of which
730 are full. Anticipated growth for the fall 2001 required substantial effort to
house all students. The Chalmers Center and the Business Department

<a id="ga30-p489"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=491 printed_page=489 -->

currently occupy the first floor of Rymer Hall, which will be converted back to
housing for the fall of 2002. At that point, Maclellan Hall, Rymer Hall,
Founders’ Center, Carter Hall and the Student Apartments will provide
housing for 788 students. Housing demands have grown 42% from 1993 to fall
2001 while the housing availability has increased only 38%.
Covenant College & Reformed University Fellowship Partnership
College and RUF officials gathered through the spring semester to discuss how
combined efforts might better minister to the needs of Covenant students. In
July, the Tennessee Presbytery (with Covenant College’s strong endorsement)
called the Rev. Jed Johnston to the Covenant campus to work with Dr. Bill
Davis, Dr. Don Graham, and Dean Raymond in establishing a joint effort
educating the youth of the PCA not only at the College but across the nation
and eventually around the world.
Priesthill Services
Covenant College continues to enjoy a working relationship with the
Psychological Studies Institutes (PSI) at the Chattanooga Bible Institute (CBI).
Three counselors were assigned to the campus this year to complete a 42-hour
contract for counseling service. The counselors spend 93% of their on-campus
time providing direct or indirect counseling. These counselors provide the
following services to our students:
1. Professional counseling from a biblical perspective to assist students,
faculty and staff
2. Testing and evaluations of students
3. Out-patient referrals for psychiatric and mental health care
4. Consultation with Resident Advisors on resident mental health
concerns
5. Opportunities for small group interaction on various topics related to
emotional, mental, physical, and spiritual issues.
WIC and a Growing Ministry to Covenant’s Women Students
Of our 766 residential students, 61% (467) are women. God has again shown
His grace to us as we have desired to hire more female faculty members. This
past year two more women joined our faculty. However, the limited number of
female faculty members still makes it difficult for our women students to
obtain the desired level of mentoring by female faculty members. While our
women on the faculty and staff serve as good role models for the female
students, they are simply overwhelmed by the nature of their work and the
sheer number of women students on campus. Male students that desire to be
mentored by male faculty have a five-to-one opportunity compared to female

<a id="ga30-p490"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=492 printed_page=490 -->

students who have an eighty-five-to-one opportunity. The percentage of new
female students who left Covenant after just one year rose from 51% in the fall
of 1998 to 61% in the fall of 1999.
Financial
Financial Aid
The College has been struggling with the desire to keep a Covenant education
both affordable and of high quality. For every dollar given in aid to a student,
one less dollar can be spent on the academic program they receive. The
College has set a goal of discounting (giving tuition-funded aid) at the rate of
29%. That goal which should provide a good balance between those tensions
has not yet been reached. The following graph demonstrates the history of the
unfunded discounting.

Tuition/Fees, Room and Board
The cost to attend Covenant has risen considerably in the last ten years. The
graph that follows shows a comparison of the cost for tuition, fees, room, and
board for Covenant and the average public, and private four-year colleges.

While Covenant has closed the financial gap on the average private college, we
feel we have surpassed the academic quality of the average private school.
This and other Institutional Reports tend to bear that out as correct.
Budget Performance
After completing another year with an operating surplus, the College remains
in good financial condition. This year’s operating budget was $21,662,397 as
compared to $7,931,630 for the 1990-91 operating budget. That represents a
173% increase in operating budgets.
Endowment
Endowment investment return for 2000-2001 was -14.99%, compared to
+ 17.9% for 1999-2000 and an equal +17.9% for 1998-1999. Colleges in
general saw reductions in their market return for last year. Our performance for
1999-2000 earned Covenant a ranking of 129th out of over 550 institutions
participating in the national endowment return survey. Covenant ranks 42nd in
three-year return and 47th in ten-year return among the colleges in that survey.
Including endowment investment return and new gifts, Covenant’s total
endowment has grown 1,225% since 1990.

<a id="ga30-p492"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=494 printed_page=492 -->

Church Giving
This past year 607 churches expressed their support for the College by giving
over $681,000. Church support has risen over the last ten years, but still more
than half the PCA churches do not give to the College.

Alumni
Over the years, more and more alumni are seeing the needs at Covenant and
recognizing the value of their education. This can be seen in the alumni giving

Section II: Educational Outcomes
A variety of measures and assessments have been collected to provide evidences
of past performance and direction for future action. Both quantitative and
qualitative methods of assessment have been employed in an effort to synthesize
a holistic picture of college performance in the academic arena.
Two notes of caution regarding the limits of evidence need to be considered as
assessments are reviewed. Firstly, although the information presented here
provides a picture of institutional effectiveness, it is not a complete picture.
We do not pretend to be objective nor do we believe that specification of many
component parts provides a comprehensive picture of the academic program.
Nonetheless, it would be foolish to think that the following information has
nothing to say to us and should not be used to inform our educational planning.
Secondly, the methods of evidence gathering are subject to the same cycle of
assessment as that of the data itself. The assessment cycle sets goals, gathers
data, compares the results of that data with the pre-set goals, and makes
changes in the program, goals, or process of evidence gathering where
appropriate. As we have matured in our methods of evidence gathering,
changes have been made in the process. We have very few studies that used
the same form of measurement for more than two years. Therefore, most of
this section is based on 2000-2001 academic results.
An additional problem this year was the extremely low level of student
participation on Assessment Day in April 2000. While there is hope the
problem can be corrected this year, the data from many of those assessment
procedures is very suspect. Those instruments adversely affected by this
problem are identified with an asterisk(*).

<a id="ga30-p494"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=496 printed_page=494 -->

The last section of this report details progress the College has made through
the implementation of college planning cycles. There are many additional
educational outcomes which drive actions addressed in those plans. The feed
back loop of assessment is more easily followed in seeing the types of issues
each planning cycle addressed.
It is our desire to give honor to God for his work in our midst and to provide
evidence for decision-making that improves the quality of service to our Lord
within the call of this learning community.
There is much to be thankful for in these data. Generally the health of the
College as a faithful learning community is strong according to many measures
relative to both internal and external standards.
Academic Support Services
More details about the work of the academic support areas were reported in the
previous section. However, it should be noted that these services play a major
role in the quality of education at Covenant College. All areas of academic
support have continued to provide excellent assistance to educational
endeavors on the campus. Technology Services, Instructional Technology and
Telephone Services have all helped to provide and facilitate the use of
technical tools from a campus-wide data base system and consistent upgrades
on hardware and software to exceptional service and support for the human use
of very advanced tools. The Library continues to address issues and tactics
raised in the three-year plan adding to the staff and the collection. Audiovisual
Services continues to support classroom instruction and large venue
presentations by good stewardship of limited resources and excellent
management of student workers.
Non-traditional Programs
The bulk of this section refers to the traditional, undergraduate program of the
College. Both programs are growing in enrollment, generating income, and
having very positive influence connecting faith and practice in the lives of the
students in the programs. The Quest program continues to be the largest
department of the College and continues to make positive changes in
curriculum and delivery resulting in positive ratings by current students and
recent alumni. The Master of Education program continues to receive high
marks relative to a number of internal and external measures. Nearly ten
thousand K-12 students are influenced by the educators who attend classes
each year. A more detailed description of outcomes assessment for these
programs can be found in the Covenant College Academic Assessment 2000-
2001 in the Academic Affairs office.

<a id="ga30-p495"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=497 printed_page=495 -->

Basic Principles of Assessment Program
The integral presentation of faith embedded in academic disciplines continues
to be a strength of the College. The reformed perspective of the College is
evidenced in the variety of assessments and measurements. The focus of the
College is
...found in its motto, based on Colossians 1:18: "In All Things . . .
Christ Pre-eminent." Acknowledging Christ as the creator of all
things, as the redeemer of people fallen into sin, as the
touchstone of all truth, and as the sovereign ruler over all areas
of life, the College strives to discern and to unfold the
implications of His pre-eminence in all things. To serve this
end we seek to appropriate the mind of Christ as the biblical
perspective from which we characterize and respond to reality.
In attempting to make such a biblically grounded frame of
reference explicit and operative, we are committed to
excellence in academic inquiry, and we seek to define all areas
of the College's structure and program according to this
understanding of our purpose.
Covenant College is dedicated to presenting an academically rigorous
program, which challenges students to excel in scholarship based on biblical
principles. According to the purpose statement,
Students should be acquiring the ability to orient their whole
lives by a perspective based on scriptural revelation. For
realization of this goal the following are important:
1. Scriptural knowledge. Students should be acquiring a
working knowledge of the Scriptures, rejoicing in their
promises and allowing them to direct their thoughts and
actions in every area of life.
2. Academic inquiry. Students should be acquiring a broad
appreciation of the various aspects of creation,
becoming familiar with valid methods of inquiry into
each area of study. Each student should be acquiring
some depth in one or two academic disciplines.
3. Analytical skills. Students should be acquiring the
capacity for incisive, critical and logical thinking.
4. Communication skills. Students should be acquiring the
ability to communicate ideas clearly in both speaking
and writing.
The following results were noted for the 2000-2001 academic year:

<a id="ga30-p496"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=498 printed_page=496 -->

Bible Literacy Value-Added*
A biblical literacy test has been designed by our biblical studies department
and is administered early in the freshman year and then again late in the
sophomore year, near the completion of 12 credit hours of instruction in Bible
and doctrine. Scores are compared by mean difference for a sense of valueadded.
Confirmation
• Sophomores took the biblical literacy test which was designed by the
members of the Biblical studies department and achieved a mean score of
80.0% on the objective portion of the test. Freshmen scored a mean
percentage of 64.6% which would indicate a statistically significant gain in
biblical content knowledge as measured by this value-added comparison.
Faith Statements*
As a part of the application process at Covenant College, prospective students
respond to a demanding application form. One question on the form provides
and opportunity for students to express a personal statement of faith. During
assessment day, juniors were given the opportunity to rewrite that statement,
and a team of trained raters evaluates the statements for any changes.
The question asks: Please tell us about your personal walk with the Lord.
Ideally, your testimony should communicate a personal faith that is alive and
active, descriptive of who you are uniquely in Christ. Show how God has used
your family background, your experiences at Covenant, and individual
characteristics to make you unique and uniquely His.
A group of second-year graduate students are trained in rating change in
statements of faith according to a scale of 1 to 5, from strong regression of
faith to strong progression of faith respectively across three areas of faith
application: 1. with God; 2. with others; and 3. with the creation. Rather than
using student self-report, evaluators are trained to use a Covenant “ideal”
standard.
Confirmation
• High scores on the faith statement show growth in all areas. Scores on a
five- point scale from 1 = falling away to 5 = significant growth were:
relationship with God 4.0 (SD=0.8), relationship with others 3.8 (SD=0.9),
and relationship with creation 3.2 (SD=0.9). These scores are lower than
last year possibly due to two factors: a low turn out for the assessment and
anew rate r training criteria which targeted a covenant “ideal” as a
standard rather than a self-report of growth.

<a id="ga30-p497"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=499 printed_page=497 -->

Instructional Effectiveness - Traditional
Effective teaching is described in the faculty manual as...
...[including] concern for the learner, the learning process, the subject
matter, teaching methodology, and the teacher. For the process to be
called Christian, it must occur in the context of viewing each of the
above dimensions with biblical insight. Such biblical insight can then
lead to a Christian practice.
Students are made in the image of God and are called to participate in God’s
work and reflect His character. This means that students are created to be
active, creative, productive, purposeful beings who make sense of ideas and
experiences in order to exercise some control over the world around them.
They cannot be allowed to be merely passive recipients of information. Rather,
they must be challenged to think and conceptualize, make judgments, discern
the implications of ideas, and act upon their knowledge for the glory of God
and benefit of humankind. Instructional practices must foster the development
of these characteristics in the students.
Classroom instruction is assessed through various means at the College. In the
traditional program, it is the backbone of the College accounting for one half
of the evaluation of professors. Professors are required to have some courses
evaluated each year. These evaluations are collected and aggregated as an
indicator of college-wide instructional effectiveness. Professors also receive an
individual report for each course they choose to assess. Student course
evaluations are distributed and the dean of faculty and peers of professors
perform observations. Indications strongly support a positive performance in
this area college-wide.
Confirmations
Professors received a mean rating of 4.2 (SD=0.9) on a five-point scale for
their ability to bring a Christian perspective to their teaching.
Additionally, measurements from departmental assessments indicate a high
satisfaction of faith integration from information gathered during focus groups.
Course evaluations by students indicate a high regard for the instruction
offered. Mean ratings near or above four on a five-point scale are achieved in
all measures. Students (n= 1051) consistently rate all areas of instruction high.
Focus groups reported in departmental assessments also give high praise for
classroom instruction and the role of professors in the instructional process.
Observations by the dean of faculty give further attestation to the high quality
of instruction.
Consistently high ratings in experiential studies show that program is
particularly strong in its ability to affect authentic learning.

<a id="ga30-p498"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=500 printed_page=498 -->

Concerns
Course evaluations continue to indicate a need to investigate student
involvement in the learning process through projects and groups work.
Course evaluations tend to focus on more senior level courses (an average of
second semester sophomore, first semester junior) when appreciation for
learning may be at its best. A distribution of ratings that would follow more
core courses and students who are earlier in the college career may be helpful.
A comprehensive emphasis in practices, which enhance learning, should be
considered. Such an emphasis would move the assessment model from simply
measuring results, which confirm the current college practices to advocating a
sense of best practices.

Math Placement Value-added
In some years a comparison of math scores has been used to assess relative
growth in mathematical content. This year this assessment was not employed.
A math placement test is given at the beginning of the freshmen year and again
to juniors during assessment day. The test has a possible score of 60 points.

Major Field Achievement Tests (MFATs)
On assessment day in April, students from nine majors took the Major Fields
Achievement Tests. The scores are reported by discipline according to mean
percentile rank. The College has a generally strong showing considering that
an average college would rank at the 50th percentile in all areas. Each
department receives a detailed report and will incorporate this year’s findings
into next year’s departmental assessments.

number of test takers in any given department. That weighted departmental
