Confirmations
• MFAT scores had a weighted mean in the 72nd percentile, indicating that
our average student taking the test ranked at the top threshold of the
second quartile of similar college graduates.
• Departmental assessments indicate student responses and performance,
which are consistent with these confirmations.
National Survey of Student Engagement
Covenant College was not able to participate in the most recent National
Survey of Student Engagement (NSSE). We are scheduled to participate this
coming year. However, the results of last year are very important in the
overall assessment picture Last year there were a total of 276 participating
four-year colleges and universities involved in the study. There were 151,910
first-year and senior students participating. The study was co-sponsored by the
Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching and the Pew Forum for
Undergraduate Learning and supported by the Pew Charitable Trust.
In October 2000, national benchmarks of good educational practice were
released based on the results from the NSSE 2000. An article entitled “Are
Students Actually Learning?” appeared in the November 17, 2000 issue of The
Chronicle of Higher Education. In that article, George Kuh, a highereducation professor at Indiana University at Bloomington and director of the
National Survey of Student Engagement commented on the results of the
survey:
“There are some really hidden jewels out there. One surprise was
that some high-profile schools don't do as well as some schools
that most people have never heard of.”
The article went on to mention a list some “exemplary institutions.” Covenant
College was one of only three such institutions in the general college
classification cited for providing a high level of academic challenge for
senior students. Scores in the area of academic challenge were based upon
issues including time spent preparing for class, type and length of assignments,
analyzing, synthesizing and organizing ideas, information, or experiences,
making judgments about the value of ideas and a campus environment that
emphasized spending significant amounts of time studying and on academic
work.
Senior Integration Papers (SIPs)
every department on campus provides for aversion of a senior integration
Paper. The purpose of the paper is to provide a capstone experience for the
student and an assessment for the department. Students are to select a topic or

<a id="ga30-p501"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=503 printed_page=501 -->

project and to approach its study ina scholarly manner from a christian world
and life view. The specific assignment varies from major to major but is to be
rated on a four item, eight-point scale. Professors rate the performance of the
students relative to written expression, oral expression, content of the discipline,
and the integration of faith. However, over the last few years this method of
assessing integration of faith has proven less than adequate. The SIPs are so
broad in variety of topic and style that a common rating system seems difficult.
However, the value of the SIP as a major academic and integrative experience
remains very high, and will continue to be the capstone experience of a
student’s educational. The social problems essay has become the main
assessment tool for assessing integrative thinking. SIP documents are available
on the campus network in public folders. The documents represent all of the
SIPs that students have given permission to make available.
Confirmations
• Senior integration projects received a mean rating of 6.4 (SD=1.2) on a
scale of 1-8 for faith integration.
• Senior integration projects received a mean rating of 6.7 (SD=1.3) on a
scale of 1-8 for scholarly content.
• Students are able to communicate well on SIPs as indicated by ratings of
6.4 (SD=1.3) and 6.3 (SD=1.2) on eight-point scales for written and oral
expression respectively.
Concerns
• Various departments have made specific recommendations involving
slight adjustments of course offerings or delivery systems that may have
positive influence on achievement of these goals.
Social Problems Essay*
Juniors write an essay on assessment day each spring to describe how their
experience at Covenant College has affected their ability to think critically
from a Christian perspective regarding current social problems. Students are
asked to describe two significant challenges facing our society and to articulate
a Christian response. Essays are rated on six scales including reasoning, moral
judgment, worldview, biblical themes, personal responsibility, and sense of
history and structure. Each essay is rated by a second year graduate student,
who has been trained and achieved and acceptable level (r>.7) of inter-rater
reliability. Rather than using student self-report, evaluators are trained to use a
Covenant “ideal” standard.

<a id="ga30-p502"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=504 printed_page=502 -->

Confirmations
• Medium high scores on the social problems essay (n=20) including
reasoning 3.3 (SD=1.2), worldview 3.2 (SD 1.2), biblical themes 3.2
(SD=1.1), responsibility 3.7 (SD=1.1), and historical/structural
considerations 3.6 (SD=0.9). All scores on a five-point scale. These scores
are lower than last year possibly due to two factors: a low turn out for the
assessment and anew rate r training criteria which targeted a covenant
“ideal” as a standard rather than a self-report of growth.
Concerns
• Because of the low attendance on assessment day, the social concerns
essay and the faith statement are likely less than valid. In addition, the
new rater training will be helpful for future comparisons but are not
reliably compared with last year’s results. Plans are being made to ensure
adequate participation in the assessments this spring.
Student Development
Spirituality
While measuring the “spiritual vitality” of the student body is very difficult,
assessment efforts indicate that our students are very satisfied with their
personal growth in Christ, their Christian frame of reference and their service
for Christ. The second year of the three-year plan asks for a formal review to
determine ways to observe and evaluate “spiritual vitality” on our campus.
To enhance opportunity for spiritual growth within our community, small
groups were developed called parakletos groups. These groups were
structured so as to allow more accountability and support in a small group than
was available in the previous topical groups. From 3-5 people met in these
groups, sometimes twice per week, to enter more deeply into the members’
spiritual lives. By the spring 2001, semester we had approximately 75 such
groups in existence, with the remainder of the student body involved in the
traditional groups studying books, discussing topics, singing hymns, praying,
etc. The small group program continues to be one of the best-received and
effective portions of the chapel program.
While measuring the movement of the Spirit is never humanly possible, one
simply survey, completed by the Student Senate sought to determine if
“desiring church involvement”, “church attendance”, and “ministry
involvement” decreased or increased over the four years of a college degree.
From those responding, a one-year ‘snapshot’ developed these numbers:

<a id="ga30-p503"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=505 printed_page=503 -->

Activity New Students Seniors
% Desiring Church Involvement: 59.2% 68.75%
Average number of Church Activities per week 2.00 PW 1.69 PW
% Involved in Ministries: 28.75% 62.50%

The Intercollegiate Athletic Program
The intercollegiate athletic program has made decisions to grow in “depth” as
junior varsity teams were added in volleyball and men’s soccerwith a present
possibility of other teams ‘applying’ for a junior varsity team in the fall 200
for the fall 2002. The Athletic Committee, in keeping with the three-year plan,
has been charged with the responsibility to reconsider the College’s athletic
affiliation. For 2001 - 2002, the athletic administration and College officers
will continue to investigate the NAIA/NCAA issue and investigate the
possibility of a new conference affiliation within the NAIA.
Coaches continued to emphasize the importance of recruiting and retaining
student-athletes who are students first and athletes second. The average GPA
of the athletes remains above a 3.0.
S2001 S2000 F1999 S1998 F1997 S1997 S1995 F1994 S1994
All Sports
Average 3.14 3.19 2.98 2.97 3.04 3.06 3.05 3.02 3.03

Intramural Athletics
Approximately 80% of our student body have been involved in intramural
athletics this past year. Over the past three years, students responding to the
Student Satisfaction Survey have consistently ranked intramural sports “lower”
in importance but “higher” in satisfaction when compared to the CCCU
institutions. One of the objectives in the 1995-2000 plan was to move the
participation of students involved in intramural sports to a higher level. The
student participation has grown from 65% in 1996 to the 80-88% level in the
last few years.

Section III: Planning
Planning has become a major hallmark of Frank Brock’s presidency. He has
dedicated more time and resources to planning than any time in Covenant’s
history. During his years as President, three plans have been developed and
implemented. Both the plans and the process of planning have become more
participatory and wide reaching as each plan was developed. As each plan is
reviewed, one can see how God has blessed Covenant College through the
attainment of most of the goals in the plans.

<a id="ga30-p504"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=506 printed_page=504 -->

The first five-year plan was developed in 1988. This plan was written in a
very goal oriented fashion. The process was very narrow in scope as the
administrators representing each area of the College wrote and implemented
the plan. The goal came as an adaptation of a portion of the Shorter Statement
of Purpose: “The primary goal is to provide an environment of academic
excellence that encourages students to develop a Christian world-view.”
Following is an outline of the plan with a brief update.
STRATEGIC PLAN 1989-1994
I. Student Body
A. 700 students by ‘93-94
• Total enrollment in 1991 reached 727 and 840 by 93-94.
B. Greater admissions selectivity
• SAT entrance requirements were raised from 800 to 900.
C. Increase the number of black Americans entering to 5%
• A minority recruiter was hired and increased financial aid
provided, however, only 4% of student body was black-American.
II. Academic
A. Improve Science instruction
• Mills Hall opened in 1996.
B. Broaden degree programs
• M.Ed. students increased from 20 to 41 by 1994.
• Quest enrollment almost doubled from 74 to 146.
C. Improve library holdings
• Holdings increased by 11.3%.
D. Improve student competencies
• anew core was developed and a writing center implemented.
• First annual Institutional Effectiveness Report was published in 1991.
E. Strengthen faculty
• 8 new faculty were added.
III. Student life
A. Improved career and counseling services
B. Expanded athletics
• Women’s soccer was added.
C. Improved retention
• College-wide retention increased from 76% to 82%.
D. Suitable outdoor education programs will be integrated into the college
experience.

<a id="ga30-p505"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=507 printed_page=505 -->

IV. Development
A. Annual support will grow from $1.2 to $1.6 million by 1993-94, with
the major portion of additional revenues committed to financial aid.
• Accounting changes make comparisons difficult. Churches
giving rose from 394 to 532.
B. A Capital Campaign will be designed to support the Five-year Plan.
• A Campaign was designed to fund major building needs raising
$8,500,000.

The process for planning the second five-year plan was very broad in
participation. The process started in the Fall of 1992 and included the Board,
faculty, staff, students, Chattanooga community leaders, and alumni. Methods
of participation included alumni desserts, student surveys, discussions at small
faculty barbecues and faculty meetings, community breakfasts in Chattanooga
and a joint faculty/staff/trustee planning meeting. At each of these events, the
participants were given environmental scanning documents and discussions
were held to determine strategic directions and objectives.
STRATEGIC PLAN 1995-2000
The 1995-2000 plan goal is to:
INCREASE OUR EFFECTIVENESS IN PREPARING GRADUATES
FOR SERVICE TO GOD AS REFORMING INFLUENCERS IN
SOCIETY
STRATEGY I:
Provide the academic, leadership and personal opportunities that develop the
whole person with a special emphasis on God’s call to be a reforming
influencer.
OBJECTIVE 1: Strengthen the educational program
The following activities were designed to strengthen the educational
experience at Covenant College so that our graduates would be better prepared
for service as reforming influencers on society.
Academic
• There have been eight faculty sabbaticals granted since fall 1995.

<a id="ga30-p506"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=508 printed_page=506 -->

• In 1997, $500,000 was added to the Sanderson endowment for faculty
development. The fund totaled $2.1 million as of June 30, 1999.
• Faculty development funding has grown from $19,754 in 95Z-96 to
$93,452 budgeted in 99/00. A total of $206,216 has been spent (includes
budget for 99/00) during that period.
• Dr. Ashlock was awarded $1,000 stipend for the 6th edition of his
teaching math textbook.
• Since the fall of 1995, thirteen new faculty members have been hired in
nine fields. Twelve of the thirteen hold the Ph.D. and the thirteenth is
finishing his dissertation.
• Faculty members Dan MacDougall, Steve Kaufmann, and Don Graham
have led summer workshops on integration offaith and learning for new
(and some returning) faculty members.
• Dr. Wildeman conducted a faculty workshop on teaching writing.
• Dr. Kaufmann’s teaching load was temporarily reduced to permit time
for faculty development issues during the 97/98 academic year. Each
faculty member presented a plan for personal development funded from
the 1998-99 budget.
• Fall 97 Board meeting - new majors in Philosophy, Philosophy and
Religion, and Economics added.
• Dr. Robert Littlejohn was appointed Vice President for Academic Affairs
and Dr. Jeff Hall was appointed Dean of Faculty.
• The Student Contact Program was initiated in the fall of 1998 to develop
a learning community between a faculty member and six students. 18
faculty and 108 students are involved. The program was not successful
and was discontinued.
• Self In Society was reviewed and revised developing a stronger academic
component in orientation and a stronger relationship between the
advisor and new-student advisee.
• Self In Society academic advisors received special advising training for
new students.
• A new multi-media room opened in the fall 1997 in Mills Hall. An
instructional technologist was hired to encourage and assist faculty in
the use of technology tools available for teaching.
• Sanderson 215 has been equipped with multi-media tools to enhance
instruction delivery.
• The Management of Information Systems Committee (MISC) was
formed to oversee all information technology issues at the College.
• A new three-year plan is under development with strong faculty
leadership. The plan goal is to strengthen the College’s academic culture
with its Reformed emphasis, to serve Christ, His church and His world.

<a id="ga30-p507"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=509 printed_page=507 -->

Co-Curricular
• A CPA ’s “Student Learning Imperative” implemented which focuses on
intertwining the concepts of student learning and personal development.
• A two-year chapel examination process has been completed which
involved students and faculty members. Dr. Graham is now % time
chaplain. A policy governing visiting speakers was approved at the 1997
General Assembly.
• Realizing that student involvement improves student maturing and
retention, a high level of student participation in intercollegiate and
intramural sports has been continued during these periods of enrollment
growth. (Intercollegiate 96/97- 14%, 97/98 16%, Intramural 96/97-16%,
97/98 89%).
• An intramural director (Betsy Matthews) was hired to oversee the
program.
• A half-time person has been hired to oversee the practical work and
work-study programs in an effort to strengthen the integrity and value of
the programs. The program has been moved under the Student
Development Department and is being re-evaluated.
• Continuing relationships with the Staley foundation, Windgate
Foundation, and Women in the Church have resulted in additional
funds for speakers above and beyond normal programming resources in
the areas ofpersonal growth and family.
• The Campus Activities Board was reviewed and revised adding to their
responsibility to oversee and coordinate all campus programming.
• A second Faculty Advisor was added to the Student Senate.
• Student Senate allocated 52% of the Student Activities Fee to support
new or continuing academic clubs associated with majors up from 31%
in the fall of 1993.
• The construction of Ashe Athletic Center.
• Meetings and discussions between the teaching faculty and the office of
student development have focused on defining and developing the co-
curricular program of the College.
OBJECTIVE 2: Increase awareness of, and ability to live and work with,
people of a wide variety of culture.
The following positive activities were undertaken in a belief that such would
help us attain this objective.
• Diversity was re-defined by the multi-cultural committee. Students
qualifying for the scholarship must demonstrate racial and ethnic
identity and influence, personal involvement in racial reconciliation, a

<a id="ga30-p508"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=510 printed_page=508 -->

cultural diversity contribution to the College, and potential influence on
the thinking and actions of the majority.
• A program was started to offer financial assistance to Covenant female
or minority graduates who would return to teach at Covenant. The
program is being re-evaluated and will probably be modified to provide
more immediate results.
OBJECTIVE 3: In recognition that limited space and resources may require
Covenant to become more selective in admissions, improve the College’s
ability to recruit and admit those students most likely to benefit from a
Covenant education and provide benefit to the college community.
The College has taken the following enrollment development actions.
New Student Recruitment
• Applicants are now required to submit two essays and be interviewed
before acceptance. The application will soon be on the WEB site.
• A waiting list was started to ensure room for the better-qualified
students.
• Applications have increased from 577 applications for Fall 96 to 683 for
Fall 99.
• Development staff has been increased and well trained.
• A Visitors Center was completed to better facilitate visits.
• New literature has been designed for the recruitment efforts.
Financial Aid
• Institutional financial aid has increased from $936,000 in 1990 to over
$4,605,150 in 1998. A total of $24,566,150 in institutional financial
assistance has been awarded during that period.
• The basis for aid has shifted from need to merit while still meeting 100%
of need for the high merit students.
• A system of index points has enabled better identification of the well-
rounded scholar most apt to benefit from a Covenant education. Due to
the apparent inability of reducing our discount rate within existing
policies, a major change was instituted for 1999-2000 awarding which
excluded Covenant need based aid in the packages for students of low
merit index ratings and who also had high need.
• A Dean’s scholarship is being awarded to assist students who are mostly
self-sufficient, hold a 3.0 GPA, and are interested in civic activities.
Several new restricted or endowed scholarships have also become
available for student financial assistance.

<a id="ga30-p509"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=511 printed_page=509 -->

• The Church Partnership Program was changed for the 1998-giving year.
Students who are members of qualifying churches will receive a package
totaling up to 100% of tuition based on need. In the 1999 program year,
the per member participation rate was directly related with tuition
increases. The participation level now stands at $10 per church
member.
• Athletic Scholarship Funds were reallocated to bring the College into
compliance with Title IX. The allocation revised the 1998 women’s
scholarship pool of 48% to the 1999 pool of 52%.
Retention Efforts
• The Early Warning System for new students was implemented, resulting
in a drop in the first semester freshmen withdrawal rate to two percent.
• Two different surveys show opposite results about faculty advising.
These opposing results are being studied to determine the best course of
action. Action plans to improve some weak areas were implemented in
the Fall o f1999.
• An electronic AEGR is now on line to assist the faculty.
• The Covenant College FIPSE team visited Eastern College to participate
in the “Enhancing Student success through First-year Program
Reform. ”
• The ACT Survey for Academic Advising was incorporated into the
assessment of the College.
• New students will experience a revised Self in Society (SIS) freshmen
course. Changes to the course include:
1. A revised SIS syllabus,
2. SIS instructors trained to advise more effectively,
3. classes were scheduled during orientation week to emphasize the
academic purpose and priorities of an academic culture,
4. more sections were added to the class to improve our faculty/new-
student ratio,
5. section instructors will continue to advise their advisees after
orientation their students thereby increasing contact and
information flow, and
6. coordination between the SIS syllabus and the Career Development
Center was implemented.
7. Funds were made available for instructors to invite their advisees
into their home for continued discussions and advising.
• Members of the Covenant College FIPSE team attended the Council of
Independent College’s Conference on developing and Academic culture.
• Members of the Faculty Planning Committee, Chaplain’s Office, faculty
and office of student development attended the Trinity International

<a id="ga30-p510"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=512 printed_page=510 -->

University’s conference on spiritual formation with a specific charge of
reviewing the first-year experience at Covenant.
• Allocation of FIPSE Funds for:
1. Site Visit to Eastern College
2. Survey of Academic Advising
3. Operating Supplies
4. Faculty Development Workshop in May 1999
5. Faculty breakfasts for SIS
6. Spiritual Formation Conference
7. Fees for CIC conference Creating Cultures in Learning
8. The Curriculum Committee began discussions about revising Self in
Society I & II.
9. The Admissions Committee began discussions regarding admissions
standards.
10. The Early Warning System was expanded and revised.
11. The Practical Service program was reviewed without significant
change.
• The Academic Administration initiated a New-Student Contact Program
that focused on making contact with “at-risk” students.
The Maclellan Scholars Program
• The 1998-99 budget includes $111,200 in additional funds to keep the
award at 60% of tuition.
• Eligibility requirements have increased from 3.4 GPA to 3.6, and SAT
scores from 1200 to 1250.
OBJECTIVE 4: Complete facilities called for in the campus plan as needed
for an enrollment of 850 traditional students.
• Maclellan Residence Hall was completed for fall 1998 use. The Rymer
Hall addition is under construction and expected completion is August
2000. The Visitor's Center was also completed by April 1, 1998. The
Ashe Activity Center construction is still underway with completion
expected January 2000. The faculty lounge has been moved to the
library and renovation has been completed.
• Signage, lighting, and landscaping have been enhanced and will
continue to develop as new buildings and parking lots are added.
• The second floor of the Barnes Physical Education Center, and Carter
Hall exterior tower renovation are complete. Interior renovation to
Carter Hall resulted in additional residence space available on the
second floor, expanded mail services and a student lounge below the

<a id="ga30-p511"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=513 printed_page=511 -->

Great Hall. The first stage of a new heating system has been installed in
Carter Hall.
• The master plan has been updated to cover the needs within the present
capital campaign. However, additional need analysis is being completed
in the areas of classrooms, faculty offices, chapel, library, special events,
and dining. The S/L/A/M Collaborative, a campus master planning
firm, was hired to develop a new plan. Phase I will include an estimate
of growth potential within the existing program statements. Phase II
will develop a full master plan.
• The question of what to do after reaching 1000 students is being
addressed in preparation of the next plan
• Eleven faculty now have computing hardware, software and access to
the network.
• Depreciation of plant has become a budget line item to ensure
maintenance of facilities. This fund has grown from $319,000 in 97/97
to a budget of $900,000 in 99/2000. This represents an increase from
2.5% of budget to 6.4 %.
STRATEGY II:
Increase involvement to effect reformation in the dominant structures of our
society.
OBJECTIVE 1: Since Covenant graduates must be prepared to enter a culture
that is openly hostile to Christianity, usually measuring success on individual
desires, often at the cost of others, the College will equip students and enable
faculty to engage in dialogue with the dominant secular culture.
• An inventory of faculty involvement in various academic and
professional associations has been completed. The Faculty Status
Committee will examine the inventory and offer suggestions on ways in
which the administration might provide greater opportunity for faculty to
become actively involved in these associations.
• The Festschrift (Where Is The Salt?) has been completed and published.
It contains nine scholarly essays by members of the faculty.
• a study was conducted that showed great promise fora co-operative
study program.
OBJECTIVE 2: Increase cooperative involvement with the Church.
• Published four editions of “On-Line” in the 1996-97 academic year.
Development also mailed monthly prayer letters to churches in the 1996-
97 academic year.

<a id="ga30-p512"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=514 printed_page=512 -->

- The View was produced in 1998 as the new college publication and has received great response.
- Dennis Fogo was hired to co-ordinate all publications and public relations.
- Met with Jed Johnston (Marnie), the RUF minister at UTC, and established a potential working relationship between Covenant and UTC's RUF chapter.
- Discussions with our Bible Department indicate an interest in developing programs for local PCA churches. Two possibilities have primarily been discussed: the offering of a Bible certification program and/or instructional training for Sunday School and lay teachers via television.
- Covenant has recently hosted meetings for representatives of all the agencies of the PCA to discuss information needs. This is a first step in providing the expertise available at the College to the PCA.
- The Women in the Church love gift has provided an endowment fund for missionary children attending Covenant College.
- A PCA youth conference of middle schoolers (The Edge) held their third summer conference at Covenant.
- Dr. James Skillen served as guest lecturer for the third annual WIC Lecture Series.

***

The current plan was developed in a way different from both previous plans. The main focus of the plan was the further improvement of the academic program. The planning committee included five faculty members who also served as an academic sub-committee. They became the funnels through which all ideas and suggestions were reviewed and presented to the full planning committee for action. Therefore, this plan is very academically driven. The most recent update follows.

## The Plan for 2000-2003

GOAL: Strengthen the College's academic culture with its Reformed emphasis, to serve Christ, His church and His world.

STRATEGY I: Enhance the quality of the academic program.

A. Evaluate and address faculty privileges and load responsibilities for teaching, advising, research, and committee service; and nurture the faculty as a collegium of teacher-scholars whose scholarship enriches the

<a id="ga30-p513"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=515 printed_page=513 -->

classroom, engages their disciplines, and provides visible intellectual
resources for Christ’s church and his world.
Tactic 1: Expand the existing faculty “load” mechanism beyond contact hours
in class with students in order to include faculty responsibilities in the core
areas of advising, scholarship, committee work and (otherwise
uncompensated) service to the College. Using this mechanism, identify a
normal faculty “load.”
• Toward fulfillment of this tactic, a faculty load commission has been
meeting since September. Jeff Hall has drafted a six-page faculty load
document, which will be discussed at the next load commission meeting.
• The faculty load commission will conclude its deliberations and present
recommendations to the faculty status committee, faculty, and
administration by mid fall. Jeff Hall is chairing the commission.
Tactic 2: Reward faculty for service above and outside their normal
responsibilities.
• This tactic is being addressed partly through the efforts of the faculty
load commission. Jeff Hall and Robert Littlejohn are arranging to
provide honoraria or to supplement honoraria provided by churches for
those faculty who participate in the faculty and student forum initiative.
• When the faculty load commission concludes its deliberations, any
accepted recommendations needing funding will be placed in year three.
Tactic 3: Provide practical encouragement for faculty collaboration in
research, pedagogy-development, and publication.
• All faculty continue to receive $800for professional development.
• The academic council has received 14 requests for funding of
professor’s professional development and has funded approximately
$20,000 worth of projects.
• All faculty holding one-year contracts meet weekly for book discussions
and other professional development in preparation for the three-year
contract.
• The dean of faculty (Jeff Hall) is meeting with all faculty members
annually. In the past he has observed classes of all professors. This year
he is meeting with each to provide encouragement relative to student
evaluation interpretation and professional development.
• The academic council has received 14 requests for funding of
professor’s professional development and has funded approximately
$15,000 worth of projects.
• A rotation of evaluation has been established based on contract:
1-year contract - every year; 3-year contract - every 2 years;

<a id="ga30-p514"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=516 printed_page=514 -->

5-year contract - every 3 years
V This tactic has been completed and is now an ongoing procedure.
Tactic 4: Provide faculty-led educational opportunities for the PCA.
• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2001-2002 academic
year.
• Bill Davis has made at least two such presentations as part of the
Faculty Speakers Forum.
B. Become more intentional about continuously improving the quality and
breadth of both teaching and learning and the strength and coherence of
the academic program.
Tactic 1: Develop a comprehensive long-range academic plan.
• A draft is in preparation by the academic vice president (Robert
Littlejohn) who will seek feedback and input from the planning
subcommittee and other faculty prior to finalizing.
• A final draft will be completed by the academic sub-committee of the
Planning Team by the end of the fall semester.
Tactic 2: Establish new Procedures and Criteria for the regular review of
academic (and administrative) departments and the overall college curriculum.
• The academic vice president is working closely with the Academic
Standards committee using the new proposed accreditation criteria to
develop these procedures.
• The academic standards committee will implement external reviews by
the end of the 2001-2002 academic year at a cost of $5,000. It will be
funded from the academic affairs budget.
• Bob Harbert, Harry Pinner, and Scott Raymond will develop the plan for
administrative departments.
Tactic 3: Establish or identify the appropriate body to be responsible for the
oversight and coordination of the Core Curriculum.
• The governance task force has been meeting regularly since the
beginning of the spring semester and is addressing this issue.
• The governance task force has presented its report to the President and
addresses core oversight in the committee structure.
Tactic 4: Direct the attentions of the faculty development process to the
mentoring of good professional skills for new and continuing faculty.
• All new faculty members receive a mentor who is compensated, has an
expense account, and leads the new faculty member according to

<a id="ga30-p515"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=517 printed_page=515 -->

guidelines reconstructed by the faculty status committee during the
summer o f2000.
• All new faculty members attend the weekly development sessions for
faculty members holding one-year contracts.
• The dean of faculty visits all new faculty in their classrooms.
• All new faculty members receive an orientation at the faculty retreat.
• All new faculty members attend the main lectures of the course, The
Christian Mind.
• The activities listed above have become standard operating procedures
and will be implemented each year and funded as part of the ongoing
academic administration budget.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 5: Become more proactive in recruiting potential faculty candidates.
• Successful searches have been conducted for three positions with a
number of qualified candidates recruited to each department.
• Advertisements were placed on a number of websites, and in periodicals
in cooperation with each department.
• Departments now have a complete description of selection procedures
available in the faculty manual.
• This was originally scheduled for implementation during the 2001-2002
academic year.
• The procedures listed above have become standard operating procedures
and will be implemented each year and funded as part of the ongoing
academic administration budget.
■S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 6: Establish an honors program.
• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2001-2002 academic
year.
• This topic will be initiated early in the academic year with the
curriculum committee for any recommended action. Cost estimates are
not available. Any funding needed will be requested for the third year of
the plan.
Tactic 7: Increase the visibility of the College’s academic programs.
• A Faculty Student Speakers Forum program has been established in an
effort to bring faculty speakers to PCA churches. A brochure was
developed and mailed, placed on the web site, advertised in the PCA
News and will be featured in an upcoming View.

<a id="ga30-p516"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=518 printed_page=516 -->

• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2001-2002 academic
year.
• An ad was placed in World and distributed at 2001 General Assembly.
• World has agreed to place Covenant ads for free when space is available.
C. Cultivate a campus climate that encourages academic excellence.
Tactic 1: Add more academically oriented work-study positions.
• 15 work- study positions have been shifted from food service and
reassigned to the academic departments. To date, the larger budget
requestfor additional positions has not been funded.
• This was originally scheduled for implementation during the 2001-2002
academic year.
• Any work-study requests for academic positions will be included in the
budget request in IC2 below.
• All academic requests have been met. Attempts to do so will continue as
requested.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 2: Help students with work loads and financial pressures by raising
work-study pay.
• Hours awarded for work-study have been reduced from 5, 10, or 5 to 4,
8, or 12 respectively and the hourly wage increased from $5.15 to $6.30
per hour. This enables most students to earn the same amount of money
while reducing the work hours. This change took place in the fall of
2000 in the hopes of freeing 1-3 more hours for a student’s study time
without reducing their work-study earnings.
• This was originally scheduled for implementation during the 2001-2002
academic year.
• A budget request will be submitted to President’s Council for Fall 2002-
2003 funding to cover the $90,000 needed to balance the current work
study system as changed by this tactic.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 3: Encourage and recognize staff contributions to academic climate.
• The staff social/recognition team has been established and continues to
discuss ways to accomplish this.
• The money ($10,000) to implement these plans comes from previous
activities that were in the annual budget. The staff social and

<a id="ga30-p517"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=519 printed_page=517 -->

recognition team (SSRT) now determines how to spend the money. This
amount need not be included in planning tactics funding requests.
■/ This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 4: Change presidential scholarship point system to encourage primacy
of academic tasks.
• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2001-2002 academic
year.
• The system has been changed from 50% academic/50% other to 70%
academic/30%) other.
■S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 5: Establish additional outside speakers programs.
• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2002-2003 academic
year.
• Jay Green has been asked to coordinate outside speakers and has been
given a budget to support his activities. Included in that budget are the
Staley Lecture Series and the Windgate Foundation Lectures Series. At
present, no additional funds are being requested.
Tactic 6: Establish a hiring policy which increases diversity.
• A hiring policy has been written by the academic vice president and has
been utilized in hiring faculty currently on campus.
• The policy has become standard operating procedures and will be
implemented each year.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 7: Continue to develop and deepen freshman orientation’s focus on
academic tasks.
• Due to substantial changes in orientation over the past two years,
questions appeared as to exactly who has the authority and responsibility
to change and implement new student orientation. Dean Raymond
requested a meeting of Dr. Littlejohn, Dr. Hall, Dean Schreur, Dean D.
Miller, and Dean R. Miller to determine the responsible administrator(s)
for orientation. Consensus was reached to assign authority and
responsibility to Deans Schreur and R. Miller who will work in
conjunction to implement a successful new student orientation program.
• Through the capable leadership of Jack Fennema and many of our
faculty, this tactic is well along the road to full realization.

<a id="ga30-p518"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=520 printed_page=518 -->

• The program was assessed and adjusted for the coming year.
• The modified plan will be implemented. No additional funding is
required.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 8: Strengthen departmental identity among faculty members and
students in the disciplines.
• The academic vice president has worked closely with the campus master
planning committee and the architect for the Sanderson addition for a
departmental office cluster (like those in Mills) to be included in the
addition.
• An internal marketing focus will be implemented in conjunction with the
admissions department. This effort will focus attention on departmental
distinctives and hopefully aid in the recruitment efforts.
D. Dramatically increase the prominence and usefulness of library
resources and space providing the campus with a center for serious research
and collaborative inquiry.
Tactic 1: Contract with a professional librarian (not an architect) experienced
in library program planning to assist in preparing a written Program.
• Mr. Jay K. Lucker, retired director of MIT libraries and prominent
academic library consultant, was secured for this task. He visited
campus on August 29 and 30, 2000, and met with the building team, and
later produced a written report on library space planning for Covenant
College. From this preliminary report, a complete written program is
being developed.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 2: Budget permitting, increase the library print materials budget by
$35,000 each year for the next three years.
• For the first year of the three-year plan, the library has received an
$18,000 addition to the budget for collection development.
• A request for an additional $35,000 will be made for funding in 2001-
2002 through planning funds or capital funds and another $35,000 the
following year.
Tactic 3: Purchase additional periodical subscriptions in paper form for each
academic major as recommended by faculty.

<a id="ga30-p519"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=521 printed_page=519 -->

• This year we have purchased 24 new paper subscriptions in a number of
different disciplines including business, economics, history, literature
and music.
• This is now funded through the annual budget.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 4: Purchase electronic periodical subscriptions from Project Muse.
• The Project Muse database became available to Covenant in January
2001. It can be accessed through the library web page.
• This is now funded through the annual budget.
•/ This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 5: Hire an additional professional librarian.
• This tactic is scheduled for funding during the second year of the three-
year plan. At present, no funds have been allocated.
• If funded, ($48,410) an additional librarian will be hired. Cost based on
Assistant Professor rate of $38,728 plus 25% benefits.
Tactic 6: Hire an additional technical assistant as a support position for an
additional librarian.
• The library has been funded for an additional technical assistant
position in the first year of the three-year plan. As of January 2001, a
job description has been drawn up for a position primarily with
instructional technology responsibilities.
• The position was filled.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.

STRATEGY II:
Improve coherence and connections among the College’s constituent groups
and in its internal and external relations.
A. Nurture a climate of genuine spiritual vitality, consistent with our identity
and role as a Christ-centered college that is characterized by godly
relationships, effective communication and mutual trust.
Tactic 1: Increase understanding of and commitment to the proper role and
witness of faculty, administration, and staff in nurturing a climate of
genuine spiritual vitality at Covenant.

<a id="ga30-p520"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=522 printed_page=520 -->

• Initial meetings have occurred with some plans to be developed.
Scheduled for implementation during the 2001-2002 academic year.
• Scott Raymond, Jeff Hall, and Donovan Graham are continuing to meet
to develop a process that will facilitate a full community discussion of
this tactic.
Tactic 2: Various methods of assessment will be developed by the Chapel
committee to evaluate the current climate of spiritual vitality on campus. These
methods should be generally narrative and descriptive rather than quantitative.
• This activity has been folded into IIAl.
Tactic 3: Students, faculty, and staff will be offered opportunities to learn and
grow in the area of biblical conflict resolution, in the effective and edifying use
of personal and electronic communications, and in mutual accountability.
• A four-session training session was held by Harry Pinner for any faculty
or staff that wanted to attend. Additional, one chapel presentation was
given dealing with the issues of confession and forgiveness and a chapel
small group was formed for students who wanted to participate. Another
chapel presentation is scheduled for the spring o f2001 regarding giving
up one ’v rights in a conflict. Both Harry Pinner and Gary Raffaele have
been trained by Peacemakers Ministries in conciliation and conflict
resolution.
• Covenant has established a self-funding Peacemaker Ministries program
with Richard Pettit as Director. The program is housed in academic
administration (available to all students) through the sociology and
business departments.
Tactic 4: Following an audit of all print and electronic sources of official on
and off campus communications, a review should be made of the overall
coherence and effectiveness of these products with respect to the distinctive
academic and spiritual climate of the College and the need for complete and
timely communication on important issues.
• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2002-2003 academic
year.
B. Clarify the College’s governance and employer/employee relations to
ensure that the mission, goals and operations of the College are carried out
effectively, and that all aspects of institutional governance are made more
explicit, more predictable, and more widely understood.

<a id="ga30-p521"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=523 printed_page=521 -->

Tactic 1: Adopt the following interim governance procedures for the 2000-
2001 academic year.
• An interim governance procedure has been adopted by the faculty and
has been followed during the current academic year.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 2: The president will appoint a governance task force including three
teaching faculty to analyze governance concerns and recommend changes to
current governance practice.
• The appointed governance task force has been meeting this semester.
The academic vice president was asked by the committee to draft a
proposed long-term plan for consideration by the committee. The
requested proposed plan has been under consideration by the task force
for the past several meetings.
• The task force has submitted a plan and asked the President to respond.
He will respond by September, present the plan to the faculty in the fall,
and to the Board in March 2002.
Tactic 3: Implement an interim Staff governance structure that includes a
grievance procedure, and establish a review process.
• The structure of staff governance has been clarified through a listing of
all staff committees and their authority and responsibility.
• The Director of Human Resources will develop a staff grievance
procedure for consideration by President's council no later than
December 31, 2001.
C. Enhance the overall coherence of each student’s Covenant experience.
Tactic 1: Establish an institutional-wide policy concerning complete and
uninterrupted daytime access for students to all offices that provide student
services.
• All offices that provide student services (such as student development,
business office, records, student financial planning) have developed a
lunch hour coverage plan to ensure they are open for business during
the lunch period. Additionally, offices that that provide students in an
indirect way (such as academic affairs and the President’s office) also
remain open during the lunch period.
• This was originally scheduled for implementation during the 2002-2003
academic year.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.

<a id="ga30-p522"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=524 printed_page=522 -->

Tactic 2: The student senate will collaborate with the appropriate faculty
committee to develop a document that specifies the “Expectations and
Priorities of the Academic Culture.”
• Dean Raymond provided the student senate an NASPA publication titled
“Reasonable Expectations” for the Student Senate to consider as a
working model. Drs. Littlejohn and Hall identified the Academic
Standards Committee as the committee responsible to work with the
Student Senate to accomplish this tactic. Additional copies of the
NASPA publication “Reasonable Expectations” have been ordered for
members of the Academic Standard Committee. At the request of the
Academic Standards Committee Chairman, Student Senate appointed
three members of the Senate to work with that committee. The Academic
Standards Committee and Student Senate representatives have met to
establish a common understanding of this tactic.
• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2001-2002 academic
year.
Tactic 3: The teaching faculty and the residence life staff will work
cooperatively to ensure that the residence policies support and enhance
instruction and learning.
• Dean Raymond will be working closely with the Curriculum Committee
to develop and promote a greater understanding of common goals of the
core and residence life.
• Student Development “Expected Outcomes” document was developed for
review and revision for 2001 - 2002.
• Dean Schreur has invited 4 faculty members to join the RDs and the
student deans in reading Gilkey’s “Shantung Compound” this summer
and discussing it in the fall.
• Dean Schreur has invited Dean Hall and Dean Raymond, among others,
to read, “Making the Most of College” as a foundation for review and
revise assessment practices evaluating student learning.
• Each Residence Hall initiated programs to invite faculty involvement in
hall activities and established academic goals for the Academic year.
• Student Deans attended many faculty development sessions, faculty
forums, and faculty assembly meetings.
Tactic 4: The Career Development office will collaborate with the appropriate
faculty department to develop discipline-related life experience opportunities
for students.
• Although some internship requests have been forwarded to other
departments, the primary emphasis this school year will be to identify
student needs and to develop an internship program for the Business <6

<a id="ga30-p523"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=525 printed_page=523 -->

Biology Depts. A Business Dept. Internship Committee was convened
and chaired by Gary Raffaele. David Hoover and Chris Dodson were
also in attendance. The Committee made the following decisions: Pole
the business students upon their return in the fall to determine their
interests and needs relative to internships and then to contact
appropriate employers to explore internship possibilities. This reflects
the desire of the committee to ensure that the process will be student
driven rather than employer driven. Dr. Tim Morris and Mr. King will
meet to develop a common understanding of the types of internships that
would be appropriate for our biology students.
• The Business Committee has initiated discussions to develop internships
with TVA.
• Mr. King and Dr. Morris have developed internships at Hutcheson
Medical Center. Six biology students will be involved this academic
year.
Tactic 5: Explicate the chapel and practical service graduation requirements
rendering them coherent with the academic program.
• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2002-2003 academic
year.
Tactic 6: Develop a policy statement for academic support services regarding
reasonable accommodation and access to academic instruction.
• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2002-2003 academic
year.
• A policy was approved by the faculty that permits extended test time for
students with a documented learning disability.
Tactic 7: The office of academic affairs will coordinate and promote an
enhanced schedule of on-campus scholarly exchanges and performances.
• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2002-2003 academic
year.
Tactic 8: Evaluate the College’s national athletic affiliation and propose
actions that are consistent with the College’s purpose and objectives for its
athletic program.
• Dr. Crossman hosted faculty meetings to discuss the will of the faculty as
to NAIA or NCAA affdiation. He also informed the College
administration that the NCAA has implemented a two-year moratorium
on colleges joining the NCAA. Discussion about the possibility ofjoining
the NCAA Division III will be put off until the spring 2001. Dr.

<a id="ga30-p524"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=526 printed_page=524 -->

Crossman begins the spring sabbatical that will include a focus on
athletic affiliation and the College’s Purpose Statement.
• Discussions will continue and include cost estimates for such a change.
If additional funding is needed, it will be determined during 2001-02 and
requested for funding in the 2002-03 academic year.
D. Covenant's educational philosophy and organizational values.
Tactic 1: develop a process leading to action on a consensus educational
philosophy document that is commonly understood by most faculty
regarding its meaning and uses.
• The steering committee of the faculty assembly has, on their own
initiative, pursued this tactic and have gained faculty approval for a
process which will lead toward development of such a consensus
document.
• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2002-2003 academic
year.
• A series of faculty discussions were held during the spring semester and
a 3-day summer workshop to develop ideas related to an educational
philosophy at Covenant College.
Tactic 2: Develop a consensus organizational values document that is
commonly understood by employees of the College regarding its meaning and
uses. Also, develop tools that help examine the consistency of institutional
decisions with that document.
• Luncheons with all staff have been hosted by President Brock to discuss
the draft document he prepared on Operational Values. Gary Raffaele
has been asked to implement 360° reviews based on these principles.
• Gary Raffaele (Human Resources) is working with the administration to
determine who needs more training. Once this list is identified, Gary
and the administration will develop a training plan for all those with
extensive supervisory responsibility. We will incorporate the Shared
Responsibility document into the management training program.
STRATEGY III:
Meet specific funding goals related to strengthening the College’s academic
culture through the creative development and efficient use of resources.
A. A long range Financial Plan will be developed which takes into account
concerns about tuition dependence, donor potential (both capital and
annual), and financial aid as required to build the academic culture of the
College.

<a id="ga30-p525"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=527 printed_page=525 -->

Tactic 1: A long-range financial plan will be completed within six months of
the completion of the academic and campus master plans. There is currently no
funding beyond existing budgets projected to be needed.
• This plan will be completed within six months after academic and
campus master plans are completed.
• The financial plan will be complete within 6 months of the completion of
the academic plan.
B. The Campus Master Plan will be updated to reflect the educational
priorities of this plan, addressing the long-range facility needs of the
College and assist in future resource management.
Tactic 1: The Campus Master Plan Phase 1 will be completed by March 1 as a
preliminary base for the development of a full Campus Master Plan. Phase I
will demonstrate the size campus available within our present road and
topographical confines. The Report will be drafted by S/L/A/M and presented
to the Campus Master Planning Team, the President’s Council, and the
Properties Committee of the Board.
• The first phase of the campus master plan was completed and presented
to the Campus Master Planning Team (CMPT), President’s Council and
the Board.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 2: The complete Master Plan will be developed based on the enrollment
size and physical constraints from Phase 1. The plan will include possible
expansion outside the physical constraints from Phase 1 but retaining our
present atmosphere of academic integration. This too will be drafted by
S/L/A/M and presented to the Campus Master Planning Team; the President’s
Council, and the Properties Committee of the Board.
• The final phase of the campus master plan is almost complete. A final
workshop was held with the CMPT in the fall o f2000 and accepted. The
final report is being prepared and will be approved by the CMPT, and
President’s Council prior to the presentation to the Board of Trustees on
March 22, 2001.
• Final Draft of Campus Master Plan was presented to the Board and
adopted in March 2001.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.

<a id="ga30-p526"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=528 printed_page=526 -->

Tactic 3: Plans for a new library will be developed and the project included in
the next capital campaign. Construction to begin upon completion of funding.
A library building committee will develop specific program statements for the
library. That committee will be comprised of the Library Committee
members, additional faculty, and a significant student representation.
• The CMPT has been assigned the task to examine the recommendations
in the Campus Master Plan to confirm what additional functions should
be housed in the new library building. That work is well under way and
being coordinated with the Library Building Team. That team has meet
several times to hear the report of the library consultant (see ID1), and
discuss the issue of a multi-use building. At present, space needs and
corresponding costs are being developed to better inform the College of
the capital fund raising implications of the program.
• A library committee was formed which includes five teaching faculty
members.
• A private capital campaign is underway to raise $12 million for the
building and $8 million for endowment of the building operation and
book acquisition.
C. Develop an enrollment management plan to ensure access and retention of
a diverse population of qualified students.
Tactic 1: Reach consensus about the roles that gender balance and racial
diversity ought to play in the College’s admissions efforts and the ways
that Covenant’s commitment to diversity will be made visible.
• This is scheduled for implementation during the 2000-2001 academic
year.
• Dennis Miller has written a diversity plan that describes the College’s
position on these issues and activities accomplished in the areas of
diversity. It was agreed by the Planning Committee that any efforts to
balance gender must be done within the letter and spirit of the law.
Tactic 2: Hire an African-American recruiter who will help the College better
serve the higher education needs of the African-American community.
• Pam Lucas was hired during the summer o f2000 and has been assigned
the task of recruiting African-Americans. She also will help the College
better understand ways in which the College should serve these students.
• At present, there is an expectation of 27 new minority students (10
African-American) for fall 2001.

<a id="ga30-p527"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=529 printed_page=527 -->

Tactic 3: Establish a permanent enrollment management team.
• Mr. Charlie Phillips chaired the Ad Hoc Committee on Enrollment
Management consisting of the following individuals: Barb Schreur,
Jerry King, Rodney Miller, Paul Morton, Leda Goodman, Becky
Morton, and Charlie Phillips. The goal of the committees was
determined to be: To improve service to students and employees by
creating an opportunity for dialogue and problem-solving among those
operational areas of the College touching on student recruitment and
retention.
• President Brock assigned the responsibility to temporarily chair the
Enrollment Management Committee to Dean Raymond in the absence of
Mr. Phillips. The assignment asked for personal attention to problems
that have plagued the smooth transition of students over the years
leaving major Enrollment Management issues and directives to the
further work of a permanent committee for enrollment management.
Dean Raymond convened a temporary Enrollment Management Meeting
with Barb Schreur, Becky Morton, Jerry King and Rodney Miller to
discuss temporary initiatives that would seek to identify practices,
policies and people that may be diminishing the proper development of a
learning community.
• Areas being explored include undecided students, better information
management of student records, athletics, orientation (see IC7) and
academic advising.
• This responsibility has become a permanent assignment for Dean
Raymond.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 4: Explore a pilot joint venture with the Center for Christian Studies at
the University of Virginia.
• The Dean of Academic Administration (Dennis Miller) has been
working with the Director of the Center for Christian Studies at the
University of Virginia, Drew Trotter, during the summer and early fall to
develop an outline of possible cooperation between Covenant College
and the Center. This outline details a plan to offer Covenant College
courses for credit at the Center for students attending the University of
Virginia, with the potential for Covenant students to spend a semester at
UVA where they could avail themselves of both Covenant College and
UVA courses. President Frank Brock met with the Center for Christian
Studies Board in early November and was very well received. The Board
agreed to continue to study the feasibility of such a cooperation. Drew
Trotter met with President Brock, the Vice President of Academic

<a id="ga30-p528"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=530 printed_page=528 -->

Affairs, the Dean of Faculty, and the Dean of Academic Administration
at Covenant in late November. At that time it was agreed that Mr. Trotter
would take the original outline, and develop a formal proposal which
would be reviewed by both Covenant College and the Center for
Christian Studies. This proposal is to be completed sometime in early
2001. This was originally scheduled for implementation during the
2002-2003 academic year.
• A program is expected to begin at the University of Virginia fall 2002
and in the University of Maryland. Dr. Barker will participate in the U
of Maryland program.
Tactic 5: Raise $10,000 per student in designated funds to support newly
recruited diversity students.
• This is difficult to do not knowing how much would be needed.
However, the development office will attempt to find additional funds as
needed. A clearer picture should develop by April or May, 2001.
• There has been very little success in these efforts. All existing available
funds have been expended in an effort to matriculate the 27 students
mentioned in IIC2. $270,000 is requested for next year’s awarding cycle.
D. The budget process will be more closely linked to the tactics of the three-
year plan.
Tactic 1: The President’s Council will be asked to modify their present budget
process to include a set amount to fund the three-year plan to be implemented
during the Fall 2000 budget planning.
• The President’s Council set aside $199,000 for use by the Planning
Team for strategic initiatives. The Council will attempt to continue this

revenue projections, no additional revenue is available for plan
implementation. However, many efforts are underway to improve actual
revenues over projections. Plan strategies are considered to be one of
the top three priorities if additional revenues become available.
S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided. However, the process will be applied each year of the
plan.
Tactic 2: Each budget officer will develop a process of gathering budget
requests that ensures full College participation.
• Budget officers developed individual plans appropriate to each division.
The common element for each was the inclusion of a wider range of
participants in the funding requests phase of budget development.

<a id="ga30-p529"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=531 printed_page=529 -->

• Bob Harbert will evaluate the effectiveness of the process.
■S This tactic has been completed and no further update will be
provided.
Tactic 3: President’s council will publicize the budget process and the means
the council uses to choose among those requests, to the college community
each year as a part of the annual budget development process.
• The methodology used to develop the budget and the agendas and
minutes of the President’s Council were posted on the staff and faculty
e-boards during the fall o f2000 as the budget was being developed. This
will be done each year.
y sThis tactic has been completed and no further update will be

<a id="ga30-p530"></a>

REPORT OF
COVENANT THEOLOGICAL SEMINARY
TO THE 30™ GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
As we rejoice that the Lord has granted Covenant Theological
Seminary its 13th consecutive year of record enrollment, we have been humbled
by the limitations of our own wisdom to discern all that our God is calling us to
do. Even as we celebrate his great outpouring of blessing, our greatest challenge
has been determining how to steward the blessings that have been so abundant.
For instance, despite this Fall’s record enrollment, we have already received
many more 2002 M. Div. applications than we had last year at this same time.
As the Lord has provided such blessing “pressed down, shaken together
and overflowing,” the greatest challenge facing our faculty, administration and
trustees has been determining how to further enhance the quality and community
of pastoral instruction that has been Covenant’s hallmark. We remain convinced
that our primary calling is to train Biblically wise pastors in a community of
grace. The academic excellence and caring relationships that have drawn
students to Covenant in such numbers must be safeguarded against undisciplined
growth, even as we strive to fulfill our Gospel mission to prepare as many to
minister as we responsibly can.
With hearts committed both to our responsibility of offering the highest
quality pastoral education and to our responsibility of doing all that we can for
Christ’s Kingdom, our faculty and Board voted unanimously to raise the cap on
incoming M.Div. students that has been in effect since 1994. The number of new
M.Div. students admitted in the fall will be increased by ten, to 100. Increasing by
ten the number of students each year entering the three-year M.Div. degree, we
will add 30 to 40 M.Div. students to our student body over the next three to four
years. This new cap will probably not enable us to serve all who want to come,
but we pray that it will help us to identify and serve the highest quality candidates
for pastoral ministry. For such students, we believe that we can provide financial
and faculty resources without causing us to sacrifice the very quality and
community distinctives that have made us attractive to so many.
Our prayer is that the Lord will enable our churches to help us identify
the 100 leader students each year who have the greatest potential for pastoral
excellence, and that our Gospel commitments will be multiplied as we pour
ourselves into the training of these who will lead and train many more.
Our faculty and Board have unanimously agreed not to increase the
entering student M.Div. cap beyond 100 until we can demonstrate that our
growth will not jeopardize the quality education and nurturing community
dynamics that we believe are essential for pastoral training. This agreement has
led us to a renewed commitment to our core mission and distinctives to ensure
that the next generation of Covenant graduates is embracing the historic faith of

<a id="ga30-p531"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=533 printed_page=531 -->

our church and is properly prepared to minister these truths in our changing
world. This reaffirmation led us to identify the following distinctives of
Covenant Seminary that are reflected in the renewed Mission Statement
approved this year by our faculty and Board. Below are key distinctives,
blessings and opportunities for service that we discussed in our preparation of
the Mission Statement that appears at the end of this document:
I. Covenant Seminary’s Historic Distinctives
A. Commitment to the Inerrancy of Scripture
Covenant Seminary’s commitment to the absolute truth of God’s Word
is the bedrock of our belief. Not only does this commitment bind us to historic
orthodoxy, it ties us to an Evangelical movement worldwide that bases its faith
on what the Bible attests we are to believe concerning God and what duty God
requires of us.
Our commitment to Scripture as the basis of our faith and practice is
reflected not simply in written affirmation but in the sinew and bone of our
instruction and how we operate daily. Because we believe that the revealed
Word of God gives us supreme and infallible direction in all of life, we are
committed to:
• Expository preaching: training students to “say what God says” as
their only basis of authority in the pulpit whether their purpose is to
correct, or rebuke or encourage.
• Exegetical systematics: teaching students systematic theology
through emphasizing “what does the Bible say” rather than making
the major developments of human thought the focus of their
theology. The history and development of theology is certainly
taught, but our emphases is preparing pastors to answer the
question, “What does the Bible say.”
• Accessible and consistent original language instruction: equipping
students to see truth and preach truth from the Scriptures through
consistent training in and use of the original languages throughout
the M. Div. curriculum.
B. Commitment to Reformed Theology
Our commitment to the Reformed theology of the Westminster
Confession grows from our understanding that when the Scriptures are properly
interpreted and explained, what we call “Reformed” theology naturally springs
forth. Reformed theology is “Biblical” theology and that is why it has our
uncompromising loyalty.
Calvinist Understanding of Our Salvation
As those with the most basic understanding of what it means to be
reformed would expect, we affirm the historic Augustinian and Calvinist
perspectives regarding the sovereign work of God in our salvation.

<a id="ga30-p532"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=534 printed_page=532 -->

Additionally, we understand Reformed theology to require more of us than that
affirming that God saves his elect through grace alone as we stand against
Arminianism. We also understand the historic nature of God’s dealing with his
people in a covenant relationship to be essential to our Reformed understanding
of the unity of the Scriptures - and this Covenant theology is more than simply
standing against Dispensationalism.
Christ’s Lordship over the Whole of Life
Reformed theology is more than a Calvinistic view of predestination.
With a Covenantal view of God’s dealing with all his people and all his creation,
we understand Reformed theology to be an entire worldview - a perspective that
considers the Lordship of Christ over the whole of life. Through this view there
is no human enterprise, no created force or matter, no inch of cosmos that is not
subject to the Lordship of Christ. Thus, all of life past, present and future is
under his dominion, subject to his truth and is to be conformed to the purposes
of his glory through the lives of his people - all of whom are priests for his
worship in the sphere of life to which God has called them.
Since each person of God has a divine calling to influence his world for
Christ’s worship, we refuse to define ourselves by cultural isolation or suspicion.
we believe that we have a calling to reform our culture and that a culture being
redeemed has aspects of beauty and worth that Christians can properly
appreciate and seek to use (or transform) for Christ’s glory.
Grace for Justification and Sanctification
Because all of life is under the sovereign will of our God, we believe
that we are providing a needed voice in the church for renewing our
Confession’s emphasis on the necessity of grace for both justification and
sanctification. Our battles with Arminianism typically have made us focus more
on the role of grace at the beginning of the Christian life - in our justification -
than in our continuing sanctification. Yet, the joy and power for persevering in
Christian faithfulness come as we maintain the message of the Gospel for the
whole course of our lives. Since our deeds are only filthy rags to God, there is
too great a disproportion between our best works and God’s true holiness to
merit his favor. Our works are holy only as they are received by God in Christ,
thus grace must be at work even in the works that progressively sanctify our
lives to God’s purposes.
Our God’s willingness to receive the work of our humanly defiled hands
through the blood of his own Son is the grace that both motivates and enables
our service to him. Such a view of grace stimulates repentance, undermines
legalism and derails antinomianism. Consistent adulation of the mercy of God
in Christ is the sweet marrow of Reformed theology that grants God’s people the
Gospel power and will to combat both legalism and licentiousness. Thus, we
view it as important to help eyes to see, and lips to proclaim, the redemptive
message of God that proceeds from all the Scriptures.

<a id="ga30-p533"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=535 printed_page=533 -->

C. Commitment to the Church
We believe the Scriptures teach that the Church is the primary instrument of
personal and societal transformation. While believers are to function as salt and
light in all the world, the church is the only institution that God has deigned to be salt
and light to the whole world. In commitment to this institution of God’s calling, we
have made the preparation of leaders for Christ’s church our primaiy calling.
While we offer other degree and training programs, we place priority on
pastoral training that includes preparation for preaching, local church ministry,
church planting, mission sending and mission work through the church.
Because we are committed to the church as God’s instrument for the
transformation of souls and society, we submit ourselves to the oversight of the
Presbyterian Church in America. While we believe that God continues to work with
other denominations and churches throughout the world, we believe that it is our
Biblical responsibility to be accountable to a church as we are preparing future
leaders for the church.
II. Covenant Seminary’s Growing Distinctives
A. Commitment to Relational Preparation for Ministry
We are determined to be more than a divinity school, and are committed to
working with the local church in preparing future leaders for the church. Doing
theology in the context of the church keeps our teaching rooted in the purposes of
ministry and mission, and helps us identify teaching or attitudes that are harmful to
Christ’s purposes in the church. Teaching theology in the context of the church
helps students avoid the seminary temptation to do theology in the attack mode or in
the abstract or in arrogance - each of which can make their ministry a plague to the
church.
We try to teach students to value the theology that builds the church rather
than encouraging them to relish argument and theological debate. Important attitude
shifts occur when students learn to define orthodoxy in terms of right relationships
as well as right thought. To encourage insight into the kinds of relationships that
make for healthy churches, we make the co-curricular (what happens outside the
classroom) a vital component of each seminarian’s training for ministry. Field
service requirements, family nurture events, regular campus worship, accountability
and prayer groups, and the encouragement to live in community with one another
are among the ways that we seek to prepare students for the life of the church. Still,
no program is more important than the lives of the professors on display daily before
students. Godly, ministiy-oriented professors willing to teach, in and out of the
classroom, with gentleness and respect is the treasure God has most richly bestowed
upon us for preparing students for ministry in the church.
B. Commitment to Working with Christ’s Church Worldwide
As God has made us one of the larger seminaries in North America we
have increasingly felt our responsibility for the world beyond our familiar
church culture. We believe that God is working beyond our ranks and that it is

<a id="ga30-p534"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=536 printed_page=534 -->

our responsibility to prepare students to understand how they should participate
in divine purposes for the whole world.
Our faculty is increasingly desirous of a world mission emphasis
sweeping across our entire curriculum and being a part of every course that we
teach. As students lift their eyes to the grand purposes of God, they will not only
have a greater zeal for his purposes; they will also become more aware of how
much their own culture can limit their view of the riches of Christ’s Kingdom in
his people throughout the world. We have committed ourselves to helping
students see the Gospel riches that others possess in order make us all more
willing to respect, and learn from, believers in different cultures (both within
North America and around the world). As we learn how valuable and vital each
member of the body of Christ is for his Gospel purposes for the whole world, we
believe that believers in other cultures will also give us a more clear
understanding of all that God calls us to be for his name’s sake.
C. Commitment to Training Servants for a Changing World
We are realizing that our leadership position among seminaries causes
others to look to us for information on how to reach our rapidly changing and
increasingly secularized culture. Therefore, we are rapidly seeking to develop
materials and methods that enable our students and other church leaders to
minister in a world increasingly known for religious pluralism, political
correctness, compulsive consumerism, entertainment idolatry, racial separation,
mercy and justice indifference, family fragmentation, and a host of other societal
challenges.
To meet these challenges we are seeking to make the cultural outreach
emphases of the Francis Schaeffer Institute a more integral part of every
student’s Covenant Seminary experience. We are also providing apologetics
and Christian worldview conferences four times a year, so that students and
church leaders can learn from leading thinkers and practitioners how to minister
more effectively to our rapidly changing culture. Additionally, we provide
regular luncheon meetings and small group discussions with experienced
ministry leaders to prepare students for the challenges of contemporary culture.
To see how churches are ministering to this culture we are offering an array of
field service opportunities in local churches. Such field service is now a
graduation requirement for all M.Div. students, so that they will be involved in
