real ministry before assuming church leadership in this culture.
Finally, we have begun a series of continuing education programs for
those presently in ministry. The programs are designed to expose pastors and
other church leaders to peers and experts who will inform and inspire regarding
the ways that God is working in our changing world. We are organizing our
resources especially for the continuing support and training of Seminary
graduates during the first five years of their ministry when new ministers
typically most benefit from the insights and encouragement of experienced
church leaders.

<a id="ga30-p535"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=537 printed_page=535 -->

III. Covenant Seminary’s Gifts and Blessings:
A. Enrollment
In this thirteenth year of consecutive record enrollments, the Lord
brought 938 students from 44 states and 19 other nations this fall to
Covenant Seminary for the fall semester. Approximately 1100 students
throughout the entire year will have registered in Covenant Seminary classes.
PCA church and pastor referrals for incoming students remain very
strong and at highly increased levels over the past few years.
B. Finances
God enabled Covenant Theological Seminary to finish its fiscal year
in the black for this past year and through the last decade. During this year
of national tragedy and economic downturn, we have seen tremendous support
from our churches and donors during a time when we know their giving has
been sacrificial. We praise God for faithful supporters who are so committed to
training the next generation of church leaders that at the writing of this report
(March 2002) they have kept their rate of giving within 3% of last year.
We are thankful in this responsibility of reporting to our church to have
the privilege of thanking the many who have had such a clear vision of the need
to maintain a faithful witness to the next generation that they have given
generously to Covenant Seminary when economic pressures and national
uncertainty have made such giving difficult. Through such provision we remain
able (on average) to provide at least half of the tuition through financial aid
for each PCA student in the ordination track and under care of his presbytery.
As national crises have made us increasingly aware, the eternal things of
God are more secure and provide more hope than the greatest of human
achievements. As you consider the importance of the Gospel that is our hope, I
count it a privilege again to encourage your strong support of the institutions that
you believe most effectively provide the Word of God to this generation and the
next. God will use your faithfulness to further his Kingdom.
C. Placement
We praise God for his extraordinary blessing in providing placement for
the growing number of graduates. Every 2001 M.Div. graduate seeking
placement in an ordained ministry position and having Seminary
recommendation was placed (100% placement rate for the third
consecutive year). Over the past twelve years, over 98% percent of Master of
Divinity graduates with Seminary recommendation seeking ordained placement
have secured ministry positions. Over 95% of the graduates of the Master of
Arts in Counseling program have also continued to be placed. We remain
grateful for the confidence our churches have shown in Covenant graduates.
D. Pastor/Scholar Faculty
For over forty years, the Lord has provided Covenant Theological
Seminary an exceptionally gifted faculty of pastor/scholars to teach God’s

<a id="ga30-p536"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=538 printed_page=536 -->

inerrant Word. The professors at Covenant have the highest academic
credentials as well as significant experience in national or mission ministry.
Significant publications in progress and/or published by our Faculty in
the past year include:
Jerram Barrs The Heart of Evangelism (Crossway)
Women in the Word (Crossway)
Hans Bayer New Testament Introduction (Baker)
Bryan Chapell Holiness by Grace (Crossway)
I ’ll Love You Anyway and Always (Crossway)
Jack Collins The God of Miracles (IVP-UK)
Old Testament editor for the English Standard
Version of the Bible (Crossway)
Dan Doriani The Life of a God-made Man (Crossway)
Putting the Truth to Work: The Theory and
Practice of Bible Application (P & R)
Robert Peterson Adopted by God (P & R Publishing)
Robert Vasholz Commentary on Leviticus (Focus)
Richard Winter Still Bored in a Culture of Entertainment (IVP)
In addition to writing pursuits and their full-time teaching duties our
professors remain engaged in many avenues of ministry and mission. For
example:
Jerram Barrs (Christianity and Contemporary Culture
Professor) ministered in Slovakia and the Netherlands.
Hans Bayer (New Testament Professor) ministered in Germany,
Latvia and Hungary this year.
Dan Doriani (New Testament Professor and Dean of Faculty)
ministered in Hungary and Austria.
Phil Douglass (Church Planting and Renewal Professor)
continues to pastor Emmanuel Presbyterian Church in a
renewal ministry that uses many seminary interns.
Zack Eswine (Homiletics Professor) is teaching Homiletics in
the Ukraine and ministers regularly at a local
retirement/nursing center.
Donald Guthrie (Christian Education Professor and Academic
Vice President) is chair-elect of the Evangelical Seminary
Deans’ Council.
Nelson Jennings (World Mission Professor) continues to
conduct mission trips for seminaiy students and others.
Areas visited this past year include Central Europe and
the Middle East.
David Jones (Theology and Ethics Professor) chaired the
Evangelical Task Group on “The Public Character of

<a id="ga30-p537"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=539 printed_page=537 -->

Theological Education” for the Association of
Theological Schools.”
Jim Meek (Assoc. Dean for Academics) is engaged ina major
study of current church leaders for Pulpit and Pew.
Robert Peterson (Systematics Professor) served as interim
pastor for Hope Presbyterian Church in Collinsville, IL.
Dan Zink (Biblical Counseling Professor) directs the New Hope
Counseling Ministry of Chesterfield Presbyterian Church.
E. Facilities
In keeping with its desire to conduct pastoral preparation in a
community context, Covenant made a major investment in acquiring the Gulf
Drive apartment complex for married students. The Gulf Drive apartments that
many seminary families have enjoyed in recent years were acquired by the
Seminary when the owner, who has been so kind to students, indicated his plans
to retire and sell the property. Anticipating that new ownership would not
maintain the family character and economical pricing of the apartments,
Covenant purchased the entire 44-unit complex so that many more students will
have the opportunity to live together as a Covenant community during their
seminary years. The Gulf Drive complex adjoins the Atherton Drive apartments
that are also owned by Covenant. Both apartment complexes allow us to offer
students very reasonably priced housing in a centrally located area. We
anticipate providing a shuttle service from the apartments to help students with
transportation costs and to ease the parking pressures on the Seminary campus.
IV. Covenant Seminary’s Opportunities to Serve
A. Covenant’s ACCESS Program
Over 160 students enrolled in Covenant ACCESS, the distanceeducation training program introduced at last year’s General Assembly.
Through ACCESS, students around the world are earning accredited
degrees and receiving lay training over the Internet and through
audio/visual resources.
ACCESS is one of only three such programs accredited by the
Association of Theological Schools to offer Master of Arts degrees. As a result
we are now serving present and future church leaders throughout the world who
want advanced seminary training but are not able to move to a seminary.
Church officers, Sunday School teachers, missionaries, campus ministry
workers, and devoted lay persons from all walks of life are receiving the
highest quality seminary training both in the Master of Arts degree program
and through a variety of non-degree, media resources.
Students preparing for ordained ministry in the PCA can also earn
significant portions of their M. Div. degree through ACCESS. We are
committed to residential training for full pastoral preparation that we believe
should involve a community of peers, professors and local churches. Still, we

<a id="ga30-p538"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=540 printed_page=538 -->

want to share with our church and world the wonderful resources that God has
given us to defend and propagate faith in the inerrancy of his Word and the
wonder of his grace.
B. Youth in Ministry Institute
The Youth in Ministry Institute (YIMI) continues to receive accolades
for training youth ministers and for providing high-school-age youth with
seminary resources to think in a deeper way about vocational ministry.
At last year’s General Assembly, we informed you that we had been
invited to apply for increased funding from the Lilly Endowment for this
program. We rejoice now to announce that we were granted an additional $600-
thousand for the continuance of this important program for the future of our
Church’s youth and youth pastors.
C. Francis Schaeffer Institute
The mission of the Francis Schaeffer Institute is to help Christians
address the concerns of our culture with the claims of Christ.
The “Understanding Culture” lectures of the Schaeffer Institute this year
were:
Breathtaking Decisions: Bioethics from a Christian
Perspective
Yearning for Glory: Understanding Christ’s Lordship in the
Arts, Business, Education, Counseling, Science and
Medicine.
The “Witness to the World” apologetics lectures included:
Two Worlds Under One Roof: A Biblical Look at Youth
Culture (jointly sponsored with YIMI and the Counseling
Department)
Christian Witness to Islam, Judaism and Buddhism
The popular Friday Nights at the Institute where students and professors
address contemporary topics at local, secular bookstores continues to equip our
church’s present and future leaders to introduce a Christian world view to
persons outside traditional church circles. Churches around the country continue
to use the model begun at Covenant Seminary as a way of reaching un-churched
persons in their communities. Topics addressed on Friday nights this year have
included:
Longing for Belonging: Hospitality in a Homeless Culture
Still Bored in a Culture of Entertainment: Rediscovering
Passion and Wonder
D. Field Service
Increasingly we are cooperating with local churches in the training of
students for ministry. We require Covenant Seminary students to “train for
ministry in the context of the church” in order to receive their degree. We see
as increasingly important the requirement that every ordination-track student

<a id="ga30-p539"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=541 printed_page=539 -->

have at least 300 hours of field service ina local ministry supervised by a local
pastor or session. Our goal is to engage heart as well as mind, and to use church
experiences as well as academic challenges to instill wisdom regarding God’s
Word and love for his people.
Conclusion
As we consider the outpouring of blessing from our Lord in maintaining
our faithfulness to the historic distinctives of Covenant Theological Seminary, in
expanding our current mission, in providing expanding opportunities for our
service, and in allowing us to have the resources to pursue this mission through
the generosity of his people, our hearts fill with praise for him. We now pray
that our God will use this grace to fill our hearts with the gratitude that grants
continued strength for his purposes and submission to his will - with loving zeal
for his Son.
Respectfully Submitted,
/s/ Bryan Chapell
President

Recommendations
1. That the General Assembly give thanks to God for the ministry of Covenant
Theological Seminary, for its faithfulness to the Scriptures, the Reformed
faith, and the Great Commission, for its students and graduates, its faculty
and staff, its Trustees, and for those who support the Seminary through their
prayers and gifts.
2. That the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of Covenant Theological
Seminary by contributing the Partnership Shares approved by the General Assembly.
3. That the minutes of the meetings of the Board of Trustees of Covenant
Theological Seminary for April 27-28, 2001, September 28-29, 2001, and
February 1-2, 2002 be approved; and that the minutes of the meeting of the
Executive Committee of the Board of Trustees for December 7, 2001,
March 1, 2002 and March 22, 2002 be approved.
4. That the financial audit for Covenant Theological Seminary for the fiscal
year ending June 30, 2001 by Humes and Barrington, CPA be received.
5. That the proposed 2002-2003 budget of Covenant Theological Seminary be
approved.
6. That the Assembly pray for the ministry of Covenant Theological Seminary.

<a id="ga30-p540"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=542 printed_page=540 -->

# COVENANT THEOLOGICAL SEMINARY MISSION STATEMENT

The purpose of Covenant Theological Seminary is to train servants of the triune God to walk with God, to interpret and communicate God's Word, and to serve and lead God's people.

## CORE VALUES

The following core values amplify, clarify and protect the Seminary's understanding of its purpose.

- **Christ-centered ministry**: We believe that a seminary education is successful only if—at its end—the student knows Jesus Christ more intimately than at its beginning.
- **Biblical authority**: We believe that the Bible is the Word of God and thus it is our only infallible rule of faith and practice. We believe in the plenary, verbal inspiration of the Scriptures by the Holy Spirit and, thus, we affirm the inerency of the original manuscripts whose objective truth it is our responsibility to interpret in accord with the principles of Scripture and to proclaim in accord with the imperatives of the Gospel.
- **Grace foundation**: We believe that the foundation for all that we do must be the Gospel of grace—our absolute confidence in God's acceptance provided through his redemptive work as the supreme motivation and enablement for love and holiness.
- **Relational emphasis**: We believe that the relationship between students and professors must take a meaningful place alongside teaching content, so that we may affect the entire character of the student for ministry. Therefore, we seek to develop a faculty of pastor-scholars and a staff which both individually and as a community effectively model what it means to walk with God, interpret and communicate God's Word, and lead God's people.
- **Pastoral training**: We believe that our primary task is to train students for pastoral ministry (including church planting, campus ministry, chophiracy, and missionary service). To accomplish this task, we have gathered a faculty of pastor-scholars experienced in ministry (as understood by our denominational standards and institutional history), as well as other educational resources. These enable us to serve other students and the broader Christian community, while continuing to enhance pastoral training. To strengthen pastoral training and all of our educational programs, we continually seek to assess and improve student learning and development.
- **Church leadership**: We believe that, as the national seminary of the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA), it is our responsibility to provide intellectual training and ministry models that are true to the Westminster Standards and the historic distinctives of Presbyterian orthodoxy, while equipping the next generation of Christian servants for effective church leadership in a changing world. At the same time, because we recognize that a seminary alone can never fully equip students for these tasks, we seek to work in partnership with local churches to accomplish our purpose.
- **Kingdom perspective**: We believe that God's purpose is the gathering of his people from every nation and the renewal of all things. He calls his church to active involvement with the world's peoples and cultures, carrying out the mission of bringing the Gospel to those who do not believe and expressing Christ's lordship in every area of life. In order to train students to make disciples of the nations, our faculty, staff, and students must increasingly reflect the ethnic and cultural richness of God's worldwide church.

## EDUCATIONAL PROGRAMS

The Seminary seeks to fulfill its purpose through the following educational programs.

- Covenant Seminary seeks to fulfill its purpose primarily by preparing students for pastoral ministry in accordance with the standards of the PCA. As the national seminary of the PCA, Covenant seeks particularly to prepare students for the pastoral ministry of that denomination. At the same time, Covenant actively seeks to serve students preparing for ministry in a variety of other denominational and non-denominational contexts. The Master of Divinity program is the first professional degree preparing students primarily for pastoral ministry. The Master of Theology and Doctor of Ministry degrees provide additional academic and practical training for students already holding the M.Div. degree.
- Covenant Seminary seeks to fulfill its purpose by preparing students for other non-vocational and vocational roles in churches and other Christian ministries. The Master of Arts (Theological Studies) and the Graduate Certificate provide biblical and theological training that equips lay people to bring an informed Christian perspective to a variety of secular occupations and non-ordained ministries. The Master of Arts (Eusgritical Theology) is an academic and research degree which prepares students pursuing academic leadership for advanced study at the doctoral level. The Master of Arts in Counseling prepares students to work as counselors especially in church-related settings, and for state licensure and work in other agencies.
- Covenant Seminary seeks to fulfill its purpose by providing a variety of lifelong learning opportunities for individuals and churches, including adult continuing education classes, seminars, publications and media resources, as well as by providing theological leadership for the PCA and the Christian community through such means as faculty publications, service on denominational and professional committees, and speaking opportunities.

## COMPETENCIES

In fulfilling this purpose, Covenant seeks to develop the following competencies in its graduates. We believe that each of these competencies is intimately and necessarily related to the others. Effective ministry requires spiritually sensitive application and integration of all of these competencies.

... to walk with God ...
- **Grace**: Understands and is personally committed to the Gospel of grace as understood in our standards.
- **Lifestyle**: Exhibits spiritual maturity and Christ-like character growing out of the love of Christ.
- **Servanthood**: Demonstrates a heart to serve God and others in one's family, church, and world.

... to interpret and communicate God's Word ...
- **Knowledge**: Understands and clearly explains key biblical and theological facts and concepts in historical perspective.
- **Theological skills**: Demonstrates ability to interpret and thoughtfully apply the Bible (in the original languages where appropriate) to issues of doctrine, life, and ministry.
- **Conviction**: Holds and articulates a coherent Christian theology and worldview informed by our doctoral standards, and interacts critically and respectfully with other approaches.
- **Communication**: Communicates effectively both orally and in writing.

... and to lead God's people.
- **Relational Skills**: Relates to others with evident respect, sensitivity, and concern to serve, even when there are differences of culture, belief, or values.
- **Leadership**: Leads others in walking with God in accordance with gifts and callings (e.g., preaching, teaching, counseling, evangelizing, mercy ministry)—modeling Christ's courage, love and humility.
- **Christ's Lordship**: Seeks to bring Christ's lordship to bear in all areas of life and culture—individual and corporate, private and public.
- **World Vision**: Seeks to advance the cause of Christ among diverse peoples and cultures within North America and throughout the world.

<a id="ga30-p541"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=543 printed_page=541 -->

INTERCHURCH RELATIONS COMMITTEE REPORT
TO THE THIRTIETH GENERAL ASSEMBLY, 2002

Membership
TE Paul R. Gilchrist RE Jack Merry
TE Bruce Howes RE David R. Lambert
TE Derek Thomas, Chairman RE Robert Ashlock, Secretary
TE Wm. S. Barker, Alternate RE John L. Marshall, Alternate
TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk (Ex officio member, “RAO” 3-2, j)
TE Ken Thompson (Advisory member, MTW consultant)
II. Meetings
September 25, 2001
December 18, 2001
March 26, 2002
III. Information Items
A. Since the last meeting of the General Assembly the North
American Presbyterian and Reformed Council (NAPARC) has
voted to continue discussions among the constituent member
Churches regarding the possibilities of eventual Church union.
B. At its 2001 fall meeting NAP ARC unanimously voted to
recommend to the General Assemblies and General Synods of the
constituent member Churches to terminate the membership in
NAP ARC of the Christian Reformed Church in North America.
C. The second major issue NAPARC dealt with was the report of a
study committee that had been appointed the previous year to
collate the similarities and differences in theology, polity, and
practice of the NAPARC member Churches with a view toward
possible Church union among them. TE R. Irfon Hughes (PCA),
TE Bruce Stewart (RPCNA) and TE Ronald L. Potter (RCUS)
served on the committee. There was a general discussion of the
report and some additional information included. The council
decided to continue the study committee and that each
denomination’s Interchurch Relations Committee appoint
members of their choosing to serve on the committee as the

<a id="ga30-p542"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=544 printed_page=542 -->

discussions continue. The committee is to work out a mutually
acceptable definition of “organic Church union.” There is a union
of a sort existing already among the NAPARC Churches by virtue
of their being part of the same council. The major matters that we
hold in common are 1) a commitment to the inerrancy and
authority of Scripture, 2) a commitment to the Reformed Faith and
Covenant Theology, and 3) a commitment to representative church
government. There are differences among us, however, some of
which are negotiable and others that may not be capable of
resolution sufficiently to effect Church union. For example the
raison d ’etre of the Korean American Presbyterian Church is to
minister to the Korean immigrant community. The RCUS uses
the Three Forms of Unity rather than the Westminster Standards.
There are many similarities and few differences between the two.
However, the RCUS is a strict subscriptionist denomination (like
the CRCNA) and the PCA has never adopted a strict
subscriptionist position. The issue of subscription is being debated
within the PCA presently. The RPCNA holds to exclusive a
capella psalmody. A proposal formerly suggested in the 1980’s
was to have theological affinity presbyteries within a merged
Church to accommodate those who hold to exclusive a capella
psalmody. The OPC has a view of church government more
influenced by Hodge’s views than by Thomwell. The ARP
initiated the renewal of possible union talks within NAPARC.
Talks will continue as we work through the various issues.
D. The observer from the United Reformed Churches of North
America indicated that the URCNA does not intend to join
NAPARC within the next few years.
E. The observer from L’Eglise Reformee du Quebec (the Reformed
Church of Quebec) indicated that L’Eglise Reformee du Quebec is
desirous of being received into NAPARC. They had sent a letter
of request to the former secretary of NAPARC that somehow was
not delivered. The matter will come up next year.Itwas
suggested that denominations that had planted churches inQuebec
(PCA and OPC) that are now part of the ERQ send letters of
endorsement to NAPARC regarding the ERQ.
F. General Assemblies and Synods for 2002 will be asfollows:
1. ar P- June 10-14 - Bonclarken, NC
2. KAPC - May 21-24-Chicago, IL

<a id="ga30-p543"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=545 printed_page=543 -->

3. PCA - June 18-21 - Birmingham, AL
4. RCUS - May 20-23 - Sutton, NB
5. RPCNA- July 6-12 - Parksville, MO
6. O PC - June 12-19 - Wenham, MA
G. Since the last written report of the IRC to the General Assembly,
the President of the National Association of Evangelicals, Dr.
Kevin Mannoia, has resigned. Dr. Leith Anderson, Pastor of the
Wooddale Church (Evangelical Free Church), is serving as interim
President.
H. Due to the transitional situation within the NAE (ex. The
resignation of the President, the relocation of the NAE Offices,
financial exigencies, there being no 2002 General Convention of
the NAE, etc.), the matter of the membership rule change to allow
denominations who are members of the National Council of
Churches to be admitted to the NAE contingent upon their
subscribing to the NAE doctrinal statement has been postponed.
The PCA Stated Clerk now serves on the Executive Committee of
the NAE and will keep the matter before the NAE when it
reformulates its rules.
IV. Items Discussed and Actions Taken by the Committee
A. A Rationale to North American Presbyterian and Reformed
Council (NAPARC) for the termination of the membership of the
Christian Reformed Church in North America in NAPARC be
prepared and submitted. The CRCNA delegation to NAPARC in
2000 requested that the PCA delegation present to NAPARC in
2001 a Rationale for the Termination of the CRCNA’s
membership in NAPARC. The IRC prepared such a rationale (see
“Attachment A” below) and presented it to the 2001 meeting of
NAPARC. Your IRC believes that the CRCNA circumvented its
constitution to effect the ordination of women as ministers and
ruling elders.
In 1995 the General Synod of the CRCNA approved a motion that
the word “male” in the section of the CRCNA Church Order,
Article 3-a, that restricted the offices of minister and ruling elder
to men be “declared inoperative” by classes (presbyteries) who
wished to do so. the action was taken by a vote of a general
Synod rather than through the process of actually revising the

<a id="ga30-p544"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=546 printed_page=544 -->

Church Order by the ordinary process. As a result, NAPARC
began the process in 1996 to suspend the CRCNA from voting
privileges in NAPARC. In 1998, the suspension went into effect.
CRCNA Synod 2000 reaffirmed the controversial decision of
Synod 1995.
At the 2000 meeting of NAPARC, after consideration of the
termination of the membership of the CRCNA in NAPARC
(initiated by the PCA), the matter was deferred to the 2001
meeting with the request that the Presbyterian Church in America
delegation provide a specific rationale for the proposed action. A
Rationale was prepared by the PCA-IRC and mailed to the
NAPARC delegations before the 2001 council meeting. The
CRCNA presented their response at the 2001 meeting (see
“Attachment B”).
At the 2000 meeting, the CRCNA delegation offered several
reasons why the CRCNA should not be dismissed from NAPARC.
Their principal arguments were 1) A Theological-historical
Argument, based on the Belgic Confession Article XXX, 2) A
Constitutional Argument based on NAPARC’s article II. Basis of
the Council, (the inerrancy and authority of Scripture and doctrinal
integrity expressed through Reformed doctrinal standards) and 3)
Pastoral concerns regarding the past, present and future
relationship of CRCNA churches, ministers, officers and members
with one another should the CRCNA be dismissed from
NAPARC.
The CRCNA delegation argued that the French and Latin versions
of the Belgic confession, Article XXX used generic, rather than
gender-specific terms. In other words, that “persons” [both male
and female] could be ministers and ruling elders in the Church, not
just “men.” The CRCNA delegation did not argue from the whole
of Article XXX that focuses on who is fit for ministry. The PCA
delegation, in response, argued, that the Christian Reformed
Church, by reaffirming its stand in favor of the ordination of
women, has acted contrarily to Article XXX of the Belgic
Confession, which states concerning the government of, and
offices in, the church: “By these means every thing will be carried
on in the Church with good order and decency, when faithful men
are chosen, according to the rule prescribed by St. Paul to
Timothy.” Although the term for “faithful men” can be taken as
generic (“tels personnages ... fideles” in the French), the Scriptural

<a id="ga30-p545"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=547 printed_page=545 -->

references to Paul’s Pastoral Epistles (the footnote references are
“1 Tim. iii.l, etc.; Tit. i. 5, etc.”) provide gender-explicit language
(“mias gunaikos aner” = “husband of one wife”) which confines
the offices to men rather than women.
The CRCNA delegation argued that the NAPARC constitution
(Article II, BASIS OF THE COUNCIL) deals with theology, not
Church polity and that the matter of ordaining women was a
matter of polity, not theology. Article II reads as follows:
Confessing Jesus Christ as only Savior and Sovereign
Lord over all of life, we affirm the basis of the
fellowship of Presbyterian and Reformed Churches to be
full commitment to the Bible in its entirety as the Word
of God written, without error in all its parts and to its
teaching as set forth in the Heidelberg Catechism, the
Belgic Confession, the Canons of Dort, the Westminster Confession of Faith, and the Westminster Larger and
Shorter Catechisms.
That the adopted basis of fellowship be regarded as
warrant for the establishment of a formal relationship in
the nature of a council, that is, a fellowship that enables
the constituent churches to advise, counsel and
cooperate in various matters with one another and to
hold out before each other the desirability and need for
organic union of churches of like faith and practice. (II.
Basis of the Council.)
The CRCNA delegation argued that it would be inconsistent and
grievous for NAPARC to expel the CRCNA from membership in
NAPARC on the denominational level and at the same time have
sacramental communion with the CRC members, receive church
members by transfer, send young people to NAPARC educational
institutions, and transfer ministers across NAPARC
denominational lines.
After extended and earnest discussion by the various delegations,
the council approved the following resolution:
Resolved that NAPARC initiate the process terminating the
membership of the CRCNA in NAPARC and that NAPARC

<a id="ga30-p546"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=548 printed_page=546 -->

submit the matter to the General Assemblies and General
Synods for action on the following grounds:
* We believe that the CRCNA has had opportunity over
the last several years to reverse the controversial
action of CRCNA Synod 1995 in response to
NAPARC’s suspension of the CRCNA from voting-
member status in NAPARC. In 1996 the process was
begun in NAPARC (initiated by the PCA) to suspend
the CRCNA from NAPARC membership after
CRCNA Synod 1995 had approved the ordination of
women to the offices of ruling elder and minister. The
underlying hermeneutical issue was cited by the PCA
in correspondence between the two highest
judicatories of the PCA and CRCNA (Minutes of the
25th General Assembly of the PCA, 1997, pp. 375-
383). In 1998, the suspension from membership was
effected. CRCNA Synod 2000 did not reverse but
reaffirmed and approved the ordination of women to
the offices of ruling elder and minister.
■ We believe that the CRCNA has acted contrarily to
the NAPARC constitution (II. Basis of the Council),
against the teaching of the Word of God and the
Belgic Confession, Article XXX by declaring the
word male in Article 3-a of the Church Order to be
inoperative and authorizing the churches under its
jurisdiction (should they desire) to ordain and install
women in the offices of elder, minister, and
evangelist, thus circumventing her own constitution.
the general assemblies and general synods of the NAPARC-
member denominations must now vote on the issue within the next
three years for it to go into effect.
After the council vote was taken, Dr. David Engelhard, General
Secretary of the CRCNA expressed his sadness that the council
had taken the action. Dr. Engelhard pointed out that the CRCNA
had been instrumental in the formation of NAPARC. He noted,
however, that the CRCNA, in his opinion, would not regard the
action as an act of ecclesiastical discipline. He then read from II
Corinthians 13:11-14 and the CRCNA delegation departed. It
was a solemn moment.

<a id="ga30-p547"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=549 printed_page=547 -->

B. The IRC elected TE Irfon Hughes (former IRC chairman) and the
Stated Clerk to serve on the NAPARC Committee that continues
discussions regarding the possibilities of Church union.
C. The IRC decided to ask MNA to provide information regarding
L’Eglise Reformee du Quebec.
D. The IRC voted (in September, 2001) to correspond with the
United Reformed Churches in response to their letters to us, and
open conversations with them regarding possible formal relations.
The United Reformed Churches is a Reformed denomination
formed by churches in the USA and Canada that felt conscience-
bound to leave the CRCNA. The IRC voted in December 2001 to
recommend to the Assembly that the PCA enter into
corresponding relations with the United Reformed Churches.
E. Representatives to general synods and general assemblies of other
NAPARC churches for 2002 were appointed as follows:
1. ARP (June 10-14, Bonclarken, NC) TE L. Roy Taylor
2. KAPC (May 21-24, Chicago, IL) TE Michael Marcey
3. RCUS (May 20-23, Sutton, NB) (Chairman Thomas will appoint)
4. RPCNA (July 6-12, Parksville, MO) TE Daniel Dermyer
5. OPC (July 12-19, Wenham, MA) TE Timothy Keller or
TE Terry Gyger

#### V. Recommendations

A. That Fraternal Delegates, Corresponding Delegates, and Ecclesiastical
Observers be welcomed and invited to address the Assembly.
B. That other visiting ministers, including the Rev. Turgay Uchal, be
introduced to the Assembly (BCO 13-13).
C. That the PCA enter into corresponding relations with the United
Reformed Churches of North America.
D. That the Assembly approve the termination of the membership in the
North American Presbyterian and Reformed Council of the Christian
Reformed Church in North America on the following grounds:
■ We believe that the CRCNA has had opportunity over the last
several years to reverse the controversial action of CRCNA Synod
1995 in response to NAPARC’s suspension the CRCNA from

<a id="ga30-p548"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=550 printed_page=548 -->

voting-member status in NAPARC. In 1996 the process was begun
in NAPARC (initiated by the PCA) to suspend the CRCNA from
NAPARC membership after CRCNA Synod 1995 had approved
the ordination of women to the offices of ruling elder and minister.
The underlying hermeneutical issue was cited by the PCA in
correspondence between the two highest judicatories of the PCA
and CRCNA (Minutes of the 25th General Assembly of the PCA,
1997, pp. 375-383). In 1998, the suspension from membership was
effected. CRCNA Synod 2000 did not reverse but reaffirmed and
approved the ordination of women to the offices of ruling elder and
minister.
* We believe that the CRCNA has acted contrarily to the NAPARC
constitution (II. Basis of the Council), against the teaching of the
Word of God and the Belgic Confession, Article XXX by declaring
the word male in Article 3-a of the Church Order to be inoperative
and authorizing the churches under its jurisdiction (should they
desire) to ordain and install women in the offices of elder, minister,
and evangelist, thus circumventing her own constitution.

<a id="ga30-p549"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=551 printed_page=549 -->

ATTACHMENT A
RATIONALE FOR ACTION REGARDING CRCNA MEMBERSHIP IN
NAPARC
INTRODUCTION
It should be noted that the PCA takes no delight in recommending that the
Christian Reformed Church in North America’s membership in NAPARC be
terminated. It grieves us that this action should be taken.
As background to this action, it needs to be noted that there is an
underlying hermeneutical problem that goes back at least as far as 1972,
culminating in the adoption of Report 44 by the Synod of the CRCNA (see pp.
493-546, Acts of Synod, 1972). The issue of hermeneutics was raised in
NAPARC in the early 1980’s when an Interim Study Committee on
Hermeneutics was erected. This committee reported to NAPARC in 1983
(NAPARC Minutes, 1983, item 10, including Appendix J, “A Reformed
Testimony on Hermeneutics”). The report outlines the issues, clearly
expressing the concerns about views held on Scripture. It has been understood
by most NAPARC Churches that these views represented a change of position
within the CRCNA regarding the Word of God/The Holy Scriptures, which
has resulted in the ordination of women to the offices of ruling elder and
minister. In 1996 the process was begun in NAPARC (initiated by the PCA)
to suspend the CRCNA from NAPARC membership after CRCNA Synod
1995 had approved the ordination of women to the offices of ruling elder and
minister. The underlying hermeneutical issue was cited by the PCA in
correspondence between the two highest judicatories of the PCA and CRCNA
(Minutes of the 25th General Assembly of the PCA, 1997, pp. 375-383). In
1998, the suspension from membership was effected. CRCNA Synod 2000
did not reverse but reaffirmed and approved the ordination of women to the
offices of ruling elder and minister.
At the 2000 meeting of NAPARC, after consideration of the termination
of the membership of the CRCNA in NAPARC (initiated by the PCA), the
matter was deferred to the 2001 meeting with the request that the Presbyterian Church in America delegation provide a specific rationale for the proposed
action. The minutes record (XXVII. NEW BUSINESS),
In light of Communication 2 from the PCA General Assembly
concerning the CRCNA, it was moved to terminate the
membership of the CRCNA in NAPARC since the CRC has
not rescinded its position on the ordination of women to the
office of ruling elder and minister.

<a id="ga30-p550"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=552 printed_page=550 -->

The procedural motion was made to delay the vote on whether
to terminate the membership of the CRCNA in NAPARC until
the meeting of 2001 and that the PCA be asked to formulate
grounds for such an action. Adopted.
At the 2000 meeting the CRCNA delegation offered several reasons why
the CRCNA should not be dismissed from NAPARC. As we recall, their
principal arguments were 1) A Theological-historical Argument, based on the
Belgic Confession Article 30, 2) A Constitutional Argument based on
NAPARC’s article II. Basis of the Council, and 3) Pastoral concerns regarding
the past, present and future relationship of CRCNA churches, ministers,
officers and members with one another should the CRCNA be dismissed from
NAPARC,
The PCA offers the following rationale, in response to the request of the
2000 meeting of NAPARC. It is the general direction, i.e. the hermeneutical
issue, we believe, that has resulted in the CRCNA’s change of position on the
particular issue of the ordination of women and has brought us to this
lamentable juncture. That general matter has been addressed previously as
noted above. There are three basic reasons why we believe that NAPARC
should proceed with the proposed membership termination. These are, 1)
Theological-Historical Considerations, 2) the Issue of Constitutional Integrity,
and 3) Pastoral Concerns.

### I. THEOLOGICAL-HISTORICAL CONSIDERATIONS

The Christian Reformed Church, by reaffirming its stand in favor of the
ordination of women, has acted contrarily to Article XXX of the Belgic
Confession, which states concerning the government of, and offices in, the
church: “By these means every thing will be carried on in the Church with
good order and decency, when faithful men are chosen, according to the rule
prescribed by St. Paul to Timothy.” Although the term for “faithful men” can
be taken as generic (“tels personnages ... fideles” in the French), the Scriptural
references to Paul’s pastoral epistles (the footnote references are “1 Tim. iii.l,
etc.; Tit. i. 5, etc.”) provide gender-explicit language (“mias gunaikos aner” =
“husband of one wife”), which confines the offices to men rather than women.
Historically the Reformed churches of the Reformation era restricted the
offices to men, as shown in what John H. Leith termed “the most universal of
Reformed Creeds” (CREEDS OF THE CHURCH, 1963, page 131), the
Second Helvetic Confession of 1566, which also has the largest section on the
ministers of the church, their institution and offices. Chapter XVIII.8 says:

<a id="ga30-p551"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=553 printed_page=551 -->

"Not any one may be elected, but capable men distinguished by sufficient consecrated learning, pious eloquence, simple wisdom, lastly, by moderation and an honorable reputation, according to that apostolic rule which is compiled by the apostle in I Tim., ch. 3, and Titus, ch. 1." (translation of Arthur C. Cochrane in REFORMED CONFESSIONS OF THE 16TH CENTURY, 1966, page 271) Again, the expression “capable men” can be taken as generic (“homines idonei” in the Latin), but the Scripture references lead to gender-explicit language.

Clearly, the intent of the Reformed confessions of the sixteenth century, and the practice of the Reformed churches, was to confine the offices of the church to men.¹

## II. THE ISSUE OF CONSTITUTIONAL INTEGRITY

We wish to call the attention of NAPARC not only to the constitution of NAPARC but also to the CRCNA’s constitution as well. The NAPARC constitution states,

Confessing Jesus Christ as only Savior and Sovereign Lord over all of life, we affirm the basis of the fellowship of Presbyterian and Reformed Churches to be full commitment to the Bible in its entirety as the Word of God written, without error in all its parts and to its teaching as set forth in the Heidelberg Catechism, the Belgic Confession, the Canons of Dort, the Westminster Confession of Faith, and the Westminster Larger and Shorter Catechisms.

That the adopted basis of fellowship be regarded as warrant for the establishment of a formal relationship in the nature of a council, that is, a fellowship that enables the constituent churches to advise, counsel and cooperate in various matters with one another and to hold out before each other the desirability and need for organic union of churches of like faith and practice. (II. Basis of the Council.)

¹ In Presbyterian circles, the ordination of women to the office of minister is of twentieth century origin as well. In 1956 Margaret Towner was the first woman to be ordained as a minister of the United Presbyterian Church. In 1965 Rachel Henderlite was the first woman ordained by the Presbyterian Church in the United States. (See “The Presbyterian Survey,” Bicentennial Issue, June 1988, pp. 97, 98).

<a id="ga30-p552"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=554 printed_page=552 -->

We remind the council that the primary basis of our fellowship is the Bible
and that our doctrinal standards are secondary to the Bible. Whether or not a
teaching or practice is consistent with the Word of God is of chief concern as a
foundation for mutual theological accountability within the Council.
There is also the matter of the CRCNA’s setting aside the explicit
provision of its constitution by the action of Synod, without going through the
constitutional process of actually amending the constitution.
A classis may, in keeping with its understanding of the biblical
position on the role of women in ecclesiastical office and in
response to local needs and circumstances, declare the word
male in Article 3-a of the Church Order is inoperative and
authorize the churches under its jurisdiction to ordain and
install women in the offices of elder, minister, and evangelist.
(See Acts of Synod 2000, pp. 687-95, 696-99, 706.)
We realize that the issue of the ordination of women is a controversial and
emotionally charged matter. We also recognize that families and friends as
well as churches and classes are divided on this matter. Some in our culture
regard it as a civil rights issue rather than a theological issue. We understand
that the CRCNA sought some solution to a controversial issue that many
denominations face. We believe, however, that the CRCNA has disregarded
its own constitution by the expediency of declaring a provision of its Church
Order to be inoperative rather than going through the process of amending that
article of the Church Order. No Reformed Church should resort to an
expedient circumventing of its constitution in dealing with theological issues.
III. PASTORAL CONCERNS
From the earnest discussion in the 2000 NAPARC meeting and from the
printed materials the CRCNA delegation presented to the 2000 NAPARC
meeting, it is clearly evident that the majority of classes, churches, and
members do not favor the policy adopted by Synod 1995 and Synod 2000 to
“declare the word male in Article 3-a of the Church Order is inoperative and
authorize the churches under its jurisdiction to ordain and install women in the
offices of elder, minister, and evangelist.” The enactment of that policy has
resulted in a number of churches leaving the CRCNA, and has divided
churches, classes, and families.
NAPARC Churches have appreciated the fellowship and joint ministries
between themselves and the CRCNA. The PCA churches and church courts
have been happy to serve with our CRCNA brothers and sisters in mercy

<a id="ga30-p553"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=555 printed_page=553 -->

ministries such as Bethany Christian Services, pro-life advocacy, counseling
and adoption ministries. There has been a transfer of ministers and members
between the CRCNA and other NAPARC Churches. In churches where open
communion is practiced, we have enjoyed fellowship at the Table of the Lord.
Our young people have attended educational institutions within NAPARC
constituency. There have been marriages within the NAPARC family. We
treasure these relationships. Such associations may continue, in the event the
CRCNA is no longer a constituent member of NAPARC. Local and individual
fellowship may be maintained regardless of what action the Council and the
General Assemblies and Synods of NAPARC take, just as many of us have
fellowship with other Christian friends in churches and communions not
associated with NAPARC. as churches we are to “advise and counsel” one
another. Since our advice and counsel, and subsequent suspension of the
CRCNA from NAPARC did not have the desired effect of the Synod’s
reversing its position, we earnestly pray that the more serious action of
dismissing her from the council will have a pastoral influence to persuade the
Synod to reverse her policy.
CONCLUSION
• We believe that the argument advanced that the Belgic Confession, Article
30 does not forbid and indeed allows for the ordination of women is
theologically, historically, logically, and biblically inaccurate and that the
CRCNA has acted contrarily to Article XXX of the Belgic Confession in
adopting a policy of ordaining women.
• We believe that the CRCNA has acted contrarily to the NAPARC
constitution (II. Basis of the Council) against the teaching of the Word of
God and the Belgic Confession, Article XXX by declaring the word male
in Article 3-a of the Church Order is inoperative and authorizing the
churches under its jurisdiction to ordain and install women in the offices of
elder, minister, and evangelist, thus circumventing her own constitution.
• We believe that the serious action of the Council’s expelling the CRCNA
may be used of the Lord to persuade the Synod to heed the teaching of the
Word of God, the historic practice of Reformed and Presbyterian
Churches, the majority opinion of the CRCNA’s own members, and the
concerted opinion of NAPARC denominations to reverse her actions on
the women’s ordination issue, and reconsider the hermeneutical process
that led her to depart from her former position.

<a id="ga30-p554"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=556 printed_page=554 -->

ATTACHMENT B
CRCNA RESPONSE TO RATIONALE

November 14, 2001

Dear Brothers:
This is not a pleasant day for NAPARC or for the CRC. In 1975 there was
considerable excitement about and high expectations for NAPARC and its role
in North America. Today marks the reversal of the unity that was so much
desired twenty-six years ago. For that we are sorry.
Introduction
Thank you for allowing us an opportunity to address some of the issues raised
in the document distributed to this meeting entitled: “Rationale for Action
Regarding CRCNA Membership in NAPARC.” We understand the document
to constitute the grounds requested to support the recommendation made last
year, namely that the membership of the CRCNA in NAPARC be terminated.
With that in mind we will address ourselves seriatim to the reasons adduced.
In the introduction it is alleged that hermeneutical problems in the CRCNA
can be traced to the adoption of Report 44 (“The Nature and Extent of Biblical
Authority”) in 1972. It is implied that this report with its hermeneutic
represented a change of position within the CRC regarding the Word of God,
Holy Scripture. It is further alleged that the ordination of women to the offices
of elder and minister resulted from this change. We take serious exception to
the allegation that the CRCNA has changed its position on Scripture or has
departed from sound reformed hermeneutical principles in interpreting
Scripture. Furthermore, we regret that no evidence is put forth to prove the
allegation. We are left in the unenviable position of arguing against a shadowy
figure without substance. All we can do is make a counter allegation and say
that the decision of Synod 1995 to allow the ordination of women is not the
result of anew view of scripture and its authority or anew hermeneutic.
The CRCNA does and always has affirmed what the Belgic Confession says
about the Bible and its inspiration and authority (Articles 3-7). The view of
Scripture and the hermeneutical principles for its interpretation articulated in
Report 44 are consistent with the teaching of the Belgic Confession and the
conclusions of the 1961 synodical study report, “Infallibility and Inspiration in

<a id="ga30-p555"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=557 printed_page=555 -->

the Light of Scripture.” Dr. John Cooper in his pamphlet, A Cause For
Division?, demonstrated that on the basis of a Reformed confessional
understanding of Scripture and the use of traditional Reformed hermeneutical
principles a scriptural case can be made for ordaining women as elders and
ministers (see Attachment A). This was further demonstrated by the
committee that advised Synod 2000 regarding women in office when they set
forth the biblical basis for the two positions on this issue within the church.
The same committee, using the same view of Scripture and the same
hermeneutical principles and practice presented the scriptural warrant for each
of the positions. The differences in the CRCNA on women in office result
from different interpretations of Scripture (exegesis), not from different views
of Scripture itself and its authority, or from different hermeneutical principles
and practice.
The idea that two or more persons using an identical hermeneutic can arrive at
varying conclusions is supported by the data of public record presented to
NAPARC in 1983. In that year, NAPARC received a report from its
Committee on Hermeneutics, which included a cover letter from W.H.F.
Kuykendall (convener of the committee), “A Reformed Testimony on
Hermeneutics” (see Attachment B), and a set of minutes from the committee’s
April 15-16, 1983 meeting. The cover letter made two major points: (1)
“Your committee discovered that at a certain fundamental level there seems to
be encouraging agreement on hermeneutic principles” and (2) “though
encouraging agreement is evident on basic principles, sometimes when these
principles are put to work in specific areas varying conclusions are reached.”
That, we believe, continues to be the heart of the matter. For this “Rationale”
to suggest that the CRC’s view of the Bible is flawed, and not present one
shred of evidence, except what might be called a “varying conclusion”
regarding women in office, is unfair to the CRC.
NAPARC delegates need also to be reminded that the document “A Reformed
Testimony on Hermeneutics” was as much the product of CRC input as it was
the product of any other church here present. The CRC delegates to NAPARC
in 1983 accepted the report and affirmed the truths just like the delegates from
your churches. Furthermore, the two CRC signatories to the Committee on
Hermeneutics report both served as members of the CRC committee that wrote
the report on “The Nature and Extent of Biblical Authority.” To suggest then,
that there is a fundamental hermeneutical disconnect between your churches
and the CRC cannot be substantiated with the data submitted to this meeting.
The “Rationale” presents three basic reasons why NAPARC should proceed to
terminate the CRCNA’s membership: 1) Theological-Historical
Considerations, 2) Constitutional Integrity, and 3) Pastoral Concerns.

<a id="ga30-p556"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=558 printed_page=556 -->

I. Theological-Historical Considerations
The “Rationale” contends that the CRCNA by allowing the ordination of
women acted contrary to Article 30 of the Belgic Confession. Even though the
“Rationale” acknowledges that the article’s reference to “faithful men” can be
taken as generic, it goes on to assert that because the reference to the rule
prescribed by St. Paul to Timothy is gender explicit the confession confines the
offices to males. It is further contended that the intent of the Reformed
confessions of the sixteenth century (presumably also the Belgic) and the
practice of the Reformed churches was clearly to confine the offices of the
church to men.
We agree with the judgment that historically it was the general practice of the
Reformed churches to confine the offices to men. We, however, vigorously
disagree with the judgment that it was the intention of Article 30 to limit the
offices to men. If that had been the intention, it could have and would have
been explicitly stated. Some of the French-speaking churches in the Low
Lands where the Confession originated and was first adopted had women
deacons. Moreover, the reference to Paul’s advice to Timothy does not
warrant the judgment that the article has a gender-explicit intention. The
scriptural references in the footnotes are later additions and not part of the
original text of the Confessions. The CRCNA has always confessed and
continues to confess what is confessed in Article 30, namely,
“that the true church ought to be governed according to the
spiritual order that our Lord has taught us in his Word, ... that by
this means true religion is preserved, ... and that by this means
everything will be done in good order when such persons are
elected who are faithful and chosen according to the rule that
Paul gave Timothy.”
The principles for orderliness and godliness that Paul was concerned about in
giving the rule to Timothy are maintained in the CRCNA. The word translated
above as persons (personages) is not gender explicit.
II. The Issue of Constitutional Integrity
A. the constitution of na parc
The “Rationale” calls attention to both NAPARC’s constitution and what it
refers to as the CRCNA’s constitution. With respect to the Constitution of
NAPARC we note that the “Basis of the Council” is identified as the
following:

<a id="ga30-p557"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=559 printed_page=557 -->

Confessing Jesus Christ as only Savior and Sovereign Lord over
all of life, we affirm the basis of the fellowship of Presbyterian
and Reformed Churches to be full commitment to the Bible in its
entirety as the Word of God written, without error in all its parts
and to its teaching as set forth in the Heidelberg Catechism, the
Belgic Confession, the Canons of Dort, the Westminster Confession of Faith, and the Westminster Larger and Shorter
Catechisms. That the adopted basis of fellowship be regarded as
warrant for the establishment of a formal relationship of the
nature of a council, that is, a fellowship that enables the
constituent churches to advise, counsel, and cooperate in various
matters with one another and hold out before each other the
desirability and need for organic union of churches that are of
like faith and practice.
The CRC has in the past and does today commit itself “to the Bible in its
entirety as the Word of God written, without error in all its parts, and to its
teaching as set forth in the Heidelberg Catechism, the Belgic Confession and
the Canons of Dort ...”. The CRC affirms the NAPARC Constitution and
attests that its own church life is in conformity to it. In the absence of credible
evidence that the CRC has actually violated the “Basis of the Council” this
body has no moral, theological, or legal grounds for dismissing the CRC.
B. the constitution of crcna
The CRCNA recognizes various levels of authority in the church. Scripture is
the highest followed by the church’s confessions, its Church Order and
synodical decisions and in this order. Presbyterians historically have made
greater claims scripturally for their polity, Books of Government, and
Constitutions than have churches that stand in the continental Reformed
tradition. Among some Presbyterians matters of church polity are viewed as
virtually synonymous with confessional articles of faith. The CRCNA
understands the issue of women in office as a matter of polity, not a doctrinal
or confessional matter.
While shaped and informed by Scripture, the Church order in the CRCNA is
considered a servant of the gospel and the church’s ministry rather than a
constitution or strictly legal document. The decision of the synod in 1995 to
declare the word male inoperative under certain conditions was not a simple
pragmatic or expedient decision. The church had struggled for a long time
over the issue, and recognized that there were two views in the church both of
which honored the authority of Scripture and followed sound Reformed
hermeneutical principles. Out of respect for the scripturally informed

<a id="ga30-p558"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=560 printed_page=558 -->

consciences of those who believe that Scripture allows women to serve in the
offices of elder and minister and in recognition of the needs and circumstances
of local churches in carrying out their ministry, the synod made the declaration
which allows for regional exceptions to the Church Order.
Christian Reformed synods have on other occasions permitted churches and
classes to conduct their ministry in ways that were not entirely in agreement
with the Church Order without first amending it. For example, already in 1890
the English-speaking churches of Classis Flackensack were permitted to sing
hymns when the CRC Church Order allowed for Psalm singing only.
Currently, some classes are permitted to delegate deacons to the classical
assembly along with ministers and elders even though the Church Order
prescribes that the assembly be comprised of elders and ministers (Church
Order Article 40-a). In addition, exceptions have been permitted for Church
Order Articles 4-a, 52-b, 52-c, and 53. A practice pejoratively referred to as
“expedient” in the “Rationale” is actually an established and time-tested
practice in the CRC.
III. Pastoral Concerns
Most of what the “Rationale” says regarding pastoral concerns supports
retaining rather than terminating the CRCNA’s membership in NAPARC. The
only exception being the earnest prayer that dismissing the CRCNA from the
council will have a pastoral influence on the CRCNA. in response to this we
simply affirm the CRCNA’s agreement with the council’s basis, constitution
and by-laws. We also believe the CRCNA’s life and practice conform to these
foundational documents and that therefore our church should not be dismissed
from the council.
Conclusion
1. We have demonstrated that the allegations re problems in the CRC’s
Reformed hermeneutic are inappropriate and inaccurate. This ground
cannot support the recommendation to terminate the CRC’s membership.
2. We have demonstrated that the CRC has not acted contrary to the intention
of Belgic Confession, Article 30. To assert that Article 30 theologically,
historically, logically, and biblically forbids the ordination of women is to
misconstrue and restrict the text and context of that Confession.
3. We have demonstrated that the CRCNA has not acted contrary to the
Constitution of NAPARC and the “Basis of the Council.” No credible

<a id="ga30-p561"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=561 printed_page=null -->

arguments have been put forth that prove the CRCNA has misinterpreted
its own Confession or wrongly handled the Word of truth.
We are in no position to know what good or bad results will follow from a
decision of NAPARC to expel the CRCNA from membership. It seems
strange to us, however, for this organization to use a hope-for result as a
ground for an action that in all other respects is groundless. This confuses
purpose (reasons for acting) and result (hoped-for consequences), and
indicates that the “Rationale” is operating with the idea that the end
justifies the means.
D. Engelhard
L. Hofman
H. Zwaanstra

<a id="ga30-p560"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=562 printed_page=560 -->

THE REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE ON
MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA
TO THE 30th GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF
THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

#### I. INTRODUCTION

Advancing the Gospel through Renewing the Church
“The Spirit of the Sovereign LORD is on me, because the
LORD has anointed me to preach good news to the poor. He
has sent me to bind up the brokenhearted, to proclaim
freedom for the captives and release from darkness for the
prisoners, to proclaim the year of the LORD's favor and the
day of vengeance of our God, to comfort all who mourn, and
provide for those who grieve in Zion - to bestow on them a
crown of beauty instead of ashes, the oil of gladness instead
of mourning, and a garment of praise instead of a spirit of
despair. They will be called oaks of righteousness, a planting
of the lord for the display of his splendor. ”
Isaiah 61:1-13
With every passing year, North America is becoming a more secular
society in desperate need of the transforming power of the Gospel of our Lord
Jesus Christ. The events of September 11th and following have reminded us
again of how transitory this life is and how needy we are as a people for the
Gospel. Reports from across North America from our church planters and
around the world from our chaplains reveal an openness to the truth of God not
seen for years. The time is ripe to advance the Gospel.
“I am not ashamed of the gospel, because it is the power of
God for the salvation of everyone who believes: first for the
Jew, then for the Gentile. For in the gospel a righteousness
from God is revealed, a righteousness that is by faith from
first to last, just a sit is written: ‘The righteous will live by
faith. ’’’Romans 1:16,17
The Apostle Paul advanced the Gospel in a secular Roman society
similar to ours today. He began his missionary journeys, which were in reality
church planting missions, from the church at Antioch. Syrian Antioch was the
third largest city in the Roman Empire. It was a multi-cultural city at the
crossroads of trade. From this urban mix, the Gospel ran freely from city to
city and from region to region so that it was said of the transforming power of

<a id="ga30-p561"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=563 printed_page=561 -->

the Gospel that, “the world has been turned upside down.” We pray that in the
years to come this will be said of us!
Mission to North America (MNA) serves the churches and presbyteries of
the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) in advancing the Gospel among all
the peoples of the United States and Canada. This is primarily done, in
obedience to the Great Commission of our Lord, by providing leadership and
coordinating resources for planting healthy and multiplying churches who
reflect the distinctives with which the PCA was founded: true to the Scriptures,
the reformed faith and the Great Commission of Jesus Christ.
MNA’s vision, as assigned by the General Assembly, is to plant
churches in all kinds of communities and among every people group in North
America, believing that starting new churches and forming church planting
movements is the most effective means to reach lost people with the Good
News of Jesus Christ. At the same time, planting new churches increases the
resource base for supporting missions throughout the nations of the world.
To this end, Mission to North America has established the following
priorities for planting churches:
From Centralization to “Grass Roots” Leadership: MNA’s goal is that the
vision for advancing the Kingdom be led by the churches, presbyteries and
church planting movements across the PCA, rather than by a centralized
leadership. The vision for church planting is increasingly owned and driven at
the grass roots level. MNA brings leadership at the denominational level and
provides the resources or partnerships necessary to carry out this vision
through recruiting, assessing, funding, placing, training and mentoring church
planters.
Some evidences of the grass roots vision taking hold:
■ The need for church planters is far exceeding the supply,
as increasing numbers of churches and presbyteries
identify places to plant churches and provide funding.
■ Church planters seeking their support are seeing growing
commitments in funding; on average, the PCA is now
investing much larger amounts of support in new church
plants, thus providing more of the human resources
needed to establish a new work in strength.
■ Churches increasingly are joining together, both
informally and through defined structures, to provide
leadership and financial resources for church planting.
From Networks to Church Planting Movements: Increasingly, PCA churches
are bringing resources together: forming networks to plant new churches. A

<a id="ga30-p562"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=564 printed_page=562 -->

network maybe a group of churches within a presbytery, it maybe a
presbytery, or it may combine the efforts of churches in two or more
presbyteries. MNA’s goal is to assist networks in becoming healthy church
planting movements - sustaining a multiplication of churches, working with
church planters from recruiting to mentoring, seeking to develop ministries
that will advance the church among all ethnic groups and socio-economic
classes and having a vision to serve outside their defined area.
The North Florida Presbytery upon its inception was organized as a
church-planting presbytery. 8 of the 16 churches that make up the presbytery
have united their resources to aggressively plant churches throughout NE
Florida. Their goal is to have one PCA church for every 50,000 people.
The Southwest Church Planting Network joins together 38 churches in
three Presbyteries to plant churches throughout Texas, New Mexico and
Arizona. Unique to this network is the vision to start Reformed University
Fellowship along with church plants in college communities.
From Being Primarily Mono-Cultural to Multi-Cultural: The PCA is
predominantly an Anglo denomination. The Lord is bringing the nations to
North America, and the PCA should be a Church reaching all of North
America. MNA’s goal is to facilitate the PCA in reaching all the people
groups of North America with the Gospel. This includes reaching both new
immigrants, as well as succeeding generations of ethnic groups who live in
North America.
Census 2000 revealed how multicultural the United States has become
as a nation. The Flispanic population increased 58% since 1990, making them
at 35.3 million people the largest minority group in the US. African Americans
and Asians also showed increases in population with corresponding cultural
influence. To reach such a diverse population, MNA has supported church
planting among African Americans, Hispanics, Brazilians, Haitians, and
Koreans.
From Emphasizing the Suburbs to Planting Churches within the City: While
the PCA is largely a suburban church, the Lord is blessing in establishing new
churches in the city. MNA’s goal is to facilitate a great movement of city
churches, while also continuing to plant churches in the suburbs. A further
goal is to encourage churches in every circumstance to seek to impact with the
Gospel the key centers of influence closest to them, or key centers of which
they are an integral part.
From the lead church in this movement, Redeemer Presbyterian in
Manhattan has now become a large and growing church Metropolitan New
York City area, in addition to providing leadership for ministry in the city
across North America. By the Lord’s grace, the PCA can now celebrate a
growing presence in the center cities of Boston, Toronto, Vancouver, Seattle,

<a id="ga30-p563"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=565 printed_page=563 -->

Portland (OR), San Francisco, San Diego, Denver, St. Louis, New Orleans,
and Chicago, as well as in many other smaller cities across North America.
From Complacency to Compassion: Participation in ministries of mercy and
justice is growing in the PCA. MNA’s goal is that the PCA recognize the
Lord’s call to minister through deeds of mercy that accompany the preaching
of the Gospel. Mercy and justice are major themes of Scripture and ought to
be an integral part of every church. As Jesus preached the Gospel and healed
the sick, so we are called to preach the Word and demonstrate the compassion
of our Lord with our deeds.
Growth in preaching the Word and ministering in deed the mercy of
our Lord is seen on several fronts:
■ A growing number of churches that focus particularly on
communities of economic as well as spiritual need.
■ Ministries and churches that focus on serving people who
face specific kinds of practical needs.
■ A growing number of churches whose communities are
mainly middle and higher income are seeking ways to
serve lower income communities as well as serve specific
needs that affect people across socio-economic lines,
such as those who are limited by handicaps, suffer
addictions and experience family problems.
In addition to church planting, Mission to North America coordinates
resources for the revitalization of PCA churches already established, and
provides leadership and endorsement for chaplains who serve under the
auspices of the PCA, while serving in the United States Armed Forces, as well
as other institutions.
If the Gospel is the power of God for the salvation of everyone who
believes, then we should spare no effort to herald this transforming message.
Our very prayers should reflect this belief. Our financial resources in the
Partnership Share and gifts ought to support this endeavor, and we must be
willing to send out our very best laborers into the fields “white for the harvest”
of souls. The church at Antioch gave up their best when it sent out Paul and
Barnabas to plant churches throughout the Roman Empire.
“They preached the Good News in that city and won a large
number of disciples. Then they returned to Lystra, Iconium
and Antioch, strengthening the disciples and encouraging
them to remain true to the faith. ” Acts 14:21,22

<a id="ga30-p564"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=566 printed_page=564 -->

II. MINISTRIES
A. Church Planting
GA/MNA Church Planting’s purpose is to provide leadership and
coordinate resources for the churches and presbyteries of the PCA in selecting,
funding, placing, coaching and equipping church planters. it is with avery
strong sense of call by God that Mission to North America labors for the
expansion of the Lord’s Church - Advancing the Gospel through Renewing
the Church. We count it a privilege to labor in this commitment and call,
given to us by the General Assembly, and pray that the entire PCA will become
a Church Multiplication Movement.
The reality of the spiritually declining condition of the broader church
in the United States and Canada is staggering. These realities also present the
PCA with a great opportunity to respond with effort and resources committed
to the missionary work of the Church in North America.
For the PCA to truly become a church multiplication movement, the
following must be strong. GA/MNA focuses its resources on serving PCA
churches and presbyteries in these activities.
• Prayer for the advancement of the Gospel and the growth of
the Church
• Recruiting Church Planters
• Assessing Potential Church Planters
• Training Church Planters and Key Church Leaders
• Assisting Church Planters in Fund Raising
• Placing Church Planters on the Field
• Coaching Church Planters in the Process
• Building a Denominational Team of Church Planting
Leadership
Concerted Prayer: Prayer is the foundation of all Christian endeavors. It is
through prayer that God works to advance His Kingdom. It is significant that
our Lord’s command is to pray that He will send the laborers. We call upon
the General Assembly to join in focused, concerted prayer for the advancement
of the Kingdom through church planting. Only if the Spirit of God is at work
through our efforts will our efforts be effective in advancing the Kingdom.
We remind the Assembly of the recommendations passed last year at
the 29th General Assembly, and urge this same spirit and practice of prayer
again through the coming year:
That the General Assembly pause now for a 15
minute season of prayer for the advancement of the
Gospel in North America - praying for the following
renewed commitments:

<a id="ga30-p565"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=567 printed_page=565 -->

• A renewed realization that the church is built by our Lord
• A renewed commitment to prayer
• A renewed commitment to unity in the church
• A renewed commitment to reaching the many cultures,
ethnic/racial groups and socio-economic groups in North
America
• A renewed commitment to mercy ministries - preaching the
Gospel in Word and showing the mercies of Christ in deed
• A renewed commitment to the kind of stewardship of the
Lord's resources that will give greater priority to the
advancement of the Gospel
• A greater commitment to functional teamwork
That the General Assembly call upon our
presbyteries to set aside a significant portion of their
meetings to pray for the advancement of the Gospel
through the revitalization of our existing churches and
through the planting of new churches - throughout North
America and throughout the world.
That the General Assembly encourage each
congregation and presbytery of the General Assembly to
seek the Lord’s calling for them in the advancement of the
Gospel. 1 Corinthians 12:7 and Ephesians 4:7 promise
that the Spirit of God has given each a gift for the
common good and the building of the church. Just as
there is a calling to every local church in reaching the
local community and the world with the Gospel, so there
is a calling for every congregation in church planting -
reaching our “Judea and Samaria,” as well as our
“Jerusalem” and the “uttermost parts.”
By God’s power, lives are transformed by the Gospel as we wait upon
Him in prayer. GA/MNA communication pieces, Multiply and the web site
(www.pcanet.org), give a high priority to prayer by suggesting weekly prayer
requests on a regional basis for the advancement of the church. The
Intercessor for all regions may be accessed on the web site, offering greater
opportunity for intercession for ministry across North America.
Recruiting Church Planters: The recruiting of church planters is the highest
priority among GA/MNA services. As of March 2001, the key leadership staff
position for this crucial work was vacant. MNA asks that the Assembly join in
prayer for the Lord’s provision of a Teaching Elder to provide the leadership
necessary for this work to advance. Pray also for the financial resources

<a id="ga30-p566"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=568 printed_page=566 -->

needed for this work: as churches increase their Partnership Share/Askings
giving, more resources will be devoted to recruiting church planters.
Assessing Potential Church Planters: One of the most strategic functions of
MNA is to assist the PCA in the assessment of potential church planters
through a careful evaluation of a candidate’s calling, character, and
competencies. The competencies evaluated include personal (e.g. integrity,
prayer, humility, and spiritual vitality), professional (e.g. preaching,
evangelism, management, and leadership), and interpersonal (e.g. flexibility,
likeability, sensitivity, dynamism, and emotional stability). Both the church
planting candidate himself and his wife are included in this process. Their
marriage and family life and the wife’s support for and role in a potential
church plant are critical factors to assess.
All this is accomplished through a lengthy process of interviews,
psychological evaluations, references, and exercises culminating in a four-day
retreat offered three times a year with a team of trained, qualified assessors. In
2001, 37 men/couples were assessed by the Assessment Center. The goal in
each case is to assist every couple “not to think more highly of himself than he
ought to think; but to think so as to have sober judgment, as God has allotted
to each a measure of faith” (Romans 12:3), assisting each couple in
discovering and pursuing their unique calling in the service of our Lord Jesus
Christ.
The assessing of church planting candidates is one of the strongest
ministries of MNA and is considered one of the finest in America. However,
there are still several goals to pursue and some new ideas to consider that
might make it even better such as:
(1) Help the presbyteries, churches, and leaders of the
PCA better understand how the Assessment Center works and
what it has to offer.
(2) Improve the Pre-Assessment process by establishing
more specific qualifications and training for those doing the
pre-assessment interviews.
(3) Provide a variety of assessments sometime in the
future that target certain types of candidates more directly
such as seminary students, urban contexts, and ethnic
backgrounds.
4) Provide a more flexible assessment process overall
that would help assess candidates at various and earlier stages
of development, thus better providing encouragement and
guidance to those considering church planting.
Placing Church Planters on the Field: The placement process begins with a
church planter committing to a set of biblical core values, which God has put
on his heart. These values form the foundation of a vision that will shape the

<a id="ga30-p567"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=569 printed_page=567 -->

church. After the vision is solidified and documented, the location that will
best facilitate this vision is then determined by a sense of the Lord’s call to the
church planter, and mutual agreement with the presbytery, in consultation
with the GA/MNA church planting staff. GA/MNA staff then offers assistance
to the presbytery and the church planter in the many practical steps and
administrative details that are necessary to begin the work.
Men are being assessed, qualified, and placed in many different
demographic situations. Some in places where there are core groups who wish
