to have a new church, and others where there is no core group but still great
need. Also men are being placed in many different demographic profiles.
Some areas are large cities; others are smaller communities, both with a
sensitivity to recognize where there is need and where God is at work.
Training Church Planters: GA/MNA not only assists in recruiting,
assessment, and placing qualified church planters, but also provides the
support and training necessary to fulfill their calling. This is provided through
a variety of venues.
(1) Boot Camp - Immediately following a candidate’s
qualification by the Assessment Center he attends this two-
day training event. This experience is designed to provide the
candidate with a basic orientation to the critical steps involved
in planting a church, to the services provided them through
MNA, and the expectations of the MNA Committee. It is also
a workshop in the initial development of such things as
establishing core values and a philosophy of ministry,
formulating vision, developing a plan for fundraising, and the
use of learning contracts in apprenticeships.
(2) The National Church Planter Training Conference - This
annual conference has recently averaged about 150 church
planters and their wives as well as other leaders who receive
training in all facets of church planting and are renewed in the
overall vision of MNA for planting churches across North
America. it is an invigorating time of worship, learning, and
fellowship. It is a great opportunity for leaders and church
planters from all around the country, all at various stages of
development, to come together to build and renew
relationships and to share stories and ideas that stimulate,
encourage, and assist each other.
(3) Regional Training Conferences - In addition to the National
Conference, several regions host annual training conferences
that not only provide similar training in church planting but
complement the National Conference by providing training
that addresses issues unique to each region. They also tend to

<a id="ga30-p568"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=570 printed_page=568 -->

address a wider range of issues such as church revitalization,
preaching refreshers, and planting and/or ministering in small,
rural communities. Leaders from within the region generally
provide preaching, worship, testimonies, and workshops.
(4) Church Planting Apprenticeships - These apprenticeships
are designed to provide on-the-job preparation through
personalized coaching for those men who appear to have the
calling and gifts but lack the experience necessary to lead a
church plant. An apprenticeship may be from one to two years
depending on the age and experience of the apprentice and the
needs and vision of the mother church. In addition to such
important, hands-on training, apprenticeships have the added
benefit of pushing church planting down to the local
congregation from the presbytery committees, allowing our
congregations to become directly and personally involved in
the mission of the church. They also provide a context for the
church planter to take the time to prayerfully consider the
location of a church plant and to lay the groundwork fora
potential plant without the pressure of moving on site
independently and trying to “make” something happen right
away.
Coaching Church Planters - One of the most important and pressing
challenges in church planting is to provide more guidance and support for
those church planters who are on the field. MNA staff seeks to arrange -
primarily through local churches, presbyteries or networks/movements - for
adequate spiritual accountability and encouragement and better mentoring in
the skills and challenges of church planting. It is also a goal to minister more
effectively to the wives of church planters who deal with unique challenges
and stress in ministry.
Building a Denominational Team of Church Planting Leadership: As the
vision for church planting continues to grow among particular churches,
presbyteries, and among church planting networks (which include a wide
variety of formal and informal structures), GA/MNA fulfills a growing role of
serving, coordinating and taking the initiative to develop the resources
necessary for the vision for an advancing church to be fulfilled. GA/MNA
brings this leadership through several avenues:
(1) Regional Coordinators: GA/MNA Regional Coordinators
serve churches that want to reproduce themselves with
daughter churches, and presbyteries that are proactive in
multiplying churches. They assist launch teams (core groups)
and presbytery MNA chairmen in recruiting church planters.

<a id="ga30-p569"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=571 printed_page=569 -->

Regional Coordinators assist in the assessment process as
assessors. They assist the church planters in the development
of their strategic plan to move to the field, help them develop
learning contracts, assist church planters with fund raising,
and provide valuable counsel regarding choosing the location
for the church. They provide valuable coaching to the church
planters through the process of starting the church, and serve
the presbytery MNA committees in their geographic areas by
helping to identify areas where new churches are needed
when there is no launch team. All regional coordinators serve
in that role on a part-time or volunteer basis.
(2) Presbytery MNA Chairmen: The second annual gathering of
MNA Presbytery Chairmen was held in October 2001. In
2002, this gathering will take place in the Atlanta area on
November 12-14. Through this training ministry, MNA
offers the opportunity to gather the chairmen of MNA
committees from around the country to address such issues
as:

- **a.** Understanding the chairman’s job description.
- **b.** Who should be an MNA chairman and who should be on a committee.
- **c.** How to lead an effective committee and ministry.
- **d.** How to work with the GA/MNA committee and clarifying how GA/MNA can serve as a resource or work in partnership with the presbytery.
- **e.** Listening to the concerns and ideas of the presbytery

MNA chairmen.

- **f.** How to develop and communicate a presbytery vision.
- **g.** How to develop a presbytery church planting strategy in keeping with that vision.
- **h.** How to recruit men and raise funds.
- **i.** How to support/coach church planters on the field.

(3) Church Planting Networks: A “network” is simply of group
of churches and leaders who have committed to working
together to reach a certain area or people group with the
Gospel, through the planting of churches. It may consist of the
churches of a presbytery, or within a presbytery such as a
metropolitan area. it may transcend a presbytery by targeting a
particular people-group or combine the efforts of two or more
presbyteries. GA/MNA works with these networks in a
coordinating and partnership role to “cross pollinate” with
each other by sharing ideas, trouble-shooting, praying
together, and encouraging one another. GA/MNA encourages

<a id="ga30-p570"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=572 printed_page=570 -->

all such efforts that enhance the ability of particular churches
and presbyteries to fulfill their calling to build church planting
movements. GA/MNA also encourages all such
networks/movements to work within and promote the
distinctives of the Presbyterian form of government -
believing that the primary purpose for which a presbytery
exists is to plant churches.
The Midwest Region: The Midwest Region of the PCA consists of 13 states
and 8 presbyteries. It includes such major metropolitan areas as Chicago,
Detroit, Minneapolis, St. Louis, Columbus, Indianapolis, and Kansas City and
totals over 70 million people. However, more than 50 million people do not
attend any church making, it a very large and strategic mission field. The
cities, particularly Chicago and Detroit, are filled with people fromevery
different nationality and ethnic group imaginable. In this vast area the PCA has
only 150 churches totaling approximately 20,000 members, a large percentage
of whom are in one city, St. Louis.
In response to this great challenge and opportunity, the presbyteries
and churches of this region have united around a mission statement of
“Working Together to Reach the Heart of America.” The vision is “That God,
by His Spirit, through the cooperative prayers and efforts of the Midwest
churches, will ignite a powerful church planting and vitalization movement
that impacts the entire region with the Gospel of Jesus Christ, and helps
transform the face of the PCA.” In the other words, the desire is to see such a
movement of the Spirit in our presbyteries and churches, in every state, city,
and town, that so many people come to Christ, and so many churches are
planted, that our society, our nation, and even the world, feels the impact and
the PCA becomes more of a truly multicultural denomination to the glory of
God. Some of the goals in this vision are:
(1) To plant 100 churches over the next 5 years as well as
building inthe structures and dynamics that will facilitate an
additional 750 churches being planted over the next 25 years,
thus growing to 1000 churches overall.
(2) That attendance during this time period will grow to
100,000, with at least one quarter of this growth being
through conversions.
(3) That at least 250 of the churches planted will be
multicultural in membership, or will at least include major
non-Anglo ethnic groups.
(4) That every presbytery, region, metropolitan area, and
church will have a unifying, catalytic vision that fits the
overall vision for the Midwest and the PCA itself.

<a id="ga30-p571"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=573 printed_page=571 -->

Effective January 1, 2002, MNA called TE Ted Powers to serve as
Assessment and Training Coordinator for MNA and as Midwest Regional
Coordinator. Ted has served as Midwest Regional Coordinator for the past 5
years on a volunteer basis. The full-time commitment will bring additional
time and leadership resources to the region; in his assessment and training role,
Ted will serve all of MNA - not limited to the Midwest region.
Currently, there are over 25 mission churches in the Midwest,
including several multicultural and ethnic works such as Korean (both first and
second generation plants), African American, Filipino, and Haitian. A Midwest
Executive Council who works with Midwest Coordinator TE Ted Powers in
planning, coordinating, and oversight guides the work. The Midwest Regional
Conference for Church Planting and Vitalization is held annually in Chicago as
well as an annual Key Leaders Conference held in St. Louis. TE Jimmy Dodd
is helping to design a coaching ministry for the region while coaching several
church planters himself. TE Young Ik Cho works with Ted as Assistant for
Asian Ministries.
Progress in 2001
(These numbers include all church planters solely by presbyteries as well as
those assessed by GA/MNA and approved by the GA/MNA Committee.)

Lone Star Presbyterian, Ft. Worth, TX—Russ McAnulty

<a id="ga30-p572"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=574 printed_page=572 -->

Northern Illinois Grace North Shore, Northbrook, IL—Bill James
Grace Chicago, Chicago, IL—Bob Reid
New Hope Haitian Community Church, Chicago, IL~Hector Mardy
Christ Church, Bourbonnais, IL -Scott Lemenager
Pacific Christ Church of Pasadena, Pasadena, ca -M ark Kuiper
Christ Church, Ojai, ca -R oy Bennett
Pacific Northwest Intown Presbyterian, Portland, OR—Charles Garland
Philadelphia Rock of Israel Church, NE Philadelphia, PA—Ilya Lizorkin
All Nations Mission, W Philadelphia, PA—Bagudekia “Bagu” Alobeyo
Potomac LaPlata Community Church, LaPlata, MD-Jamie McGregor (Apprentice)
Rocky Mountain St. Vrain Presbyterian, Longmont, co -E C Bell
South Texas Emanuel Church, Austin, tx -M att Boulter (Apprentice)
South Florida redeemer of north Miami, Miami, FL—Sainvil Dorcinvil
San Harbor Presbyterian, Tequesta, FL-Michael Miller (Apprentice)
Southwest Florida St. Petersburg Presbyterian, St. Petersburg, fl -David Harding
Susquenna Valley Harvest Presbyterian, Lancaster, PA—Bill Massey
B. Multicultural Ministries
State of the Task
During 2001, we continued to witness a growing interest and
commitment in the PCA to communicate the Gospel among ethnic groups that
are rapidly growing in North America. Several states are already at least 51%
non-white in composition. Around the year 2050 the growth of ethnic
population groups will be such that over 50% of the total population of the
United States will be non-white. Hispanics, though divided by their
nationalistic backgrounds, are now commonly recognized as the largest
minority group in the United States. The PCA, historically motivated and
recognized for its Matthew 28 Great Commission zeal and efforts, now has an
unprecedented cross-cultural evangelistic opportunity and challenge right at its
own front door. These growing groups are the PCA’s “Samaritan” mission
field (Acts 1:8-9).
The growing interest in multicultural ministries and church planting is
observed in part in the following ways:
1) Church and presbytery leaders communicate frequently
with GA/MNA personnel seeking help in evangelizing
and working with ethnic groups as part of the changing
demographics surrounding their churches.
2) An increasing number of second-generation leaders from
various ethnic backgrounds are entering reformed
seminaries and non-traditional presbytery sponsored
leadership training programs.
3) Presbyteries are designing and implementing non-
traditional ways to train and credential Teaching Elders

<a id="ga30-p573"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=575 printed_page=573 -->

(in accordance with PCA standards) from within the
ethnic groups in which churches need to be planted.
4) Articles in Multiply and Intercessor have highlighted
multicultural church planting and have generated much
interest and response.
5) There is an increasing interest by some presbyteries,
churches, and church planters to start churches that are
intentional in reaching the ethnic populations in their
geographical areas.
6) A few PCA churches, instead of selling their property
and moving, have made an intentional decision to make
the changes necessary to reach out to people around them
who are different in terms of socio-economic level or
ethnic background.
The purpose of MNA Multicultural Ministries is to provide leadership
and coordinate the resources necessary for PCA churches and presbyteries to
start and develop healthy, multiplying, and reformed multicultural and crosscultural churches, which are part of church multiplication movements.
The term multicultural is used by GA/MNA in two ways. (1) To
describe the work of ethnic-specific church planting through which
congregations are formed that are largely homogeneous (in most cases, the
main homogeneous factor is that the group of people reached have a primary
language other than English) and non-white; (2) To describe specific churches
who include leaders and members from a variety of ethnic backgrounds,
worshipping and serving God together.
Multicultural Advances in 2001
1) Ethnic and multicultural church planters continue to be assessed,
placed, and coached in church planting. Most of GA/MNA’s
assessment centers held during 2001 saw more than 30% of the
candidates having a multicultural focus.
2) There are more than 20 PCA churches and groups working with
Hispanics around the PCA in various ministries; English as a
Second Language is one of the primary service programs.
3) There are three organized Hispanic congregations in the PCA
(Miami, Houston, McAllen TX).
4) There are currently twelve PCA Hispanic church plants in the United
States. (Laredo, TX - 3; Miami, FL - 2; Alexandria, VA - 1;
Austin, TX - 1; Escondido, CA - 1; El Paso, TX - 2;
Birmingham, AL - 2).

<a id="ga30-p574"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=576 printed_page=574 -->

5) There are now around 25 African American teaching elders and
seminary students in the PCA. TE Wy Plummer is MNA African
American Movement Leader.
6) There continues to be major Korean first-generation participation
in the PCA with TE Henry Koh providing leadership as MNA
Korean Ministries Coordinator. Many second-generation/English
speaking Koreans are enrolled in reformed seminaries and are
anxious to begin multicultural English speaking congregations
around the PCA.
7) Some PCA churches are seeking to add ethnic teaching elders to
their staff.
8) MNA Brazilian Movement Leader TE Osni Ferreira, now counts
13 Brazilian (Portuguese speaking) churches and mission
churches who are providing most of the new PCA Brazilian
church plant funding. Tampa and Chicago are identified as the
next priorities for Brazilian church plants.
9) The PCA now counts five Haitian churches and mission churches
in the United States. Several Haitian leaders are in seminary. A
PCA presbytery on the island of Haiti is being discussed and
planned by MNA and members of Southern Florida Presbytery.
Dony St. Germain is MNA Haitian Movement Leader.
The most significant advance in terms of addressing the need for more
ethnic church planters is the focus on training ethnic leaders through the use of
church planting apprenticeship programs, through which men who appear to
have the gifts and skills for church planting work, under the mentoring
leadership of a church planting pastor prior to their taking the lead in planting
a church on their own.
Korean Ministries:
Reaching “First Generation ” Koreans (those who were born in Korea, and
for whom Korean is their primary language for worship, as well as for
everyday discourse within their communities):
There are over 180 PCA Korean language churches that are members
of seven Korean language presbyteries. TE Henry Koh, GA/MNA Korean
Ministries Coordinator, serves these churches with a particular focus on
encouraging their participation in the fellowship and financial support of the
PCA, and in working with the Korean pastors in their commitment to the
reformed faith.
The annual conference of the Coalition of Korean Churches in PCA
met at First Korean Presbyterian Church of St. Louis, in Bridgeton, MO, from
February 11-13, 2002 with the conference theme of Healthy Church. The
purpose of the Coalition is to maintain Christian fellowship and close
relationships among Korean churches in the PCA and to discuss and carry out
ministry activities in cooperation with one another.

<a id="ga30-p575"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=577 printed_page=575 -->

TE Harry Reeder of Briarwood Presbyterian Church, in Birmingham,
AL was the main speaker of the event. TE Roy Taylor also gave a lecture on
PCA Administration and Book of Church Order. Other seminar speakers were
TE Kwang Ja Park and TE Daniel Kim. New president and officers of the
Coalition were chosen during this annual event. The Korean Women in the
Church (WIC) also conducted their own seminars, elected a new president and
discussed their annual project.
Reaching “Second Generation” Koreans (those of Korean family background
who were bom in the United States, and whose primary language is English):
The second MNA Korean English Ministry Pastors’ Conference was
held January 28-30, 2002, at Korean Central Presbyterian Church, Vienna,
VA, in the Washington, DC area. The conference theme was Preaching to
Multiethnic America. About 190 participated from all across the United
States. TE Timothy Keller, senior pastor of Redeemer Presbyterian Church in
New York City spoke on “The Mission of the Church” from Acts 16, and TE
Tom Allen, senior pastor of Grace Seattle, also presented on “Worship
Development” and “Facilitation of an Evangelistic Church Culture.” TE Henry
Koh presented the “Korean-American Ministries Plan in the PCA,”
Attachment C. Through the conference, many who are not members of the
PCA expressed interest in the PCA and also a number of potential second
generation church planters were identified. The prayers of the General Assembly are requested as men train further for this crucial ministry, with its
great challenge of reaching people with the Gospel, even as they experience
great transition in their own lives and the life of their families and churches.
as apart of this plan, MNA is presenting to this General Assembly a
recommendation to create the Korean-American bilingual presbytery as a
transition from Korean speaking presbyteries to English speaking presbyteries.
We believe through such a presbytery emerging second-generation multiethnic
church planting leadership can be trained.
C. Thanksgiving Ministries — Urban and Mercy
“The Spirit of the Sovereign Lord is on me, because the Lord has
anointed me to preach good news to the poor. He has sent me to bind
up the brokenhearted, to proclaim freedom for the captives and
release from darkness for the prisoners...” Isaiah 61:1
This keynote text for the GA/MNA report continues to be especially
applicable to the very important work of MNA Urban and Mercy Ministries.
Urban and mercy ministry development has been growing since the PCA
began. The events of September 11, 2001, brought even more urgency to the
Lord’s calling to this vital part of our ministry as a church. The Spirit is at
work, convincing us of His call to show the mercies of our Lord, even as we

<a id="ga30-p576"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=578 printed_page=576 -->

preach the Gospel in some of the most challenging communities in North
America and in response to ever-greater needs. The Lord is blessing, bringing
response to the Gospel by the power of His Spirit, along with addressing
personal and community needs at a significant level.
GA/MNA Urban and Mercy Ministries continues its ongoing efforts from the
past and also is working to increase their effectiveness:
• MNA and Christian Education and Publications again are
planning for a conference on mercy ministries March 13-15, 2003.
Based on the strong participation of the March 2001 conference, the
2003 event will be nearly twice as long and will seek to bring many
more practical helps to those attending, for implementing ministry in
their home churches.
• In May 2001, Covenant College’s Chalmers Center for
Economic Development offered its first Christian Economic
Development Institute. MNA hopes that the churches of the PCA will
take advantage of this training resource as a means of equipping
church members in developing ministries in their own communities
and in neighboring communities of need.
• In response to the events of September 11, MNA is evaluating
the capabilities of PCA churches and presbyteries to respond to
disasters, both natural and those caused by terrorists, and will seek to
guide and encourage the churches and presbyteries in ways they may
minister in such situations beyond giving financial support only. See
the next section for a report on the response of the PCA to the events
of September 11.
• The annual Christian Community Development Association
(CCDA) meeting is a gathering point for PCA-affiliated urban and
mercy ministries. In 2001, at least 100 from PCA-affiliated ministries
attended the CCDA conference in Dallas. This conference remains
one of the best sources of expertise, experience and biblical
perspective from others engaged in such ministries. In 2002, CCDA
will meet in Los Angeles, November 6-10.
• At each General Assembly, GA/MNA sponsors a dinner, at
which time the work of one or more urban and mercy ministries is
presented. The dinner is open by advance reservation to all who wish
to attend.
• A directory of PCA-affiliated urban and mercy ministries is
available from GA/MNA on request. We invite the churches of the
PCA to visit our web site. Since the people and organizations listed in
the directory welcome opportunities to encourage and provide
consultation in forming new ministries, this directory is a valuable

<a id="ga30-p577"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=579 printed_page=577 -->

resource for any PCA church who is involved in urban and mercy
ministries, or who wishes to begin such ministries in their community.
PCA churches and presbyteries are encouraged to avail themselves of
the great wealth of experience that already exists in the PCA, as they
seek to develop or increase ministry in their own communities.
• Leadership development: The Thanksgiving Offering
continues to be a resource for encouraging the development of urban
and mercy leadership in the PCA. Primarily, the funds are used to
sponsor internships for new staff members, seminary students or
college students working in urban and mercy ministries. By the end
of February 2002, more than $62,000 was received in response to the
2001 Thanksgiving Offering appeal.
Beginning with the 11th General Assembly in 1983, Bethany Christian
Services has been endorsed by the PCA, upon GA/MNA’s annual review and
recommendation. We note with gratitude the services of Bethany to thousands
of Christian families throughout its 56 years of service, and commend Bethany
to the PCA for continued involvement and support. Their report is found as
Attachment A.

### D. MNA CHAPLAIN MINISTRIES

“Those who know their God shall prove themselves strong,
shall stand firm and do mighty things. ” Daniel 11:32
Core Values and Beliefs: GA/MNA Chaplain Ministries, in concert with the
doctrinal teachings of the PCA, and in obedience to the Great Commission of
our Lord, provides the organization, and thus the means, by which Teaching
Elders are commissioned and sent to minister to personnel and family
members within our government institutions (Military, Veteran Affairs, Law
Enforcement Agencies, State and Federal Prisons).
The Chaplaincy provides the PCA an opportunity and effective
manner for patterning ministry after the Scriptural and apostolic model of
taking the Gospel into the marketplace (Acts 17:17). Recently, in the January
2002 publication of Tabletalk, the devotional for the day of the 14th provided
insight on Romans 1:14-15, “I am a debtor both to Greeks and to the
barbarians, both to the wise and to the unwise. ”
The editor writes, “Paul is telling the Romans that he is sent to all the
world’s peoples, cultured and uncultured, educated and non-educated,
sophisticated and simple alike. The Gospel is for all of them, for ‘God shows
personal favoritism to no man’ (Gal. 2:6). This emissary of the Gospel feels
compelled to go to all classes of people. Clearly he sees his message as the
Good News for all. The world is his field.” This thought, along with many

<a id="ga30-p578"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=580 printed_page=578 -->

other teachings of the Scripture, provides the theological basis for the mission
of Chaplain Ministries.
Mission of MNA Chaplain Ministries: The Coordinator and Assistant
Coordinator’s mission, on behalf of the PCA, through GA/MNA is:
(1) To recruit, ecclesiastically endorse and send Teaching Elders
who are ordained to serve as Evangelists as they minister to
personnel in our government institutions;
(2) To oversee, encourage, supervise and train chaplains;
(3) To pastor all PCA Chaplains and their family members;
(4) To continually enhance the sense of “connectionalism”
between chaplains and their respective presbyteries and thus,
the denomination; and
(5) To raise an annual budget of $175,000 for the purpose of
supporting the mission of GA/MNA Chaplain Ministries.
Scriptural Purpose for the Chaplaincy:
(1) Evangelism;
(2) Pastoring, discipling and encouraging Christians;
(3) To communicate a Christian world view/message to all people
who are employed by our government institutions that will
enable and motivate the hearer, thus the institution to “give
reverence for the Holy Scriptures as God’s Word, and to be
received as the final instruction, source and norm for faith and
life.” (A New Systematic Theology of the Christian Faith,
Robert L. Reymond).
Progress and Challenges (Year 2001)
(1) Statistics:
Number of PCA Chaplains:
• US Army (Active Duty): 44, Reserve/National
Guard: 29
• US Navy (Active Duty): 20, Reserves: 6
• US AF (Active Duty) 11, Reserves/Air Guard: 8
• Chaplain Candidates: 13
• Veteran Affairs: 5
• State/Federal Prison: 6
• Other: 32

<a id="ga30-p579"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=581 printed_page=579 -->

(2) Number of military, Active Duty accessions during 2001: 6
U.S. Army
Robert R. Allman Evangel Presbytery
Thomas Faichney North Georgia Presbytery
Thomas McCort Illiana Presbytery
Michael Rightmyer North Texas Presbytery
“Bo” Welch Nashville Presbytery
Edward Yurus South Florida Presbytery
U.S. Air Force
John Sackett Potomac Presbytery
Brendon O’Dowd Rocky Mt. Presbytery
Craig Cowley North Texas
U.S. Navy
Johan Baik Korean Northwest
Chaplain Candidates
Bruce Sidebotham Rocky Mt. Presbytery
Jeffrey Schrage Missouri Presbytery
David Suh South Coast Presbytery
Mark Winton Nashville Presbytery
Jamie Freeland Tennessee Valley Presbytery
Daniel Kang Palmetto Presbytery
Civilian Chaplaincy (Hospital)
Michael Stewart Central Georgia Presbytery
Denominational Transfers (into PCA)
Richard Young Northern California Presbytery
Retirements
Stephen Leonard Missouri Presbytery
Charles Morrison Transfer to Potomac Presbytery
summary of ministry
The Chaplaincy provides unlimited opportunities to take the Gospel to
the market place. There seem to be endless calls for counseling, visitation, and
various other means to present a witness.
A couple of our young and creative chaplains have initiated and are
conducting a weekly worship service with the specific intention of reaching the
“troops,” most of whom are very young adults. Each week between 100 and
125 soldiers attend the service. A significant number of soldiers and family
members have come to faith in Jesus Christ as Savior because of this ministry.
Recently, during a visit to Ft. Campbell, KY, the chaplains shared with me the
number of cards that were filled out by the soldiers attending the service
during that particular month. Many of them expressed a desire to come to faith
in Christ. During the week, our chaplains or a designated representative call
on these individuals.

<a id="ga30-p580"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=582 printed_page=580 -->

Since the initiation of the war against terrorism the intensity of the
military mission has dramatically increased. In like manner, the intensity of
the workload for chaplains has increased. Service members, along with their
families, are more prone to seek out the services of chaplains during wartime.
Consequently, since 11 September there has been a significant increase in the
number of people coming to faith in Jesus Christ as Savior. Christians are
more aware of the need for spiritual growth and commitment to the tenants of
their faith. Commanders, as a general rule, are using their chaplains with
greater sincerity and enthusiasm. One of our chaplains who was accessioned
in 2001 wrote what summarizes the feelings of most of our chaplains:
I arrived at the base on 15 March and it seems I have not had
a second to catch my breath. Being so busy also means the
ministry opportunities are abundant. i am the pastor of the
Gospel Service that ministers to about 150 people. A little
different for a Presbyterian but we love it...they (the
congregation) are hungry’ to grow, and eager to serve. I have
already done a good deal of counseling with people who
struggle to live in a difficult place. Also, I am responsible for
flight line ministries as well as the Young Adults. It is
overwhelming but at the same time it is exciting to see what
the Lord is doing. As more evidence of God’s work, He has
assembled many gifted Christians here, including many in
leadership positions.
-Chaplain (CAPT) Brendon O ’Dowd, Minot Air Force Base
Several of our chaplains are deployed with units that are directly or
indirectly involved with the propagation of the war. (In actuality, the entire
military is in support of the war effort.) As a result, in the eyes of most
military personnel, spirituality (salvation and spiritual growth) takes on an
increased importance.
All three branches of the military (Army, Navy* and Air Force) have
requested additional chaplains. Although a specific number of PCA chaplains
has not been requested, the personnel sections of the three Chiefs of Chaplains
has requested additional PCA chaplains.
* Navy provides Chaplains to the Marines and Coast Guard.
Challenges and Needs
(1) The greatest need is to enlist prayer support for our chaplains
who are not directly involved in and with a civilian PCA congregation
(Active Duty Military, Veteran Affairs, and Prison Chaplains). To
assist in meeting this need we are requesting PCA congregations to

<a id="ga30-p581"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=583 printed_page=581 -->

sponsor a chaplain. Sponsorship includes a commitment to
consistently pray for the chaplain, his ministry, and family. Currently,
approximately 240 congregations sponsor a chaplain in prayer. In
addition, sponsoring congregations are requested to provide financial
support to Chaplain Ministries.
(2) Due to the increased demand for military chaplains a strong
emphasis is being placed on recruiting. This will be accomplished
through visitation at seminaries, presbytery meetings (when permitted
to present the Chaplaincy) and various other means, such as letters,
publicity, etc.
(3) Due to the increasing demands placed upon Chaplain
Ministries (recruiting, pastoral requirement, travel,
oversight/management, training, publicity, administrative assistance)
the annual budget, subject to the approval of the GA/MNA
Coordinator is being increased from $150,000 to $175,000. The goal
of enlisting 300 congregations to include Chaplain Ministries in their
annual budget continues. At present approximately 235 churches
support this ministry.
(4) There continues to be a need to “connect” the chaplaincy with
the presbyteries, thus the denomination. Due to the requirements of
the mission and assignment procedures, most military Active Duty
chaplains are unable to attend all scheduled presbytery meetings.
However, regardless of their situation, they are urged to attend at least
one meeting per year. If the mission permits, and if the distance is
within reason, they are urged to attend each meeting. Chaplains
stationed overseas find it impossible to attend. Therefore, each
chaplain is to provide a quarterly report to the presbytery MNA
Chairman. We ask that the Presbytery MNA Committee inform the
presbytery of the chaplain’s ministry and include him in prayer.
Conclusion
Providing Chaplains to our government institutions is a tremendous,
God-given opportunity for our Church. it is an organized manner in which we
are able to take the Gospel to the marketplace. as were a din romans 1: 14-
15, Paul sees himself as “a debtor both to the Greeks and the barbarians, both
to the wise and the unwise. ” The Great Commission is our mandate to take the
message to our military, Veteran Affairs, and prisons. Your prayer and
financial support is needed.
Many people ask if chaplains are able to preach the Gospel without
compromise. Yes, of course. However, because they minister in a “pluralistic
environment” there are times when they are challenged. This gives us more of

<a id="ga30-p582"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=584 printed_page=582 -->

a reason for praying for them, supporting them and unifying our efforts in
sponsoring such a vital ministry.
E. Church Vitality
The mission of GA/MNA Church Vitality is to encourage and equip
PCA churches to watch and pray for revival as we work and pray for more
vital churches that advance the Gospel by renewing the Church.
Watching and Praying for Revival
The Kingdom Campaign, a strategy based on Acts 1:14-15 to build
Kingdom intercessors, continued to be embraced by the Church in 2001.
Local church training events were held in five cities and presbytery-level
training was accomplished in two PCA presbyteries as well as at the Midwest
Regional Conference. Plans for 2002 include three presbytery events and up to
six local church events.
Apprentice training for The Kingdom Campaign has begun with ten
men currently in training. In addition, thirty more men have expressed an
interest in this ministry. A training event for these men was scheduled for
April 23-25, 2002 in the Atlanta area.
Key Covenant Fellowships are designed to help participants in their
study of revival, as they continue to watch and pray for it. Small groups across
america a reusing the spirit of revival and the helpful Leader’s Guide for
spirit of revival to learn more about and pray for revival.
In November 2001, the fourth annual Convocation on Revival and
Reformation was held at Trinity Presbyterian Church, Jackson, MS. Over 110
pastors and ruling elders studied the pattern of revival from the book of Acts
entitled Revive Us Again, as presented by keynote speaker Rev. Eric
Alexander. The Convocation for this year will be held October 15-17, 2002.
The topic of the convocation will be Rediscovering the Paradigms and
Principles of Biblical Manhood and Womanhood and will be addressed by two
plenary speakers: Dr. Ray Ortlund and Dr. Bruce Ware.
Working and Praying for More Vital Churches
The Embers to a Flame Conference at Briarwood Presbyterian Church
in Birmingham, AL provided training and encouragement to a record number
of pastors and ruling elders, some from as far away as New Zealand, Brazil,
Chili, and Northern Ireland. This conference is the main source of
revitalization teaching for churches on a statistical plateau or in decline. An
exciting development in this ministry was the translation of the materials into
the Korean language. A Korean Embers Conference was held March 5-8,
2001 and an African-American Embers Conference in November 2001.
The launch of the first Barnabas Institute occurred in August 2001.
Nine pastors and their wives were given the opportunity to evaluate and

<a id="ga30-p583"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=585 printed_page=583 -->

confirm their giftedness for leading their churches through the work of
revitalization. Eight of these couples are now enrolled in Fanning the Flames,
a two-year mentoring process. The second Barnabas Institute is scheduled to
be held in Atlanta July 8-11, 2002.
Materials for Days of Prayer and Fasting continue to be developed
and made available to presbyteries, churches and other groups who want to
hold this type of event. They are available free upon request.
Pastors-at-Large and Consulting Services are other ministries
available to churches and pastors in order to provide counsel, advice,
encouragement and further training in Church Vitality.
F. GA/MNA Stewardship
As we trust the Lord to advance the Gospel through renewing His
church, the Lord calls us to commit our time, talent and treasure to that
advancement. GA/MNA seeks to exercise a careful stewardship of all the
resources the PCA entrusts to her, and at the same time to encourage the
churches and members of the PCA to invest their resources in building the
Kingdom. GA/MNA ministries offer the opportunity for the Lord’s people to
use their resources in this way. From the Apostle Paul, “I thank my God every
time I remember you. In all my prayers for all of you, I always pray with joy
because of your partnership in the Gospel from the first day until now...” As
churches and individuals give of their time, talent and treasure, they enter into
a partnership, which provides the base for sending the Lord’s servants into the
harvest field.
Communications
• Multiply offers quarterly communication of what the Lord is doing
through various ministries of the PCA that advance the Gospel.
Weekly content for intercessory prayer with a regional focus is
included in each issue.
• The GA/MNA web site (www.pcanet.org/mna) is being built over
time into a resource for all kinds of information and referral sources
for ministries related to building the church. Multiply and the
Intercessor content for all regions is included on the web site.
Partnership Share/Askings Giving
In 2001, church giving to GA/MNA increased 8% over 2000. While
GA/MNA expresses gratitude to God for this increase, especially in a year that
saw as many unpredictable economic factors as did 2001, it must be
recognized that such increases provide for a “maintenance” level of ministry at
best and do not equip us to advance the Gospel in greater measure. GA/MNA

<a id="ga30-p584"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=586 printed_page=584 -->

encourages the churches of the PCA to make this giving to all committees and
agencies a high priority. The Lord is at work through the PCA in advancing
His Kingdom. In order for the PCA to grow stronger over the years, the
member churches must be committed to denominational support, just as a
particular church can be strong financially only if her members give faithfully.
GA/MNA is seeking to build endowment funds, in order to provide greater
resources for annual operations and program support.
Church Planting Project Funding:
(1) All church planters are supported by gifts designated for their
particular projects. GA/MNA invites churches and
individuals to consider joining as partners with a church
planter, enlisting him as a missionary sent and supported by
their church. There are no administrative transfer fees taken
from this project support. Every dollar given to a designated
ministry or project that is related to GA/MNA is used directly
and fully for that ministry or project. Since the inception of
this approach in 1999, all church planters have been able to
raise at least the minimum of support required, and all have
received at least as much - and in most cases far more -
support than they would have when support was raised by
GA/MNA staff. The increased support provides better
resources for a stronger launching of the church plant.
(2) Ethnic or multicultural church planters and movement leaders
require a special priority for project support. Often the
churches they lead in planting take longer to reach self-
support, yet these men often lack the strong networks of
family and friends in the PCA who will commit to their
support. GA/MNA strongly encourages churches to give a
high priority to supporting church planters who do not have a
background in the PCA, and who thus lack a strong personal
network through whom to raise support.
(3) GA/MNA welcomes contributions toward endowment funds
for the support of multicultural church planting, for annual
operations support, and for urban and mercy ministries, in
order to increase the number of church planters and others
who can respond to the call to minister to those in need.
(4) The Timothy Fund for Church Planting Apprentices is a
special fund to supplement the support of men who have been
graduated from seminary, but who need further training
before they begin to lead a church plant. Thus far, gifts
totaling $545,350 have been contributed to this fund,
substantially assisting many apprentices.

<a id="ga30-p585"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=587 printed_page=585 -->

Church Vitality and Chaplain Ministries
These two ministries are supported almost entirely by designated gifts,
since Partnership Share/Askings giving is insufficient to sustain them. These
ministries fulfill a vital role, and the potential for expansion of ministry is
great, as additional resources are provided.
Estate Design and Endowment Services
GA/MNA utilizes and recommends the services of the PCA
Foundation in assisting individuals in their stewardship. In addition to the
regular PCA Foundation programs, GA/MNA Advise and Consult Funds are
also available, managed by the PCA Foundation.
Five Million Fund for Church Buildings
Providing interest-free loans of up to $80,000, this fund continues to
be a helpful source for churches as they put together funding packages for their
building programs. Though the funds are limited, they often make a difference
in the church’s ability to qualify for a loan, or they provide additional working
cash that is very necessary. This fund is supported by the return payments of
the churches to whom loans are made, a swell as by donations.
G. Disaster Response
Blessed to be a Blessing in Times Like These
...PCA response to September 11, 2001, Terrorist Attacks
“They will be called the Holy People, the Redeemed of the Lord;
and you will be sought after, the city no longer deserted. ”
Isaiah 62:12
When the planes struck the World Trade Center on the morning of
September 11, Manir Buitrago was at work as usual at the Coffee Station in the
mall beneath the towers. Fortunately, he and countless others were able to
escape unharmed before tons of concrete came crashing down.
Manir was overjoyed to have survived the terrible disaster, but several
weeks later without a job and only meager unemployment checks, this young
native of Columbia was struggling to support himself, his wife, and their son.
When he heard that Redeemer Presbyterian in Manhattan was providing
assistance to people affected by the attacks, he contacted the church. Manir
was one of more than a thousand who received tangible support through
Redeemer Presbyterian after September 11.
“Through God’s providence,” says Redeemer’s executive pastor Terry
Gyger, “our church was able to respond efficiently and effectively at a

<a id="ga30-p586"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=588 printed_page=586 -->

moment’s notice because we already had five vehicles of ministry in place
ready to mobilize.”
Those five ministries are the Diaconate, Hope for New York, the
Urban Church Planting Center, Fellowship Groups, and the Counseling Center.
The Diaconate provides training and directs the work of members who give
their time to help individuals and families. Hope for New York donates funds
and coordinates volunteers for more than 30 different service agencies in the
metro area—from the Bowery Mission to Arukah Medical Ministry. Last year,
some 2,000 of Redeemer’s congregation volunteered their time with these
agencies.
Even though these ministries employ some 23 full-time staff,
Redeemer hired 13 additional people after the September disaster to help
manage the demand. Scores of people from the congregation volunteered their
time—handing out Bibles, praying with workers near the site, and visiting
firehouses with food, flowers, and encouragement.
Through Redeemer’s network of fellowship groups (approximately
130 are scattered throughout the metro area), the church was able to spread the
word about its mercy ministries and to discover needs. The Urban Church
Planting Center and Metro New York Presbytery, which partner with churches
through the area, were also channels for identifying needs and distributing
assistance. Later in the fall, Redeemer began providing grants to other
churches so they could provide assistance directly. In addition, the center set
up satellite locations for dispensing relief at other churches.
All of the churches of the Metro New York Presbytery dealt with
needs related to the September 11 attack on the World Trade Center. In
particular, however, Redeemer New Jersey, with congregations in Hoboken
and Teaneck, New Jersey, and Astoria Community Church, a mission church
in the Queens, were involved in ministry in major ways.
PCA churches and members responded with unprecedented
generosity; the following figures are totals through February 28, 2002
unless otherwise noted:
In response to the appeal sent out by Mission to North America,
$852,200 was received from churches and individual members of the PCA.
$550,000 was forwarded to Redeemer during October of 2001. Additionally,
$202,200 was forwarded to Redeemer on April 16, 2002. The balance of
$100,000 will be used in the Washington DC area or forwarded to Redeemer at
a later date.
In addition, Redeemer New York received $1,720,912 directly from
churches, individuals and other ministries and organizations. Funds had been
dispersed as follows:

<a id="ga30-p587"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=589 printed_page=587 -->

direct diaconate mercy including counseling as of jan 31 $976,915
Diaconate Supplies 10.117
Total Diaconate $987,032
Grants To Churches $251,957
To Other Ministries 78.557
Total Grants $330,514
Administration $202.366
Total Administration $202,366
It is anticipated that all remaining funds will be usedfor:
1. Diaconate Mercy care to cover anticipated WTC needs over the
next six months.
2. Ongoing Counseling Services where WTC-related demand
continues to rise at an increasing rate.
Note the Administration ” above refers to Redeemer Presbyterian’s cost
indirect administration of the relief funds. Mission to North America
received no fees for administering these funds. All administrative charges
by Redeemer were for direct administration of the relieffunds. Redeemer
used none of the moneys for any other purpose.
Redeemer’s Counseling Center is conducting three kinds of support
groups -
• Managing Your Career after September 11
• Financial Recovery after September
• Grief/Trauma Recovery Groups
The Grief/Trauma Recovery Groups are serving many who lost
family members in the Towers. In January 2002, the Center had 503 sessions,
which is a 98% increase over January 2001 and as of February 2002, was
receiving 30+ phone calls per 24-hour period.
Many who suffered loss of family and friends in the September 11
disaster, or whose homes, property or businesses were affected, will continue
to face emotional and spiritual challenges, in addition to monetary issues for a
long time to come. The generous giving will provide resources through which
the churches of the New York metro area can continue to minister to these
needs.
Washington, DC: PCA churches in the metro area include many members
who are in the employment of the federal government, and who therefore were
substantially affected either in coming alongside those who lost loved ones, or

<a id="ga30-p588"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=590 printed_page=588 -->

in having their employment responsibilities substantially changed due to the
heightened security and mobilization of the military.
Potomac Presbytery, through the leadership of local PCA deacons, is
providing assistance to families who lost jobs indirectly as a result of the attack
on the Pentagon.
Primarily hotel and transportation workers who lost employment due
to travel industry cutbacks, these families began to experience the worst of the
effects six months after September 11, when unemployment benefits and other
forms of assistance began to run out for those who had not yet found new
employment. $100,000 of the funds given is assigned for this purpose. If any
of these funds are not needed, they will be forwarded to Redeemer
Presbyterian for use in New York.
Please continue to make prayer for our brothers and sisters in
these two key cities a matter of high priority. There are great
opportunities and a great call for ministry. Pray that we will be found
faithful as the needs of people in these communities continue to be
addressed over time.

#### III. RECOMMENDATIONS

1. That having reviewed the work of the GA/MNA Coordinator during
2001 according to the General Assembly guidelines, the GA/MNA
Committee commends TE James C. Bland, III for his excellent
leadership, with thanks to the Lord for the good results in GA/MNA
ministry during 2001, and recommends his re-election as GA/MNA
Coordinator for another year.
2. That the General Assembly express its gratitude to God for the staff of
Mission to North America and for their faithful and effective work.

That the General Assembly thank God for the increasing church
planting that is occurring under local church and presbytery
initiatives; thank God for the labor in the Gospel of all PCA church
planters, multicultural church planters and missionaries, chaplains.
Further, that the General Assembly encourage faithful prayer that the
Lord will send additional laborers through GA/MNA into the fields
that are ripe for harvest in North America.
4. That PCA churches be reminded of their covenant obligation to give
high priority to giving GA/MNA and all other PCA Committees and
Agencies the Partnership Share/Askings, so that the Gospel is
advanced in greater measure.
That the General Assembly express its gratitude to the Lord for a
Thanksgiving Offering of more than $62,000, and commend to the
churches of the PCA the opportunity to support Urban and Mercy
Ministries, preferably through an offering taken during the
Thanksgiving season; and further, that churches consider how they
may directly participate in greater measure in Urban and Mercy
Ministries.
That the General Assembly express thanks to God for the long and
effective ministry of Bethany Christian Services in the area of
pregnancy counseling and adoption, encourage continued support by
churches and presbyteries, and invite the Bethany representative to

<a id="ga30-p590"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=592 printed_page=590 -->

speak to the Assembly for five minutes at this, or a more opportune
time.
7. That the General Assembly commend to the churches these resources
and opportunities for ministry available through GA/MNA:
• The opportunity to seek the Lord’s leading in church planting or
the chaplaincy.
• The opportunity to participate in prayer and financial support
(designated support in addition to Partnership Share/Asking
Giving) for:
■ Church Planting among the many different regions,
cultures and ethnic groups in North America;
■ Church Planting regional coordinators and movement
leaders;
■ Church Planting apprenticeships (through the Timothy
Fund);
■ Church Vitality;
■ Chaplain Ministries (especially through The Chaplain
Guardian Corps).
• The opportunity to build relationships of fellowship and ministry
with Korean churches and other ethnic groups within the PCA, as well
as with other true believers in our local communities.
• Utilize the resources of Church Vitality materials and
consultation services.
8. That Overture 22 from New River Presbytery be answered in the
affirmative that, effective September 1, 2002, a new Presbytery of the
Blue Ridge be formed by the division of New River Presbytery (along
with churches from James River Presbytery and Potomac Presbytery -
see separate overtures) as follows:

- **a.** New Boundaries of New River Presbytery: All of West Virginia except for the counties of Morgan, Berkeley and Jefferson.
- **b.** Boundaries of the newly formed Blue Ridge Presbytery: All churches and missions in Virginia, bounded on the north and east and including the counties (and contained independent cities) of

Frederick,Clarke, Fauquier, Culpepper, Orange, Louisa,
Fluvanna, Albemarle, Nelson, Appomattox, Campbell, and
Pittsylvania and bounded on the west and including the counties of
Giles, Pulaski, and Carroll (east of 1-77).
c. Revised boundaries of James River Presbytery will be as follows:
All of Virginia south and east of and including the following
counties, except for the counties of Accomac and Northampton on
the Eastern Shore: Northumberland, Westmoreland, King George,

<a id="ga30-p591"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=593 printed_page=591 -->

Stafford, Spotsylvania, Hanover, Goochland, Cumberland,
Buckingham, Prince Edward, Charlotte, and Halifax,
d. Revised Virginia boundaries of Potomac Presbytery will be as
follows: The Virginia counties of Arlington, Fairfax, Prince
William, and Loudon, including the cities within their borders.
9. That Overture 8 from James River Presbytery requesting revision of
the presbytery boundaries to include all of Virginia south and east of
and including the following counties, except for the counties of
Accomac and Northhampton on the Eastern Shore: Northcumberland,
Westmoreland, King George, Stafford, Spotsylvania, Hanover,
Goochland, Cumberland, Buckingham, Prince Edward, Charlotte, and
Halifax, be answered in the affirmative.
10. That the General Assembly re-elect TE James C. Pakala and TE
Douglas E. Lee to another term on the Presbyterian and Reformed
Joint Commission on Chaplains and Military Personnel (PRJC) as
members of the Class of 2006.
11. That the General Assembly adopt the 2003 GA/MNA Budget and
commend it to the churches for their support.
12. That the General Assembly establish a new bilingual Korean
language and English language presbytery according to the
following guidelines:
1) This presbytery will be called the Korean-American Presbytery.
2) This presbytery will be non-geographical as approved by the
General Assembly in 1982, drawing churches from the current
Korean language presbyteries across the PCA.
3) The presbytery will be divided in the future as it grows,
according to regular guidelines and process for presbytery
divisions.
4) Any PCA churches who belong to Korean language
presbyteries may join the convening group to form the
presbytery without examination.
5) This presbytery will be constituted when at least 5 PCA
churches have voted to join. MNA Korean Ministries
Coordinator Henry Koh will be the point of contact for this
process and the one to whom congregational votes are
reported. The PCA Stated Clerk will appoint the convener of
the presbytery.
6) All the presbytery documents, including minutes, will be kept
in English and Korean.
7) All the business of the presbytery will be conducted in
English, with Korean translation provided whenever
necessary.

<a id="ga30-p592"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=594 printed_page=592 -->

8) This presbytery would also be available to the Stated Clerk’s
office to help facilitate the review of Presbytery records from
the Korean language churches and presbyteries.
13. That the General Assembly give thanks to the Lord for the generous
response of the churches and members of the PCA to the special needs
resulting from the September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks in New York
City and Washington, D.C. And that the General Assembly give
thanks for God’s grace through the network of PCA churches in the
metropolitan cities who were able and willing to minister to their
communities in significant ways.
Further, that churches be encouraged:
To develop plans for ministry in case disasters should occur
in their communities, including ministry not only to PCA
families, but to other members of the Body of Christ, as well
as ministries proclaiming Christ’s mercy in Word and deed to
those outside the Body of Christ.
To respond with generous giving when appeals for disaster
response are issued through MNA for disasters occurring in
the United States and Canada. Churches and individuals are
encouraged to give through MNA, in order to reduce
accounting and receipting costs for recipient churches and
presbyteries, noting that MNA charges no administrative fees
for the channeling of these gifts.
Respectfully Submitted,
Committee on Mission to North America

ATTACHMENT A
BETHANY CHRISTIAN SERVICES
REPORT TO THE 30™ GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Psalm 48 speaks to the gratuitousness of our Lord and the
unfailingness of His love. The year 2001 was a year of tumult; personal safety
was threatened; the economy stalled; jobs were lost and stress on many
families brought them to the breaking point. But, through every trial, God
continued to surround us with His love.
When families are confronted with pressure and stress, children and
young people often bear the brunt of the results. In fact, isn’t it interesting that
in Mark 10:13-16, Jesus talks about the importance of children immediately
after he talks about divorce. Could He possibly be emphasizing the
importance of stability and family in the lives of children?
Building and providing loving, nurturing, Christian families for
children is the mission of Bethany Christian Services ... and 2001 was a
challenging year for us, too. Once again, we served more families and
children than ever before. One example of that ministry growth is the 1586
children who joined their adoptive families as a result of Bethany’s ministry ...
88 more children than the previous year. It was our privilege to be an
instrument of our Lord’s love throughout the year.
Bethany’s commitment to the Sanctity of Human Life continues as the
core value around which our ministry revolves. The first contact we have with
a young person needing care frequently occurs through Bethany Lifeline (1-
800-BETHANY), which uses volunteers to serve young people with
unplanned pregnancies. Tina is just one example of a call received through
Lifeline.
“Tina just found out she was pregnant when she called
Lifeline. Her baby was just five weeks old. Tina already had
an 18-year-old child. She was not at all sure she wanted to
marry the father of the child she was carrying. He would not
attend church with her and in fact ridiculed her for her
commitment to Christ. To make matters worse, Tina’s sister
advised her to “get rid of the baby ”. Our Lifeline volunteer
had a long talk with Tina about her baby and the meaning of
her commitment to Christ. They talked very specifically about
the forgiveness Jesus offered her. After the conversation, Tina
committed that she would not have an abortion and would
come to Bethany for additional counseling. ”

<a id="ga30-p594"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=596 printed_page=594 -->

Bethany’s ministry does not end with saving lives from abortion,
however. In fact, when a young person chooses life for her child, our work
with her is just beginning. The next responsibility is to lead that young person
through a process that chooses the best alternative for her and her child ...
parenthood or adoptive planning.
Throughout the counseling process, we often have the joy of seeing
pain transformed into joy. An unintended pregnancy frequently highlights the
agony of a young person who is lonely, feeling worthless, and alienated from
her family. Christian counseling helps that young person realize that she is a
reflection of God’s image and that He loves and forgives her.
One of the options presented to young people is that of adoptive
planning. Adoption isn’t chosen by every young person but is by a significant
number of the young people whom we counsel. The fact that many who find
themselves pregnant don’t even consider adoption as an option is one
propelling force that drives us to share the beauty of adoption with as many
young people as we can. We have the privilege of sharing with young people
that adoptive planning for their child is a parallel concept to that of God
adopting us into His family.
Bethany is not only unique in our commitment to adoption; we are
even more unique in our commitment to find homes for children with special
needs. In fact, over 40% of the 1586 children for whom we found adoptive
families were children who were: significantly medically or emotionally
challenged; part of a sibling group; somewhat older; or children of color. We
believe that the Christian’s commitment to protect and honor the life of all
children is the real test of our commitment to life.
When referring to “Bethany’s ministry”, we realize that we are truly
referring to your ministry. We have the joy of extending your Christian love to
families, young people and children in 77 locations around the United States
and in 13 foreign countries. But really, Bethany is only the conduit of your
love and concern. Your people serve on our Boards of Directors; they serve as
foster parents; they welcome children into their homes through adoption; they
contribute thousands of volunteer hours (11,305 in 2001); they pray and they
give of their financial resources - $1,360,000 last year. Truly Bethany’s
ministry is a beautiful example of the Christian body working in harmony.
It is our joy to be your representatives as we continue the work Jesus
began on earth. As we prepare for the year ahead, it is our prayer that He will
continue to bless Bethany’s ministry and that He will continue to bless the
Presbyterian Church in America through your involvement with us.

<a id="ga30-p595"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=597 printed_page=595 -->
