ATTACHMENT C
KOREAN-AMERICAN MINISTRIES PLAN IN THE PCA
(Its Past, Present and Future)
Rev. I. Henry Koh
July 20, 2001
I. PROLOGUE
A. Background and History
What is Korean-American ministry in the Presbyterian Church in America (henceforth, PCA)? When did it start and where is it going? Why
does it exist and why do we need it? What is God’s purpose in bringing
Korean Presbyterians to America and especially to the PCA? What are we to
think of the 180 Korean churches and 300 Korean Teaching Elders in the
PCA? These are the questions that we need to attempt to answer before we
can develop a plan for Korean-American Ministries in the PCA.
What follows is a brief history of Korean Presbyterianism. Korean
Presbyterianism evolved as a result of the work done by American
Presbyterian missionaries during the 19th and 20th centuries. God blessed the
Korean Presbyterian churches with growth beyond expectation. Now there are
close to 8 million Presbyterians in Korea who are zealous for evangelism and
missions beyond the borders of Korea. In fact, according the latest statistics,
South Korea sends more cross-cultural missionaries to the world than any
other country except for the United States. It is extraordinary that a nation as
small as Korea can accomplish this. It is all the more remarkable in light of
the fact that Christianity is only about a century old in this nation. Of course,
it is not Korean Christians, but God Himself who has used this country to
achieve His great purposes. What is God’s plan for Korean Christians in
redemptive history? It is undeniable that God is using Korean Christians in
some special way in this century to fulfill His Great Commission.
Starting in the early 1970s, Korean Christians have immigrated to this
country. Korean immigrant history is only about 30 years old, and the latest
statistics report that there are approximately 2 million Koreans living in
America. Yet, they have planted about 4000 churches across the US and have
sent a large percentage of students to many evangelical and reformed
seminaries across this country (about 15-35% depending on seminary).
Furthermore, many Korean-Americans (henceforth, Ko-Ams) have received
advanced degrees from these seminaries. Korean Christians have formed their
own denominations in the US in accordance with their backgrounds from
Korea, but some have joined American denominations like the PCA.

<a id="ga30-p599"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=601 printed_page=599 -->

B. The Purpose of God in bringing Korean Christians to the US
Why did God bring Korean Christians to America? Korea was once
considered a “hermit kingdom”—completely closed to the rest of the world,
until as late as the end of the 19th century. Korean people never lived
anywhere outside of their own country throughout their long history. But at
the beginning of 20th century, after Christianity arrived in Korea, God began
very deliberately to push the Korean people beyond their borders—first by the
invasion of Japan, later by the encroachment of communism, and then, by
economic necessity—to China, Japan, the former Soviet Union, the US, and
the rest of the world. Now they are one of the most scattered people in the
world. Today, there is almost no country in the globe where Korean people do
not have a foothold. But again, what is God’s purpose in bringing Korean
Christians to the US and especially into the PCA? I believe God has brought
Korean Christians to the US in order to use them to fulfill the Great
Commission of Christ in cooperation with Christians here in America.
Why in America? I believe the US has been endowed with special
blessings from God in order to reach the world with the Gospel of Jesus Christ
in the 21st Century. Perhaps one could argue that America in the 21st century is
the Rome of the 1st century! All political, economic, and technological roads
seem to lead and go through America now. I believe God intends to use
Christianity in the US to fulfill His Great Commission in these last days as He
used Rome in late antiquity to spread the Good News to the entire civilized
world. Moreover, God is using the English language today as he used the
Greek language in the days of the early Christians. Therefore, it is very
significant for God to bring Korean Christians to the US. Here, Korean
Christians are equipped with the language and culture needed to reach the rest
of the world.
Why the PCA? The Presbyterian Church in America is admittedly a
smaller and younger denomination, but it is without a doubt one of the fastest
growing denominations in the US. Perhaps, one could argue that she is the
only reformed denomination with vital vision of the Great Commission. The
late Francis Schaeffer once said that the PCA is the hope for America. If his
assessment is true, then the PCA is the hope for the world. She is the
denomination that balances the rich tradition of reformed theology with the
dynamic faith and vision necessary to reach America and the world with the
Gospel of Christ through church planting. Korean Presbyterians in the PCA,
whether they realize it or not, have been given the great privilege and
responsibility of working together with American Presbyterians to fulfill the
task of Great Commission.

<a id="ga30-p600"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=602 printed_page=600 -->

II. MISSION
A. The Acts 1:8 Principle
If God has brought Korean Presbyterians to America in order to fulfill
the Great Commission of Christ, Korean Christians must realize that
they have to obey God’s command to preach the Gospel to America
first. “But you will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes on
you; and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and
Samaria, and to the ends of the earth” (Acts 1:8). Korean Christians
must face their Judea and Samaria and then meet the need to be
witnesses to the ends of the earth. How will they meet this call? They
will meet this call not by isolating themselves within their own
communities, but in cooperation with other American Christians, by
becoming active witnesses in multi-ethnic America.
B. The Benefits of Multi-Culturalism
In their attempt to reach other ethnic people in America with the
Gospel, first-generation Korean-American Christians face many
challenges due to linguistic and cultural differences. But second-
generation Ko-Ams have a much greater hope. growing up ina
Korean family and within a first-generation Korean church, second
generation Ko-Ams have inherited the fervent faith of their fathers and
the belief in rigorous academic pursuits found in the Korean tradition.
At the same time, they are also completely American in language and
culture. As such I believe that these multi-cultural, second-generation
Ko-Ams make perfect candidates to not only reach multi-ethnic
America but also the rest of the world with the Gospel.
Because they experience the variety of challenges that are part and
parcel of being a minority in the US, second-generation Ko-Ams
possess the potential to empathize with all the trials that other ethnic
minorities inevitably encounter. Perhaps more importantly, ethnic
minorities from other people groups may find many commonalties
with Ko-Ams that could not be found with people within the bounds
of western culture. Furthermore, many second-generation Ko-Ams
have mastery of the English language along with the status of US
citizenship. As a result, more opportunities can present themselves to
multi-cultural Ko-Ams that may not appear for those from mono-
cultural backgrounds. They are like the Christian missionary of first
century, for example, who possessed the dual resources of mastery of
the common Greek language and the benefits of Roman citizenship.
C. Passing the Torch
Korean first-generation churches in the US have been busy reaching
ethnic Koreans by planting Korean-language churches and by sending
and supporting Korean missionaries to reach the world with the

<a id="ga30-p603"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=603 printed_page=null -->

Gospel. So far they have done well in reaching out to Koreans in
America by planting about 4000 churches and by sending and
supporting thousands of missionaries around the world. But now they
have to be alerted to the urgent priority at hand—reaching their own
children, the second generation. Many second-generation Ko-Ams are
leaving the church in droves as soon as they leave their home for
college and university. Many first-generation Koreans have failed in
passing on the faith to their own second-generation children.
Admittedly, there are tremendous cultural and linguistic challenges
that separate them both. But first-generation Koreans and their
churches need to develop and implement new strategies to not only
close off these generational gaps but, more importantly, to pass the
torch of the Gospel in order that they might be used of the Lord to be a
part of God’s Great Commission.
What will happen to the faithful and fervent church-planting
and missionary zeal of the first-generation Korean church if the
second generation is neglected? Who will take over the church
planting in America and abroad and evangelical endeavor of the first
generation, unless the first generation brings up and trains leaders
from the second generation? Indeed, it is God who calls, but we are
responsible for praying that the Lord of harvest sends forth laborers
from the second generation. There can be no hope for first-generation
Korean churches unless they reach, train, and develop second-
generation leaders for Kingdom work. I believe God sent Korean
Christians to America not only to plant 4000 Korean-language
churches but also to raise and foster leadership from second-
generation Ko-Ams so that they can continue to reach out to America
and to the rest of the world through church planting. Thus, strategies
and development of Korean ministries in the PCA should be divided
into first- and second-generation categories.
First-Generation Ministry: Its Strengths and Limitations; its
Opportunities and Obstacles
There are approximately 180 Korean churches in the PCA within their
own Korean-language presbyteries. But we should aim to bring more
independent Korean Presbyterian churches into the PCA as such
opportunities present themselves. There are many first-generation
Korean pastors within these independent churches whose theological
and ecclesiological vision coincides with the vision of the PCA. They
should be approached and persuaded to join the PCA. As for second-
generation Ko-Am pastors and pastors-to-be, there is no doubt that the
PCA continues to possess a positive image. The PCA is highly
regarded by first-generation Korean pastors as well because of its
conservative and reformed faith standards. We do, however, have

<a id="ga30-p602"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=604 printed_page=602 -->

some roadblocks. Some Korean Presbyterian churches, although they
have indicated a preference for the PCA, nevertheless shy away
because of a strong dislike for some pastors and Korean presbyteries
already within the PCA. It is evident that attracting other Korean
Presbyterian churches into PCA depends very much on the quality of
Korean pastors in PCA and the reputation of Korean-language
presbyteries. Our task, then, is to raise the quality and the standard of
Korean pastors and the Korean-language presbyteries. Korean
churches in the PCA have the enormous potential to attract more
Korean Presbyterian pastors and churches—not to even mention the
second-generation—into the PCA if they raise the bar on their own
standard of quality. How do we accomplish this? What are some
steps we can take to bring this about? It is indeed a complex and
multi-faceted task that requires much prayer and planning. We also
have to keep in mind that there are approximately 22 Korean first
generation churches already in English-language Presbyteries and
some Korean first-generation churches, not to mention secondgeneration multiethnic churches, prefer English-language
geographical presbyteries over Korean-language presbyteries. It is
also my desire that English-language Presbyteries be open and
sensitive in handling the incoming, new Korean pastors.
1. We are thankful that many Korean churches have chosen the
PCA to be their denomination and as such we should continue
to foster closer working relationships with them. The PCA
could provide much assistance to these churches as they attempt
to assimilate themselves into the mainstream of the PCA in their
theology, their standards of faith, their vision of church
planting, their conformity to PCA polity via the Book of Church Order (henceforth, BCO), and their participation in various
committees and conferences. This is but one way the quality of
Korean churches in the PCA will improve. Korean churches
alone, however, cannot accomplish this. there needs to be a
multi-level effort within PCA to help Korean churches integrate
themselves better into the denomination. As things stand,
Korean churches remain largely isolated—there are sizable gaps
between the mainstream of the PCA and the Korean churches.
This is due, primarily, to the differences in language and
culture. We also need the involvement of Anglo-American
pastors, churches and presbyteries for this effort. One cannot
expect perfect assimilation of Korean churches into the
denomination until perhaps the second generation of Korean
churches. Nevertheless, it is important to seek such assimilation
from the first generation, in order to lay the groundwork for
later generations of Korean-American churches.

<a id="ga30-p603"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=605 printed_page=603 -->

2. The roadblocks for the assimilation are primarily within two
spheres: language and church polity. There is an urgent need to
translate and edit all appropriate documents into the Korean
language—for example, including BCO (which has already
been translated but needs further revision and republication).
Furthermore, we need to consider how to best train first-
generation Ko-Am pastors and sessions to use the BCO
appropriately for their church and presbyteries. Often, they
ignore the BCO, because of a lack of knowledge and experience
with it. As a result, they will follow older habits and
conventions from their experiences in Korea, at both session
and presbytery levels. There exists a need to develop better
methods of educating, re-educating, and maintaining a protocol
of continuing this education of Korean-American pastors and
sessions with the knowledge of the BCO. This is an extremely
important task. How we accomplish this will require further
study and planning.
3. As mentioned earlier, Korean Churches in the PCA need to
realize the urgency of preparing for second-generation Ko-Am
ministry not only for their own survival but also for the task of
fulfilling the Great Commission. They need to train second-
generation leaders, so that these leaders in return will not only
be able to train their own children in Sunday school, in junior
and senior high school groups, but also be able to plant multi
ethnic churches. They need to be constantly reminded of the
importance and priority of this mission and should be
encouraged to increase the budget for carrying out this task in
raising second-generation ministry leaders.
4. Korean churches should be encouraged to participate in
partnership share giving in the PCA. Unfortunately, most
Korean churches are small and struggling, and as such they are
unable to give in large amounts. But in so far as they are
partners in ministry, they need to be encouraged to give
appropriately to causes outside of their local church spheres.
5. It is reported that only about 10% of Korean teaching elders
participate in the “tax shelter annuity”—the pension plan of the
PCA. Korean pastors need to be covered with health insurance
and with the Long-Term Disability Plan of the PCA. Other
benefit plans available through Insurance, Annuity and Relief
need to be made known to these pastors and sessions. Most of
the benefit program brochures have been translated into Korean
language, but we need to persuade the sessions and
congregations in the Korean church to participate in these

<a id="ga30-p604"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=606 printed_page=604 -->

pension plans and other benefits available through IAR and also
encourage presbyteries to enforce the plan.
Korean churches need help in the training of Sunday school
teachers and in choosing proper educational materials. Some
seminars for Sunday school teachers through Korean
presbyteries have been held in the past, but further efforts need
to be coordinated to provide training for all Korean
presbyteries. Secondly, I believe the PCA has excellent
educational materials published through Great Commission
Publications. On the one hand, translation into the Korean
language is not an issue as most of the materials are used for
English-speaking second-generation children. Unfortunately,
not all Korean PCA churches utilize these materials for their
educational programs. More thought needs to be given as to
how we can better promote GCP materials in our Korean
churches and presbyteries.
Korean-language presbyteries should elect bilingual pastors as
their Stated Clerks so that they will be able to communicate
effectively with the Stated Clerk of the General Assembly. A
bilingual Stated Clerk will also be able to communicate more
effectively the English-language documents he receives from
the General Assembly to Korean-speaking presbytery members.
There should also be periodic training of Stated Clerks in
Korean-language presbyteries in order to conform Korean
presbyteries to the BCO and other standards of the PCA.
Korean presbyteries should aim to have all documents in both
languages if they are going to retain second-generation pastors,
licentiates, and other ministry candidates. If possible, they
should endeavor to conduct the presbytery meeting in both
languages. In order to assimilate Korean presbyteries into the
PCA, it maybe helpful if English-language presbyteries and
Korean-language presbyteries meet together once a year or once
every two years not only for fellowship but also to participate in
joint projects such as multi-ethnic church planting.
The Coalition of Korean Churches, an association of Korean
Churches in the PCA, was formed in 1998 in order “to maintain
Christian fellowship among the Korean Churches in the PCA
and to discuss and carry out consultation and evangelical
ministry cooperatively” (CKC-PCA Operating Manual 1-3).
The Coalition needs to be careful in not deviating from the
original purpose of its creation and assume the role of a synod.
Not only is this contrary to Presbyterian polity, it can be a
potential hotbed of Korean church politics.

<a id="ga30-p605"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=607 printed_page=605 -->

E. Second-Generation Ministry: Its Strengths and Limitations; its
Opportunities and Obstacles
As alluded to earlier, second-generation Korean-American ministry is
the hope of Korean ministries within the PCA. They bring enormous
potential to the PCA for multi-ethnic church planting in the US and in
the world if certain factors are considered. For example, Ko-Am
leaders need to be properly trained. I believe one of the reasons why
first-generation Korean ministry in the PCA exists is for the raising of
second-generation leadership in the PCA. When second-generation
leadership training occurs, Korean-language presbyteries may
eventually be phased out. However, the task of training second-
generation Ko-Am leadership cannot be done by the first generation
alone. This is because of the multi-faceted challenges inherent in the
convergence of two different languages and cultures. Simply put, most
first-generation Korean pastors are not equipped to mentor, teach,
coach, train, and support second-generation leaders. Help is needed
from culturally-sensitive Anglo-American pastors, who not only
possess the vision for multi-ethnic ministry but also the resources to
mentor, coach, and train second-generation Ko-Am church planters.
Financial support is also needed from both Korean and Anglo-
American Churches for the effort of multi-ethnic church planting by
second-generation Ko-Am church planters. We thank God that the
PCA is a denomination that not only has the tools and resources to
train second-generation Ko-Am church planters but also have the
vision for the multi-ethnic church planting.
1. America is increasingly becoming a multi-ethnic society with
more than 150 language groups living side by side in its cities.
America is a country where you can reach the world without
leaving the country. How do we reach these populations with
the Gospel of Jesus Christ? “And how can they believe in the
one of whom they have not heard? And how can they hear
without someone preaching to them? And how can they
preach unless they are sent?” (Rom. 10:14-15). In order to
spread the Gospel of Jesus Christ to the world, two things are
needed: (1) spirit-filled church planters, and (2) financial
resources to plant churches. It seems that the time is ripe to
recruit multi-ethnic church planters from second-generation
Ko-Ams. In 1999, MNA sponsored the first Korean English-
Ministry Pastors ’ Conference at the Korean Central
Presbyterian Church in Vienna, VA. Much to my surprise,
more than 110 pastors, leaders, and educators showed up
despite meager publicity that was made primarily through
email. We can seek out and find potential multi-ethnic church

<a id="ga30-p606"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=608 printed_page=606 -->

planters from second-generation Ko-Ams (many of whom are
already in major reformed seminaries in the US) by visiting
them and by inviting them to the annual Korean EM Pastors ’
Conference and making the PCA known to them. “The harvest
is plentiful but the workers are few. Ask the Lord of the
harvest, therefore, to send out workers into his harvest field”
(Matt. 9:37-38).
2. MNA possesses one of the most outstanding tools for the
church planter: the Assessment Center that meets three times a
year. (I understand MTW also has an assessment program for
potential missionary candidates.) We need to make the
Assessment Center known to the aspiring second-generation
Ko-Am church planters and invite them to come be assessed. I
highly recommend the assessment program to young
seminarians and graduates even if they do not intend to church
plant. I believe it is an excellent training resource for any
serious young pastor and leader.
3. The training of second-generation Ko-Am church planters and
pastors: There are church planting training conferences
annually each summer available to all church planters.
Second-generation Ko-Am church planters and pastors should
be encouraged to attend and should be assisted financially if it
is possible and necessary. I understand that such conferences
are also held regionally. Regardless, and if possible, second-
generation Ko-Am pastors should be sought out actively and
intentionally to participate in such events and in order to
recruit and train them.
4. Second-generation Ko-Am church planters should be
encouraged to seek internships and apprenticeships in Anglo-
American churches in urban areas. Apprenticeships under
qualified Anglo-American church planters will provide them
with broader experiences and training to serve in the multi
cultural America. Funds should be raised for this purpose as
well.
5. Second-generation Ko-Ams should be encouraged to serve as
assistant or associate pastors under qualified senior pastors in
Anglo-American churches in urban areas. The few years that
they could commit to such an experience will provide deeper
cultural understanding and training as pastors and church
planters. i myself served a san assistant pastor ina large
Anglo-American church right after I graduated from seminary
for two years back in 1970. This experience gave me

<a id="ga30-p607"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=609 printed_page=607 -->

invaluable understanding and training to see insights into
multi-cultural America.
6. For those second-generation Ko-Ams who are already planting
multi-ethnic churches, culturally attuned and experienced
pastors (either Korean or Anglo) must train them as mentors
and coaches in an ongoing relationship for a few years or
more. Someday, these second-generation pastors will be able
to mentor, coach, and teach others who follow them.
7. Second-generation Ko-Ams Women’s Ministry: The
importance of women in the church for Gospel ministry can
never be underestimated. More than half of the church
members are women. They are highly educated and motivated
Korean second-generation women needing guidance and
training to be more effective servants of the Lord. WIC
(Women in the Church) of the PCA can offer outstanding
leadership trainings for the Korean second-generation women.
8. Fundraising: When it comes to second-generation Ko-Am
ministry, fundraising is perhaps the most challenging
component. For example, funds must be raised to run the
annual EM Pastors ’ Conference, to assist candidates to come
to the Assessment Center and other church planting training
conferences, and to initiate and enter apprenticeship programs.
Funds should be raised from both first-generation Korean
churches as well as from Anglo-American churches.
9. The significance of the second-generation Ko-Am ministry
and its vision should be shared not only with all first-
generation Korean churches but also with Anglo-American
churches in PCA. We must seek convincing cooperation from
both first-generation Korean churches and Anglo-American
churches for the future of the denomination. it is my prayer
that second-generation Ko-Ams will be able to significantly
contribute to the PCA as we seek to become a truly multi
cultural and multi-ethnic denomination that is honoring to
God.
III. PLANNING: Long Term and Short Term
A. Long Term Planning
1. What will Korean-American Ministries in the PCA look like
twenty years from now? Perhaps a brief look back to Korean
ministries in the PCA twenty years ago might help us. I came
into the PCA about twenty-one years ago (1980) from the old
northern Presbyterian Church (UPCUSA). At that time, the
PCA had only a handful of Korean churches and pastors. The

<a id="ga30-p608"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=610 printed_page=608 -->

formation of a non-geographical Korean-language Presbytery
in 1982 was an excellent decision on the part of the General Assembly for the advancement of Korean ministries in the
PCA. By doing so, the PCA showed tremendous foresight in
vision and strategy than other denominations in promoting
Korean ministries. Korean-language presbyteries grew,
starting from one presbytery to the present number of seven
presbyteries. It is possible that they may grow to an even
greater number. But twenty years from now, will Korean
language presbyteries exist in the PCA? It will depend very
much on how many new Korean immigrants come into
America.
If the inflow of immigration from Korea were to stop, Koreanlanguage presbyteries would probably be phased out by then.
We would no longer need first-generation Korean-language
presbyteries. But what about second-generation Ko-Am
ministry? Would they perfectly blend and assimilate into the
mainstream of the PCA, making the denomination a truly
multi-ethnic and multi-cultural one? In twenty years, a thirtyyear old second-generation Ko-Am will be fifty years old and
their children (the third generation) will be in their twenties
and teens. Would we be able to accomplish our dream of
multi-ethnic and multi-cultural churches in the PCA through
second-generation Ko-Ams by then? It will all depend on
what we do with second-generation Ko-Ams now; that is, on
what we do to train and support them to be competent multi
ethnic church planters. Now is the time for the PCA to
mentor, coach, teach, and train Korean-American leaders to
become a potent force in our vision of becoming a truly multi
cultural denomination. May we not miss our opportunity
before it is too late.
Provided that the PCA has done the work of raising secondgeneration Ko-Am leaders now, I can envision that the PCA in
twenty years will be a vibrant, dynamic, and growing multi
ethnic and multi-cultural denomination. We must make our
plan for Korean-American ministries with that vision in our
minds.
What about ten years from now? What will Ko-Am ministries
in the PCA look like?
Who will train the growing number of Ko-Am leaders in the
PCA? I believe in ten years the leadership of Korean churches
will be in the hands of the 1.5- and second-generation Ko-Am
leadership. Many first-generations pastors are retiring at a

<a id="ga30-p609"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=611 printed_page=609 -->

rapid rate. I know of two pastors in two large Korean
churches in the east coast who have retired. In both of these
prominent churches, 1.5-generation pastors have replaced
them. In ten years, most of the pastors of large Korean
churches in the US will retire and will be succeeded—most
probably by a 1.5-generation pastor who will be able to
minister to both the first and second generation of Korean
immigrant congregation members. In the meantime, second-
generation Ko-Am leaders will reach an age for fruitful
ministry. They will be able to help and train their younger
church planters with the experiences and skills they have
gained. They will also be perfectly blended and assimilated
into the mainstream of the PCA at all levels of participation.
The key assumption is that these second-generation leaders
have been appropriately trained and mentored to assume the
mantle of trainer and mentor.
3. Since the needs of second-generation Ko-Am leadership are
unique, the creation of an institute specifically geared toward
the development of resources for these leaders must be
considered. This think-tank/resource center could provide a
variety of educational and ministry resources needed to aid the
second-generation Ko-Am and the Asian-American leader, in
general, in his training and development. as apart of the
PCA, this institute should be affiliated with a seminary where
the assets of a graduate school can be utilized for the
development and promotion of materials and events.
B. Short Term Plan
1. Making a Network of Second-Generation Leaders: We must reach
out to second-generation Ko-Am seminarians as well as to those
who have recently graduated from seminary and are currently
serving in first-generation Korean churches and help connect them
together into a network of pastors, leaders, and educators from
within and without the PCA. Second-generation Ko-Am leaders
are in many ways isolated without adequate guidance and
leadership from older pastors and mentors. They should be sought
out in every way possible and brought together for mutual
edification and resource. This can be accomplished through a
variety of means:
(a) Key Korean-American and Anglo-American leaders in urban
centers like Los Angeles, New York, and Chicago need to be
contacted and brought together for consultation and
strategizing—especially in connecting second-generation Ko-
Ams with potential mentors and trainers.

<a id="ga30-p610"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=612 printed_page=610 -->

(b) Major evangelical seminaries must be visited to network
future Ko-Am leaders with each other and with the PCA.
Furthermore, as the number of Ko-Ams in teaching and
administrative roles within evangelical seminaries increase,
the PCA needs to intentionally build relationships with the key
individuals in order to facilitate networking.
(c) The annual English-Ministry Pastors’ Conference provides
not only an opportunity for those within the PCA to network
but also an avenue for those interested in the PCA to find out
more about this denomination.
2. Training Second-Generation Leaders as Church Planters: The
training and development of future leaders can be accomplished
through a variety of means:
(a) A network of culturally attuned pastors/mentors within the
PCA needs to be developed so that potential second-
generation Ko-Am pastors and planters can be matched with
key mentors in a short-term internship program. As I
mentioned previously, most, if not all second-generation Ko-
Ams are unable to receive any mentoring through first-
generation Korean pastors and churches. As such, we need to
consider how to raise the necessary funds to bring this
innovative yet necessary program about.
(b) The “Assessment Center” needs to be promoted and utilized
for the training of future second-generation Ko-Am pastors
and planters. As participants are assessed and trained as
church planters, we can train them for fruitful ministry in the
PCA. A further benefit is that the Assessment Center often
acts as a gateway connecting second-generation Ko-Am
leaders into the main body of the PCA. Already a number of
second-generation Ko-Ams from other denominations that
have come through this program are now a part of the PCA.
(c) The annual EM Pastors ’ Conference is another avenue by
which we can aid in the training of future leaders in the PCA.
I am excited as Tim Keller sees this vital vision and has
agreed to speak at the 2002 EM Pastors ’ Conference this
coming January and has also committed to speak every two
years thereafter.
3. A Leadership Conference for the Korean second-generation
women should be planned jointly by MNA and WIC in order to
train the women leaders in the second-generation multiethnic
churches.
4. Sharing the Vision of Korean Ministries: This is in fact the largest
aspect of the work. The vision has to be shared with both first-

<a id="ga30-p611"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=613 printed_page=611 -->

and second-generation Ko-Am pastors and churches as well as
with Anglo-American churches. I, therefore, need to take every
opportunity that comes before me to share the vision with them.
Without this, plans cannot move ahead, prayers cannot be focused,
and funds cannot be raised. Every opportunity to share the vision
with Anglo-American churches should also be pursued in order
that they may also play a part in participating in the Korean-
American ministry strategy within the PCA.
IV. POSTSCRIPT
This ministry plan cannot be carried on without prayer and the
guidance of the Holy Spirit. As such, the plan will need to be constantly
revised, added, and updated.
Respectfully submitted,
I. Henry Koh
Coordinator of Korean Ministries
Mission to North America
Presbyterian Church in America

<a id="ga30-p612"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=614 printed_page=612 -->

SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT
OF THE
COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA
TO THE 30™ GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE PCA
The Committee on Mission to North America submits the following
recommendation as a Supplement to the Report of the Committee on Mission
to North America to the 30th General Assembly:
Recommendations:
10. That the General Assembly re-elect TE James C. Pakala and TE
Douglas E. Lee to another term on the Presbyterian and Reformed
Joint Commission on Chaplains and Military Personnel (PRJC) as
members of the Class of 2006.
14. That Overture 13 from Session of Faith Reformed Presbyterian Church
(see Overtures, p. 54) be answered in the negative.
Rationale:
■ This overture originated from the Session of Faith Reformed
Church in Fairmont, Virginia. It was rejected by New River
Presbytery. Per RAO 10-10, the Session forwarded the
overture to General Assembly.
■ Two other PCA churches are located in the counties which
this Session asks to have moved to Pittsburgh Presbytery.
These two churches are opposed to the change. Thus, of the
three churches directly affected by the change, two are
opposed.
■ Pittsburgh Presbytery has not taken action on this change.
15. That Overture 16 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery to redefine the
geographical boundaries of Rocky Mountain Presbytery to include the
State of Montana” [see 30-31, III, 15, p. 184] be answered in the
affirmative.
16. That Overture 23 from the New York State Presbytery to redraw New
York State Presbytery boundary be answered in the affirmative,
pending approval from the Ascension Presbytery. [See 30-31, III, 16,
p. 185, for text of Overture 23 and Communication 5 on pg. 186.]

<a id="ga30-p613"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=615 printed_page=613 -->

REPORT FROM
THE COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO THE WORLD
TO THE THIRTIETH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
June 2002
Mission to the World is the mission-sending agency of the PCA,
helping to fulfill the Great Commission by advancing
reformed and covenantal church-planting movements
through word and deed in strategic areas worldwide.
Mission to the World is the mission sending agency of the PCA...
MTW is committed to being a servant to the PCA. by that we mean we
exist to serve the missionaries and the churches of the PCA. We believe
that one way we are serving the PCA is by hosting the first ever PCA-
wide missions conference in November 2002. The theme of the
conference is “Grasping God’s Grace Personally To Give God’s Grace
Globally.” We are planning expectantly for 2000-3000 people from the
PCA to gather in Atlanta to be challenged by plenary messages from Paul
Kooistra and Joe Novenson focused on the grace of God. We will also
have over 200 workshops with topics such as hosting a dynamic church
missions conference, ministry to street children, sending short-term
teams, and church planting in global cities.
MTW is also charged with providing the best possible service to the
missionaries of the PCA. we desire to be effective, encouraging and
efficient in our administrative ministry. We serve by identifying,
selecting, training and sending the men and women of the PCA who
believe God is leading them to cross-cultural ministry around the globe.
A major effort we undertook in 2001 was a comprehensive redesign of
our organizational operations and structures. The primary change was a
complete reworking of our field operations structure. We now have five
international directors: Ken Thompson, Latin America; John Rollo, Sub-
Saharan Africa; Paul Taylor, Asia/Pacific; Brian Deringer, Europe; and
Scott Seaton, Enterprise for Christian-Muslim Relations. We believe the
changes from our organizational redesign will fundamentally improve the
advancement of our church planting and enhance our ministry to
missionaries.

<a id="ga30-p614"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=616 printed_page=614 -->

MTW has also seen advances in the training and equipping of our
missionaries. Our Global Training and Development (GTD) team, which
we initiated three years ago, has helped us make significant improvements
in the equipping and ongoing development of our missionaries. GTD is a
unique, global team comprised primarily of missionaries who are tasked
with training and organizational development within MTW. The GTD
team led a training event for our worldwide leaders in November 2001.
Missionaries who attended evaluated it as the highest quality training
event we have ever had, and we believe it has led us to more
organizational alignment and commitment than ever around our primary
purpose of advancing church-planting movements. The GTD team will
also inaugurate our eight-week pre-field training program—the New York
Urban Internship—planned for the summer of 2002. Over fifty new long
term missionaries will participate in the training that is focused on church
planting, cross-cultural urban living and ministry, team dynamics and
language acquisition. This training was preceded by a two-week pilot
program in 2001.
We are pleased that we are seeing church partnership models begin to
materialize. It is encouraging to see MTW play an enabling, catalytic role
for churches in the PCA who desire to meaningfully partner together for
global missions. We have had several meetings with numerous churches
in the last couple of months focusing on emerging partnerships for several
places in Latin America, Asia, Africa and the Arab world. God is
gracious and faithful to gift our denomination with a passion and
commitment for the Great Commission. In that regard, we believe the
November missions conference will enable us to make significant
progress in this area.
In 2001 MTW experienced its greatest numerical growth of long-term
missionaries since 1996. We ended 2001 with a net increase of 32 long
term missionaries for a total of 551. We have been hovering around 500
for many years. We are praying this growth trend will continue in 2002
and beyond, and we are encouraged that many of these new missionaries
are headed toward ministry with Enterprise, our newly created focus on
Muslim ministry. The harvest is plentiful and the laborers are few.
Please pray with us that the Lord of the Harvest would thrust out laborers
to the field.

<a id="ga30-p615"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=617 printed_page=615 -->

...helping to fulfill the Great Commission by advancing reformed and
covenantal church-planting movements...
MTW is committed to advancing church-planting movements as our
primary mission. We uphold the doctrines of sovereign grace and the
truth that God saves His people as described in the Westminster
Standards. The Holy Scriptures are inerrant and authoritative, relating the
reliable history of God’s covenantal relationship with His children. In
regard to that relationship, it is clear that missions is a divine enterprise in
which God commands and graciously accepts the obedience of His
children. Our foundation for missions is the dual truth that God chose in
eternity those who will be saved and that He also uses us as instruments in
the process of saving them.
MTW believes a church-planting movement is a Spirit-directed, vision-
driven, sustained expansion of Christ's Kingdom among a particular
geographic or ethnic people. This is accomplished through indigenously
led churches resulting in ongoing church multiplication and renewal that
significantly impacts all levels of society—spiritually, socially and
culturally. We advance church-planting movements by partnering with an
existing group or denomination that is reformed and covenantal. When
there is no such group, we may establish a new reformed and covenantal
denomination. As the Holy Spirit leads we seek opportunities to impact
others from our Reformed and covenantal perspective. MTW believes
that the Church is God's instrument to complete the missionary task. Our
approach is to advance movements of the gospel through the planting of
self-propagating, self-supporting, and self-governing churches, which live
out a dynamic Reformed and covenantal faith through trained and
empowered leadership. We presently have over 150 national church-
planting partners that we work with in our global church-planting efforts.
By God’s grace MTW has seen significant advancement of church
planting in numerous places throughout the world. Two such locations
are the Philippines and Ukraine. Our Philippine missionaries have seen
the planting of over 50 churches in the last nine years, all by Filipinos.
The vision of the Philippine church-planting team as they went to the field
was "Thrust to the City, Thrust to the Province, Thrust to the World." We
now believe that we have seen the third phase of that vision begin to take
shape as the Filipino church has sent its first fully-supported missionary
to Japan to work alongside MTW's church-planting team in Chiba.
In July 2001 over 300 people experienced a glorious worship service in
Odessa as we joined with the Evangelical Presbyterian Church of Ukraine

<a id="ga30-p616"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=618 printed_page=616 -->

in dedicating the restored sanctuary of the Odessa Reformed Church. We
pray that this wonderfully restored church structure will lend much more
credibility to that ever-expanding indigenously led denomination. We
praise God for this church-planting movement that began in 1993 with the
ministry of one-year missionaries serving with the CoMission.
...through word and deed in strategic areas worldwide.
MTW is committed to seeing the gospel of Jesus Christ not only
proclaimed in all its fullness, but also demonstrated through meeting
physical, emotional, social or cultural needs, in the context of church
planting. We are involved in a variety of deed ministries that greatly
enhance our church planting.
StreetChild Mission International, MTW’s ministry to street children,
finished its first full calendar year in 2001. It was a significant year
because we opened our first two children’s homes in Manila. The two
homes, one for girls and one for boys, are now caring for a total of about
thirty children. If those children were not in these church-based homes,
they would be on the street. Instead, the children are flourishing with a
new hope and a new life. We are presently developing additional
StreetChild ministries in Mexico, Ukraine and Kenya. In Brazil in 2001
we conducted our first-ever comprehensive training for those involved in
this growing ministry around the world. About 20 MTW staff and
missionaries were in attendance.
We have also seen recent growth in our medical ministry. In 2001, MTW
sent out 31 short-term medical teams to serve in 11 countries. Just over
300 individuals ministered with us. As we plan for future medical work,
we are focused on three targeted areas of medical ministry: academic
teams, disaster response teams and AIDS ministry teams. The academic
teams share the gospel with medical professionals while sharing
important medical knowledge. Our disaster response teams are a great
way to support our church-planting teams during times of crisis.
Response teams were deployed seven different times in 2001. While
AIDS ministry is not new to MTW, we have sought to enlarge it and
make it more intentional by establishing it as a separate program. The
first AIDS ministry under this new program will be in Kibera, one of the
largest and most needy slum areas of Nairobi, Kenya. A Kenyan church
planter and project coordinator will be leading this new initiative. We
will also continue to send general medical teams to areas where they are
requested.

<a id="ga30-p617"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=619 printed_page=617 -->

The paragraph at the beginning of this report is the MTW Purpose Statement.
It is more than just words. It is the compass that guides the missions ministry
of the PCA through its agent, Mission to the World. Please pray with us that
God will continue to grant us wisdom, compassion and grace. To God be the
glory.
In 2001, there were 551 career missionaries, 158 two-year missionaries and
154 intern applicants for a total mission force around the world of 863
missionaries. As well, there were 6,540 short-term workers. The breakdown
of the missionaries is as follows:
1. CHURCH PLANTING 344
MTW - Direct 313
Urban 278
Hinterland 27
University Ministries 8
Co-Op 31

2. THEOLOGICAL EDUCATION 44
MTW -D irect 27
Co-Op 17

3. OTHER 150
MTW - Direct 40
Administration 12
Education 20
Medical 7
Nurture / Counseling 1
Co-Op 110
Administration 32
Education 9
Medical 7
Nuture/Counseling 17
Translation Support 39
University Ministries 6

4. LEAVE OF ABSENCE 13
TOTAL LONG-TERM MISSIONARIES 551
5. SHORT-TERM
Two-Year 158
Intern 2-11 Months 154
Two-Week 6540
NATIONAL PARTNERS* 143
* Indigenous church-planting leaders

<a id="ga30-p618"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=620 printed_page=618 -->

PROVISIONAL PRESBYTERIES
Below is the report of MTW’s work with provisional presbyteries outside the
USA for the calendar year 2001. Where we have the information, we have
listed attendees by elder status and name. In some cases many of the attendees
are elders in training attending as observers.
UKRAINE
• May 17,2001
Attendance: TE Eric Huber, TE Clay Quarterman, TE Dave Smith, TE Mel
Pike, plus 16 Ukrainians.
• October 3, 2001

Pike, plus 21 Ukrainians

Adama

CZECH REPUBLIC

Attendance: TE Sid Anderson, TE Dr. Tom Johnson, RE Rene Drapala, and
RE Mirek Stica
HIGHLIGHTS for ENTERPRISE FOR CHRISTIAN-MUSLIM
RELATIONS
Although MTW workers have been serving in Muslim contexts for many
years, it was only last June that Mission to the World created a new emphasis
for spurring our denomination to reach Muslims. This effort is called
Enterprise, which has three overarching purposes.

<a id="ga30-p619"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=621 printed_page=619 -->

First, we want to create partnerships of churches, MTW and the field that will
work closely together to develop and support all aspects of ministry. Last fall,
several partnerships were formed, which are currently at various stages of
development. Those that have met at leasttwice, however, are seeing how
churches of all sizes can deeply connect to a specific field, and do so in
coordinated manner. We are looking to these partnerships to be engaged at
virtually every level of work related to a field.
These partnerships would contribute to the second purpose, seeing the church
mobilized to send laborers—PCA members, missionaries from other countries,
and national believers—throughout the Muslim world. In the first year of our
existence, we have needed to focus on issues of identity,promotion,
networking and security, in order to establish a foundation for the future. We
are very encouraged at the number of people who are interested in serving
Muslims, an interest which has only heightened since September 11th. Indeed,
we are seeing many new candidates applying for service through MTW.
Finally, these workers would contribute toward the third puipose, the
development of national church-planting movements. Towards this ultimate
goal, MTW has one or two teams in most regions of the Muslim world, but we
are admittedly spread very thin. We have had two opportunities for the team
leaders to interact and learn from each other, and are exploring ways to train
more effectively in church planting among Muslims. A few highlights from
these teams:
In Central Asia we are coming alongside national church planters, helping
them fulfill their vision to reach their own and surrounding countries. The
main church continues to grow, and this year plans to establish two new works
in other cities, which will include MTW workers. In another country, we
recently opened up a new field for MTW and are looking to expand as God
raises up people and resources.
Our Arab World teams are reporting slow but steady progress. Potential
believers face tremendous obstacles even to explore a relationship with Christ,
and will likely suffer persecution for any form of public identification. Still,
God continues to work to establish his church. New MTW works are starting
this year, as well as opportunities to support the small but faithful national
church.
Teams are being established in Europe, providing an excellent opportunity to
reach Muslims in a more open context, as well as train workers to serve in
Islamic countries.

<a id="ga30-p620"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=622 printed_page=620 -->

HIGHLIGHTS for ASIA/PACIFIC
The most encouraging activity in the last year has been the completion of the
study stage for the proposed work in China. After two years of hearing from
experts (Chinese church leaders, mission leaders and government experts), we
have completed the strategy and plan. That strategy and plan have been
checked by two trips, one to California to meet with Chinese church and
mission leaders in this country, and the second to China to do the same there.
We found unanimous confirmation of the plan so are now moving to
implementation. By the end of 2002 there should be a good number of
workers either in China or on their way in order to help in the development of
the church.
Plans are underway for the development of a new seminary in Japan. Fifty
years ago a seminary was developed for the Presbyterian Church in Japan. In
the 1970s efforts were made to place that school fully in the hands of the
national church. Since the church was not strong enough to carry it, the school
was merged with the Tokyo Christian Seminary. Today there are few pastors
and church leaders available for recruitment from that school. Therefore, a
new school will be formed that will again enable the church to move ahead.
Our team in Bangkok, Thailand has completed language school and is
beginning the great work of church planting. Initial contacts are being found
through teaching English, and through university ministry. There have already
been converts, which is a tremendous encouragement since it usually takes
many years for Thais to become Christians.
Plans are coming to fruition to develop a significant church-planting
movement through our missionaries in India. Some very effective ministries
have been developed for reaching children. Grace Academy began over 10
years ago to educate the children of national workers and to reach out to the
local community, now has an enrollment of 500 students. Literally thousands
have come to know Christ through the ministries in India. And we have had
Presbyterian Theological Seminary since the 1960s. Now we want to leverage
these ministries into a strong and effective church-planting ministry. Pray for
David Fiol, our country director, and his son Phil who will lead in this new
church-planting endeavor.

HIGHLIGHTS for EUROPE
Our teams in Europe are moving forward, developing various ministries in the
areas of publishing, theological education, leadership development, church
planting, and general evangelism. One of the highlights of the year was the
dedication of the Odessa Presbyterian Church in July. The church stands as an

<a id="ga30-p621"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=623 printed_page=621 -->

encouragement to the faithfulness of God in raising up this congregation of
believers and allowing them to see the restoration of a Reformed church after
so many years of Communism. The newest strategic thrust of the MTW
Ukraine church-planting team is aimed at facilitating a Reformed churchplanting movement in Ukraine and other Russian speaking countries.
Several new ministries continue to gain momentum in Scotland, Germany,
Romania and Sweden. The Confessing Church in Germany continues to
struggle and there has been some discouragement, but it continues moving
ahead during these early days of development. In Scotland we are seeking to
place missionaries in strategic ministries to preserve and expand the work of
confessing churches. The team in Sweden has seen an outpouring of God’s
Spirit on their work in their preparatory phase in moving toward church
planting. Through a sports outreach program, they have many contacts
involved in Bible study. Our first career missionary for Romania has arrived
on the field and is studying the language. Short-term teams have been very
effective in Romania and the work appears to be off to a very good start.
The Europe area, as a whole, is not known as a mature harvest field, and the
response to the gospel is often long and slow. Missionaries who have labored
there for years still struggle to get a healthy, vibrant, reproducing church
established. We are looking at the entire region, trying to determine the best
way to advance God’s kingdom and provide effective support for the workers
in Europe. A Europe redesign team is currently surveying the missionaries in
Europe, asking for feedback on MTW’s ministries, support, and needs
throughout the region.
In addition to the official team sites, we also support the work of numerous
missionaries working strategically in seminaries, church planting, publications,
counseling, church renewal, divorce recovery, leadership development for
street child ministry, evangelism, and in support of national associates in
several countries.

HIGHLIGHTS for SUB-SAHARA AFRICA
In Africa, teaching and training are the key issues. This applies to works that
have been underway for some time, such as Cote d'Ivoire and Senegal, or to
relatively new ministries, such as Nigeria and South Africa. In the longerduration ministries, we are seeking men to be trained as teaching and ruling
elders in order to be equipped to lead the church into the future. We are also
training men in church planting and church leadership.
In newer works, evangelism is more in the forefront, but MTW is also
committed to in-depth training. One of the major problems faced by

<a id="ga30-p622"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=624 printed_page=622 -->

"Christian Africa" is the lack of depth in both teaching and in understanding.
Shallow biblical understanding explains, for example, why two Christian tribes
in Rwanda would attempt to annihilate each other, or pastors would ask, "Are
my Muslim brothers really not saved?"
In Southern Africa we are establishing programs to reach what we hope will
be thousands of pastors who are currently leading churches, but who have little
or no real training. We are looking to create programs that do not require them
to travel to the West, but will keep them in their churches while they are
learning. Few, if any, can afford the cost of leaving home for three years of
schooling, and in any case many could not pass the entrance requirements.
Another area that has marked this past year is an increased focus on peace and
order. With the events of 9/11, we have been reminded of the suddenness with
which terror can strike. It was not necessary to evacuate any MTW personnel
from Africa, but we did put many of them on heightened alert, with some of
them checking in daily by email for a period of time. We required all
personnel to review their contingency plans and upgrade those as necessary.
That has been completed.
Final developments are the new programs in Kenya around street children and
HIV/AIDS. These are initial thrusts into Africa with others to follow. Work
with AIDS is not new to MTW—we have missionaries who have been
involved in that for some time. These programs, however, reflect an increased
emphasis for which we hope to mobilize churches and individuals across the
PCA.
Lastly, and significantly, we need workers. Although Africa has one of the
smallest groups of MTW missionaries, it is one of the largest areas in land
mass and population. While we are working to support nationals and their
leadership, there is a still a great need for workers to teach, train, and
evangelize.

HIGHLIGHTS for LATIN AMERICA/CARIBBEAN
Church planting, in partnership with the National Presbyterian Church of
Mexico (NPC), continues to be the primary ministry model in Mexico. God is
raising up Mexican church planters, calling men to the ministry, raising up
training programs, and infusing a church- planting enthusiasm into all of our
hearts. Many PCA churches are joining in as ministry partners to help with
this church-planting movement. There are new ministry opportunities in
Mexico that we believe will further strengthen the church-planting movement.
One new ministry will be the formation of a new campus ministry team in
Mexico City. One campus in Mexico City has 300,000 commuter students.

<a id="ga30-p623"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=625 printed_page=623 -->

Another ministry will focus more on the poor. A ministry team is forming, in
conjunction with the NPC, to reach out to orphans and street children in the
city of Acapulco. This is a joint effort between the PCA and the NPC of
Mexico. Pray for wisdom as we seek to live out the gospel among the poor.
The work in Central America is growing as God is calling PCA members to
minister in these small countries. New missionaries are being sent to
Honduras and to Nicaragua this year. The opportunities to partner together
in ministry are growing. The needs are great and we pray that the Lord will
call many more to join us as we seek to plant strong reformed and covenantal
church-planting movements in Central America.
We have church-planting teams serving in three countries of South America:
Ecuador, Peru and Chile. These teams are focused on middle-class church
planting, church planting among the very poor, and church planting in the
“Hinterlands” (among tribal and indigenous unreached people groups). These
church-planting efforts involve a multiplicity of ministry opportunities from
college outreach, to street children outreach to medical team outreaches. All
of these build and strengthen these church-planting efforts. Theological
training is a significant value and ministry focus and we would ask you to join
us in praying for God to raise up leaders from these nations who have a heart
for God and for the gospel. Join us in praising the Lord for the men and
women He is raising up within the PCA—people with hearts burdened to take
the gospel to a spectrum of people groups on this continent.
Many of you grieved when a missionary airplane was shot down last year in
the jungles of Peru. The PCA has a missionary pilot flying in the same region,
Craig Gahagen. Craig and Heather have been ministering for 13 years in a
cooperative agreement with South America Mission. With that floatplane
being shot down last year, the Amazon region was left with only one plane.
Craig and the team, with which he works, have had a significant increase in
demand for his services. At this time we are in serious need of another
floatplane. Please join us in praying for the resources to purchase and put into
operation another tool (an airplane in this case) that God can use to get the
gospel into the far reaches of the Amazon jungles.
RECOMMENDATIONS
The Committee on Mission to the World makes the following
recommendations to the Thirtieth General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America:
1. That the month of October 2002, be set aside as a month of prayer for the
“10/40 Window” and world evangelization, and that God would send
many more laborers into His harvest field.

<a id="ga30-p624"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=626 printed_page=624 -->

2. That the General Assembly urge the churches to set aside a portion of their
giving for the suffering peoples of the world; to th a tend be it
recommended that a special offering for relief and mercy (Compassion
Ministry) be taken during 2003 to be distributed by MTW.
3. That the General Assembly set aside Sunday, November 10, 2002, as a day
of prayer and fasting for the persecuted church worldwide.
4. With gratitude to God for the life, ministry, and service of Paul D.
Kooistra, the Committee on Mission to the World enthusiastically
recommends that Dr. Kooistra be re-elected as coordinator of mission to
the World. We strongly commend his performance during 2001-2002
especially in the following areas:
• His forthright demeanor and management have instilled trust between
members of the Atlanta staff, between the Atlanta staff and field
missionaries and between MTW and the churches of the PCA.
• His exemplary spiritual leadership of MTW especially in the
development and teaching of Living in Grace.
• His encouragement and leadership of the organizational redesign
process as the implementation of the results of this redesign resulting
in even greater efficiency and unity throughout the entire MTW
organization.
• His encouragement and leadership in fund-raising, construction, and
move to the new building.
5. That the proposed budget of MTW, as presented through the
Administrative Committee, be approved.
6. That the minutes of the meeting of March 7-9, 2001, be accepted.
7. That the minutes of the meeting of November 7-9, 2001, be accepted.
Respectfully submitted,

Robert V. Massengill, Chairman
Committee on Mission to the World

<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=627 printed_page=625 -->

MINUTES OF THE NOMINATING COMMITTEE
OF THE THIRTIETH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
The Nominating Committee of the General Assembly convened in Atlanta,
Georgia at the Crowne Plaza Hotel on Saturday, March 23, 2002. The meeting
was called to order at 9:00 am byte jack Jagoditsch, Chairman and TE Arch
Warren opened the meeting in prayer.
The Chairman welcomed the Committee and recognized the two guests from the
PCA Office - TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk and Diane Hitzfeld, Office
Manager. Roll Call was taken by circulating a roster. Thirty-one Committee
members were in attendance as follows and nine additional members submitted
initial ballots by mail:
Ascension RE William Mitchell (2003), Secretary
Calvary TE Richard Thomas (2003)
Central Carolina RE Harold Jones (2002)
Central Georgia TE John C. Jagoditsch (2002), Chairman
Chesapeake RE George Anderson (2004)
Eastern Carolina TE Ben Inman (2002)
Evangel TE Tom Cheely (2004)
Gulf Coast TE Bruce Sinclair (2003)
Heritage TE Jim Brown (2003)
Iowa TE Wayne Larson (2004)
James River RE Clyde Vick (2003)
Mississippi Valley TE Earl Adams (2002)
Nashville TE Arch Warren (2003)
New Jersey RE Jack Dever (2004)
New River TE Dave Stewart (2004)
North Florida TE Bill Lamkin (2004)
North Georgia TE George F. Ganey III (2004)
North Texas TE Jeffrey R. Weir (2004)
Northern California TE Lewis A. Ruff, Jr. (2004)
Ohio Valley RE Chuck Hickey (2003)
Pacific TE J Phillip Clark (2002)
Pacific Northwest RE Don Robertson (2002)
Potomac RE Robert Morrison (2004)
Rocky Mountain TE Steve Reese (2003)
South Texas RE Claude Roberts (2002)
Southeast Alabama TE Stephen Estock (2002)
Southeast Louisiana TE Steven C. Leonard (2004)
Southwest Florida RE Henry W hitmore (2003)
Susquehanna Valley RE W. Lee Troup (2003)
Western Carolina RE Stephen Lutz (2002)
Westminster RE Joe Reynolds (2002)

<a id="ga30-p632"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=634 printed_page=632 -->

Chairman Jagoditsch led the committee in a devotional from Matt. 9: 35-38
(and John 4 & 13) followed by a time of prayer. Afterwards, the Chairman
asked TE Lewis Ruff if he would pray for TE Cameron Kirker who was
hindered from attending by his mother’s illness.
M/S that we suspend the rules regarding both fax submissions and late
submissions received by March 1st. During the discussion, it was noted that
there were extraordinary circumstances that warranted the exception. The
question was called for, seconded and carried. Motion Carried.
Chairman Jagoditsch presented to the group a series of matters affecting the
nominating process. it was noted t hate jn us baum was nominated to 2
committees. By common consent, a biographical form received from Ronald
McNally will be placed in the hands of the subcommittee compiling the
Covenant Seminary nominations. Osni Ferreira’s name is withdrawn from the
MNA committee because he is an employee per RAO 4-7. Ridge Haven’s
2003 appointee Norman Reed is subject to approval by the General Assembly.
Subcommittee assignments were made and the main meeting was divided into
subcommittees for deliberation. The subcommittees were instructed to recess
for lunch after concluding their deliberations.
The Nominating Committee reconvened as a committee of the whole at 12:30
PM and the Chairman asked TE Steven Leonard to lead in prayer. Reports of
the subcommittees were received and discussed and the committee developed
a slate of nominees to be presented to the General Assembly.
Nominations were entertained for Chairman and Secretary of the 2003 GA
Nominating Committee. The Committee elected RE William Mitchell from
Ascension Presbytery as Chairman and TE Bill Lamkin from North Florida
Presbytery as Secretary.
The Chairman announced that the 2002 Committee will meet on Wednesday,
June 19, 2002 at the General Assembly at the Call of the Chairman and that the
2003 GA Nominating Committee is scheduled to meet on March 22, 2003.
The Committee discussed proposed changes to the Nominating Committee
process and forms. M/S/C that we appoint the Chairman and Secretary as a
subcommittee to work with the Stated Clerk’s office to develop a draft of the
proposed changes. Proposed changes will be sent to the Committee about one
month prior to GA.

<a id="ga30-p633"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=635 printed_page=633 -->

It was moved and seconded that we adjourn this meeting of the 2002 GA
Nominating Committee. The meeting was adjourned in prayer by TE Jeffrey
Weir at 2:53 PM.
Respectfully Submitted,
/s/ William G. Mitchell, Secretary /s/ John C. Jagoditsch, Chairman

TE William C. Hughes RE Pat Hodge

<a id="ga30-p634"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=636 printed_page=634 -->

D. Biographical Sketches:
RE Joe A. Baker: Rocky Mountain. Has been a member of the PCA since
1979 and recently retired from U.S. Navy. While in U.S. Navy he
served as an active member of several PCA churches, most recently at
Village Seven PC. Currently chairman of the Personnel and Policy
Committee, member of Life and Nominating Committee. Serves as
member of the Administrative Committee and Credentials Committee
of the Rocky Mountain Presbytery. Has top level experience in
military manpower analysis, budgeting, administration, programs and
policies, operational assessment and training development.
TE Wayne Herring: Covenant. Member of the PCA since its inception.
Served on MNA permanent committee, Theological Examining
Committee, Chairman of Presbytery MNA Committee. Served as RTS
professor and administrator of staff of multi-staff church. Personally
planted four churches. Strong supporter of the Administrative
Committee.
RE Pat Hodge: Calvary. Owner of Hodge Frozen Food Center. Has served in
the military, is active in local church as teacher and Trustee and is
involved in the local community. Has served on a number of
Presbytery level committees, including Moderator of Calvary
Presbytery. Has served on Committee of Commissioners for MNA
and is currently an Alternate member of AC.
TE William C. Hughes: Mississippi Valley. Has served for sixteen years as
Executive Minister at church of 3,000, giving evidence of God’s gift
of administration. Served for 20 years as Chairman of Joint
Presbytery Committee for Reformed University Ministries. Has broad
experience on Presbytery and General Assembly Committees
including two terms on the AC, serving as secretary and vice-chairman
during last term.
TE William (Billy) Joseph, III: Warrior. Campus minister since 1980. Has
served on numerous Presbytery committees and as Moderator of
Warrior Presbytery. Has served on numerous Committees of
Commissioners (twice on AC) and GA Nominating Committee.
Currently serving as Alternate on AC.

B. To Be Elected:
Class of 2006
TWO TEs TWO REs
Alternates

C. Nominations:
Class of 2006
TE Robert Dekker RE Bob Beasley
TE Dave Matthews RE Ross Cook
Alternates
TE Terry L. Johnson RE Joseph R. Westerlund
D. Biographical Sketches:
RE Bob Beasley: Western Carolina. Served in U.S. Army. Has taught Bible
studies since 1974 in church, homes, and business offices. Formed a
database publishing firm. Graduate of WTS-Califomia with MA in
Theology. Has had four books published on theological subjects. Has
served as RE in the OPC and currently serving as Clerk of Session at
Trinity PC, Asheville, NC. Serves on Trinity’s Adult Education
Committee and the WNC Presbytery Shepherding Committee.
RE Ross Cook: North Georgia. Ruling Elder at Covenant PC, Fayetteville,
GA. Has served on Presbytery-level CE committees, currently serving
as alternate on g ace & P Permanent Committee. Served 10 years as
Business Administrator for the AC of the PCA, and has served on the
Covenant College Board of Trustees. Served in the military,

<a id="ga30-p636"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=638 printed_page=636 -->

graduated from U.S. Naval Academy; has held VP positions at several
businesses.
TE Robert Dekker: Gulf Coast. Has served on multiple committees dealing
with Christian Education. Has worked with WIC leaders in three
different presbyteries. Has been involved in promoting teacher and
parent training events and is especially interested in developing a spirit
of cooperation among PCA churches, multiplying their resources and
joining together in sponsorship of regional training for church officers
and teachers.
TE Terry L. Johnson: Central Georgia. Senior pastor Independent PC of
Savannah, GA. Member, Board of Trustees WTS-Califomia. Has
previously served on Permanent Committee, CE & P. Founding
Board, Veritas Academy in Savannah. Author (or editor) of several
books on worship.
TE Dave W. Matthews: Evangel. Director of Children’s Ministries Briarwood
PC (6 years). Member of CE Committee in Evangel Presbytery
(chairman for 5 years), member of Sunday School Convention Boards,
in Dallas and Charlotte, 10 years of youth work, 3 years Director of
Christian Education in Dallas, TX. Currently teaching Christian
Education at Birmingham Theological Seminary. Currently finishing
term on ga permanent ce & P Committee.
RE Joseph R.Westerlund: Rocky Mountain. Clerk of Session, Village Seven
PC, Chairman of Presbytery CE & P Committee, served on GA
Committees of Commissioners for CE & P, Covenant College. Plant
Manager for Air Products and Chemicals, Inc. Serves in USAF
Reserve and past City Ministry Director for Family Life Marriage
Conference.

B. To Be Elected:
Class of 2006

Alternates

C. Nominations:
Class of 2006
TE Larry Hoop RE David F. Yates
Alternates
TE Morton H. Smith RE E. J. Nusbaum
D. Biographical Sketches:
TE Larry Hoop: Iowa. Has served as IVCF campus staff member, Director of
CE, Associate Pastor and Senior Pastor. On Presbytery level, has
served as Clerk, Moderator, Vice-moderator, Prosecutor and
Respondent, and on several different committees. For General Assembly, has served three times on RPR Committee, Committee of
Commissioners for B & O (5 times), Committee of Commissioners,
IRC, and for past year has been Alternate for CCB.
RE E.J. Nusbaum: Rocky Mountain. Graduate of USMA, Westpoint. Served
a number of years in the military. Has been a Ruling Elder since 1987,
Clerk of Session for 5 years, taught BCO to officer candidates at
Village Seven PC, serves on Presbytery MNA Committee. Served on
host committee for 25th GA, Committee of Commissioners for AC and
Ridge Haven, and Chairman of Committee of Commissioners for Bills
and Overtures at 29th GA.
TE Morton H. Smith: Western Carolina. Currently serving as Professor of
Systematic Theology at Greenville Presbyterian Theological
Seminary. Served on the Committee to produce the BCO for the PCA.
Served 15 years as Stated Clerk of the General Assembly. Has
authored a Commentary on the BCO of the PCA. Teaching a course
on Ecclesiology and Polity, which deals with the PCA BCO.
RE David Yates: Missouri. Ruling Elder at Covenant PC, St. Louis. He is a
partner in a law firm that has its principal office in St. Louis with a
branch in Washington, DC. His primary practice for 32 years has been
representing management in labor relations, equal employment, and
employee benefit matters. He has a particular interest in the influence
of Reformed Theology on jurisprudence as part of God’s redemptive

<a id="ga30-p638"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=640 printed_page=638 -->

plan. He is currently enrolled in Theological Studies program at
Covenant Seminary. He has served this last year as an Alternate on
the CCB.

<a id="ga30-p639"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=641 printed_page=639 -->

C. Nominations:
Class of 2006
TE Bruce R. Fiol RE Stephen Briggs
TE Larry G. Mininger RE Mark Good
TE George W. Robertson RE Mark W. Harris
RE J. Thomas Holton
D. Biographical Sketches:
RE Stephen Briggs: Metro New York. Dr. Briggs has served as dean and
academic vice president at three different colleges and is currently the
Provost and Vice President for Academic Affairs at The College of
New Jersey. He has a thorough understanding of the internal
operations and academic programs of colleges through his background
in higher education. He has been a Ruling Elder in the PCA since
1989 serving in three different churches. He is Chair of the Board of
the Jonathan Edwards Institute. As a Christian parent, educator and
committed member of the PCA he has a vested interest in the
continued maturation and fidelity of Covenant College.
TE Bruce R. Fiol: Southwest Florida. He is the Senior Pastor at Marco PC,
Marco Island, Florida, the state with the second largest number of
students at Covenant. He has previously served on many committees
of both the General Assembly and his presbytery. He and his wife are
both graduates of Covenant College, as are three of their four children,
so he is well acquainted with the mission and vision of Covenant.
RE Mark Good: Chesapeake. Dr. Good is the father of two children who have
attended Covenant. he is a licensed certified social worker with ama
in religion and counseling from CTS, and a PhD in social work. He
has studied under Francis Schaeffer, has developed and taught courses
for Chesapeake Theological Seminary for 13 years. He has been a
Ruling Elder at Evangelical PC in Annapolis since 1989.
RE Mark W. Harris: Ohio Valley. Currently serving on the Board. He is
Chairman of the College’s Endowment and Investment Committee.
He has served on the Executive, Stewardship, Candidates and
Credentials, MNA, and Ministerial Welfare Committees of Presbytery.
Has been trustee, officer and president of the Richmond public
schools. His professional experience includes newspaper
management, investments and finance. He was one of the original
elders and members of the core group of Christ PC in Richmond, IN.
RE J. Thomas Holton: Evangel. Mr. Holton is Chairman Emeritus of
Sherman International Corp., a concrete and concrete products
company in the Southeast. he is a graduate of Princeton, attended
Harvard Graduate School of Business Administration and is a retired

<a id="ga30-p640"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=642 printed_page=640 -->

Naval Officer. He has been a past Director of the Federal Reserve
Bank of Atlanta. he is a ruling elder at briar wood pc and is
currently serving on the Board of Covenant College as chair of the
campaign to build a new library.
TE Larry G. Mininger: Presbytery of the South, OPC. Senior Pastor at Lake
Sherwood PC (OPC) in Ocoee, Florida. All five of his children have
attended Covenant College, and he actively promotes Covenant both
through his many contacts in the OPC and through having served as
Moderator of the OPC General Assembly in 1999. He has served as
adjunct professor at RTS, Orlando. Covenant’s Bylaws permit a
representative of the OPC. This denomination is not currently
represented on the Board.
TE George W. Robertson: Missouri. Senior pastor at Covenant PC, St. Louis
since 1990. Graduate of Covenant College and Covenant Seminary.
He has served on the Board of Trustees of Covenant College since
1993 and is currently serving as an Instructor at Covenant Theological
Seminary. He is currently serving on the Board of Covenant College
and is Chairman of the Academic Affairs Committee.

C. Nominations:
