D. Biographical Sketches:
TE Michael Campbell: Southern Florida. He has served since 1997 as pastor
of the pine lands pc in Miami, FL, a multi-ethnic church. A graduate
of King College and Covenant Theological Seminary. He is currently
finishing his first term on CTS Board, serving on its Academic
Committee. As an African-American, Rev. Campbell assists the
Seminary as it seeks to serve students from all ethnic groups.
RE Stephen R. Doty: Western Carolina. Mr. Doty is a Ruling Elder at Arden
PC in Arden, NC. He has served in the USAF and as a meteorologist
with the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration. He has
served his church on numerous committees, as Sunday School
Superintendent and most recently as Chairman of the Finance and
Stewardship Committees. He has served General Assembly as a
member of committees of commissioners. His son is a recent CTS
graduate. Mr. Doty is finishing his first term on the CTS Board.
RE Edward S. Harris: Missouri. Currently serving on the Covenant Seminary
Board, Mr. Harris has served on the Board several times. He has
served as a Ruling Elder in churches in N. Texas and Great Lakes
Presbyteries (from which he was first elected to the Board). Presently
he is an elder at covenant pc in St. Louis. He has worked as editor
and publisher of a newspaper and currently serves as President of
Harris Harper Counsel, Inc., a Registered Investment Advisor. He has

<a id="ga30-p642"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=644 printed_page=642 -->

been involved in new church plants in Texas and Indiana. He has
served on Presbytery MNA Committee and Campus Ministry
Subcommittee. He has previously served on the Board of PCA
Foundation. He is also currently serving on the CTS Board’s Finance
and Executive Committees as Chairman of the Investment
Subcommittee.
RE S. Fleetwood Maddox, Jr.: Central Georgia. Dr. Maddox has served as
Deacon and Elder at First PC in Macon, GA since 1965. A graduate of
Mercer University and the Medical College of Georgia, Dr. Maddox is
an ophthalmologist with the Central Georgia Eye Institute in Macon.
He and his wife serve as Board members on the counseling center at
First PC and of the Covenant Adoption Service. He participates each
year in mission trips. He was first elected to the CTS Board in 1993
and served several years as Chairman of the Development Committee.
After a mandatory year off he is eligible for re-election.
RE Ronald McNalley: North Texas. Mr. McNalley is a Ruling Elder at Christ
PC in Flower Mound, TX, where he has served on the Session for 12
years, including 5 years as Clerk. He is the owner and President of
Employee Benefit Resources, Inc. He has served N. Texas Presbytery
as a member of the Candidates and Exams, Ministerial Relations and
Nominating Committees. He has served on the board of Missionary
Health Plans assisting evangelical missionaries. This would be Mr.
McNalley’s first term on the Board of Trustees.
TE Jung Kon Suh: Korean Central. Since graduating from Seminary, Rev.
Suh has been Pastor of the Korean PC of St. Louis. he is a graduate
of Covenant Theological Seminary, Sung Kyun Kwan University in
Korean, and U. of Georgia (MBA). Many of the seminary’s Korean
students receive practical experience in ministry by working in his
congregation. He serves on Presbytery’s Candidates and Credentials
Committee. He would be a key representative of the Seminary to
Koreans within the PCA.

TE Walter Schriver RE Thomas R. Park
D. Biographical Sketches:
RE Thomas R. Park: Gulf Coast. Dr. Park is Vice President of a major
investment firm and a Ruling Elder at Wildwood Church, Tallahassee.
He has experience as a development officer, USMC officer, college
academic, investment officer, entrepreneur and government staffer.
With his broad experience and education he would provide the PCAF
Board with valuable expertise.
TE Walter J. Schriver: N. Georgia. Mr. Schriver is just completing a two-year
term (he filled a vacancy) as a director of the PCAF. His business,
church, and PCA experience provide a good background for service
with the PCAF. In his current ministry with Perimeter Church, he is
familiar with many of the same issues and charitable services as is the
PCAF. He is personally familiar with estate planning issues and has
taught and counseled in the broad spectrum of Christian stewardship.
Prior to full time ministry he had 25 years’ experience in the health
care industry.

Three Members (Teaching or Ruling Elders, or Deacons)
C. Nominations:
Class of 2006
RE Thomas W. Harris, Jr.
RE Ralph Paden
RE Thomas J. Stein
D. Biographical Sketches:
RE Thomas W. Harris, Jr.: Evangel. Currently serving as Chairman of the
IAR Board of Trustees and of their Search Committee. Long time
member and Ruling Elder of Briarwood PC, Birmingham. Recently
retired as Administrator of Briarwood PC. Served as President,
Chairman of the Board and CEO of a major savings and loan
association. he is also serving as Co-Chair of the Local Arrangements
Committee for the 30th General Assembly.
RE Ralph Paden: Tennessee Valley. Mr. Paden is a retired Vice President of
Provident Life Insurance Company and has served the PCA over the
last 12 years, first as Chairman of the Committee of Commissioners of
IAR, Chairman of the Insurance Committee for three years, Chairman
of IAR, and two years as IAR’s representative to the AC. Currently a
member of IAR’s Search Committee. He has served Lookout
Mountain PC as deacon, ruling elder and Vice Moderator of the
Session. On Presbytery level he has served on several committees.
RE Thomas J. Stein: Ohio Valley. Mr. Stein has served for many years as a
Ruling Elder of The Church of the Covenant, Cincinnati as Clerk and
Moderator of Session. He has also served his Presbytery as Moderator
and on several Committees. He has attended most General Assemblies

<a id="ga30-p645"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=647 printed_page=645 -->

since the joining and receiving in 1982. He has broad experience in
investments, life and health insurance, and employee benefits. He is
currently Chairman of IAR's Insurance Committee and also a member
of the Director Search Committee.

A. Present Personnel:

B. To Be Elected:
Class of 2005
ONE TE ONE RE
Alternates
ONE TE ONE RE
C. Nominations:
Class of 2005
TE William S. Barker RE John L. Marshall
Alternates
TE Derek Thomas, alt. RE James D. Walters, Jr.
D. Biographical Sketches:
TE William S. Barker: Philadelphia. Professor Emeritus of Church History,
having taught many years at WTS-Philadelphia. Has served as
Moderator of GA (1994), on 25th Anniversary Committee, Creation
Study Committee and on the Board of Covenant College. Has
contacts with most of the NAPARC denominations from working at
the Presbyterian Journal and WTS; has represented the PCA at NAE.

<a id="ga30-p646"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=648 printed_page=646 -->

RE John L. Marshall: Philadelphia. Ruling Elder at Calvary PC in Willow
Grove, PA and Director of Development for the mid-Atlantic region
and director of alumni relations for WTS. Serves as church bulletin
editor for GCP, editing old and writing new devotional pieces that
appear on the back of church bulletins produced by GCP. Currently
on licensure committee of Philadelphia Presbytery.
TE Derek W.H. Thomas: Mississippi Valley. Originally ordained and served
in the Evangelical Presbyterian Church of Northern Ireland. Served on
the Inter Church Relations Committee for six years of that
denomination. Founding committee member of International Council
of Reformed Churches. Served as Moderator of the EPC of N. Ireland
in 1992. Associate professor of Systematic and Reformed Theology at
RTS. He has served on several Presbytery committees and this past
year has been Chairman of the IRC.
RE James D. Walters, Jr.: Calvary. Ruling Elder in Greenwood PC,
Greenwood, SC, with active teaching ministry. Has been Moderator
of Presbytery and served on several Presbytery committees.
Previously has served a term on GA IRC. Served on IRC Commission
visiting CRC relative to their impending suspension from NAPARC.
Addressed General Synod of the ARP twice as observer from PCA’s
IRC. Professional experience as forester with the SC Forestry
Commission.

B. To Be Elected:

TWO TEs TWO REs
Alternates
ONE TE ONE RE
C. Nominations:
Class of 2006
TE Bruce R. Finn RE John P. Durie
TE Thurman Williams RE Chip Fellers
Alternates
TE Don Ward RE Thomas E. Newton
D. Biographical Sketches:
RE John P. Durie: South Texas. In his capacity as Executive Director of
Ministry at Christ the King PC in Houston, RE Durie is tasked with
developing and executing a church planting vision and strategy. Prior
professional experience (18 years) in commercial banking. Currently
on the MNA Committee of South Texas Presbytery. Since formation
of Christ the King he has served as Team Leader of the Missions and
Evangelism Team, Elder Liaison, and has been involved in developing
and overseeing their overall missions strategy.
RE Chip Fellers: Gulf Coast. Involved in planting Eastern Shore PC in
Fairhope, AL and has been a Ruling Elder since particularization in
1989. Has been Presbytery’s MNA Committee Chairman for last 2/4
years and has served on two mission church commissions. Served in
U.S. Army. Currently Ministry Coordinator and Church Administrator
for his church since 1997. Has served on GA Committee of
Commissioners for CE & P (1998).
TE Bruce R. Finn: Philadelphia. Has served in three different congregations
in three different presbyteries. Founding pastor in 1986 of Bucks
County Church. Church planting is his passion and specialty.
Chairman for 5 years of Church Planting Committee of Presbytery
during which time Presbytery has planted more churches and raised
more funds than at any previous time. Involved in planting multi
ethnic churches in Philadelphia.
RE Thomas E. Newton: Southeast Alabama. He has served as a Ruling Elder
at First PC, Prattville, AL for 16 years. Has been active as a teacher

<a id="ga30-p648"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=650 printed_page=648 -->

for several SS classes. Has served on the Presbytery’s MNA
Committee for 8 years as well as the Examinations Committee and
Special Committee, which resulted in formation of SE AL Presbytery.
Professionally is the Senior Partner with Newton, Oldacre &
McDonald, a regional shopping center development firm.
TE Don Ward: James River. Organizing Pastor and Senior Pastor of Grace
Community Church, Charlottesville, VA. Also served as Organizing
Pastor of South Dayton PC, Dayton, OH. Has served on Committee of
Commissioners for Ridge Haven. Currently on the Mission of
Presbytery Committee for James River and has served for extended
periods on the Church and Ministerial Relations, and Candidates and
Credentials Committees of Great Lakes Presbytery.
TE Thurman Williams: Chesapeake. Pastor of New Song Community Church
in Baltimore, an interracial church. Served on Potomac Presbytery’s
MNA Committee and currently serves on Chesapeake’s Special
Ministries Committee. Committed to increasing the PCA’s awareness
on issues of race, culture and diversity.

Alternates

C. Nominations:
Class of 2006
TE William G. Hay RE L. B. (Pete) Austin, III
TE M. Scot Sherman RE John Vanderveld
Alternates
TE Joe Creech RE Ron Pohl
D. Biographical Sketches:
RE L.B. (Pete) Austin, III: Tennessee Valley. RE austin is a
developer/contractor and president of Austin Building Corp.,
Chattanooga. He is a Ruling Elder at First PC, Chattanooga where he
has served on its World Missions Committee for 15 years and is
currently vice chairman. He has traveled to numerous missions fields
and has led MTW/Impact project trips. In the last 26 years he has
served five terms on the MTW Committee, serving both as chairman
and vice chairman. RE Austin currently serves on the MTW Advisory
Committee. He served as the Moderator of the 11th General Assembly.
TE Joseph L. Creech: Central Florida. TE Creech served with MTW on the
Acapulco churching planting team from 1978-1986. Since then he has
served as Missions Pastor at Orangewood PC in Orlando. He has
traveled and visited missionaries in Latin America, Europe and the
Caribbean. He has organized and led dozens of trips to the mission
field. For many years he served on the MTW Committee of C. Florida
Presbytery. he has a masters of miss io log ya swell as a masters of
Marriage and Family Counseling and has a passion to serve
missionaries.
TE William G. Hay: Evangel. Founder and Senior Pastor of Covenant PC,
Birmingham (1978-present) Has served on MNA Committee of
Presbytery, currently Chairman of MTW Committee for Presbytery.
Has been Trustee of Covenant Theological Seminary and on Advisory
Committee for MTW. Team Pastor and Pastor Associate for the Chile
team for the past six years.
RE Ron Pohl: Missouri. A Ruling Elder and Session member since 1996 at
Kirk of the Hills, St. Louis. Has served his church on numerous
committees, i.e. Missions, Benevolence, Candidate Evaluation,
Outreach Ministry Team, Kingdom-focused Prayer Coordinator,
Sponsor of Young Married’s SS Class, co-teacher for 2 years in Walk
Thru the Bible Adult SS Class. Worked in Human Resources for 12

<a id="ga30-p650"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=652 printed_page=650 -->

years, Plant Operations 18 years, and currently as Business Consultant
with Ernst and Young LLP.
TE M. Scott Sherman: North Georgia. Senior Pastor of Intown Community
Church, Atlanta. Has been involved in church planting in cross-
cultural and urban contexts at Redeemer PC, New York and
organizing pastor for Village Church in Greenwich Village. Hosted
mission teams to NYC for 10 years and at Intown is currently
developing new focus on mission in the Muslim world (including
mercy ministries, Afghan refugees, among Muslims in Atlanta). Has
made numerous missions trips and serves as pastor to a significant
number of leaders and employees of MTW.
RE John Vanderveld: North Texas. Mr. Vanderveld is a Ruling Elder of Park
Cities PC, providing leadership in Evangelism and world and domestic
mission programs. Has served as Chairman of Committee of
Commissioners for MTW at 28th GA. and a member of N. Texas
Presbytery MTW Committee. He was President and CEO of a waste
management company with more than 30 years in environmental
industries with extensive international experience. Has been on the
advisory Board of Southwest Legal Foundation and has served on the
Board of Directors of Living Bibles International and International
Bible Society.

D. Biographical Sketches:
TE John Furman: New River. TE Furman is presently concluding his first
term on Ridge Haven Board. He comes with a wide background and
associations in camp and conference centers. He has done
undergraduate work in camp management. He has previously served
for five years on the Committee of Commissioners for Ridge Haven.
TE Roger A. Newell: Gulf Coast. TE Newell is currently serving as Pastor of
New Philadelphia PC in Quincy, Florida. In his earlier years, he
served several summers as camp counselor at Ridge Haven and has
directed a Jr. High camp there as well. Additionally, he has recently
been a speaker fora Jr. High camp there. He brings knowledge,
understanding and appreciation for the ministry of Ridge Haven.
TE J. Paul Poyner, III: Palmetto. After pastoring churches in Canada,
Mississippi, Alabama, and South Carolina, TE Poyner is currently
Senior Pastor of Eau Claire PC, Columbia, SC. He has served with
MTW staff and for 8 years as missionary in Laos and Indonesia. He
has served on various Presbytery Committees and on the GA
Committee of Commissioners for MTW and Ridge Haven.

D. Biographical Sketches:
RE David Brown: Great Lakes. Ruling Elder at Christ Covenant Reformed
Church, Columbus, OH since 1995. Member of Great Lakes MNA
Subcommittee on Campus Ministries, having also served as Chairman.
The RUM Transition Committee requests that he be elected to serve
on this Committee. Professionally, he is employed by the US Postal
Service.
TE Matthew Brown: Metropolitan New York. TE Brown is Associate Pastor
at Trinity PC, Rye, NY. He is currently serving his fourth year as
Chairman of the Campus Ministry Committee in his Presbytery and
served on the RUM subcommittee of MNA and has served as interim
campus minister at New York University. He has been instrumental in
launching and supporting the work of RUF ministries in the Greater
New York area.
TE Jonathan Inman: Western Carolina. TE Inman has been organizing pastor
of Grace & Peace PCA in Ashville, NC. in his presbytery he is
Chairman of Candidates, Examinations & Education Committee. A

<a id="ga30-p653"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=655 printed_page=653 -->

member of the GA-RUM Committee and previous member of
Committees of Commissioners. Previously has served as Director of
Student Services for Harvest, USA and worked with students from a
number of campuses. Finishing initial one year short term.
TE Steve Malone: Evangel. TE Malone is minister of Red Mountain Church
(mission), Birmingham. Has served for 11 years as Campus Minister
at Delta State and Auburn University. Has coordinated RUF Staff
Training for two years, served as a Campus Minister Representative to
the GA/MNA Sub-Committee for four years, was Campus Minister
Representative on the Search Committee for the RUM Coordinator.
RE John J. Marshall: Central Georgia. Ruling Elder of Trinity PC,
Statesboro, GA for 15 years. Has served on Presbytery MNA, Bills &
Overtures and Judicial Business Committees, Chairman Campus Subcommittee. serve dona number of ga committees of
Commissioners, MNA Sub-Committee on Campus Ministries and
Permanent Committee on RUM for short term.
RE J. David Woodard: Calvary. re woodard has been a faculty member a ta
major university for 20 years, currently holder of the J. Strom Thurmond
Chair in Government at Clemson University in the Department of
Political Science. Has been a PCA Ruling Elder for 16 years.

B. To Be Elected:
Class of 2006
THREE TEs THREE REs
C. Nominations:
Class of 2006
TE Charles E. McGowan RE J. Howard (Howie) Donahoe
TE Erwin Morrison RE Terrill Elniff
TE G. Dewey Roberts RE John B. White, Jr.
D. Biographical Sketches:
RE J. Howard (Howie) Donahoe: Central Carolina. A commercial airline
pilot, Mr. Donahoe has been a Ruling Elder for the last 16 years and is
currently Clerk of Session at Christ Covenant Church, Charlotte, NC
as well as Worship Planning Team member, and adult SS teacher. Has
been past Moderator of Pittsburgh Presbytery and member of several
committees and commissions. On the GA level he has been
Commissioner to 10 GA’s, serving on a number of Committees and
Committees of Commissioners. Currently he is completing his first
term on the GA SJC and during that time has written several Majority
and Concurring Opinions.
RE Terrill I. Elniff: Western Carolina. A Certified Public Accountant, RE
Elniff is a member of the Session of Covenant RPC, Asheville, NC.
He is presently Treasurer for his Presbytery, and has also served on the
Special Judicial Commission, and Candidates and Examinations
Committee of Presbytery. He has served on the GA Theological
Examining Committee and the Committee of Commissioners on Bills
and Overtures, 1996.
TE Charles E. McGowan: Nashville. Senior Pastor of Christ PC, Nashville,
TN. He has served six years on the MNA Committee, including one
year as Chairman. He has been Moderator of Southeast Alabama,
Tennessee Valley, and Nashville Presbyteries as well as Moderator of
the 24th General Assembly. He is completing one term on the SJC
during which he gained valuable experience as a member of several
panels.
TE Erwin Morrison: Philadelphia. TE Morrison has been organizing pastor
and now Senior Pastor of Covenant PC in Hatboro, PA. Has served as
Moderator of Presbytery, member of Presbytery AC, Chairman of
Presbytery MTW Committee, member and now Chairman of
Presbytery Judicial Business Committee, a frequent consultant
concerning judicial and constitutional questions. He comes with a
knowledge of judicial case decisions and a thorough knowledge of the
BCO and Westminster Standards.
TE G. Dewey Roberts: Gulf Coast. TE Roberts is pastor of Cornerstone PC,
Destin, FL. Throughout his years in the PCA he has served in a

<a id="ga30-p655"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=657 printed_page=655 -->

number of capacities at the Presbytery and General Assembly level.
He has been Stated Clerk of Mid-America Presbytery, as well as
numerous committees of Presbytery. He has served two different
terms on the GA SJCs, serving on several panels and chairing two of
them. He has served as Army Reserve Chaplain, being mobilized for
Desert Storm.
RE John B. White, Jr.: North Georgia. RE White is a retired Vice President of
and current Consultant to the Coca-Cola Company. He is former
Clerk of Session of Westminster PC, Atlanta. He has also served as
Moderator of the 17th GA and Assistant Parliamentarian at GA for 10
years. He has been a member of the GA SJC since 1990 serving either
as Vice Chairman or Chairman. Additionally he has served on several
GA Committees and currently serves on the PCA Long Range
Planning Committee. In N. Georgia Presbytery he has been Moderator
on 8 different occasions and has been Presbytery Parliamentarian for
nine years and presently serves in that position.

D. Biographical Sketches:
RE Warren Bennett: Mississippi Valley. Dr. Bennett is currently serving as
Chair of the Computer Science Dept, at Belhaven College, Jackson,
MS and has priorand currentservice with the US Army Corps of
Engineers. Ordained as a Ruling Elder in 1996, he wasactive on the
Session as Chairman of the Worship Committee and Missions
Committee and assisted in development and delivery of officer
training. For many years has served as adult, young adult and youth
class teacher. For four years has served on the Mississippi Valley
Presbytery Credentials Committee as written sermon examiner and
alternate examiner in sacraments and church polity.
TE Michael D. Bolus: Fellowship. TE Bolus has served over the many years
of the PCA’s existence on many Presbtery committees, chairing many
of them. On the General Assembly level he has served a variety of
committees convened during the Assemblies, chairing several of them.
He was one of the original members of the Standing Judicial
Committee, serving for 12 years. As Chairman of the Central Georgia
Presbytery Committee on the Minister and His Work, he supervised
the examination of ministers entering presbytery, developing an
extensive written examination for those standing for ordination.
TE Ligon Duncan: Mississippi Valley. As past Chairman of the Assembly’s
Theological Examining Committee, TE Duncan is currently serving as
Senior Pastor of First PC, Jackson, MS. He has served as Professor of
Systematic Theology at Reformed Theological Seminary, Jackson,
conducting numerous prospective-faculty theological examinations.
Additionally, TE Duncan has served as Moderator of Calvary
Presbytery and Moderator of Mississippi Valley Presbytery. He
served as Vice-Chairman of the GA Creation Study Committee and
member of the Search Committee for Coordinator for RUM. He also
serves as a member of the PCA’s Strategic Planning Committee.
RE Robert Thetford: Southeast Alabama. RE Thetford has been a Ruling
Elder since 1990 serving Trinity PC, Montgomery, AL. In this
capacity he has served on a number of committees, including Christian
Education, Worship and Congregational Care and Shepherding, as
well as directing the youth program. At present, he is a practicing
attorney in Alabama and Georgia with emphasis upon mediation of
personal and business disputes between Christians.

<a id="ga30-p657"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=659 printed_page=657 -->

MINUTES OF THE NOMINATING COMMITTEE
OF THE THIRTIETH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
The Nominating Committee of the General Assembly convened in
Birmingham, Alabama at the Briarwood Church on Wednesday, June 19,
2002. The meeting was called to order at 6:47 pm byte jack Jagoditsch,
Chairman and TE John Nordan opened the meeting in prayer.
The Chairman welcomed the Committee and Cameron Kirker was given the
floor to share a matter of personal privilege. Roll Call was taken by circulating
a roster. Twenty-six Committee members were in attendance:
Ascension RE
Calvary TE
Central Carolina RE
Central Georgia TE
Covenant RE
Eastern Carolina TE
Evangel TE
Gulf Coast TE
Iowa TE
Nashville TE
North Florida TE
North Georgia TE
Northern California TE
Ohio Valley RE
Palmetto TE
Philadelphia RE
Potomac RE
South Texas RE
Southeast Alabama TE
Southeast Louisiana TE
Southern New England TE
Southwest TE
Susquehanna Valley RE
Warrior RE
Western Carolina RE
Westminster RE
TE Rick Downs led in a prayer of intercession on behalf of committee member
Jeff Weir

<a id="ga30-p658"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=660 printed_page=658 -->

M/S that we approve the changes to the Manual of Operations of the
Nominating Committee [see Appendix J, p. 662] and the changes to the
Nominee Biographical Form. Carried unanimously. [See Appendix J, p. 673.]
The committee took a short break for dinner and then discussed each of the
floor nominations. All of the floor nominees were reviewed for eligibility and
the Secretary and the Chairman were directed to prepare the additional
materials for the Assembly.
Erwin Morrison met with the Committee and indicated his intention to
withdraw his name from nomination for the SJC.
M/S/C to adjourn. TE Bruce Sinclair closed in prayer at 7:23 PM.
The following constitutes the report of the Nominating Committee as respects
the floor nominations:
COMMITTEE FOR CHRISTIAN EDUCATION AND PUBLICATIONS
For Class of 2006 Alternate
Nominating Committee Nominee Floor Nominee
TE Terry L. Johnson TE P. Legree Finch
TE Terry L. Johnson: Central Georgia. Senior pastor Independent PC of
Savannah, GA. Member, Board of Trustees WTS-Califomia.
Finishing term as member Permanent Committee, CE & P. Founding
Board, Veritas Academy in Savannah. Author (or editor) of several
books on worship.
TE Legree Finch: North Georgia. He has served pastorates in Illinois, Texas,
and Georgia and previously served on the CE&P Committee. He has
special interest and experience in children’s Christian education.
COMMITTEE ON CONSTITUTIONAL BUSINESS
For Class of 2006
Nominating Committee Nominee Floor Nominee
TE Larry Hoop TE Andrew Webb
TE Larry Hoop: Iowa. Has served as IVCF campus staff member, Director of
CE, Associate Pastor and Senior Pastor. On Presbytery level has
served as Clerk, Moderator, Vice-moderator, Prosecutor and
Respondent, several different committees. For General Assembly has
served three times on RPR Committee, Committee of Commissioners
for B & O (5 times), Com. of Commissioners, IRC and for past year
has been Alternate for CCB.
TE Andrew Webb: Central Carolina. Currently serving as a church planter in
Fayetteville, NC. He has served on numerous committees of
Presbytery and several Committees of Commissioners at General

<a id="ga30-p659"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=661 printed_page=659 -->

Assembly. Prior to his ordination, he worked for several years in
Washington D. C. with the Bureau of National Affairs and the
International Republican Institute.
BOARD OF TRUSTEES FOR THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA FOUNDATION
For Class of 2006
Nominating Committee Nominee Floor Nominee
RE Thomas R Park RE Rick Riehl
RE Thomas R. Park: Gulf Coast. Dr. Park is Vice President of a major
investment firm and a Ruling Elder at Wildwood Church, Tallahassee.
He has experience as a development officer, USMC officer, college
academic, investment officer, entrepreneur and government staffer.
RE Rick Riehl : Chesapeake. Rick is a Ruling Elder of Chapelgate PCA in
Marriottville, MD. He is currently employed by the American Cancer
Society as Vice President of Special Projects and their nationwide
vehicle donation program.
BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF RIDGE HAVEN
For Class of 2003
Nominating Committee Nominee Floor Nominee
TE Roger A Newell RE Glen Norwell
TE Roger A. Newell: Gulf Coast. TE Newell is currently serving as Pastor of
New Philadelphia PC in Quincy, Florida. In his earlier years, he
served several summers as camp counselor at Ridge Haven and has
directed a Jr. High camp there as well. Additionally, he has recently
been a speaker fora Jr. High camp there.
RE Glen Norwell: North Georgia. As Project Manager for Electric
Construction Co., he would be an asset to Ridge Haven during this
time of major development and improvement. He would also add
another Ruling Elder to the Ridge Haven Board.
For Class of 2007
Nominating Committee Nominee Floor Nominee
TE J Paul Poyner III RE Mike Smith
TE J Paul Poyner III RE Lindsey Tippins
TE J Paul Poyner III RE Harrison L (Harry) King
TE John Furman RE Burton (Bud) Bass
TE J. Paul Poyner, III: Palmetto. After pastoring churches in Canada,
Mississippi, Alabama, and South Carolina, TE Poyner is currently
Senior Pastor of Eau Claire PC, Columbia, SC. He has served with
MTW staff and for 8 years as missionary in Laos and Indonesia. He

<a id="ga30-p660"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=662 printed_page=660 -->

has served on various Presbytery Committees and on the GA
Committee of Commissioners for MTW and Ridge Haven.
TE John Furman: New River. TE Furman is presently concluding his first
term on Ridge Haven Board. He comes with a wide background and
associations in camp and conference centers. He has done
undergraduate work in camp management. He has previously served
for five years on the Committee of Commissioners for Ridge Haven.
RE Mike Smith: Grace. Has been a member of Bay Street Presbyterian Church
since 1976. Elected Deacon in 1985 and Elder in 1997. Youth Director
for two years. Led two mission trips to Mexico. Sunday School teacher
for 25 years. Division Manager of Hattiesburg Recreation Dept.
RE Lindsey Tippins: North Georgia. Mr. Tippins has served two previous
terms on the Board and has a vital interest in the ministry of Ridge
Haven. He is a sewer contractor and installed the water system at
Ridge Haven. He recently assisted in the North Carolina mandated
upgrade of the water system.
RE Harrison R. King: Calvary. Involved in development of Ridge Haven from
its earliest days. Harry’s expertise in building construction and
maintenance has benefited Ridge Haven in the past as it would in the
future. His seasoned wisdom as a businessman would be an
encouragement to the Board and to the ministry of Ridge Haven.
RE Burton (Bud) Bass: West Carolina. Mr. Bass is a retired General
Contractor. He built all of the present buildings at Ridge Haven. His
experience should be most helpful in the efficient and economical
completion of the currently budgeted building alterations.
COMMITTEE ON REFORMED UNIVERSITY MINISTRIES
For Alternate 2006
Nominating Committee Nominee Floor Nominee
TE Steve Malone TE Melvin Sensenig
TE Steve Malone: Evangel. TE Malone is minister of Red Mountain Church
(mission), Birmingham. He has served for 11 years as Campus
Minister at Delta State and Auburn University. He has coordinated
RUF Staff Training for two years, served as a Campus Minister
Representative to the GA/MNA Sub-Committee for four years, and
was the Campus Minister Representative on the Search Committee for
the RUM Coordinator.
TE Melvin Sensenig: Southern New England. TE Sensenig previously served
on the MNA committee while RUM was under MNA and worked with
RUM in conjunction with the establishment of ministries in
Cambridge and New Haven. He also served on various Presbytery
level committees including Missions and the Interchurch Relations
Committee of Commissioners. Before joining the PCA, Mel served on
the staff of a local church-based campus ministry and had additional

<a id="ga30-p661"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=663 printed_page=661 -->

experience in ministry geared toward international students. As a part
of his church planting work in Providence, RI he has established
contacts with various campus ministries in order to lay the
groundwork for establishment of a campus ministry there.
STANDING JUDICIAL COMMISSION
For Class of 2006
Nominating Committee Nominee Floor Nominee
Vacant due to Morrison withdrawal TE Terry Gyger
RE Terrill Elniff RE Grant McCabe
RE Terrill I. Elniff: Western Carolina. A Certified Public Accountant, RE
Elniff is a member of the Session of Covenant RPC, Asheville, NC.
He is presently Treasurer for his Presbytery, and has also served on the
Special Judicial Commission, and Candidates and Examinations
Committee of Presbytery. He has served on the GA Theological

and Overtures, 1996.
TE. Terry Gyger: Metro New York. Terry has a long history of service and

Cambridge MA. Terry served as Coordinator of Mission to North
America for the PCA. He is currently on staff of Redeemer
Presbyterian Church in New York where he is in charge of Church
planting ministries.
RE. Grant McCabe: Philadelphia. Grant is completing his 1st term on the
SJC. Grant served 8 years as the Clerk of Session and administrator
for his home church. He has served as Moderator of Philadelphia
Presbytery and has also served on the Judicial Commission of that
Presbytery. grant is a lawyer and is a member of the philadelphia bar
Association as well as the Pennsylvania and American Bar
Association.
For Class of 2003 (vacancy created by RE Mark Belz)

He has served as Clerk of Session, Moderator of the Missouri

MANUAL OF OPERATIONS
NOMINATING COMMITTEE
OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
INTRODUCTION
A. the Book of Church Order (BCO) of the Presbyterian Church in America has established the guiding principles for the formation of
committees within the denomination. These principles appear in
Chapter 14 concerning the structure of the General Assembly, as
follows:
BCO 14-1
7. The Assembly's committees are to serve and not to direct any
Church judicatories. They are not to establish policy, but
rather execute policy established by the General Assembly.
8. The committees serve the Church through the duties assigned
by the General Assembly.
9. The Assembly's committees are to include proportionate
representation of all presbyteries, wherever possible.
10. The committees are to be established on the basis of an equal
number between teaching and ruling elders.
These principles are based upon the PCA's commitment to be a 'grass roots'
denomination, with leadership rising from the bottom up, rather than modeling
a hierarchical, top-down form of leadership. Included within these principles
is the important concept that committees should not be self-perpetuating, but
that the General Assembly should elect its own leadership. This flows from
the clear Biblical teaching of verses such as:
a. Deuteronomy 1:9-13 (NIV) "At that time I said to you, 'You
are too heavy a burden for me to carry alone. The Lord your
God has increased your numbers so that today you are as
many as the stars in the sky. May the Lord, the God of your
fathers, increase you a thousand times and bless you as he has
promised! But how can I bear your problems and your
burdens and your disputes all by myself? Choose some wise,
understanding and respected men from each of your tribes,
and I will set them over you. ’”
b. Acts 6:2-3 (NIV) "So the Twelve gathered all the disciples
together and said, 'It would not be right for us to neglect the

<a id="ga30-p663"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=665 printed_page=663 -->

ministry of the word of god in order to wait on tables.
Brothers, choose seven men from among you who are known
to be full of the Spirit and wisdom. We will turn this
responsibility over to them...
B. In order to assist the General Assembly in determining who might be
the best qualified men to perform the duties required of the various
committees and boards, a Nominating Committee, consisting of
members elected directly by the Presbyteries, has been established.
The duties of this Committee are set forth as follows:
BCO 14-1
11. a nominating committees hall be comprised of one
representative elected by each Presbytery in the following
manner. Each Presbytery shall be assigned to a class by the
stated clerk based on its date of formation. The members shall
serve in classes of three-year terms, alternating between
ruling and teaching elders. When necessary, unexpired terms
shall be filled by an elder of the same class, teaching or
ruling.
This committee is to present all nominations for which it is
responsible to the next meeting of the assembly from a slate of
men nominated by the Presbyteries. Presbyteries shall utilize
the nominating forms provided by the stated clerk for their
nominations. Each presbytery may present one teaching elder
and one ruling elderfor each committee or agency.
In addition to nominees for expired terms, the Committee shall
nominate for each permanent committee one ruling and one
teaching elder as alternates to fill any vacancies that may
occur during the year. Each alternate shoidd attend each
meeting and fill any vacancy necessary to meet a quorum. In
addition to the new nominees from the Presbyteries, alternates
not assuming any vacancies during a year will be
automatically considered by the Nominating Committee as
candidates for nomination to that same committee.
C. Based on these principles and guidelines, and because of the built-in
turnover in the makeup of this group, the Nominating Committee of
the General Assembly has adopted the following Manual of
Operations to assist with the orderly conduct of its business.

<a id="ga30-p664"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=666 printed_page=664 -->

PART I: PURPOSE AND RESPONSIBILITIES
OF THE NOMINATING COMMITTEE (NC)
A. PURPOSE: To glorify Christ by:
1. Helping the General Assembly nominations process to be
orderly (1 Cor 14:40). To that end the NC will regularly:

- **a.** Review and refine the operating procedures in accordance with General Assembly policies.
- **b.** Report to the General Assembly any changes to committee procedures, including any changes to this manual.

2. presenting a report of a list of the most qualified candidates as
nominees for possible election by the General Assembly each
year. In developing this list the NC will consider:

- **a.** The qualifications of each candidate recommended by the Presbyteries.
- **b.** The needs of the individual committees, agencies and commissions
- **c.** A proportionate representation of all Presbyteries, wherever possible (BCO 14-1.9)

B. DUTIES OF THE MEMBERS:
1. Prayerful consideration of the nominations process and the
nominees beginning with receipt of assembled copies of all
nominating forms (known as the 'Rainbow Book') and ending
when elections are completed at each General Assembly.
2. Attendance at ALL meetings of the NC (Including the Spring
Stated Meeting and the General Assembly Stated Meeting).
Members will bring their 'Rainbow Books' to both meetings.
3. Participation in the process, including:

<a id="ga30-p665"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=667 printed_page=665 -->

a. Prompt submission of preliminary votes prior to the
Spring Stated Meeting (see II.A.5, pg. B-36), if unable
to attend.

- **b.** Participation in discussion at both sub-committee and full NC meetings.
- **c.** Encouraging and instructing their sending Presbyteries to be faithful in developing and recruiting leaders from among their members and in presenting names for possible nomination to the NC in a timely and proper manner.
- **d.** Serving as liaison between the NC and the

Presbyteries.
C. DUTIES OF OFFICERS: There shall be two officers of the NC:
1. Chairman. His duties shall be:
a. Prepare an agenda for and conduct the meetings of the
NC.

- **b.** Work with the Stated Clerk to accomplish necessary administrative activities.
- **c.** Appoint all sub-committee Chairmen and ad-hoc committee members, when needed.
- **d.** Communicate to the General Assembly on behalf of the committee.

2. Secretary. His duties shall be:

- **a.** To keep an accurate record of minutes of all NC meetings.
- **b.** Subject to approval by the Chairman, prepare brief biographical summaries for both committee nominees and floor nominees for the committee's report to

General Assembly.

<a id="ga30-p666"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=668 printed_page=666 -->

- **c.** In the event the Chairman is unable to serve at any given meeting, to act as a convener and conduct the election of a Chairman pro-tem.
- **d.** If the Secretary is unable to serve, the Committee will elect a replacement as needed.

D. DUTIES OF THE OFFICE OF STATED CLERK:
1. Provide secretarial and record keeping for the NC.
2. Handle the planning and payment of travel, housing, and
meeting facility needs for the Spring Stated Meeting. It is
understood that expenses to attend the General Assembly
Stated Meeting are to be borne by the sending
church/agency/presbytery of the NC member.
3. Refer all procedural or policy questions about NC procedures
not covered by this manual to the Chairman for appropriate
action.
4. Provide all materials needed by the Presbyteries to meet the
requirements of the NC annually through the Handbook For
Presbytery Clerks.
5. Prepare a tabulation form, in consultation with the officers of
the NC, on which to tally the preliminary votes of the
members of the NC. (See II.B.2, pg. B-38)
6. Advise the Presbyteries that current Alternates on Boards and
Committees that desire to will be considered automatically by
the Nominating Committee as candidates to that same
Committee (per BCO 14-1. 11) and that the original
biographical form will be applied. An alternate must inform
the Stated Clerk of his Presbytery and concurrently the GA
Stated Clerk if he does not desire to be considered as a
nominee in fef the coming year, should forward their
Biographical Form and letter of recommendation directly to
the Office of the PCA Stated Clerk, four months prior to the
General Assembly.

<a id="ga30-p669"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=669 printed_page=null -->

7. Assemble the ‘Rainbow Book’ to include:

- **a.** Agenda for Spring Stated Meeting
- **b.** Extracts from BCO, RAO, and Bylaws relative to the

NC

- **c.** List of ineligible members for nominations
- **d.** Distribution of committee/boards by presbytery representation
- **e.** Spreadsheet of committee/boards by presbytery representation class
- **f.** List showing current NC members by class and presbytery, and class needed to elect
- **g.** Qualifications, responsibilities and/or job descriptions provided by each committee and board
- **h.** Presbytery Nominee Biographical Form and letter of recommendation (on one page, one side for each)

from nominees approved by each presbytery and
eligible alternates’ forms

- **i.** Communications from all committees and agencies authorized to communicate directly with the NC
- **j.** The Nomination Committee Manual of Operations

8. Immediately following the spring meeting of the NC, notify
the men who have been nominated by the committee.
PART II: THE NOMINATING PROCESS
Timetable for all events in the nominating process are set in order to
meet deadlines for mailing of the Commissioners Handbooks prior to
each General Assembly and will be as follows (see attached chart):
1. August 31 st PCA Stated Clerk sends Nominations
Package to the Presbytery Stated
Clerks and members of the NC
2. Fall Presbytery meetings Presbyteries are strongly encouraged
to choose nominees at a meeting in
the fall of the year.
3. 4 Months prior to GA a. Presbytery Nominees Biographical
Forms must be received by the PCA
Stated Clerk's office. No forms will
be accepted after this date (see RAO
7-4.e).

<a id="ga30-p668"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=670 printed_page=668 -->

- **b.** Names of members of the NC submitted by Presbytery Stated Clerks
- **c.** Deadline for receipt by the chairman of then c of communications from all committees and agencies authorized to communicate directly with the NC.

These communications will be
included in the Rainbow Books for
consideration by all members of the
NC in their preliminary voting.
4. 1st Business Day of March Office of the PCA Stated Clerk mails
Rainbow Books to all members of the
NC.
5. 3rd Saturday of March Preliminary vote forms from
5 pm EST Friday prior members of the NC who are unable to
to Spring Stated Meeting attend the Spring Stated Meeting due
of the NC at Office of PCA Stated Clerk.
6. 4th Saturday of March Spring Stated Meeting of the NC
7. 2nd Saturday of April NC final report, including
biographical summaries, due at Office
of PCA Stated Clerk
8. Mid-May (approximately) GA Commissioner's Handbooks mailed
9. Mid-June (approximately) Meeting of the General Assembly
10. After Close of Floor General Assembly Stated Meeting of
NC
B. Forms to be used in the Nominating Process
1. Presbytery Nominee Biographical Form for General Assembly.
This form will consist of two sides. The first side will include
all instructions concerning submission of the form and a place
for the Presbytery Stated Clerk or a designated representative
of Presbytery to sign, authenticating that the individual named

<a id="ga30-p669"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=671 printed_page=669 -->

in the form was approved by vote of his Presbytery and the
date that action occurred. This page may be hand printed. It
is then given to the nominee. When submitted to the Office of
the PCA Stated Clerk, it will remain in their files and be used
if the nominee is ultimately elected to a Board, Committee or
Commission.
The reverse side will include at the top an area for the
identification of the nominee. The remainder of the page is to
be used by the nominee to include any information he would
like the NC to consider in his behalf, including (but not
limited to) past/present service on Presbytery and GA
committees; education; vocational experience; and other
pertinent information. a copy of this page will become apart
of the 'Rainbow Book' that is sent to every member of the NC.
This form will be designed and periodically revised by the NC
after consulting with the Office of the PCA Stated Clerk. The
following requirements for this second page will be stressed in
the instructions:

- **a.** On tThis page Section D MUST be typed or computer printed, with a minimum of one-inch margins and 10 point type size. Hand written/printed copies will not be accepted.
- **b.** This page is to be just that - ONE PAGE. If more than one page is submitted to the Office of the Stated

Clerk, only the first page of that submission will be
included in the 'Rainbow Book'.
Each nominee is encouraged to obtain a letter of
recommendation from any elder (Teaching or Ruling) in his
own Presbytery. This recommendation is limited to one page
and must be either typed or computer printed. It must be
submitted to the Office of the PCA Stated Clerk along with the
nomination form. When the members of the NC receive the
'Rainbow Books', the letter of recommendation will be copied
on the back of the Nominee Biographical Form.
2. The following instructions apply to the entire submission of
the forms listed above:

<a id="ga30-p670"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=672 printed_page=670 -->

a. The nominee is responsible to mail or skipfax his
submission to the Office of the PCA Stated Clerk.
Emails are not accepted.
&-------Only the ORIGINALS of these pages will be accepted
no faxes. (Since the Office of the PCA Stated Clerk
must prepare more than 50 copies of each submission,
originals are necessary).
erb. The Biographical Form and letter of recommendation
must be submitted together in the same envelope. The
Office of the PCA Stated Clerk will not be responsible
for forms which arrive separately.
drC. The forms must arrive in the Office-of the PCA Stated
Q erk-be post-marked or dated by the fax machine by
the deadline date printed on the cover pagein Section

### B. LATE SUBMISSIONS WILL NOT BE

CONSIDERED BY THE NC.
3r3. NC Preliminary Vote Forms. A form will be designed each
year by the Office of the PCA Stated Clerk, in consultation
with the officers of the NC, on which to tally the preliminary
votes of the members of the NC. This preliminary vote tally
will show only raw numbers of votes per place (e.g. 1st, 2nd,
3rd, etc.).

PART III: SPRING STATED MEETING OF THE NC
A. Time and place: This meeting will normally be held on the 4th
Saturday of March (with adjustments made so as not to conflict with
Easter weekend). Any change in date will be determined by vote at
the previous Spring Stated meeting. The meeting will normally be
held from 10:00 a.m. to 3:00 p.m. at a hotel/conference center near the
Atlanta Airport, thus allowing for same-day fly in and fly out by many
members.
B. Items of business will include:
1. Making recommendations for nominees for each of the GA
Boards, Committees, and Commissions.

<a id="ga30-p671"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=673 printed_page=671 -->

2. Making recommendations on any other business referred to
the NC from the General Assembly, such as Overtures.
3. Making recommendations for changes to the Manual of
Operations, when needed.
4. At the close of the meeting, electing a Chairman and Secretary
for the following year (seeking to rotate the offices between
Teaching Elders and Ruling Elders whenever possible).
VI: GENERAL ASSEMBLY STATED MEETING
A. Time and Place: This meeting will be held after the close of floor
nominations at the General Assembly each year, at a time and meeting
room to be announced by the Chairman. members of then c in
attendance at the General Assembly are responsible to watch the
announcements for the call to the meeting.
B. Items of Business will include:
1. Respond to any new vacancies not identified at the time of the
Spring Stated Meeting. NC nominees for these vacancies will
be made from names which had been submitted by the
Presbyteries for the Spring Stated Meeting. (It is important
that all members of the NC bring their 'Rainbow Books' to the
General Assembly and to the meeting for this purpose.)
2. Review all floor nominations for eligibility and approve them
for submission to the General Assembly.
3. Making recommendations on any other business referred to
the NC from the General Assembly up to this time.
4. The Chairman and Secretary, with support from the Office of
the Stated Clerk, will prepare the supplemental report to the
General Assembly.

PART V: AMENDMENTS TO THE MANUAL
This manual may be amended by a majority vote of those attending any meeting
of the NC. The Amendments must be submitted in writing in advance of the
vote, giving members of the NC at least two weeks to consider possible changes.
The NC will report to the General Assembly any changes to this manual.

<a id="ga30-p672"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=674 printed_page=672 -->

Timetable for Nominating Process

Rev. 06/2002

| Sep | Oct | Nov | Dec | Jan | Feb | Mar | Apr | May | Jun |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| August 31 - PCA Stated Clerk sends Nominations Package to Presbytery Stated Clerks & Nominating Committee. | | | | | | | | | |
| Fall Presbytery Meetings Presbyteries encouraged to select their nominees. | | | | | | | | | |
| 4 months prior to GA Nomination Forms due in Stated Clerk's Office. Deadline for Committees and Agencies to suggest nominees. | | | | | | | | | |
| Last Friday in February Rainbow Books mailed to Nominating Committee. | | | | | | | | | |
| 5 pm EST of the Friday prior to the Spring Stated Meeting of the Nominating Committee Preliminary Vote Forms due to Stated Clerk from Nominating Committee members unable to attend. | | | | | | | | | |

PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
FOUNDATION, INC.
REPORT TO THE 30™ GENERAL ASSEMBLY
The PCA Foundation is pleased to report that, despite the economic
uncertainties and the difficulties in the equity markets, the PCA Foundation’s
ministry during 2001 resulted in record grant distributions to Christian
ministries. The Lord continues to bless the work of the Foundation, now in its
third year on a self-supporting basis.
These distributions, or grants to ministry, reached an all time high during 2001
— $6.5 million. This compares to $6.1 million in 2000, an increase of $0.4
million or 7%. The overall increase in distributions was due to a significant
increase in distributions to PCA churches: $4.7 million in 2001 compared to
$3.8 million in 2000 — an increase of $0.9 million or 24%. Distributions to
PCA Committees and Agencies declined to $0.7 million in 2001 from $1.0
million in 2000, while distributions to non-PCA ministries decreased to $1.1
million in 2001 from $1.3 million in 2000.
As we have the opportunity to work with more PCA churches and their
members, distributions to PCA churches increase.
The 2001 distributions (grants) to ministry by the Foundation were as follows:
Mission to the World $159,000
Mission to North America 58,000
Christian Education and Publications 7,000
Administrative Committee 23,000
IAR-Ministerial Relief 17,000
Reformed University Ministries 30,000
Covenant College 60,000
Covenant Seminary 164,000
PCA Foundation 219,000
Ridge Haven 2,000
Total Committees & Agencies 739,000
PCA Churches 4,692,000
Other Christian Ministries 1.070.000
Total $6,501,000
It has been exciting to see the Lord use the Foundation during 2001 in
Kingdom ministry, and continue to use it.

<a id="ga30-p676"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=678 printed_page=676 -->

Total assets as of December 31, 2001 were $24.2 million, compared with
$25.0 million as of December 31, 2000. it is important to note that, while
growth in total assets is useful, it does not tell the whole story about what the
Foundation does and accomplishes in its ministry. Much of what we do results
in gifts coming into the Foundation and going right back out as distributions to
ministries within a relatively short period of time — often within the same
year. Therefore, the Foundation may realize significant amounts as both gifts
and distributions in a given year, yet it may not experience a substantial
change in total assets. However, we are pleased that, considering the
economic circumstances and declines in the equity markets during 2001, total
assets were not significantly impacted.
Total gifts to the Foundation during 2001 were $6.8 million. This compares to
$9.2 million in 2000. The economy and equity market difficulties during 2001
resulted in fewer gifts to the Foundation than may have ordinarily been
received. However, the $6.8 million was higher than gifts received in any
other previous year, with the exception of 2000.
During 2001, we continued to market the Designated Funds for churches,
presbyteries, and other ministries. This service has been well received, and we
believe that it will be used by more churches, presbyteries, and ministries as
the value and benefits of this service become known to them. by setting up a
Designated Fund with the Foundation, a church, presbytery or ministry
specifies the intended use of the Fund and controls distributions from it. The
Foundation invests and administrates the Fund, and can process various types
of gifts, including appreciated assets.
The Foundation plans to continue intentional marketing and servicing
individuals and families, churches, presbyteries and ministries, as well as
provide services to PCA Committees and Agencies whenever possible. During
2001, the Foundation developed a new look, slogan and marketing materials
and they have positively impacted our marketing efforts.
Because the main focus of the Foundation is not on raising funds for its own
operations, it has a unique opportunity and niche. Our ministry is providing
services and financial vehicles to help Christians carry out their stewardship
responsibilities, as well as offering services and financial vehicles to churches,
presbyteries and other ministries. The Foundation is “donor driven,” which
means that we work on the donor’s agenda, not our own. We provide
charitable services to individuals without pressuring them to give to the PCA
Foundation, or any other ministry, for operating support. The result is that
more funding is available for Kingdom building.

<a id="ga30-p677"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=679 printed_page=677 -->

The Foundation will continue to strive to effectively meet the needs of its
present and future donors, as well as the PCA: its churches, presbyteries,
Committees, and Agencies.
By God’s grace, the result of our work will be a continued increase in the gifts
to the Foundation, distributions to ministry from the Foundation, and growth
of the Foundation’s total assets.
We ask that you continue to pray for the Board and Staff of the Foundation as
they seek to continue leading the Foundation successfully into the future.
The PCA Foundation’s Recommendations are:
1. That the financial audit for the PCA Foundation, Inc. for the calendar
year ended December 31, 2001 by Capin Crouse, LLP be received.
2. That the proposed 2003 Budget of the PCA Foundation, Inc. be
approved.
3. That the Minutes of Board meetings of August 10, 2001 and March 8,
2002 be approved.
Respectfully Submitted,
/s/ Randel N. Stair
President

REPORT OF THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE
INSURANCE, ANNUITY AND RELIEF FUNDS
TO THE THIRTIETH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
Statement of Purpose
the purpose of the board of trustees of Insurance, Annuity and Relief
Funds of the Presbyterian Church in America is to glorify God
through serving His servants who minister in the Church by offering
financial products to provide for the health and welfare, retirement
and relief needs of the denomination’s ministers, missionaries, lay
church workers and their families, and to serve the churches,
committees and agencies of the PCA and related organizations by
sponsoring and endorsing a denominational property and liability
insurance program.
Summary
The events of 2001 will linger with us for years to come. 2001, what a year!
The recession, coupled with the second successive year of stock market
reversals, would have been sufficient to test all but the super optimistic. Then
came the catastrophe of September 11, a day that we will never forget.
In an attempt to revive the economy, the Federal Reserve Board made one
short-term interest rate cut after another. By year-end early signs of the hoped
for recovery seemed on the horizon but were still a bit blurred.
How did all the PCA retirement and insurance plans fare during this turbulent
period? The number of retirement plan participants continued to increase.
Equity returns reflected the stock market volatility. Bonds and other fixed
income instruments did better. The insurance plans either held their own
(Health and Optional Life) or grew (Term Life and Long Term Disability).
Record giving to the Ministerial Relief Fund was recorded through the 2000
Christmas Offering. Activity through the Relief ministry grew as well. More
churches were added to the PCA-endorsed GuideOne property and liability
insurance program. All things considered, it was not a bad year for your Board
of Trustees and Staff.

<a id="ga30-p680"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=682 printed_page=680 -->

Total assets under management declined slightly, reflecting the weak stock
market. Most benchmark indexes, including the Dow Jones Industrials, the
S&P 500 and the Nasdaq, produced negative returns for the year. At year-end,
assets stood at $175.7 million, compared with $176.8 million for 2000, a
decrease of 0.6%.
Following are some of the highlights of 2001.
• Moved to the new PCA office building in late August. Our Staff is quite
pleased with the new facilities.
• Changed insurance administrators from The Grotenhuis Group to The
Commerce Group April 1.
• Added an eleventh investment option for retirement plan participants.
• Changed three retirement plan investment option category names to better
describe the underlying mutual fund objectives.
Please refer to Attachment One [Appendix L, p. 687] - PROGRAM
SUMMARY - for comparative statistical information on the PCA benefit
plans for the past three years.
Retirement Plans
New enrollments in the PCA Tax Sheltered Annuity Plan were 330. Total
enrollment, including retirees on benefit, surviving spouses and those with
frozen accounts, at year-end stood at 4,662. Retirement plan assets were
$170.0 million, compared to $171.3 million for 2000, a 0.8% decrease. Net
returns for the retirement funds are shown in the chart below. During 2001 we
changed the category names for three investment options as follows:
Aggressive Growth became Mid Cap Growth; Growth became Large Cap
Growth; Growth and Income became Value. The underlying mutual fund in
the Mid Cap Growth category was changed from AIM Constellation A Fund to
Artisan Mid Cap Fund. We added an eleventh investment option category—
Small Cap—in December. The underlying mutual fund for this option is
Fidelity Advisor Value Strategies T Fund. The 2001 return for this fund listed
below is for the entire year, to make it consistent with the other funds.

<a id="ga30-p681"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=683 printed_page=681 -->

2000 2001
PCA Equity Fund 2.2% -7.4%
PCA Balanced Fund -2.1% -3.2%
PCA Income Fund 8.2% 7.0%
Small Cap Fund n/a 12.1%
Mid Cap Fund -10.3% -21.6%
Large Cap Growth Fund -20.3% -14.7%
Value Fund 13.6% 6.0%
Stock Index Fund -9.2% -12.2%
Foreign Fund -3.7% -7.7%
Bond Fund 8.9% 11.9%
Money Market 6.0% 3.8%
It is significant that, in a year in which the Dow Jones (-5.4%), S&P 500 (-
11.9%) and Nasdaq (-21.1%%) were all negative, two of our equity funds
(Small Cap and Value) produced positive results. Overall, five of the eleven
funds were up for the year. Bonds generally out-performed equities, as
reflected in the returns of the PCA Income Fund and the Bond Fund versus all
the equity funds, except the Small Cap and Value Funds.
Insurance Plans
Health Plans
The health plans basically held their own for the year—beginning the year
with total enrollments of 805 and ending the year with 804. The Pacific Life
plan had unfavorable claims experience, resulting in an overall premium
increase of 26.5% for 2002, compared to a 4.8% increase for 2001. Benefit
changes also increased individual and family annual medical deductibles and
co-pays for prescription drugs. For calendar year 2001 we placed a cap of
$1,000 for the 30% coinsurance for brand name drugs. The insurance carrier
would not fund this cap, so we did it with reserves in our health insurance
fund. We provide annual open enrollment for our health plans, in December
and July, respectively.
The significant 2002 premium increase for the Pacific Life health plan is a
concern for the Trustees. We fear that there may be an unmanageable
reduction in participation. In January several churches left the plan. The Staff
and Board of Trustees are monitoring the situation closely. If participation
drops more than 10%, the insurer has the option to re-rate the premiums. If
that happens, the plan may be forced to close down, probably leaving some
participants unable to find alternative coverage. Our conclusion is that unless
the General Assembly makes the hard decision to mandate coverage in the
PCA health plan, the demise of the plan may be imminent.

<a id="ga30-p682"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=684 printed_page=682 -->

Long-Term Disability Plan
Standard Insurance of Oregon became our LTD carrier January 1, 2000, under
a contract, which, in most respects, duplicated the benefits under our prior
contract with UNUM. Enrollment in the plan has continued to increase.
Based on good experience we were able to reduce premiums for 2001 from
.75% to .65% of compensation for those covered under Social Security and
from 1.00% to .90% for those not under Social Security. The premiums
remain unchanged for 2002.
Life Insurance Plans
Continued good claims experience and other favorable factors enabled the
Trustees to maintain the PCA Term Life Insurance Plan premium at $20 per
month in 2002. This plan has a single premium for all participants, but
reduces the amount of coverage in five-year age brackets. By contrast, the
Optional Life Insurance Plan provides for participant flexibility as to amount
of coverage—from $20,000 to $250,000 in $10,000 increments—with agebased premiums in five-year brackets. It also provides for spouse and
dependent children coverages, at the option of the participant.
Ministerial Relief
During 2001, 12 ministers, 1 missionary’s wife, 1 minister’s widow, and 6 lay
employees died. Mrs. Ann Llewelyn continues to minister to a growing
number of ministry widows by telephone counseling, letters and personal
visits. In addition we supply regular monthly financial assistance to certain
needy retired and disabled ministers, missionaries and lay church employees
and their surviving widows and dependent children. The Relief Fund also
provides periodic payments on a case-by-case basis to help pay medical and
dental bills for eligible recipients. We have also paid health insurance
premiums for limited time periods for some PCA ministers between calls and
for some disabled ministers and lay workers, subject to annual review by the
Trustees.
The annual Christmas Offering provides primary funding for relief activities.
Through March 15, 2002, the offering for Christmas 2001 was over $359,000.
Many thanks to the churches and individuals who gave.
Incorporation and Name Change

<a id="ga30-p683"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=685 printed_page=683 -->

For several years we have considered incorporating our agency, as permitted
by the PCA Rules of Assembly Operations (RAO 4-3.), as a means of limiting
liability to the denomination in case of any possible future legal actions
brought against the agency. While we know of no such action and do not
anticipate any, we believe it prudent to take this step at the present time.
Accordingly, the Board of Trustees incorporated under the name of PCA
Retirement and Benefits, Inc. earlier this year. Recommendations 7-11 below
relate to the incorporation and name change.
Legislative Changes
Social Security
The United States Congress passed legislation allowing ministers who had
previously opted out of Social Security to opt back into the system during
2000 or 2001. That window closed on April 15, 2002, the deadline for filing
2001 taxes.
EGTRRA
The Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act (EGTRRA) of 2001
included a number of provisions which will benefit ministers, missionaries and
church lay employees who participate in the PCA Retirement Plan during 2002
and beyond. They include the following:
• Repealed the Exclusion Allowance (which will reduce confusion and
administrative hassle related to calculating maximum contributions)
• Increased contribution limits for 2002 (with scheduled annual increases
thereafter)

- **a.** Total annual employer and employee contributions: lesser of 100% of salary or $40,000
- **b.** Annual salary reduction (employee) maximum contribution: $11,000
- **c.** Annual catch-up salary reduction contributions for those age 50 and older (in addition to b. above): $1,000

• Low income saver tax credit for first $2,000 employee contribution to
403(b) plans or IRAs.
Credit Individual AGI Joint AGI
50% $ 0-$15,000 $0 - $30,000
20% $15,001 - $16,250 $30,001 - $32,500
10% $16,251 - $25,000 $32,501 - $50,000

<a id="ga30-p684"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=686 printed_page=684 -->

Staff
Following are our current staff members:
James L. Hughes, Director
Terry Aiello, Accountant
Robert Bishop, Field Representative
Thomas Bryant, Business Manager
Michael Hall, Field Representative
Brenda Hoyt, Secretary/Receptionist
Sally Kaplan, Administrative Assistant
Ann Llewelyn, Relief Representative
Ryan Todt, Service Associate
Steve Wykoff, Service Associate
A Personal Word from the Director
It has been my privilege to serve as your Director since November 1, 1981. It
is my intention to retire from this position December 31, 2002. I would like to
express my sincere thanks and appreciation to all the fine Trustees who have
served on the Board during these 21 years, to all the wonderful Staff members
who have worked alongside me, to the Coordinators, Directors and Presidents
of the other PCA Committees, Agencies and Institutions with whom I have
served, to the ministers, missionaries, and lay employees of the churches,
schools, and other PCA related organizations and to the retirees and ministry
widows whom we serve. It has been a joy and, at times, a challenge, but God
has blessed our joint efforts to provide quality employee benefit and
ministerial relief programs for the PCA. I will miss all of you.

<a id="ga30-p685"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=687 printed_page=685 -->

RECOMMENDATIONS:
1. That the Minutes of Board meetings of August 10, 2001, November 2,
2001 and March 1, 2002 be approved.
2. That the audit report dated December 31, 2001 by Capin Crouse LLP
be received.
3. That the General Assembly approve the use of Capin Crouse LLP, to
conduct the 2002 audit.
4. That the 2003 budget be received with the understanding that it is a
spending plan and will be adjusted as necessary by the Board of
Trustees to accommodate changing circumstances during the year.
5. That the General Assembly urge our member churches to participate in
the annual Christmas Offering or budget regular benevolence giving to
support relief activities through the Ministerial Relief Fund.
6. That the General Assembly approve the Fourteenth Amendment and
Proposed Termination of the Presbyterian Church in America Money
Purchase Pension Plan, the Twenty-first Amendment and Approval of
Rollover Contributions to the Presbyterian Church in America Tax-
Sheltered Annuity Plan, and the Twenty-second Amendment to the
Presbyterian Church in America Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan (see
Attachment Two [Appendix L, p. 688]), and that the resolutions
related to these amendments be adopted and included in the Minutes of
the General Assembly.
7. That the General Assembly be informed that during early 2002 the
Insurance, Annuities and Relief agency incorporated under the
direction of the Board of Trustees, as permitted in RAO 4-3, and
changed its name from “Insurance, Annuities and Relief’ to “PCA
Retirement and Benefits, Inc.”; and that the General Assembly
expressly authorize and ratify such incorporation (see Attachment
Three [Appendix L, p. 700]).
8. That the General Assembly amend the Book of Church Order 14-
1.12.b.4, to recognize the change in name for our agency by replacing
the words “Insurance, Annuities and Relief’ with “PCA Retirement
and Benefits, Inc.”
9. That the General Assembly approve the use of the name PCA
Retirement and Benefits, Inc. (PCA-RBI) immediately after the
adjournment of the 2002 General Assembly, while BCO name change
approval by the Presbyteries and 2003 General Assembly is pending.
10. That the General Assembly amend the Rules of Assembly Operations
4-3. and 5-1(4) by replacing the words “Insurance, Annuities and
Relief’ and “Insurance & Annuities” in 13-1.6. with the words “PCA

<a id="ga30-p686"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=688 printed_page=686 -->

Retirement and Benefits, Inc.” and changing the words “Touching
insurance & annuities” to “Touching retirement, insurance and
ministerial relief.”
11. That the General Assembly amend the PCA Corporate Bylaws Article
VI. 1., paragraph 3, line 2 by replacing the words “Insurance, Annuities
and Relief’ with the words “PCA Retirement and Benefits, Inc.”
12. That the General Assembly amend the PCA Corporate Bylaws Article
VI, 1, paragraph 2 to delete references to the “Trust Agreement for the
Annuity Fund for Ministers of the Presbyterian Church in America,”
the “Trust Agreement for the Annuity Fund for Lay Employees of the
Presbyterian Church in America,” and the “Trust Agreement for the
Money Purchase Pension Plan of the Presbyterian Church in America;” and to replace “the Trust Agreement for the Voluntary Tax-
Sheltered Annuity Plan of the Presbyterian Church in America” with
“the Trust Agreement for the Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan of the
Presbyterian Church in America.”
13. That the General Assembly ratify the Fifth Amendment to the Trust
Agreement for the Presbyterian Church in America Voluntary Tax-
Sheltered Annuity Plan (see Attachment Four [Appendix L, p. 722]),
and that the resolution related to this Amendment be adopted and
included in the Minutes of the General Assembly.
14. That the General Assembly mandate coverage in the PCA health plans
for all active ministers and church lay employees who are current
residents of the United States and who are not covered under a
spouse’s group health plan, and that the Presbyteries be responsible for
implementing and enforcing this mandate.
Respectfully Submitted,
Board of Trustees
Thomas W. Harris, Jr., Chairman
James L. Hughes, Director
