ATTACHMENT TWO
FOURTEENTH AMENDMENT
TO AND PROPOSED TERMINATION OF THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
MONEY PURCHASE PENSION PLAN
THIS AMENDMENT TO AND PROPOSED TERMINATION OF
the Presbyterian Church in America Money Purchase Pension Plan (hereinafter
referred to as the “Plan”), made this 2nd day of November, 2001, by the Board
of Trustees of the Insurance, Annuity and Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church in America (hereinafter referred to as the “Trustees”), to be effective as
noted below;
WITNESSETH:

WHEREAS, the Presbyterian Church in America sponsors and
maintains the Plan as a qualified plan under Section 401(a) of the Internal
Revenue Code of 1986, as amended (“Code”), and a church plan under Section
414(e) of such Code; and

WHEREAS, Section 11.01 of the Plan reserves to the Trustees the
right to alter or amend the Plan subject to the approval of the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America (“General Assembly”); and

WHEREAS, Section 11.02 of the Plan reserves to the Trustees the
right, subject to prior approval of the General Assembly, to terminate the Plan
and its related trust; and

WHEREAS, Section 11.03 of the Plan reserves to the Trustees the
right, subject to prior approval of the General Assembly, to require payment of
all benefits under the Plan in the form of lump sum distributions; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the Trustees to amend the Plan to permit
a distributee to elect to have any portion of an eligible rollover distribution
(including after-tax amounts in certain circumstances) paid directly to an
annuity contract, custodial account, or church retirement income account
described in section 403(b) of the Code, or to an eligible deferred
compensation plan described in section 457(b) of the Code which is
maintained by an eligible employer described in section 457(e)(1)(A) of the
Code, as required by the Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act
of 2001, Pub. L. No. 107-16; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the Trustees to terminate the Plan and to
distribute all benefits under the Plan in lump sum distributions, in order to
enhance administrative efficiency and decrease cost;

NOW THEREFORE, IT IS RESOLVED that the Plan be amended as
provided in Exhibit A, effective January 1, 2002; and it is

<a id="ga30-p689"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=691 printed_page=689 -->

FURTHER RESOLVED that the Trustees recommend to the General Assembly that it approve the termination of the Plan and its related trust, and
approve a requirement that all benefits under the Plan be paid in the form of
lump sum distributions as soon as reasonably possible after January 1, 2002;
and it is
FURTHER RESOLVED that immediately upon approval by the
General Assembly the Plan be terminated automatically, that as soon as
reasonably possible after approval by the General Assembly and January 1,
2002 all benefits under the Plan be paid in the form of lump sum distributions,
and that upon approval by the General Assembly and complete payment of all
benefits under the Plan the related trust be terminated automatically, all
without the necessity of further action by the Trustees; and it is
FURTHER RESOLVED, that the Director of the Insurance, Annuity
and Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church in America is hereby authorized
to take any and all actions necessary and desirable to carry out this and the
foregoing resolutions.
IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the Trustees have caused this Fourteenth
Amendment and Proposed Termination to be executed on the day and year
first above written.
TRUSTEES:
BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE
INSURANCE, ANNUITY AND RELIEF
FUNDS OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

EXHIBIT A
Section 7.10(b)(1) is amended by inserting after “any distribution to
the extent such distribution is required under section 401(a)(9) of the
Code,” the following phrase, “any distribution which is made upon
hardship of the employee,”.
Section 7.10(b)(i) of the Plan is hereby further amended by adding a
new sentence at the end to read as follows:
Notwithstanding the foregoing, an eligible rollover distribution does
include the portion of a distribution that is not includible in gross
income if such portion is transferred in a direct trustee-to-trustee
transfer to a qualified trust described in section 401(a) of the Code
which is part of a plan which is a defined contribution plan and which
agrees to separately account for amounts so transferred, including
separately accounting for the potion of such distribution which is

<a id="ga30-p690"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=692 printed_page=690 -->

includible in gross income and the portion of such distribution which
is not so includible, or such portion is transferred to an individual
retirement account described in section 408(a) of the Code, or an
individual retirement annuity described in section 408(b) of the Code.
Section 7.10(b)(ii) of the Plan is hereby amended as follows:
In the first sentence replace “or a qualified trust described in section
401(a) of the Code,” with “a qualified trust described in section 401(a)
of the Code, an annuity contract described in section 403(b) of the
Code (including a 403(b)(9) church retirement income account), or an
eligible deferred compensation plan described in section 457(b) of the
Code which is maintained by an eligible employer described in section
457(e)(1)(A) of the Code”.
Section 7.10(b)(ii) of the Plan is hereby further amended by deleting
the last sentence.

TWENTY-FIRST AMENDMENT
AND APPROVAL OF ROLLOVER CONTRIBUTIONS TO THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
TAX-SHELTERED ANNUITY PLAN
THIS AMENDMENT AND APPROVAL OF ROLLOVER
CONTRIBUTIONS to the Presbyterian Church in America Tax-Sheltered
Annuity Plan (hereinafter referred to as the “Plan”), made this 2nd day of
November, 2001, by the Board of Trustees of the Insurance, Annuity and
Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church in America (hereinafter referred to as
the “Trustees”), to be effective as noted below;
WITNESSETH:

WHEREAS, the Presbyterian Church in America sponsors and
maintains the Plan as a retirement income account under Section 403(b)(9) of
the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended; and

WHEREAS, Section 11.01 of the Plan reserves to the Trustees the
right to alter or amend the Plan subject to the approval of the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the Trustees to amend the Plan to permit
a distributee to elect to have any portion of an eligible rollover distribution
(including after-tax amounts in certain circumstances) paid directly to a
qualified trust described in section 401(a) of the Code, an annuity plan
described in section 403(a) of the Code, or an eligible deferred compensation

<a id="ga30-p691"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=693 printed_page=691 -->

plan described in section 457(b) of the Code which is maintained by an eligible
employer described in section 457(e)(1)(A) of the Code, as required by the
Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2001, Pub. L. No.
107-16; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the Trustees to amend the Plan to clarity
that it may accept an eligible rollover distribution paid from a qualified trust,
an annuity plan, or an eligible deferred compensation plan described in the
foregoing recital, upon receipt of approval by the Trustees; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the Trustees to approve the receipt of
any eligible rollover distribution from the Presbyterian Church in America
Money Purchase Pension Plan on or after January 1, 2002;

NOW THEREFORE, IT IS RESOLVED that the Plan be amended as
provided in Exhibit A, effective January 1, 2002; and it is
FURTHER RESOLVED, that the Trustees hereby approve the receipt
of any eligible rollover distribution from the Presbyterian Church in America
Money Purchase Pension Plan on or after January 1, 2002; and it is
FURTHER RESOLVED, that the Director of the Insurance, Annuity
and Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church in America is hereby authorized
to take any and all actions necessary and desirable to carry out this and the
foregoing resolutions.
IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the Trustees have caused this Fourteenth
Amendment and Proposed Termination to be executed on the day and year
first above written.
TRUSTEES:
BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE
INSURANCE, ANNUITY AND RELIEF
FUNDS OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

EXHIBIT A
The Plan is hereby amended as follows:
Section 1.13 is amended to read:
1.13 The term “Rollover Contribution” shall mean a transf
of assets attributable to an annuity contract described in Code Section
403(b)(1) (including any account treated as such an annuity contract
under Code Section 403(b)), an individual retirement account
described in Code Section 408(a), an individual retirement annuity
described in Code Section 408(b), a qualified trust described in Code
Section 401(a), an annuity plan described in Code Section 403(a), or

<a id="ga30-p692"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=694 printed_page=692 -->

an eligible deferred compensation plan described in Code Section
457(b) which is maintained by an eligible employer described in Code
Section 457(e)(1)(A), to the Trust Fund which transfer is made in
accordance with Code Section 403(b)(8) and (10), 408(d)(3)(A)(iii),
402(c), or 457(e)(16). The term Rollover Contribution shall also
include a direct transfer of assets from one Code Section 403(b)
annuity to the Trustees of this Plan. Rollover Contributions to the Plan
shall be permitted only upon receipt of approval by the Trustees.
Section 1.14 is amended to read:
1.14 The term "Rollover Distribution" shall mean a transfer of
assets from the Trust Fund to an annuity contract described in Code
Section 403(b)(1) (including any account treated as such an annuity
contract under Code Section 403(b)), an individual retirement account
described in Code Section 408(a), an individual retirement annuity
described in Code Section 408(b), a qualified trust described in Code
Section 401(a), an annuity plan described in Code Section 403(a), or
an eligible deferred compensation plan described in Code Section
457(b) which is maintained by an eligible employer described in Code
Section 457(e)(1)(A), which transfer is made in accordance with Code
Section 403(b)(8). Rollover Distributions shall be permitted in
accordance with Section 6.12 and Code Section 403(b)(10) or, in all
other cases, upon receipt of approval by the Trustees.
Section 6.12(b)(1) is amended by inserting after “any distribution to
the extent such distribution is required under section 401(a)(9) of the
Code as made applicable by Section 403(b)(10) of the Code,” the
following phrase, “any distribution which is made upon hardship of
the employee.”
Section 6.12(b)(1) is further amended by adding a new sentence at the
end to read:
Notwithstanding the foregoing, an eligible rollover distribution does
include the portion of a distribution that is not includible in gross
income if such portion is transferred in a direct trustee-to-trustee
transfer to an annuity contract described in section 403(b) of the Code
(including custodial accounts described in section 403(b)(7) of the
Code and retirement income accounts described in section 403(b)(9) of
the Code) which includes an agreement to separately account for
amounts so transferred, including separately accounting for the potion
of such distribution which is includible in gross income and the
portion of such distribution which is not so includible, or such portion

<a id="ga30-p693"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=695 printed_page=693 -->

is transferred to an individual retirement account described in section
408(a) of the Code, or an individual retirement annuity described in
section 408(b) of the Code.
Section 6.12(b)(2) is amended to read:
(2) Eligible retirement plan: An eligible retirement plan is an
individual retirement account described in section 408(a) of the Code,
an individual retirement annuity described in section 408(b) of the
Code, an annuity contract described in section 403(b) of the Code
(including custodial accounts described in section 403(b)(7) of the
Code and retirement income accounts described in section 403(b)(9) of
the Code), a qualified trust described in Code Section 401(a), an
annuity plan described in section 403(a) of the Code, or an eligible
deferred compensation plan described in Code Section 457(b) which is
maintained by an eligible employer described in Code Section
457(e)(1)(A) that accepts the distributee's eligible rollover distribution.

TWENTY-SECOND AMENDMENT TO THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
TAX-SHELTERED ANNUITY PLAN
THIS AMENDMENT to the Presbyterian Church in America Tax-
Sheltered Annuity Plan (hereinafter referred to as the “Plan”), made this 1st
day of March, 2002, by the Board of Trustees of the Insurance, Annuity and
Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church in America (hereinafter referred to as
the “Trustees”), to be effective as noted below;
WITNESSETH:

WHEREAS, the Presbyterian Church in America sponsors and
maintains the Plan as a retirement income account under Section 403(b)(9) of
the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended; and

WHEREAS, Section 11.01 of the Plan reserves to the Trustees the
right to alter or amend the Plan subject to the approval of the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the Trustees to amend the Plan to reflect
contribution-limitations and other rules instituted by the Economic Growth and
Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2001, Pub. L. No. 107-16;

NOW THEREFORE, IT IS RESOLVED that the Plan be amended as
provided in Exhibit A, effective January 1, 2002; and it is
FURTHER RESOLVED, that the Director of the Insurance, Annuity
and Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church in America is hereby authorized

<a id="ga30-p694"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=696 printed_page=694 -->

to take any and all actions necessary and desirable to carry out this and the
foregoing resolutions.

Second Amendment to be executed on the day and year first above written.

BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE
INSURANCE, ANNUITY AND RELIEF
FUNDS OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

EXHIBIT A
The Plan is hereby amended as follows:
1. article v is amended to read:
ARTICLE V
LIMITATION ON CONTRIBUTIONS
5.01 Maximum Contributions.
(a) Contributions to a Participant’s Account for any calendar year,
regardless of source, may not exceed the Participant's Defined
Contribution Limit as determined in Subsection (b) of this Section
5.01 below, or any other applicable limit imposed by the Internal
Revenue Code as amended.
Neither the Trustees nor any person or organization engaged by them
to provide administrative services in connection with this Plan have
any duty to determine whether contributions to any Participant’s
Account for any calendar year comply with the foregoing restrictions
or to assure that they do comply.
(b) A Participant's Defined Contribution Limit for any calendar year
is the amount determined under Code Section 415(c), which currently
provides in substantial part as follows, or the corresponding provision
of any future United States internal revenue law, as amended:

<a id="ga30-p695"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=697 printed_page=695 -->

(1) General Rule: The Participant’s Defined Contribution
Limit is equal to the lesser of:
(A) 100% of the Participant's Compensation
(excluding any amount excludable from income under
Code Section 107) in such calendar year, or
(B) $40,000, or such greater amount as is permitted
under Code Section 415(c) as adjusted under Code
Section 415(d)(1)(C).
(2) Controlled Employer: For purposes of determining a
Participant's Defined Contribution Limit, in the case of a
Participant who has more than 50% control of an employer (a
“Controlled Employer") within the meaning of Code Section
415(h), contributions to a plan of the Controlled Employer that
constitute annual additions (as defined in Code Section
415(c)(2)) to the Participant are treated as contributions to this
Plan.
(3) Special Elections: Notwithstanding any provision of this
Subsection (b) of Section 5.01 to the contrary, the Defined
Contribution Limit of a Participant who has made an election
under Code Section 415(c)(7) (providing in general for a
special contribution limit of $10,000 per year for church
employees, with contributions taken into account under this
special limit not to exceed $40,000) is determined under such
Code Section and the Regulations issued thereunder.
(4) Compensation Definition. For purposes of this Subsection
(b) of this Section 5.01, a “Participant’s Compensation” has
the meaning given that term in Code Section 403(b)(3) or
Code Section 415(c)(3)(C) for the particular circumstances of
the Participant. Under Code Section 403(b)(3) a “Participant’s
Compensation” has a specialized meaning in the case of a
Participant who is a self-employed individual as described in
Code Section 401(c)(1), and under Code Section 415(c)(3)(C)
a “Participant’s Compensation” may have a specialized
meaning in the case of a Participant who is permanently and
totally disabled as defined in Code Section 22(e)(3). Subject
to Code Sections 403(b)(3) and 415(c)(3)(C), a Participant’s
Compensation means the compensation received from an
Employer which is includible in gross income (computed
without regard to Code Section 911) for the most recent period

<a id="ga30-p696"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=698 printed_page=696 -->

(ending not later than the close of the taxable year) which
under Code Section 403(b)(4) may be counted as one year of
service. A Participant’s Compensation does not include any
amount contributed by the Employer under this Plan, except as
provided below, or any amount received by a former
Employee after the fifth taxable year following the taxable
year in which such Employee was terminated. A Participant’s
Compensation does include (A) any contribution (including a
contribution under this Plan) under a Salary Reduction
Agreement, and any other elective deferral (as defined in Code
Section 402(g)(3), and (B) any amount which is contributed or
deferred by the Employer at the election of the Employee and
which is not includible in the gross income of the Employee
by reason of Code Sections 125, 132(f)(4), or 457.
(c) Contributions to a Participant’s Account pursuant to a Salary
Reduction Agreement also may not exceed the limitation on elective
deferrals imposed by the Code as amended. The limitation on elective
deferrals for any calendar year is the applicable dollar amount
determined under Code Section 402(g), plus an additional amount
determined for certain Employees under Code Section 414(v), which
Code sections currently provide in substantial part as follows, or the
corresponding provisions of any future United States internal revenue
law, as amended:
(1) General Rule: The limitations on elective deferrals under
Code Section 402(g) are the following amounts for the
following years:
2002 $11,000
2003 $12,000
2004 $13,000
2005 $14,000
2006 and thereafter $15,000
The limitation for Year 2006 will be adjusted each year
thereafter for the cost-of-living in accordance with Code
Section 402(g)(4), effective as of January 1 of each calendar
year. To the extent that the contribution limitation under Code
Section 402(g) is violated, such violation will only affect the
individual participant with respect to whom the excess
contribution is made and shall affect no other Plan participant.

<a id="ga30-p697"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=699 printed_page=697 -->

(2) Increase for Certain Long-Term Employees: The
limitation on elective deferrals may be increased under Code
Section 402(g)(7). Subject to that Code Section the limitation
on elective deferrals may be increased for any year for an
Employee of a Church or another qualified organization who
has completed 15 or more years of service with his or her
Employer, by the least of the following: (a) $3,000, (b)
$15,000 reduced by additional elective deferrals permitted by
this special rule, or (c) the excess of $5,000 multiplied by the
number of years of service of the Employee with the qualified
Employer over the elective deferrals made by the Employer on
behalf of such Employee for prior taxable years.
(3) Increase for Employees Over Age 50: The limitation on
elective deferrals may be increased also under Code Section
414(v). Subject to that Code Section the limitation on elective
deferrals may be increased for any year for an Employee who
has attained age 50 before the end of the year, by the
following amounts for the following years, not to exceed the
excess of the Employee’s compensation (as defined in Code
Section 415(c)(3)) over the Employee’s other elective
deferrals made without regard to this special rule:
2002 $1,000
2003 $2,000
2004 $3,000
2005 $4,000
2006 and thereafter $5,000
The limitation for Year 2006 will be adjusted each year
thereafter for the cost-of-living in accordance with Code
Section 414(v)(2)(C), effective as of January 1 of each
calendar year. An elective deferral made under this special
rule is not subject to any other contribution limitation imposed
by the Code, and is not considered when applying such
contribution limits to other contributions made for the
Employee.
(4) Distribution of Excess Deferrals: Notwithstanding any
other provision of this Plan, the Trustees may distribute to
the Participant any portion of elective deferrals in excess
of the limitation imposed in this Section (c), plus any
income allocable to that excess, no later than the first
April 15 following the close of the taxable year (only to

<a id="ga30-p698"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=700 printed_page=698 -->

the extent permitted by Code Section 402(g)(2)(A)). The
Participant first must have allocated the amount of all
such excess deferrals among the plans under which the
deferrals were made (in case deferrals have been made
under more than one plan) and have notified the Trustees
of the portion of such excess deferrals allocated to this
Plan, by the first March 1 following the close of the
taxable year.
(5) Responsibility for Compliance: Neither the Trustees nor
any person or organization engaged by them to provide
administrative services in connection with this Plan have any
duty to determine whether contributions to any Participant’s
Account for any calendar year comply with the limitations
imposed in this Subsection (c) or to assure that they do
comply, or to assure that any excess deferrals are timely
allocated to the Participant’s Account.
(d) Notwithstanding any other provision of this Section 5.01, to the
extent this Plan is governed by chapter 43 of title 38, United States
Code (relating to employment and reemployment rights of members of
the uniformed services), contributions required to be permitted by
Code Section 414(u)(2) will be permitted in accordance with such
Code Section.
2. Section 6.08(c)(3) is amended by replacing “12 months following receipt of
the hardship distribution” with “6 months following receipt of the hardship
distribution”.
3. Section 6.12(b)(1) is amended to read:
Eligible rollover distribution: An eligible rollover distribution is any
distribution of all or any portion of the balance to the credit of the
distributee, except that an eligible rollover distribution does not include:
any distribution that is one of a series of substantially equal periodic
payments (not less frequently than annually) made for the life (or life
expectancy) of the distributee or the joint lives (or joint life expectancies) of
the distributee and the distributee's designated beneficiary, or for a specified
period of ten years or more; any distribution to the extent such distribution
is required under section 401(a)(9) of the Code as made applicable by
Section 403(b)(10) of the Code; any distribution which is made upon
hardship of the employee; and the portion of any distribution that is not
includible in gross income.

<a id="ga30-p699"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=701 printed_page=699 -->

EXCERPT FROM THE MINUTES
OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

WHEREAS, the Board of Trustees of the Insurance, Annuity and
Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church has approved adoption of the
Fourteenth Amendment and Proposed Termination of the Presbyterian Church in America Money Purchase Pension Plan, the Twenty-first Amendment and
Approval of Rollover Contributions to the Presbyterian Church in America
Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan, and the Twenty-second Amendment to the
Presbyterian Church in America Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan, subject to
approval by the General Assembly;

WHEREAS, the General Assembly has determined that these
amendments recommended by the Trustees should be approved;

RESOLVED, that adoption of the amendments is hereby ratified by
the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America.

Presbyterian Church in America hereby certifies that attached hereto is a true
and correct Excerpt from the Minutes of a Meeting of the General Assembly,
duly called, convened and held in Birmingham, Alabama, June 18-21, 2002,
and that such Resolution therein contained is still in full force and effect.

day of______________ , 2002.

ATTACHMENT THREE
ARTICLES OF INCORPORATION OF
PCA RETIREMENT AND BENEFITS. INC.
A NONPROFIT CORPORATION

ARTICLE I. NAME
The name of the corporation is:
PCA Retirement and Benefits, Inc.
ARTICLE II. AUTHORITY
The corporation is organized pursuant to the provisions of the Georgia
Nonprofit Corporation Code as amended.
ARTICLE III. PURPOSES
The corporation is organized exclusively for religious and charitable
purposes within the meaning of Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue
Code (or the corresponding provision of any future United States internal
revenue law), including but not limited to administration of plans and
programs for the provision of retirement benefits or welfare benefits, or both,
for ministers of the Presbyterian Church in America and employees of that
church or its member churches, schools and related organizations, and
distributions to or for the use of organizations exempt at the time under Section
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code (or the corresponding provision of any
future United States internal revenue law). Subject to the foregoing, the
corporation has all powers authorized for nonprofit corporations, and not
prohibited to nonprofit corporations, under the Georgia Nonprofit Corporation
Code. The corporation serves as the entity identified as the “Insurance,
Annuities and Relief’ agency and as “Insurance & Annuities” in the Rules of Assembly Operations as Amended by the 29th General Assembly of that
church. The corporation is subject to the provisions of those Rules and of the
Corporate Bylaws of Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) as
Amended by the 29th General Assembly of that church, as amended, that
govern that entity.
ARTICLE IV. DURATION
The corporation has perpetual duration.
ARTICLE V. RESTRICTIONS
Section 1. No Private Inurement. No part of the net earnings or
property of the corporation may inure to the benefit of, or be distributable to,
its directors, trustees, officers, or other private persons; except that the
corporation is authorized and empowered to pay reasonable compensation for
services rendered and to make payments and distributions in furtherance of the

<a id="ga30-p701"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=703 printed_page=701 -->

purposes set forth in Article III hereof. The corporation may not have capital
stock or shareholders.
Section 2. No Substantial Lobbying. No substantial part of the
activities of the corporation may be the carrying on of propaganda, or
otherwise attempting to influence legislation, provided that the corporation
may make the election provided in Section 501(h) of the Internal Revenue
Code (or the corresponding provision of any future United States internal
revenue law) and thereafter may engage in such activities to the extent an
organization is permitted to do so under that section without being denied its
exemption from taxation under Section 501(a) of the Internal Revenue Code.
Section 3. No Political Campaigning. The corporation may not
participate in, or intervene in (including the publishing or distribution of
statements), any political campaign on behalf of or in opposition to any
candidate for public office.
Section 4. Irrevocable Dedication. The income and assets of the
corporation are irrevocably dedicated to its exclusive purposes.
ARTICLE VI. TRUSTEES
Section 1. Number. The Board of Trustees will consist of not fewer
than one (1) member, and of not more than a maximum number determined by
the Bylaws of the corporation as amended from time to time.
Section 2. Powers. The Board of Trustees governs the corporation,
and has all the rights and powers of a board of directors under the laws of the
State of Georgia and of the United States, as well as such other rights and
authority as are herein granted. The Board of Trustees must relate to the
General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America as a committee must
relate to that General Assembly under the church’s Constitution, consisting of
its doctrinal standards set forth in the Westminster Confession of Faith,
together with the Larger and Shorter Catechisms, and the Book of Church Order. The rights and powers of the Board of Trustees include, but are not
limited to, the power to adopt and amend the Bylaws and other corporate
governing documents (except these Articles of Incorporation), by a majority
vote, in any way not inconsistent with the Articles of Incorporation, the
provisions of the Corporate Bylaws of Presbyterian Church in America (A
Corporation) applicable to this corporation, the Constitution of the
Presbyterian Church in America as described above, the laws of the State of
Georgia, or the laws of the United States.
Section 3. Term. The term of each member of the Board of Trustees
will be as established in the Bylaws.
Section 4. Election. Trustees may be elected solely by the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America (“General Assembly”), upon
the expiration of a Trustee's term or a vacancy for any reason (including
positions created by an increase in the number of Trustees). Election by the
General Assembly as a trustee of retirement, welfare benefit, or relief funds
maintained for the ministers or lay employees of the Presbyterian Church in

<a id="ga30-p702"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=704 printed_page=702 -->

America, its member churches, or related organizations, and their
beneficiaries, will constitute election as a member of the Board of Trustees of
this corporation. If the General Assembly is unable to elect a successor
Trustee(s) so that no Trustee remains on the Board of Trustees, the appropriate
court of the county in which the principal Georgia office (or if none the
Georgia registered office) of the corporation is then located may appoint such
successor.
Section 5. Amendment. The Board of Trustees has the power to
amend these Articles of Incorporation, by a majority vote of the Trustees then
in office, in any way not inconsistent with the provisions of the Corporate
Bylaws of Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) applicable to this
corporation, the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America, or the
laws of the State of Georgia or of the United States.
Section 6. Initial Trustees. The initial Board of Trustees consists of
twelve members, whose names and addresses are:
Thomas W. Harris, Jr. Robert L. Roane
4677 Caldwell Mill Road Covenant Presbyterian Church
Birmingham, AL 35243 1919 Cloverfield Drive
Katy, Texas, 77494
Thomas J. Stein William H. Brockman
3255 Ravine Place 6308 Queens Chapel Road
Maineville, OH 45039-8716 University Park, MD 20782
James A. Mezzanotte Ralph S. Paden
6521 Trenton Place 222 West Brow Oval
Charlotte, NC 28226 Lookout Mountain, TN 37350
Gary D. Campbell David L. Anderegg, Jr.
Kennedy Capital Management Pinewoods Presbyterian Church
10829 Olive Boulevard 2198 Highway 297A
St. Louis, MO 63141-7379 Cantonment, FL 32533
Glenn A. Fogle T. Ramon Perdue
1035 Westover Road 1033 Scenic Highway
Kansas City, MO 64113 Lookout Mountain, GA 30750
Earle E. Morris, Jr. J.Allen Wright
159 Lake Murray Terrace UBS/Paine Webber, Inc.
Lexington, SC 29072 3455 Peachtree Road, NE
Suite 1700
Atlanta, GA 30326

<a id="ga30-p703"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=705 printed_page=703 -->

ARTICLE VII. NO MEMBERS
Section 1. No Members. The corporation has no members.
ARTICLE VIII. POWERS
Section 1. General. The corporation has all the rights and powers
customary and proper for tax exempt nonprofit corporations, including the
powers specifically enumerated in Section 14-3-302 of the Georgia Code as
amended.
Section 2. Restrictions. Notwithstanding any other provisions of
these Articles of Incorporation, the corporation may not carry on any activities
not permitted to be carried on by a corporation exempt from federal income tax
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, orb ya corporation to
which contributions are deductible under Sections 170(b)(1)(A) or (B) and
170(c)(2) of the Internal Revenue Code (or the corresponding provisions of
any future United States internal revenue law).
Section 3. Charitable Trusteeship Etc. The corporation is empowered
to hold or administer property for the purposes stated in Article III, including
the power to act as trustee.
ARTICLE IX. DISSOLUTION
Section 1. Dissolution. The Board of Trustees may cease
corporate activities and dissolve and liquidate the corporation, by two-thirds
vote.
Section 2. Liquidation. Upon the dissolution of the corporation, the
Board of Trustees must pay or make provision for the payment of all of the
liabilities of the corporation, and must thereafter dispose of all of the assets of
the corporation to the Presbyterian Church in America (a Corporation)
provided that it at the time qualifies as an exempt organization under Section
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code (or the corresponding provision of any
future United State internal revenue law); and otherwise (i) exclusively for one
or more exempt purposes within the meaning of Section 501(c)(3), in such
manner as the Board of Trustees may determine, or (ii) to such organization or
organizations organized and operated exclusively for charitable, educational,
religious, literary, or scientific purposes as at the time qualify as an exempt
organization or organizations under Section 501(c)(3), and at the time are
described in Section 170(c)(2) of such Code, as the Board of Trustees may
determine.
Section 3. Contingent Provision. If any such assets are not so
disposed of, the appropriate court of the county in which the principal Georgia
office (or if none the Georgia registered office) of the corporation is then
located will dispose of such assets exclusively for the purposes stated in
Article III herein, and exclusively to such organization or organizations which
are organized and operated exclusively for such purposes and at the time
qualify as an exempt organization or organizations under such Section
501(c)(3), as said court may determine.

<a id="ga30-p704"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=706 printed_page=704 -->

ARTICLE X. CONTINGENT RESTRICTIONS
Section 1. Contingent Restrictions. In the event that the corporation is
determined by the Internal Revenue Service to be a private foundation within
the meaning of Section 509 of the Internal Revenue Code (or the
corresponding provision of any future United States internal revenue law), and
only during the period during which such determination applies,
notwithstanding any other provision of these Articles of Incorporation, this
Article X applies and the corporation must: (1) not engage in any act of "selfdealing" (as defined in Section 4941(d) of the Internal Revenue Code) that
would subject the corporation to tax under Section 4941 of the Internal
Revenue Code; (2) distribute its income for each taxable year for the purposes
specified in Article III herein at such time, in such manner, and in such
amounts as are necessary to avoid subjecting the corporation to tax under
Section 4942 of the Internal Revenue Code; (3) not retain any "excess business
holdings" (as defined in Section 4943(c) of the Internal Revenue Code) that
would subject the corporation to tax under Section 4943 of the Internal
Revenue Code; (4) not make any investments that would jeopardize the
carrying out of any of the exempt purposes of the corporation (within the
meaning of Section 4944 of the Internal Revenue Code) and thereby subject
the corporation to tax under Section 4944 of the Internal Revenue Code; and
(5) not make any "taxable expenditures" (as defined in Section 4945(d) of the
Internal Revenue Code) that would subject the corporation to tax under Section
4945 of the Internal Revenue Code.
Section 2. Definition. each reference in this article x to a
section of the Internal Revenue Code is to be deemed to include the
corresponding provisions of any future United States internal revenue law.
ARTICLE XI. INITIAL OFFICE AND AGENT
Section 1. Registered Office. The name and street address and county
of the initial registered agent and initial registered office of the corporation are
Timothy W. Townsend, 1000 Mansell Exchange West, Suite 180, Alpharetta,
Fulton County, Georgia 30022.
Section 2. Principal Office. The mailing address of the initial
principal office is 1700 North Brown Road, Suite 106, Lawrenceville, Georgia
30043.
ARTICLE XII. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY
Section 1. Limitation. The liability of a Trustee to the corporation or
its members, if any, for monetary damages for any action taken, or any failure
to take any action, as a Trustee is hereby eliminated entirely to the extent
permitted by the Code, except liability: (i) For any appropriation, in violation

<a id="ga30-p705"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=707 printed_page=705 -->

of his duties, of any business opportunity of the corporation; (ii) For acts or
omissions which involve intentional misconduct or a knowing violation of
laws; (iii) For the types of liability set forth in Georgia Code Sections 14-3-860
through 14-3-864 as amended; or (iv) For any transaction from which the
Trustee received an improper personal benefit.
Section 2. No Effect on Prior Liability. Such provision does not
eliminate or limit the liability of a Trustee for any act or omission occurring
prior to the date of these Articles of Incorporation when such provision
becomes effective.
Section 3. Amendment. Any repeal or modification of the provisions
of this Article will be prospective only, and will not adversely affect any
limitation on the personal liability of a Trustee of the corporation with respect
to any act or omission occurring prior to the effective date of such repeal or
modification, and must be approved by 90% of the Trustees present at a duly
noticed meeting with a quorum present. In the event of any amendment of the
Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code to authorize the further elimination or
limitation of liability of Trustees, then the liability of a Trustee of the
corporation will be limited to the fullest extent permitted by the amended
Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code, in addition to the limitation on personal
liability provided herein, without the necessity of amendment of these Articles
of Incorporation.
Section 4. Severability. In the event that any provision of this Article
(including a clause) is held by a court of competent jurisdiction to be invalid,
void, or otherwise unenforceable, the remaining provisions are severable and
will remain enforceable to the fullest extent permitted by law.
ARTICLE XIII. INCORPORATOR(S)
Section 1. Incorporator(s). The name and address of the
incorporator(s), who is a citizen(s) of the United States, is:
Timothy W. Townsend, Townsend McKee, P.C.
1000 Mansell Exchange West, Suite 180
Alpharetta, Georgia 30022
IN WITNESS WFIEREOF, the undersigned incorporator or attorney for and
representative of the incorporator has executed these Articles of Incorporation,
pursuant to the Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code.
Timothy W. Townsend, Incorporator, Townsend McKee, P.C.
1000 Mansell Exchange West, Suite 180
Alpharetta, Georgia 30022

This February 15, 2002.

### I. GOVERNING LAW AND DEFINITIONS

(Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code §§ 14-3-101 through-141)
1.01 Governing Law. The corporation is governed by the
nonelective provisions of the Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code as amended
(the "Code"), and is to be construed insofar as possible to be consistent
therewith.
1.02 Definitions. The definitions that appear in the Code Section
14-3-140 as amended are incorporated by reference.
1.03 Registered Office and Agent. The registered office and agent
are governed by the Code Sections 14-3-501 through -504 as amended.
1.04 Emergency. Emergency powers are provided by the Code
Section 14-3-303 as amended.
1.05 Relation to Presbyterian Church in America. The corporation
serves as the entity identified as the “Insurance, Annuities and Relief’ agency
and as “Insurance & Annuities” in the Rules of Assembly Operations as

<a id="ga30-p709"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=711 printed_page=709 -->

Amended by the 29th General Assembly of that church. The corporation is
subject to the provisions of those Rules and of the Corporate Bylaws of
Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) as Amended by the 29th
General Assembly of that church, as amended, that govern that entity. The
corporation must submit its budget to the Administrative Committee of the
General Assembly, and the General Assembly must approve its budget before
it becomes effective. Although the General Assembly establishes policy for
this corporation to execute, this corporation does not serve as the agent of the
General Assembly or of the Presbyterian Church in America (a Corporation).
1.06 Governance by Constitution, Corporate Bylaws, and
Assembly Rules. The corporation is governed by the applicable provisions of
the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America, as amended,
consisting of its doctrinal standards set forth in the Westminster Confession of Faith, together with the Larger and Shorter Catechisms, and the Book of Church Order, by the applicable provisions of the Corporate Bylaws of
Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation), as amended, and by the
applicable provisions of the Rules of Assembly Operations, all of which are
incorporated herein by this reference and govern to the extent of any
inconsistency with these Bylaws. The foregoing are identified herein as the
“PCA Governing Documents.”
1.07 Relation to Trust Funds. The corporation on behalf of the
Board of Trustees of the Insurance, Annuity & Relief Funds of the
Presbyterian Church in America administers retirement, welfare benefit, or
relief funds maintained for the ministers or lay employees of the Presbyterian Church in America, its member churches, or related organizations, and their
beneficiaries. No action of the corporation, its board of trustees, or its officers
may conflict with provisions of the trusts maintained for those funds.
II. MEMBERS
(Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code §§ 14-3-601 through -630)
The corporation does not have members.
III. TRUSTEES
(Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code §§ 14-3-801 through-831)
3.01 Duties of Trustees. (a) All corporate powers shall b
exercised by or under the authority of, and the business and affairs of the
corporation shall be managed under the direction of, the board of trustees,
subject to any limitation in the Articles of Incorporation, these Bylaws, or the
PCA Governing Documents that is otherwise lawful, (b) No limitation upon
the authority of the trustees is effective against persons, other than trustees,
who are without actual knowledge of the limitation, (c) The Board of Trustees

<a id="ga30-p710"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=712 printed_page=710 -->

must relate to the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America as
a committee must relate to that General Assembly under the church’s
Constitution.
3.02 Qualifications of Trustees, (a) Trustees must be natural
persons who are 18 years of age or older, but need not be residents of this state
nor officers of the corporation, (b) Each trustee must be a Teaching Elder,
Ruling Elder or Deacon in the Presbyterian Church in America, as those terms
are defined in the PCA Governing Documents, (c) A trustee may not serve
during his term on any other committee or agency of the Presbyterian Church in America, except that he may serve during his term as a permanent
committee representative on the Administrative Committee of the General Assembly, and he may serve during his term on Ad Interim Committees of the
General Assembly, (d) A trustee may not be an employee of the corporation.
3.03 Number and Election of Trustees, (a) The number of trustees
is twelve, (b) Nominations for election to the board of trustees are received
from the presbyteries of the Presbyterian Church in America and presented to
the General Assembly pursuant to the procedure prescribed by the Book of Church Order of that church. The board of trustees may make requests to the
presbyteries to nominate specific men to the board, and may submit to the
General Assembly Nominating Committee letters of recommendation
concerning particular nominees from the Presbyteries, (c) The General Assembly elects at each Assembly a class of trustees to begin service
immediately following the conclusion of the Assembly. Election by the
General Assembly as a trustee of retirement, welfare benefit, and relief funds
maintained for the ministers or lay employees of the Presbyterian Church in America and its member churches, and their beneficiaries, will constitute
election as a member of the board of trustees of this corporation, (d) The
chairman and vice-chairman of the board of trustees must be members of the
board of trustees, and are elected to their positions by the board of trustees.
3.04 Permissibility of Class Election. Trustees are divided into
four classes of three men each. Each trustee will hold office for the four-year
term to which he is elected and until his successor has been elected and
qualified, or until his earlier resignation, removal from office, or death.
3.05 Terms of Trustees, (a) Each initial trustee of the corporation
will serve a term co-extensive with the term he is serving as a trustee of
retirement, welfare benefit, and relief funds maintained for the ministers and
lay employees of the Presbyterian Church in America and its member
churches, and their beneficiaries, (b) Thereafter, the terms of trustees expire at
the end of the annual meeting of the General Assembly in the fourth year
following their election, (c) A trustee may serve two consecutive terms after
which there must be a one-year interval before he may be re-elected, (d) A
decrease in the number of trustees does not shorten an incumbent trustee's
term, (e) a trustee elected to fill a vacancy is elected to serve the un expired

<a id="ga30-p711"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=713 printed_page=711 -->

term of his predecessor in office, (f) Despite the expiration of a trustee's term,
he continues to serve until his successor is elected and qualifies or until there is
a decrease in the number of trustees.
3.06 Staggered Terms for Trustees, (a) The board of trustees is
divided into four classes, with each class containing three men. (b) If while
the trustees have staggered terms the number of trustees is changed, any
increase or decrease in the number of trustees must be so apportioned among
the classes as to make all classes as nearly equal in number as possible.
3.07 Resignation of Trustees, (a) A trustee may resign at any time
by delivering written notice to the board of trustees or its chairman, or to the
president or secretary of the corporation, (b) A resignation is effective when
the notice is delivered unless the notice specifies a later effective date.
3.08 Removal of Trustees. A trustee may be removed from office
pursuant to the following procedure, (a) The General Assembly may remove
one or more trustees with or without cause, (b) If the trustees have staggered
terms as provided above, trustees nonetheless may be removed with or without
cause.
3.09 Vacancy on the Board, (a) If a vacancy occurs on the board of
trustees, including a vacancy resulting from an increase in the number of
trustees, the vacancy may be filled only by the General Assembly, (b) If no
trustee remains, and the vacancy is not filled by the General Assembly, any
person may submit an application to the superior court of the county where the
registered office of the corporation is located, (c) Except as provided by the
General Assembly, a vacancy that will occur a ta specific later date (by reason
of a resignation effective a ta later date or otherwise) may be filled before the
vacancy occurs but the new trustee may not take office until the vacancy
occurs.
3.10 Meetings of the Board, (a) The board of trustees may hold
regular or special meetings in or out of this state, (b) Members of the board of
trustees or any committee designated by it may participate in a regular or
special meeting of such board or committee, if they so request, or may conduct
the meeting through the use of any means of conference telephone or similar
communications equipment by which all persons participating in the meeting
can hear each other. Participation in a meeting by this means shall constitute
presence in person at the meeting, (c) The board of trustees should keep
minutes in accordance with the guidelines of the Rules of Assembly Operations of the Presbyterian Church in America, as amended.
3.11 Action without Meeting, (a) Action required or permitted by
the Bylaws to be taken at a board of trustees' meeting may be taken without a
meeting if the action is taken by all members of the board. The action must be
evidenced by one or more written consents describing the action taken, signed
by each trustee, and delivered to the corporation for inclusion in the minutes or
filing with the corporate records, (b) A consent signed under this section has

<a id="ga30-p712"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=714 printed_page=712 -->

the effect of a meeting vote and may be described as such in any document,
(c) Such written consents may be delivered by facsimile transmission, and the
facsimile shall be deemed to be the original.
3.12 Notice of Meeting, (a) Regular meetings of the board of
trustees may be held without notice of the date, time, place, or purpose of the
meeting, except that meetings at which officers will be elected must be
preceded by notice that these elections will take place. The board of trustees
must hold at least two regular meetings per year, (b) Special meetings of the
board of trustees must be preceded by at least ten days' notice of the date, time,
and place of the meeting. The notice must describe the purpose of the special
meeting.
3.13 Waiver of Notice, (a) A trustee may waive any notice
required to be given, before or after the date and time stated in the notice.
Except as provided by subsection (b) of this section, the waiver must be in
writing, signed by the trustee entitled to the notice, and delivered to the
corporation for inclusion in the minutes or filing with the corporate records, (b)
A trustee's attendance at or participation in a meeting waives any required
notice to him of the meeting unless the trustee at the beginning of the meeting
(or promptly upon his arrival) objects to holding the meeting or transacting
business at the meeting and does not thereafter vote for or assent to action
taken at the meeting.
3.14 Quorum and Voting, (a) A majority of the number of trustees
then in office constitutes a quorum for the transaction of business, (b) If a
quorum is present when a vote is taken, the affirmative vote of a majority of
trustees present is the act of the board of trustees, unless the Code, the Articles
of Incorporation, these Bylaws, or the PCA Governing Documents require the
vote of a greater number of trustees, (c) a trustee who is present a ta meeting
of the board of trustees or a committee of the board of trustees when corporate
action is taken is deemed to have assented to the action taken unless: (1) he
objects at the beginning of the meeting (or promptly upon his arrival) to
holding it or transacting business at the meeting; (2) his dissent or abstention
from the action taken is entered in the minutes of the meeting; or (3) he
delivers written notice of his dissent or abstention to the presiding officer of
the meeting before its adjournment or to the corporation immediately after
adjournment of the meeting.
3.15 Committees of Trustees, (a) The board of trustees may by
resolution adopted by a majority of the full board of trustees, create one or
more committees and appoint persons to serve on them as members. Each
committee may have one or more members, who serve at the pleasure of the
board of trustees, (b) Committees may include persons who are not trustees,
but the chairman of each committee must be a trustee, (b) Sections 3.10-3.14,
which govern meetings, action without meetings, notice and waiver of notice,
and quorum and voting requirements of the board of trustees, apply to

<a id="ga30-p713"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=715 printed_page=713 -->

committees and their members as well, (c) To the extent specified in such
resolution or in the Articles of Incorporation or the Bylaws, each committee
may exercise the authority of the board of trustees, subject to ratification by
the full board of trustees, (d) The board, by resolution adopted in accordance
with subsection (a) of this section, may designate one or more trustees as
alternate members of any such committee, who may act in the place and stead
of any absent member or members at any meeting of such committee, (e) The
designation of any such committee and the delegation thereto of authority may
not operate to relieve the board of trustees, or any member thereof, of any
responsibility imposed by law to be exercised by the full board, (f) The board
of trustees must approve all policies and procedures of each committee.
3.16 Standards of Conduct for Trustees. The standards of conduct
for trustees shall be as prescribed by the Code and other applicable law, and by
the PCA Governing Documents.
3.17 Resolution of Deadlock, (a) If the trustees of the corporation
are deadlocked in the management of the corporate affairs and the selection of
new trustees is not able to break the deadlock and if injury to the corporation is
being suffered or is threatened by reason thereof, the superior court of the
county where the registered office of the corporation is located may,
notwithstanding any provisions of the Articles of Incorporation or these
Bylaws of the corporation to the contrary and whether or not an action is
pending for an involuntary dissolution of the corporation, appoint a provisional
trustee pursuant to the Code, (b) Action for such appointment may be filed by
one-half of the trustees or, if there are members, by members holding not less
than one-third of all the votes entitled to be cast in an election of trustees.
Notice of such action must be served upon the trustees, other than those who
have filed the action, and upon the corporation in the manner provided by law
for service of a summons and complaint, and a hearing will be held not less
than ten days after such service is effected. At such hearing all interested
persons must be given an opportunity to be heard, (c) The provisional trustee
must bean impartial person who is neither a member nora creditor of the
corporation nor related by consanguinity or affinity within the third degree, as
computed according to the civil law, to any of the trustees of the corporation or
to any judge of the court by which he is appointed. The provisional trustee
will have all the rights and powers of a trustee and will be entitled to notice of
the meetings of the board of trustees and to vote at such meetings until he is
removed by order of the court or by vote or written consent of a majority of the
trustees or of members holding a majority of the votes entitled to be cast in an
election of trustees. He will be entitled to receive such compensation as may
be agreed upon between him and the corporation; and, in the absence of such
agreement, he will be entitled to such compensation as may be fixed by the
court.

<a id="ga30-p714"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=716 printed_page=714 -->

IV. OFFICERS
(Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code §§ 14-3-840 through -846)
4.01 Required Officers, (a) The corporation must have a president,
secretary, and treasurer, and may have additional officers appointed by the
board of trustees, (b) The Theological Examining Committee of the General Assembly must approve the president and any vice president, as first and
second level administrative officers, before they assume their duties, (b) A
duly appointed officer may appoint one or more officers or assistant officers,
(c) The secretary has responsibility for preparing minutes of the trustees'
meetings and for authenticating records of the corporation, unless the board of
trustees delegates such responsibility to another officer, (d) The same
individual may simultaneously hold more than one office in a corporation, (e)
The board of trustees elects officers annually at its last regular meeting before
the meeting of the General Assembly.
4.02 Duties of Officers, (a) The president is the chief executive
officer. The president is ex officio a voting member of all committees, unless
the board provides that he will be a nonvoting member, and will attend all
meetings of the board of trustees. The president has authority to institute or
defend legal proceedings when the trustees are deadlocked, and may call
meetings of the board, (b) The secretary must keep or cause to be kept the
minutes of meetings of trustees and the other corporate records, and acts as
treasurer during that person's inability to act. The secretary must attend all
meetings of the board of trustees, and may call meetings of the board, (c) The
treasurer keeps or causes to be kept the assets and financial records of the
corporation, in the name of the corporation, and gives written reports thereon
to each meeting of the board and to the president when requested. He must
cause the corporation to pay or make provision for its liabilities, and acts as
secretary during that person's inability to act. (d) The foregoing officers and
other officers must perform such duties as are generally performed by such
officers, and such duties as are additionally conferred by the board of trustees,
(e) The president, vice president (if any), and secretary are authorized to
execute documents on behalf of the corporation without need of further
execution or attestation, if such documents are specifically authorized by the
board of trustees or within the scope of his responsibility.
4.03 Standards of Conduct for Officers. The standards of conduct
for officers are as provided by the Code and other applicable law, and the PCA
Governing Documents.
4.04 Resignation and Removal of Officers, (a) An officer may
resign at anytime by delivering notice to the corporation. A resignation is
effective when the notice is delivered unless the notice specifies a later
effective date, (b) The board of trustees may remove any officer at any time
whenever in their judgment the best interest of the corporation will be served
thereby, (c) Any vacancy in any office occurring for whatever reason may be
filled by the board of trustees.

<a id="ga30-p715"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=717 printed_page=715 -->

4.05 Contract Rights of Officers, (a) The appointment of an officer
does not itself create contract rights, (b) The removal of an officer does not
affect the officer's contract rights (if any) with the corporation. An officer’s
resignation does not affect the corporation's contract rights (if any) with the
officer.
V. INDEMNIFICATION
(Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code §§ 14-3-850
through -865)
5.01 Definitions. As used in this article V, the term:
"Corporation" includes any domestic or foreign predecessor entity of a
corporation in a merger or other transaction in which the predecessor's
existence ceased upon consummation of the transaction. (2) "Trustee" or
“Officer” means an individual who is or was a trustee or officer, respectively,
of a corporation oran individual who, while a trustee or officer of the
corporation, is or was serving at the corporation's request as a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or agent of another domestic or foreign business or
nonprofit corporation, partnership, joint venture, trust, employee benefit plan,
or other entity. A trustee or officer is considered to be serving an employee
benefit plan at the corporation's request if the trustee’s duties to the corporation
also impose duties on, or otherwise involve services by, the trustee to the plan
or to participants in or beneficiaries of the plan. Notwithstanding the
foregoing, no Trustee will be deemed to be serving as a trustee of retirement,
welfare benefit, or relief funds of the Presbyterian Church in America at the
corporation’s request; rather, all Trustees serve in that capacity at the request
of the General Assembly. “Trustee” or “Officer” includes, unless the context
requires otherwise, the estate or personal representative of a trustee. (3)
“Disinterested trustee” means a trustee who at the time of a vote referred to in
subsection (c) of Georgia Code Section 14-3-853 (relating to advance or
reimbursement of litigation expenses) ora vote or selection referred to in
subsection (b) or (c) of Georgia Code Section 14-3-855 (relating to
determination of right and authorization for payment of indemnification) is
not: (A) a party to the proceeding; or (B) an individual having a familial,
financial, professional, or employment relationship with the trustee whose
indemnification or advance for expenses is the subject of the decision being
made, which relationship would, in the circumstances, reasonably be expected
to exert an influence on the trustee’s judgment when voting on the decision
being made. (4) "Expenses" includes counsel fees. (5) "Liability" means the
obligation to pay a judgment, settlement, penalty, fine (including an excise tax
assessed with respect to an employee benefit plan), or reasonable expenses
incurred with respect to a proceeding. (6) “Official capacity” means: (A) when
used with respect to a trustee, the office of trustee in a corporation; and (B)

<a id="ga30-p716"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=718 printed_page=716 -->

when used with respect to an officer, as contemplated in Georgia Code Section
14-3-857, the office in a corporation held by the officer. "Official capacity”
does not include service for any other domestic or foreign corporation or any
partnership, joint venture, trust, employee benefit plan, or other entity. (7)
"Party" means an individual who was, is, or is threatened to be made a named
defendant or respondent ina proceeding. (8) "Proceeding" means any
threatened, pending, or completed action, suit, or proceeding whether civil,
criminal, administrative, arbitrative, or investigative and whether formal or
informal.
5.02 Court-Ordered Indemnification and Advance, (a) In addition
to the following sections, a trustee of the corporation who is a party to a
proceeding because he is a trustee may apply for indemnification or advances
for expenses to the court conducting the proceeding or to another court of
competent jurisdiction. The court may order indemnification or advance for
expenses if (1) it determines that (1) the trustee is entitled to indemnification
under Code Title 14, Chapter 3, Article 8, Part I (relating to indemnification);
or (2) it determines, in view of all the relevant circumstances, that it is fair and
reasonable (A) to indemnify the trustee; or (B) to advance expenses to the
trustee, even if he has not met the relevant standard of conduct set forth in
subsections (a) and (b) of Section 5.03, failed to comply with Section 5.06, or
was adjudged liable in a proceeding referred to in subsection (d) of Section
5.03, but if he was adjudged so liable his indemnification shall be limited to
reasonable expenses incurred in connection with the proceeding, (b) If the
court so determines that the trustee is entitled to indemnification or advance
for expenses, it may also order the corporation to pay the trustee’s reasonable
expenses to obtain court ordered indemnification or advance for expenses.
5.03 Approved Indemnification. (a) The corporation shall
indemnify an individual who is a party to a proceeding because the individual
is or was a trustee against liability incurred in the proceeding if: (1) he
conducted himself or herself in good faith; and (2) he reasonably believed: (A)
in the case of conduct in his official capacity, that his conduct was in the best
interests of the corporation; (B) in all other cases, that his conduct was at least
not opposed to the best interests of the corporation; and (C) in the case of any
criminal proceeding, he had no reasonable cause to believe his conduct was
unlawful, (b) A trustee’s conduct with respect to an employee benefit plan for
a purpose the trustee believed in good faith to be in the interests of the
participants in and beneficiaries of the plan is conduct that satisfies the
requirements of (a) above, (c) The termination of a proceeding by judgment,
order, settlement, or conviction or upon a plea of nolo contendere or its
equivalent is not, of itself, determinative that the trustee did not meet the
standard of conduct described in this Section 5.03. (d) The corporation shall
not indemnify a trustee under this Section 5.03: (1) in connection with a
proceeding by or in the right of the corporation, except for reasonable expenses

<a id="ga30-p717"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=719 printed_page=717 -->

incurred in connection with the proceeding if it is determined that the trustee
has met the relevant standard of conduct under this Section 5.03; or (2) in
connection with any other proceeding with respect to conduct for which the
trustee was adjudged liable on the basis that personal benefit was improperly
received by the trustee, whether or not involving action in the trustee’s official
capacity.
5.04 Determination of Right and Authorization for Payment of
Indemnification, (a) The corporation shall not indemnify a trustee under
Section 5.03 unless authorized thereunder and a determination has been made
for a specific proceeding that indemnification of the trustee is permissible in
the circumstances because the trustee has met the relevant standard of conduct
set forth in Section 5.03. (b) The determination shall be made: (1) if there are
two or more disinterested trustees, by the board of trustees by a majority vote
of all the disinterested trustees (a majority of whom shall for such purpose
constitute a quorum), orb ya majority of the members of a committee of two
or more disinterested trustees appointed by such a vote; (2) by special legal
counsel: (A) selected in the manner prescribed in (1) above; or (B) if there are
fewer than two disinterested trustees, selected by the board of trustees, in
which selection trustees who do not qualify as disinterested trustees may
participate; or (3) by the members, if any, but trustees who do not qualify as
disinterested trustees may not vote as members on the determination, (c)
Evaluation as to reasonableness of expenses shall be made in the same manner
as the determination that indemnification is permissible, described in (b)
above, except that if there are fewer than two disinterested trustees or if the
determination is made by special legal counsel, such evaluation shall be made
by those entitled under (b)(2) above to select special legal counsel.
5.05 Indemnification for Reasonable Expenses of Successful
Defense. The corporation shall indemnify a trustee who was successful, on the
merits or otherwise, in the defense of any proceeding to which the trustee was
a party because the trustee was a trustee of the corporation against reasonable
expenses incurred by the trustee in connection with the proceeding.
5.06 Advance or Reimbursement of Litigation Expenses, (a) The
corporation shall, before final disposition of a proceeding, advance funds to
pay for or reimburse the reasonable expenses incurred by a trustee who is a
party to a proceeding because the trustee is a trustee if the trustee delivers to
the corporation: (1) a written affirmation of the trustee’s good faith belief that
the trustee has met the relevant standard of conduct described in Section 5.03
or that the proceeding involves conduct for which liability has been eliminated
under a provision of the Articles of Incorporation authorized by Code Section
14-3-202(b)(4); and (2) the trustee’s written undertaking to repay any funds
advanced if it is ultimately determined that the trustee is not entitled to
indemnification under this Article V. Such undertaking must be an unlimited
general obligation of the trustee; the board of trustees may but need not require

<a id="ga30-p718"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=720 printed_page=718 -->

that it be secured, and may but need not accept it without reference to the
financial ability of the trustee to make repayment, (b) Authorizations under
this Section 5.06 shall be made by the board of trustees: (1) if there are two or
more disinterested trustees, by a majority vote of all the disinterested trustees
(a majority of whom shall for such purpose constitute a quorum) or by a
majority of the members of a committee of two or more disinterested trustees
appointed by such a vote; or (2) if there are fewer than two disinterested
trustees, by the affirmative vote of a majority of trustees present when a
quorum is present, in which authorization trustees who do not qualify as
disinterested trustees may participate.
5.07 Indemnification Permitted by the Code. Notwithstanding the
foregoing, the corporation must indemnify and advance expenses to a trustee
to the fullest extent permitted by the Code, and may not indemnify and
advance expenses to any trustee, officer, employee, or agent to an extent not
permitted by the Code.
5.08 Indemnification of Officers, Employees, and Agents, (a) The
corporation shall indemnify and advance expenses to an officer, and may
indemnify and advance expenses to an employee or agent by board of trustees
resolution, to the extent that the corporation indemnifies and advances
expenses to trustees pursuant to Section 5.03 above, (b) The corporation shall
indemnify an officer who was successful, on the merits or otherwise, in the
defense of any proceeding, to the same extent to which a trustee would be
entitled to indemnification under Section 5.05.
5.09 Insurance. The corporation may, but is not required to,
purchase and maintain insurance on behalf of one or more trustees, officers,
employees, or agents against liability, whether or not the corporation would
have power to indemnify him against the same liability.
5.10 Conflicting Interest Transactions. The validity and
ramifications of conflicting interest transactions are governed by the Code and
the Internal Revenue Code.
VI. AMENDMENT
(Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code §§ 14-3-1020 through -1022)
6.01 Amendment by Trustees, (a) The board of trustees may
amend or repeal the Bylaws, or adopt new Bylaws provided such amendment,
repeal, or new Bylaws do not conflict with the PCA Governing Documents.
6.02 Bylaw Increasing Quorum or Majority for Trustees, (a) A
Bylaw that fixes a greater quorum or voting requirement for the board of
trustees than is required by these present Bylaws may be adopted, amended, or
repealed by the trustees only by a majority of the entire board of trustees.

<a id="ga30-p719"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=721 printed_page=719 -->

VII. MISCELLANEOUS
7.01 Merger. These matters are governed by Georgia Nonprofit
Corporation Code Sections 14-3-1101 through -1107 as amended.
7.02 Sale of Assets. These matters are governed by Georgia
Nonprofit Corporation Code Sections 14-3-1201 through -1202 as amended.
7.03 Dissolution. These matters are governed by Georgia
Nonprofit Corporation Code Sections 14-3-1401 through -1410 as amended.
7.04 Foreign Corporation. These matters are governed by Georgia
Nonprofit Corporation Code Sections 14-3-1501 through -1510 as amended.
7.05 Records and Reports. These matters are governed by Georgia
Nonprofit Corporation Code Sections 14-3-1601 through -1622 as amended.
7.06 Meetings Generally. All meetings shall be governed by the
most recent edition of Robert's Rules of Order, except to the extent that that
book is inconsistent with these Bylaws or with the Code.
The foregoing are attested by me as the current Bylaws of the corporation,
previously adopted by the Board of Trustees. Witness my hand and seal this
, 2002 .
Glenn Fogle, Secretary

MINUTES OF ACTION OF
BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF
PCA RETIREMENT AND BENEFITS. INC.
TAKEN BY UNANIMOUS WRITTEN CONSENT
Pursuant to the provisions of the Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code
(Georgia Code 14-3-101 et seq.), we, the undersigned trustees of PCA
Retirement and Benefits, Inc., do hereby unanimously consent to and adopt the
following resolutions, which action shall have the same force and effect as if
taken by us by unanimous affirmative vote at a meeting of the board of trustees
of said corporation, duly called and held pursuant to applicable provisions of
the Georgia Nonprofit Corporation Code, and direct that the written consent to
such action shall be filed with the minutes of the proceedings of the trustees of
the corporation.
A. Articles of Incorporation.

RESOLVED, that the Articles of Incorporation of this corporation
granted by the Secretary of State of Georgia, in February, 2002, be and are
hereby accepted.

<a id="ga30-p720"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=722 printed_page=720 -->

FURTHER RESOLVED, that the secretary of the corporation is
hereby directed to place a copy of said Articles of Incorporation, together with
the certificate of the Secretary of State thereon, in the corporate records book.
B. Bylaws.

RESOLVED, that the proposed Bylaws accompanying these
minutes be and are hereby adopted as the Bylaws of this corporation.
FURTHER RESOLVED, that the secretary of the corporation is
hereby directed to incorporate said Bylaws in the corporate records book
directly preceding these Minutes, and to attest the same under his hand and
seal as the Bylaws so adopted.
C. Seal.

RESOLVED, that the seal impressed on the margin of this page is
hereby accepted as the seal of the corporation.
D. Election of Officers.

RESOLVED, that the following persons are hereby elected to the
offices shown, each officer to serve until the later of the next annual meeting
of the board of trustees or until his respective successor is duly elected and
qualified:
James L. Hughes President
Glenn Fogle Secretary
Ralph Paden Treasurer
E. Bank Accounts.

RESOLVED, that the president of the corporation is hereby
authorized to open bank and investment accounts on behalf of the corporation,
and to execute resolutions establishing such accounts on behalf of the
corporation.
FURTHER RESOLVED, that the resolutionts) in the form
attached to these minutes dealing with the designation of banks or other
institutions as depository or depositories for the corporation be and hereby are
adopted as resolutions of the board of trustees.
FURTHER RESOLVED, that any two of the following officers or
employees are authorized to sign checks on behalf of the corporation:

<a id="ga30-p721"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=723 printed_page=721 -->

President
Business Manager
Administrative Assistant
Designated Service Associate
FURTHER RESOLVED, that the proper officers of the
corporation be and hereby are authorized and directed to open such additional
accounts with said bank or any other institution as may be selected as
depositories for the corporation in the discretion of any of the officers of the
corporation, and to deposit therein funds of the corporation, drafts, checks and
notes of the corporation payable on said accounts to be made in the corporate
name.
FURTHER RESOLVED, that such officers be and hereby are
authorized to execute and deliver corporate resolutions on such forms as may
be presented or required by said banks, said forms to be completed with such
information as the executing officers may deem to be in the best interest of the
corporation.
FURTHER RESOLVED, that all such resolutions which may be
required by banks and other institutions hereafter selected by the corporation
dealing with the designation of such banks and other institutions as
depositories be and hereby are adopted as resolutions of the board of trustees;
and the secretary or assistant secretary of the corporation may hereafter attest
to and execute such bank resolutions and/or forms without additional action of
the board of trustees.
F. Fiscal Year.

RESOLVED, that the fiscal year of the corporation is hereby
determined to commence on January 1 of each year and end on December 31 of
the same year, until otherwise determined by this board of trustees.
G. Organizational Expenses.

RESOLVED, that the treasurer be and hereby is authorized to pay
all fees and expenses incident to and necessary for the organization and tax
exemption of the corporation.
H. Racially Nondiscriminatory Policy.

RESOLVED, that PCA Retirement and Benefits, Inc. shall not
discriminate in any way on the basis of race, color, or national or ethnic origin
in any of its activities or programs.
IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the hand and seal of each member of the
board of trustees is set forth below, effective as of this first day of March.
2002 .

ATTACHMENT FOUR
FIFTH AMENDMENT TO THE
TRUST AGREEMENT OF THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
TAX-SHELTERED ANNUITY PLAN
THIS IS THE FIFTH AMENDMENT to the Trust Agreement for the
Presbyterian Church in America Voluntary Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan
(hereinafter referred to as the “Trust”), being renamed the Trust Agreement for
the Presbyterian Church in America Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan.
Under the provisions of Section 6.01 of the Trust, the Trust Agreement
may be amended at any time upon approval of the General Assembly.
Accordingly, pursuant to the authority contained in Section 6.01, the Trust
Agreement is modified in the following particulars, effective upon ratification
by the General Assembly of this Fifth Amendment, to wit:
1.
The words “Voluntary Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan” are replaced in
every place they appear in the Trust with the words “Tax-Sheltered Annuity
Plan.”
2.
All parts of the Trust not inconsistent herewith are hereby ratified and
confirmed.
IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the Trustees have caused this Fifth
Amendment to be executed this 1st day of March, 2002.
TRUSTEES:
BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE
INSURANCE, ANNUITY AND RELIEF FUNDS
OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

EXCERPT FROM THE MINUTES
OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA

WHEREAS, the Board of Trustees of the Insurance, Annuity and
Relief Funds of the Presbyterian Church has approved adoption of the Fifth
Amendment to the Trust Agreement for the Presbyterian Church in America
Voluntary Tax-Sheltered Annuity Plan, and that the resolution related to this
Amendment be adopted, subject to approval by the General Assembly;

<a id="ga30-p723"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=725 printed_page=723 -->

WHEREAS, the General Assembly has determined that this
amendment recommended by the Trustees should be approved;

RESOLVED, that adoption of the amendment is hereby ratified by the
General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America.
