<a id="ga30-p724"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=726 printed_page=724 -->

report of
reformed university ministries
to the 30thG en era l assembly
of the Presbyterian Church in America
Reformed University Ministries has a goal of building the church now and for
the future. This is accomplished by: “Reaching students for Christ and
equipping students to serve.” This speaks eloquently of our commitment to
our covenant children and our obedience to the Great Commission. College
years are a time of great opportunity for reaching students with the Gospel. So
many testify in later life that their college years were the time when they
seriously considered God’s truth.
reformed university fellowship
Reformed University Fellowship (RUF) offers the truth of God’s Word to
students who are searching. By working within the context of the church, the
Body of Christ, we follow His leadership as He builds His Kingdom on earth.
Students are instructed in Evangelism and Missions, Growth in Grace,
Fellowship and Service, and a Biblical World-and-Life View. We are
strengthening the church by ministering to covenant children and by
evangelizing the lost on campuses across this nation. An ordained PCA
minister leads each RUF while actively working to accomplish goals in these
four major areas.
reformed university fellowship international
Reformed University Fellowship International (RUFI) is reaching out to “all
nations,” sharing the Gospel of Christ and training Christian internationals to
serve Him all over the globe. The hundreds of students involved in RUFI are
from at least 40 different countries. Some of these countries are restricted or
closed, prohibiting missionaries to serve there. Many of the students return to
their own countries to build the Church at home. Our four RUFI campus
ministers are privileged to introduce internationals to Christ. One of their
goals is to help Christian internationals in their struggle to live for Christ in
their own cultures.
campus interns
Campus interns allow a much broader outreach on the campuses. In this
learning and ministering position, young men and women, all recent college
graduates, work directly with a campus minister, receiving on-the-job-training

<a id="ga30-p725"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=727 printed_page=725 -->

in evangelism, small group leadership, and one-to-one ministry. A planned
program of biblical and theological training accompanies this practical service.
After their internship with Reformed University Ministries, interns disperse
into the market place, carrying to their future vocations and churches their
deepened understanding of God’s Word and experience in His service. Truly,
campus interns are learning to be “an instrument for noble purposes, made
holy, useful to the Master and prepared to do any good work” (II Timothy
2 :21 ).
ministry distinctives

Weekly large group, small groups, and one-to-one staff/student meetings
provide the structure for campus ministry. Each is essential in ministering to
college students. The weekly large group meeting teaches the Word in truth
by Bible exposition, examination, and discussion of Scripture. Small groups
focus on study, prayer, and fellowship. One-to-one meetings between students
and staff members offer in-depth discipling of Christians, evangelistic
encounters and the building of trust-confidence relationships. Each of these
three parts supports and strengthens the others. One of the most distinctive
things about the ministry of RUF is its emphasis upon the development of a
biblical world-and-life view. Only as we see through God’s perspective can
we understand the truth of any situation. Only as students learn to think
biblically will it make a real difference in the Church and the world.
Conferences
Summer Conference 2001 provided solid Bible exposition for equipping
students to better understand and live the Christian life, with teaching, training
and equipping in skills related to reaching others for Christ, and fellowship and
fun in beautiful surroundings among Christians from over 60 college and
university campuses. It was held May 14-19, 2001 and was extremely well
attended. Well over 700 students and 150 staff were able to leave the routine
of books and schedules to reflect on life, deal with questions, and be taught
from God’s Word. The theme of the conference was the Sufficiency of
Scripture and the Reverend Ray Cortese from Seven Rivers Presbyterian
Church in Lecanto, Florida and Dr. Richard Pratt, professor of Old Testament
at Reformed Theological Seminary in Orlando, Florida expounded the
Scriptures. Students were eager to receive God’s Word. The 2002 conference,
which will address the Doctrine of Justification, will be held May 13-18, 2002.
Our speakers will be the Reverend Paul Hahn from Redeemer Presbyterian
Church in Austin, Texas, and The Reverend Jeffrey Lancaster from Redeemer
Presbyterian Church in New Orleans, Louisiana.

<a id="ga30-p726"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=728 printed_page=726 -->

conferences provide activities and a relaxing atmosphere a swell as a
concentrated time for biblical teaching that is new to many students. This
teaching is done through elective workshops, which the student may choose
(two per day), small group study, prayer, and large group teaching each
evening. One campus minister rejoiced that, “Many students are
understanding the Gospel for the first time and many Christians are being
encouraged and strengthened in their walk with God.” In addition to Summer
Conference, regional Fall, Winter, and Spring conferences are held throughout
the year.
staff training
In 2001, there were seven weeks of training for the field staff. This in-depth
training is a distinctive of the ministry and provides philosophical, practical,
and reflective instruction to RUF campus ministers, interns, and staff. A
Foundations of Campus Ministry Conference was held in Atlanta, Georgia,
during August for those interested in learning how Reformed University
Ministries does its work, whether church staff, missionary or lay person. This
year the conference will be held August 5-8, 2002 at Covenant Theological
Seminary in St. Louis, Missouri.
Growth
The year 2001 was a year of growth as eight new campus ministries were
started. The new ministries are: Brown University, Covenant College,
Mississippi College, Texas Tech University, University of Georgia RUFI,
University of Oklahoma, University of Tulsa, and Washington and Lee
University. This growth placed RUF on 62 campuses nationwide, with a hope
of reaching 68 total campuses in 2002. Also, we had a total of 53 interns in
2001 and 61 are expected in 2002. Reformed University Ministries continues
to grow with at least four new ministries scheduled for 2002, two of which are
University of North Florida and Samford University.
conclusion
What is the reason for this growth and excitement? One campus minister told
us, “God’s Word is alive and active. The Gospel is powerful. That is why
RUF is growing ... RUF seeks to be faithful to God’s Word and to biblically
minister to students.”
In June 2001, the 29th General Assembly voted overwhelmingly to make
Reformed University Ministries a permanent committee of the Presbyterian Church in America. We are humbled by the confidence placed in us. Along

<a id="ga30-p727"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=729 printed_page=727 -->

with this trust comes a sense of added responsibility to proclaim the Gospel on
college campuses across the country.
Only God could bring together students and ministers from so many different
walks of life to accomplish His purposes. Each person that is touched by
Reformed University Ministries will, in turn, touch many other people in the
course of his or her life. One campus intern noted that, “A true measure of
success for RUF . . . may only be seen in the light of eternity.” How true!
And how glorious!
recommendations
1. That the General Assembly give thanks to God for the ministry of
Reformed University Ministries, for its faithfulness to the Scriptures, the
Reformed faith, the Great Commission, for the students reached by RUF,
its staff, its Permanent and Affiliated Committee members, and for those
who support the work of Reformed University Ministries through their
prayers and gifts.
2. That the General Assembly encourage the congregations and Presbyteries
of the Presbyterian Church in America to form Affiliated Committees on
Reformed University Ministries and work together to start and fund new
RUF works within their bounds.
3. That the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the
Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of Reformed
University Ministries by contributing the Partnership Shares approved by
the General Assembly.
4. That the General Assembly commend TE Billy Joseph for his 22 years of
faithful ministry and express its appreciation and praise to God for his
valuable contribution to the ministry of Reformed University Ministries at
the University of Alabama and across the denomination.
5. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the meetings of the
Committee on Reformed University Ministries for June 21, 2001 and
October 2, 2001; and the minutes of the meeting of the MNA
Subcommittee on Campus Ministries for March 1, 2001.
6. That the General Assembly receive the financial audit for Reformed
University Ministries for the fiscal year ending December 31, 2001 by
Carr, Riggs & Ingram, LLP.
7. That the General Assembly approve the 2003 budget of Reformed
University Ministries, and note with thanksgiving the opportunities and
challenges represented by the entire 2003 budget.
8. That the General Assembly receive as information the Attachment for a
listing of presbyteries and their affiliated committees, campus staff and

<a id="ga30-p728"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=730 printed_page=728 -->

location of ministries and the 2003 budget for the entire ministry including
affiliated committees.
9. That the General Assembly reelect TE Rod S. Mays as Coordinator of
Reformed University Ministries for the 2002/2003 term.
10. That the General Assembly make the following necessary changes to the
Rules of Assembly Operation to reflect the new status of Reformed
University Ministries as a Permanent Committee of the Presbyterian Church in America.
A. RAO Article IV., 4-2. — The line after "Mission to North America
(MNA)" add "Reformed University Ministries (RUM)"
B. RAO Article IV., 4-2., paragraph 2 — after "Mission to North
America" strike “and”, insert a comma
C. RAO Article IV., 4-2., paragraph 2 — after “Mission to the World”
insert "and Reformed University Ministries"
D. RAO Article IV., 4-8., line 3 -- strike "three" and insert "four"
E. RAO Article IV., 4-9., line 1 —strike "three" and insert "four"
F. RAO Article V., 5-1. line 2 — strike "nineteen (19)", and insert
"twenty (20)"
G. RAO Article V., 5-1. - strike period after "(8) Ridge Haven
Conference Center", insert a semicolon and insert "(9) Reformed
University Ministries."
H. RAO Article VI. — add new section: "6-4. The Committee on
Reformed University Ministries.” - “The affairs of the Church in the
area of campus ministry are assigned to the Committee on Reformed
University Ministries, whose duties and authority shall be designated
by the General Assembly."
I. RAO Article XIII., 13-1. — add new item "12. Reformed University
Ministries - Permanent Comm. - Touching Reformed University
Ministries"
J. Corporate Bylaws, Article V. -- insert "E. The Committee on
Reformed University Ministries" — "The affairs of the Church in the
area of campus ministries on college and university campuses are
assigned to the Committee on Reformed University Ministries, whose
duties and authority shall be designated by the General Assembly."
K. Corporate Bylaws, Article V. strike “E.” in “E. Other Committees”
and insert “F.”
L. Corporate Bylaws, Article V. strike “F.” in “F. Boards of Agencies”
and insert “G.”
11. That the General Assembly pray for the ministry of Reformed University
Ministries.

<a id="ga30-p729"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=731 printed_page=729 -->

ATTACHMENT
The Committee on Reformed University Ministries provides support services
to presbyteries whose campus ministries are affiliated with Reformed
University Ministries. The presbyteries receiving services make a contribution
toward their cost. Presbyteries and their affiliated committees are completely
responsible for the funding of ministries within their area and for determining
the budget for such ministries. Reformed University Ministries receives and
disburses funds only as directed by the presbyteries and their affiliated
committees.
From January 1, 2001 to December 31, 2001, Reformed University Ministries
received $4,298,917 and disbursed $4,262,445 as directed by presbyteries and
their affiliated committees. The funds are received for particular ministries,
which are the responsibility of a presbytery as noted below. The responsible
body receives an audit report of its funds. The following list gives the
presbyteries and their affiliated committees, campus staff, and location of
ministries receiving support services through Reformed University Ministries,
and other ministries and staff affiliated with Reformed University Ministries.

RIDGE HAVEN REPORT
TO THE THIRTIETH GENERAL ASSEMBLY
PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA
Ridge Haven exists as a camps, conference, retreat center, and residential
community to proclaim the gospel to the lost, nurture God’s people in the truth
of God’s word, and provide a place of rest, refuge, and renewal so that God is
glorified and God’s people are challenged and equipped for service and
ministry in His kingdom. - The Ridge Haven Purpose Statement
INTRODUCTION
With thankfulness for the prayers of the people of the PCA and an increasing
involvement of the people and her churches, it is our privilege to make the
following report to the General Assembly.
ACTIVITIES
2001 was a busier than normal year for Ridge Haven. Along with changes in
staffing, updating of buildings, the design and installation of a new website,
and acquiring credit card service for our customers, the State mandated water
system changes have consumed much energy and resources. Preparations for
Ridge Haven to install new playground equipment (WIC Love Gift for 2001)
also required grading and clearing work.
• New staff included the Ministry Director (November 2000), Food
Service Director, Food Service and Maintenance Assistants, and
Resident Manager.
• As provided by special gifts, building changes included Shepherds
Hall renovations and minor repairs, new paint, and new carpet. The
cafeteria was also provided with new double-stack convection ovens.
• Late in 2001 Ridge Haven moved into the modem age by making
available the use of the four main credit card companies, MasterCard,
VISA, American Express, and Discover. All registrations and
payments may now be made by credit card! The customer response
has been overwhelmingly favorable.

<a id="ga30-p737"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=739 printed_page=737 -->

• The new Ridge Haven website can be found at www.RidgeHaven.org.
The new Ridge Haven site, linked directly to the PCA site, has
provided us an even stronger presence on the World Wide Web. The
new site also provides an interactive ability to register for camps and
conferences and utilize credit card payments on line.
• Water system changes to date include a new well, refurbished main
water storage tank, reconstructed and/or newly constructed pump
houses, sections of new and/or replaced water lines, new well pump,
new boost pumps, and a new secondary storage tank. Water system
upgrade costs through the end of 2001 have been $157,013.
Contributions for the project through the end of 2001 have been
$169,356. Approximately $50,000 in additional expenses for work
already complete is anticipated in March or April of 2002. An
additional well, well house, well pump, connecting pipe, water
treatment equipment and another secondary water storage tank are
expected to be put on line before summer. Estimated costs for this last
part of the project are $80,000. As costs are exceeding contributions,
we are expecting to utilize the line of credit approved by the Twenty-
eighth General Assembly. We again appeal to the churches of the
Assembly to help us meet the cost of making the changes mandated by
the State of North Carolina to avoid any debt.
• Ridge Haven received the 2001 Love Gift at the annual PresWIC
Presidents Training Seminar on March 1 of 2002. The $75,000 gift
provided a playground, Iverson and Dean Lodge renovations, and
several other improvements. The Iverson and Dean Lodge renovations
and the installation of the new playground are expected to be
completed before the beginning of summer camp.
For these and all the blessings of the Lord we give Him praise and glory. We
ask that you join us in this thanksgiving.
FINANCIAL CONDITION
An analysis of Ridge Haven’s financial condition has been made and provided
a san introduction to the 2003 Budget proposal and notes. Partnership share
support from the churches of the Assembly over the last four-year period have
been as follows: 1998 - 30.15%, 1999 - 23.24%, 2000 - 25.75%, 2001 -
24.49%.

<a id="ga30-p738"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=740 printed_page=738 -->

TESTIMONIES
• This was the best retreat I have...been to in 7 years of youth ministry...
- YoWAW, February 2002
• Thank you for an amazing week... - Covenant Family Conference,
August 2001
• I have traveled all over this great country but think this time spent...at
Ridge Haven has been my greatest blessing. - Keenager Conference,
October 2001
• Honey, forget the cruise, we ’re coming back to Ridge Haven - Wife to
her husband - Summer, 2001
ONGOING NEEDS AND PRAYER REQUESTS
• 1,000 summer camp registrations for 2002.
• An Acts 16:31 camper response rate of 15%.
• 8,500 guests for 2002.
• Improvement of partnership share participation by 10.5% (from 24.5%
in 2001 to 35% in 2002).
• Receipt of the necessary funds for completion of the North Carolina-
mandated water system upgrades ($130,645 of the requested $300,000
is still needed).
• Our ability to communicate and present Ridge Haven effectively to all
the churches and people of the PCA through appropriate literature, our
website, and our General Assembly presentations.
RECOMMENDATIONS

<a id="ga30-p739"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=741 printed_page=739 -->

COMMITTEE TO RESPOND TO PROTEST
REGARDING MODERATOR’S RULING
ON A POINT OF ORDER CONCERNING
THE EXTENT TO WHICH
THE BILLS AND OVERTURES COMMITTEE
MAY AMEND OVERTURES
There was a point of order raised in Thursday’s session [see 30-47, p.210]:
That the Bills and Overtures Committee Report be
found out of order, in that the Committee throughout its
proceedings violated “RAO” 13-6 e.; “Manual of Operations
for the Committee of Commissioners” (C.3.c, p.3). ... as well
as our long-standing interpretation and practice under these
rules, by forbidding amendment to Overtures proposing BCO
Amendments; (and that in the context of proposed BCO
changes of tremendous importance and controversy); and that
the Committee be instructed to reconvene and adopt its report
lawfully under the rules as so specified above.
The moderator ruled against the point of order. The ruling of the moderator
was appealed to the floor. The moderator’s ruling was sustained. On Friday,
in the last session of the Assembly, a protest was made against the Assembly’s
sustaining the moderator’s ruling on the point of order. The moderator
appointed a committee of three (TE Henry Lewis Smith, TE D. Clair Davis,
RE Samuel Duncan) to respond to the protest against the Assembly’s
sustaining the moderator’s ruling on the point of order [see 30-61, p. 290], We
therefore submit the following response.
1. The point of order inaccurately alleges that it was ruled in committee
that no amendments to Overtures proposing BCO Amendments could
be made. The point at issue is not that no amendments of any sort may
be made by the Bills and Overtures Committee [B&O], but rather that
major, substantive changes may not be made by the committee for the
reason that it would constitute new business, which is prohibited by
“RAO” 13-5, d. For example perfecting language “that would
improve the statement” (“Manual of Operations for the Committee of
Commissioners” [C.3.c, p.3]) would be, and has been offered by the
committee on numerous occasions.

<a id="ga30-p740"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=742 printed_page=740 -->

2. The point of order alleges, “that the Committee throughout its
proceedings violated “RAO” 13-6 e,” which simply states that the
B&O report shall include:
Reference to overtures by number with a brief statement
of content and recommended answer. An overture
requesting an amendment of the Book of Church Order
shall be reported by the Committee of Commissioners
printing explicitly any language that would be added or
deleted from the Book of Church Order through the
adoption of the overture, and such overture shall be sent
to the presbyteries, if approved by the General Assembly, in this particular form.
“RAO” 13-6 e. only says that the committee report shall make
“reference to overtures by number with a brief statement of content
and recommended answer.” It does not give the authority to the Bills
and Overtures Committee to make substantive alterations or major
changes in the meaning or intent of a proposed amendment to the Book of Church Order. B&O could make minor, editorial changes, but not
substantive alterations or major changes in the meaning or intent of a
proposed amendment to the Book of Church Order.
3. The point of order alleges that the Committee throughout its
proceedings violated “Manual of Operations for the Committee of
Commissioners” (C.3.c, p.3). It should be noted that hierarchy of
parliamentary authorities for matters of procedure (from highest to
lowest) is: 1) The Constitution of the PCA (Westminster Standards
and Book of Church Order), 2) The “Rules of Assembly Operations,”
our own special rules of procedure, and 3) Robert’s Rules of Order
The PCA Constitution takes precedence over the “RAO” and
RRO. The “RAO” takes precedence over RRO. Moreover, the
“Manual of Operations for the Committee of Commissioners” would
be a fourth-level parliamentary authority, if that. Since the
Constitution of the PCA does not speak directly to the issue, and the
“RAO” does not specify that the B&O Committee may make major
substantive changes in the meaning or intent of a proposed amendment
to the Book of Church Order, Roberts Rules of Order would apply:
Variations in the Power of the Resolutions Committee.
In the simplest arrangements, the Resolutions
Committee has only the power to put resolutions in the
proper form, eliminate duplication where similar

<a id="ga30-p741"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=743 printed_page=741 -->

resolutions are offered, and ensure that all resolutions
relating to a specific subject will be offered ina logical
sequence. In other cases the committee is given the
authority to make substantive alterations in a resolution,
but only with the sponsor’s consent; while in others, by
the vote of the committee - sometimes a two-third’s vote
- the substance of the resolution can be altered and the
resolution can be reported as though the committee had
originated it. (RRO, 10th Edition, p. 616)
The Bills and Overtures Committee is analogous to the Resolutions
Committee in RRO parlance. To make substantive alterations (major
changes) in an overture, the Bills and Overtures Committee needs to
be “given the authority to make substantive alterations in a resolution,”
i.e., specifically authorized by our “RAO” (either with the permission
of the sponsoring Presbytery or by two-third’s vote of the committee)
to make radical revisions in an overture. There is no such
authorization in our “RAO.” The point of order is based on the
premise that silence in the “RAO” is license for the Bills and
Overtures Committee to make major substantive changes. We believe
that Robert’s Rules of Order (supra) require that the “RAO” must
explicitly give the committee the authority to make substantive
alterations in an overture proposing an amendment to the BCO. We
respectfully disagree with the author of the point of order’s use of an
argument from silence in this instance.
4. The point of order alleges that “the Committee throughout its
proceedings violated ... our long-standing interpretation and practice
under these rules, by forbidding amendment to Overtures proposing
BCO Amendments.” We acknowledge the Bills and Overtures
Committee properly has and may make alterations to improve the
language and otherwise perfect overtures and resolutions committed to
it. We reiterate that the point of order inaccurately alleges that it was
ruled that no amendments to Overtures proposing BCO Amendments
may be made. The point at issue is not that no amendments of any sort
may be made by the Bills and Overtures Committee but rather that no
major, substantive changes may be made by the committee.” Previous
attempts of that nature have been ruled out of order. For example the
Minutes of the Twenty-third General Assembly state:
Recommendation 12 as presented by the Committee of
Commissioners [on Bills and Overtures] was ruled out of
order because it was deemed that changes recommended

<a id="ga30-p742"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=744 printed_page=742 -->

by the Committee of Commissioners constituted the
introduction of new business. The Moderator’s ruling
was challenged and the Assembly upheld the ruling.
(.M21GA, p. 235)
There is evidence that there is “a long-standing interpretation and
practice” whereby the Bills and Overtures Committee may make
substantive alterations or major changes in the meaning or intent of
proposed amendments to the Book of Church Order. Yet such an
erroneous and unsanctioned long-standing interpretation and practice
does not invalidate the roberts rules of Order’s directive on the
matter:
In some organizations, a particular practice may
sometimes come to be followed as a matter of
established custom so that it is treated practically as if it
were prescribed by a rule of order. However, if such a
practice is or becomes in conflict with the parliamentary
authority or any written rule of the organization, and a
point of order citing the conflict is raised at any time,
the custom falls to the ground, and the conflicting
provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule
thereafter must be complied with, unless a specific rule
of order (or, in appropriate circumstances, a standing
rule) is added or amended to incorporate the custom. If
there is no contrary provision in the parliamentary
authority or written rules, the established custom should
be adhered to unless the assembly, by majority vote,
agrees to do otherwise.
In other words, a practice that is out of order is not made in order
simply because it is a practice of long standing. The principle of stare
decisis et non quieta movere (“to adhere to precedents, and not to
unsettle things that are established”) is a principle of civil law of
judicial decisions setting binding precedents. That is not a matter of
parliamentary procedure in church courts. F. L. Patton’s statement -
“Historical Students may tell us what the Church has thought upon the
subject, and dogmatic theologians may tell us what the Church ought
to think; but it is only as the General Assembly decides concrete cases
in appellate jurisdiction, and the principles of stare decisis may be
supposed to govern subsequent deliverances, that the area of tolerated
divergence [from the confession of Faith] can be defined” (The
revision of the confession of Faith. Read before the Presbyterian

<a id="ga30-p743"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=745 printed_page=743 -->

Social Union, New York, December 2, 1889, p. 6) - relates to judicial
appellate cases on theological controversy, not to parliamentary
procedure in church courts as relates to committee reports. In short,
we do not believe that the stare decisis argument may properly be
applied in this instance.
5. The desire of the commissioner (who was the author of the point of
order and protest) to advance another proposed BCO amendment that
was substantively different from the proposed BCO amendments sent
to the Assembly by other Presbyteries could have been accomplished
by his getting his Presbytery to approve his overture following the
procedures of “RAO” 10-4, 10-5, 10-6, or 10-10 at least sixty (60)
days prior to the opening of the General Assembly. In fact, one such
overture was presented in this manner (Overture 29 from Westminster
Presbytery, “Amend BCO 24-1 to Record All Exceptions with
Presbytery and Have Stated Clerk Report All Exceptions to the
General Assembly”).
6. Finally, a similar point of order was raised at the 24th General Assembly, at which time the moderator requested advice from the
Constitutional Business Committee (CCB). The CCB advised that
Committee of Commissioners’ amendments to overtures that go
further than mere improvements to the statement were a technical
violation, which could be resolved by having the amendment moved
from the floor of the General Assembly as a substitute for the whole.
This advice was adopted by the 24th General Assembly. Likewise this
option was available to the commissioner who raised this point of
order, but he failed to move his amendment as a substitute for the
whole on the floor of the Assembly. It should be noted that the 24th
General Assembly instructed the CCB to find a solution to this
problem, which it has apparently failed to do (see M24GA, p. 225, and
M25GA, p. 131).
Moderator’s Committee to Respond to Protest of TE David Coffin et altera:
TE Henry Lewis Smith, chairman
TE D. Clair Davis
RE Samuel Duncan

<a id="ga30-p744"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=30 pdf_page=746 printed_page=744 -->
